### Question 1: Define Cloud Computing. List any FOUR essential characteristics as per NIST. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

**Four Essential Characteristics (NIST):**
1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources provisioned automatically without human interaction.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Available over the internet from any client device.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic resource assignment.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Quick horizontal/vertical scaling.

*(The 5th characteristic is Measured Service.)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with a biomedical example for each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Model | Provider Manages | User Manages | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Hardware, networking, storage | OS, runtime, apps, data | AWS EC2 hosting a hospital's internal server |
| **PaaS** | Hardware, OS, middleware | Apps and data | Deploying a custom ML model for arrhythmia detection on Google App Engine |
| **SaaS** | Everything | Only usage/data input | Practo, Teladoc, or any hosted EHR portal |

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the three cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — with a neat diagram showing the division of responsibilities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three primary cloud service models form a layered hierarchy, with the user managing fewer responsibilities as they move up the stack.

**1. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS):**
- Provider supplies raw infrastructure: virtual machines, storage, networks, servers.
- User is responsible for installing the **Operating System, middleware, runtime, applications, and data**.
- Example: **Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine**.
- Biomedical use: Hosting a hospital's internal database server on a rented VM.

**2. Platform as a Service (PaaS):**
- Provider manages the OS, middleware, and runtime.
- User focuses only on **application code and data**.
- Example: **Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk**.
- Biomedical use: Deploying a deep-learning model for diabetic retinopathy detection without worrying about GPU driver installation.

**3. Software as a Service (SaaS):**
- Provider delivers a complete, ready-to-use application over the internet.
- User simply accesses via a browser or thin client.
- Example: **Gmail, Microsoft 365, Epic EHR systems**.
- Biomedical use: A doctor accessing Electronic Health Records from a web portal.

**Division of Responsibilities Diagram (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Application} &\rightarrow \text{User (IaaS, PaaS), Provider (SaaS)} \\
\text{Data} &\rightarrow \text{User (all models)} \\
\text{Runtime / Middleware} &\rightarrow \text{User (IaaS), Provider (PaaS, SaaS)} \\
\text{OS} &\rightarrow \text{User (IaaS), Provider (PaaS, SaaS)} \\
\text{Virtualization} &\rightarrow \text{Provider (all)} \\
\text{Server / Storage / Network} &\rightarrow \text{Provider (all)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Discuss the role of cloud computing in biomedical applications. Mention any FIVE real-world applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Cloud computing has transformed biomedical engineering by providing **scalable, cost-effective, and accessible** platforms for healthcare delivery.

**Five Real-World Biomedical Applications:**

1. **Electronic Health Records (EHR):** Cloud-hosted patient records enable cross-hospital access, ensuring continuity of care. Example: Epic, Cerner systems on private clouds.

2. **Telemedicine and Remote Patient Monitoring:** Real-time streaming of vitals (ECG, SpO2, BP) from home patients to specialists via cloud dashboards.

3. **Medical Imaging Analytics:** Cloud GPUs process **MRI/CT scans** for AI-based tumor detection. Example: **Google Cloud Healthcare API**.

4. **Genomic Data Analysis:** Petabyte-scale genomic sequencing data is stored and analyzed in the cloud. Example: The **100,000 Genomes Project** (UK) on AWS.

5. **Drug Discovery and Molecular Docking:** Cloud HPC runs molecular simulations, drastically reducing the time to identify drug candidates. Example: **Schrödinger platform on AWS**.

**Key Advantages in Biomedical Context:**
- **Scalability** during pandemics (e.g., COVID-19 vaccine data).
- **Cost reduction** — pay-per-use avoids huge capital expenditure.
- **Disaster recovery** via geographically distributed cloud regions.
- **AI/ML integration** for predictive diagnostics.
- **Interoperability** between hospitals, labs, and insurance providers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students write generic cloud advantages (cost, scalability) without connecting them *specifically* to biomedical use cases. Always link your answer to **patient data, clinical workflows, or medical devices** to score the full 7 marks. Avoid writing about "storing photos" — the examiner expects healthcare-specific examples.

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of cloud computing. List the FOUR deployment models of cloud. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cloud Architecture — Block Diagram Description:**

The cloud architecture has two ends connected via a network (typically the internet):

**Front End (Client Side):**
- The user's device: PC, laptop, mobile, or biomedical IoT sensor.
- Includes the **client-side application and interface**.

**Back End (Provider Side):**
- **Application Layer:** SaaS applications, APIs.
- **Platform Layer:** OS, middleware, runtime environments.
- **Infrastructure Layer:** Physical servers, storage arrays, network equipment.
- **Virtualization Layer:** Hypervisors that create VMs from physical hardware.
- **Management & Security Layer:** IAM, monitoring, billing, threat detection.

*(The detailed Mermaid block diagram is provided in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cloud Capacity} &= N_{servers} \times V_{per\_server} \times R_{utilization} \\
\text{where} \quad R_{utilization} &\in [0,1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Four Deployment Models of Cloud:**

1. **Public Cloud** — Open for public use; owned by third parties (AWS, Azure, GCP).
2. **Private Cloud** — Used exclusively by a single organization; offers higher control and security.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Combines public and private; allows data and application portability.
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., multiple hospitals, government health departments).

**(b)** What is virtualization? Explain its role in cloud computing with a biomedical use case. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Virtualization:**
Virtualization is the process of creating a **virtual (rather than physical) version** of computing resources — including servers, storage, networks, and operating systems — using software called a **hypervisor**.

**Role in Cloud Computing:**

1. **Resource Optimization:** One physical server runs multiple VMs, maximizing hardware utilization.
2. **Isolation:** Each VM operates independently; a failure in one does not affect others.
3. **Scalability:** New VMs can be spun up in minutes to handle load spikes.
4. **Cost Efficiency:** Reduces the need for physical hardware, lowering CapEx and OpEx.
5. **Disaster Recovery:** VMs can be migrated between physical hosts without downtime.

**Types of Hypervisors:**
- **Type 1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen).
- **Type 2 (Hosted):** Runs as software on a host OS (e.g., Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).

**Biomedical Use Case:**

A large hospital chain needs to deploy a **Hospital Information System (HIS)** plus an **AI-based radiology module** on the same physical infrastructure. Using virtualization:
- **VM-1** runs the HIS (Windows Server, SQL database).
- **VM-2** runs the AI radiology module (Linux, Python, GPU-accelerated TensorFlow).
- **VM-3** hosts the telemedicine portal.
- All VMs share the same physical server but operate in **isolated environments**, ensuring that a crash in radiology AI does not bring down the HIS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** For virtualization questions, students often **omit naming the hypervisor** or fail to distinguish between Type 1 and Type 2. Always state at least one example. Additionally, **don't confuse virtualization with containerization** (Docker). Containers share an OS kernel; VMs do not — examiners *will* deduct marks if you mix them up.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ☁️ **NIST Definition** is the gold standard — quote it verbatim in definitions.
- 🏗️ **5 Essential Characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- 📚 **3 Service Models:** IaaS (you manage OS+apps), PaaS (you manage apps only), SaaS (you use only).
- 🌐 **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ⚡ **Virtualization** is enabled by **hypervisors** — know Type 1 vs Type 2 distinction.
- 🏥 **Biomedical Applications:** EHR, Telemedicine, Medical Imaging AI, Genomics, Drug Discovery.
- ⚠️ **Cloud ≠ Everything in IoT** — Real-time biomedical signals need **Edge/Fog** processing.
- 📊 **Key Formulas to Memorize:** Availability, Annual Downtime, PUE, Storage Capacity.
- 🔐 **Compliance:** Mention **HIPAA / GDPR / DISHA** (India's Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act) when discussing biomedical data in cloud.
- 💡 **Three Nines (99.9%)** is industry baseline; **Five Nines (99.999%)** is needed for life-critical systems.
- 🚀 **Always tie cloud advantages to a clinical/hospital scenario** — generic answers lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Systems. Sensor Interfaces: ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Embedded Systems. Sensor Interfaces: ECG # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Embedded System in Biomedical Context? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > An **Embedded System** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, application-specific computational system that is designed to perform a dedicated function within a larger electrical or mechanical framework, characterized by real-time computing constraints, low power consumption, and tight hardware–software integration. In the biomedical domain, an embedded system is the "invisible brain" inside medical devices such as pacemakers, digital stethoscopes, patient monitors, and wearable ECG patches. Unlike a general-purpose computer (laptop, desktop), an embedded system is *engineered to do one job extremely well, reliably, and continuously*, often running for years on a single coin cell battery. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Nurse Station > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** > Imagine the **Central Monitoring Station in a Cardiac ICU**. Nurses don't stare at raw electrical cables; they watch a **colorful screen showing waveforms**. The waveform (ECG) is the *language of the heart*, and the embedded system is the *translator* that: > 1. **Listens** to tiny electrical whispers (0.5–5 mV) from the body via electrodes. > 2. **Amplifies** these whispers into a loud, clear signal. > 3. **Cleans** the signal of background noise (mains hum, motion artifacts). > 4. **Converts** the analog heartbeat into digital numbers. > 5. **Decides** if the heart is healthy, irregular, or in danger — and triggers an alarm. The student should remember: *Electrode = Microphone; Instrumentation Amplifier = Megaphone; ADC = Translator; Microcontroller = Judge.* ## 1.3 The ECG Signal — Standard Clinical Metrics The **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** is a graphical record of the electrical activity of the heart, captured non-invasively from the body surface using surface electrodes. The standard clinical characteristics are: | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | **Signal Amplitude** | **0.5 mV to 5 mV** (peak-peak) | | **Frequency Bandwidth** | **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** | | **Diagnostic Bandwidth** | **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** (clinical standard) | | **Standard Sampling Rate** | **250 Hz to 500 Hz** (≥ 2 × f_max) | | **Output Impedance** | < **5 kΩ** (source) | | **CMRR Required** | > **80 dB** (ideally > 100 dB) | | **Electrode Type** | Ag/AgCl (Silver/Silver-Chloride) | | **Standard Leads** | 12-lead (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Highlight:** > The most frequently asked value is the **ECG amplitude (0.5–5 mV)**. The fact that it is *microvolt-to-millivolt* level is the *primary design driver* — the entire front-end circuit exists to **boost** this signal before digitization. ## 1.4 Why a Dedicated Sensor Interface is Mandatory > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight (Syllabus Anchor):** > A microcontroller's ADC cannot directly read a bioelectric signal. Three fundamental problems exist: > 1. **Amplitude is too small** (mV range, but ADC needs 0–3.3 V or 0–5 V). > 2. **The signal rides on a large DC offset** (half-cell potential of electrodes ≈ ±300 mV). > 3. **The signal is buried in noise** (mains interference, EMG, motion artifacts). Therefore, a **Sensor Interface Circuit (SIC)** or **Analog Front End (AFE)** is mandatory. The block chain is: **Electrode → Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) → Band-Pass Filter (HPF + LPF) → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → ADC → Microcontroller (DSP) → Display/IoT Link.** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of an ECG Sensor Interface The ECG acquisition chain is universally taught in KTU as a **5-stage pipeline**. Each stage addresses a specific signal-integrity problem. ### Stage 1 — Electrodes & Lead Configuration (The Sensors) > [!NOTE] > **Einthoven's Triangle (1903):** The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form a triangle around the heart. The three standard bipolar limb leads are: > - **Lead I** = $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ > - **Lead II** = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ > - **Lead III** = $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ > - **Einthoven's Law:** $II = I + III$ (vector sum identity) ### Stage 2 — Instrumentation Amplifier (The Heart of the AFE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a 3-Op-Amp IA, not a simple op-amp?** > A bioelectric signal is *differential and floating* (no clean ground). The IA provides: > - **High Differential Gain** (typically 100–1000) > - **High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** (> 80 dB, ideally > 100 dB) > - **High Input Impedance** (> 10 GΩ) to avoid loading the body > - **Adjustable gain via a single external resistor** The ICs most often used in KTU lab contexts are **INA128, AD620, AD8221**. ### Stage 3 — Analog Filtering (Signal Cleaning) Three filters are stacked: 1. **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — fc ≈ **0.05 Hz to 0.5 Hz**, removes DC electrode offset and baseline wander. 2. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — fc ≈ **40 Hz to 150 Hz**, removes high-frequency EMG and RF noise. 3. **Notch Filter (Band-Stop)** — fo = **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA), removes power-line interference. ### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) The Nyquist theorem mandates sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For ECG, $f_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300$ Hz; clinically, **500 Hz** is the KTU-standard choice. Resolution: **10-bit to 12-bit** (1024 to 4096 levels) is adequate for ECG amplitude resolution. ### Stage 5 — Digital Signal Processing (DSP) in the Microcontroller The MCU extracts clinically meaningful parameters: - **Heart Rate (BPM)** - **R-R Interval** - **QRS Complex Duration** - **ST-Segment Elevation/Depression** - **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the equations that solve 80% of KTU numericals.** | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes | |---|---|---| | Differential Gain (3-Op-Amp IA) | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | $R_G$ is the gain-setting resistor | | CMRR (in dB) | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $A_{cm}$ is the common-mode gain | | HPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_c^{HPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | First-order RC | | LPF Cutoff Frequency | $f_c^{LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | First-order RC | | Notch Filter Centre Frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Twin-T topology | | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq. | | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = ADC resolution (bits) | | ADC Digital Output (Decimal) | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor$ | For unipolar, single-ended input | | Einthoven's Law | $V_{II} = V_{I} + V_{III}$ | Vector sum of leads | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | $V_s$ = signal, $V_n$ = noise RMS | | Heart Rate (from R-R) | $\text{BPM} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | $T_{RR}$ in seconds | | Power Line Interference | $f = 50$ Hz (India) | Notch filter required | | Electrode Half-Cell Potential | $\approx \pm 300$ mV | Ag/AgCl electrode | | ECG Bandwidth (Diagnostic) | $0.05 - 150$ Hz | Full clinical band | | ECG Bandwidth (Monitor) | $0.5 - 40$ Hz | Bedside monitor band | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse CMRR as a *ratio* with that *in dB*. Remember: $\text{CMRR} = 10^{(CMRR_{dB}/20)}$. Losing this conversion costs **2–3 marks** in numericals. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The ECG interface you design in Module 1 is the *literal foundation* of: - **Wearable fitness bands** (Mi Band, Apple Watch) — 1-lead dry electrode. - **Holter monitors** — 24-h ambulatory 3-lead recording. - **Implantable loop recorders** — sub-cutaneous ECG for arrhythmia detection. - **Telemedicine / IoT cardiac monitors** — your Module 2/3 topic will build on this AFE. - **AED (Automated External Defibrillators** — uses the same AFE for rhythm analysis. The AFE is, in industry parlance, the **"biggest moat"** in a medical IoT device — anyone can write firmware, but the analog signal chain determines clinical accuracy. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Voltage Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Typical):** Design an IA with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find the differential gain $A_d$. ### Step 1 — Write the input stage relationship The first stage consists of two op-amps ($A_1$, $A_2$) with the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the inputs at the non-inverting terminals. By the virtual-short principle, the voltage across $R_G$ equals $V_1 - V_2$. The current through $R_G$ is: $$ I_{R_G} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} $$ ### Step 2 — Current through $R_1$ resistors The same current flows through both $R_1$ resistors (no current enters the op-amp inputs). Therefore, the voltage at the outputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are: $$ V_{O1} - V_{O2} = \left(V_1 - V_2\right) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Difference amplifier (second stage) The second stage is a standard difference amplifier with gain $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$. Its output is: $$ V_{out} = \left(V_{O2} - V_{O1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} $$ ### Step 4 — Combine both stages $$ V_{out} = -\left(V_1 - V_2\right) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} $$ The overall differential gain (magnitude) is: $$ A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values $$ A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 10\text{k}}{1\text{k}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{50\text{k}}{50\text{k}} $$ $$ A_d = \left(1 + 20\right) \cdot 1 $$ $$ A_d = 21 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** The differential gain is **21 V/V** (≈ 26.4 dB). To bring a 5 mV ECG signal to 3.3 V (full ADC range), you would need additional gain stages (≈ × 100 more). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Notch Filter Component Design for 50 Hz Rejection > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Design a Twin-T notch filter with $C_1 = C_2 = 100$ nF to reject **50 Hz** Indian mains interference. Find $R_1$ and $R_2$. ### Step 1 — State the centre-frequency formula For a balanced Twin-T notch: $$ f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the design simplification $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ $$ f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C} $$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for R $$ R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_o C} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $f_o = 50$ Hz, $C = 100$ nF $$ R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ R \approx 31{,}831 \text{ } \Omega $$ ### Step 5 — Practical nearest E12 value $$ R \approx 33 \text{ k}\Omega $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** Use **$R = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$**, **$C = 100$ nF**. This gives a notch depth of **> 40 dB** at 50 Hz, which is more than sufficient to reject Indian mains hum. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V samples an ECG signal. Find (a) the LSB size, (b) the digital code for a 1.5 mV input, and (c) the percentage quantization error relative to a 5 mV peak ECG. ### Step 1 — Compute LSB $$ \Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096} $$ $$ \Delta V \approx 8.056 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 805.66 \text{ } \mu\text{V} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute digital code for 1.5 mV input $$ D = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{0.0015}{0.00080566}\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}(1.862) $$ $$ D = 2 $$ ### Step 3 — Quantization error for 5 mV peak The maximum quantization error is ±½ LSB: $$ \varepsilon_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm 402.8 \text{ } \mu\text{V} $$ Percentage error at 5 mV peak: $$ \%\text{Error} = \dfrac{402.8 \text{ }\mu\text{V}}{5000 \text{ }\mu\text{V}} \times 100 \approx 8.06\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A 12-bit ADC at 3.3 V reference has ~**8% quantization error** for a 5 mV ECG peak. This is borderline acceptable; **16-bit ADCs** (e.g., ADS1115) are preferred in clinical ECG (0.05% error). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Summary | Design Parameter | Increase → Effect on ECG Quality | Engineering Trade-off | |---|---|---| | IA Gain | Higher SNR, but ADC saturates on motion artifacts | Use 2-stage AFE; add auto-gain control | | HPF Cutoff (↑) | Faster baseline recovery, but P/T wave distortion | Use 0.05 Hz for diagnostic, 0.5 Hz for monitor | | LPF Cutoff (↓) | Less noise, but R-wave morphology is lost | Keep at 40 Hz for clinical, 150 Hz for full band | | ADC Resolution (↑) | Lower quantization noise, finer detail | Cost, power, data rate increase | | Sampling Rate (↑) | Better QRS morphology, HRV accuracy | Memory and power consumption | | CMRR (↑) | Better mains rejection | Harder to design; matched resistors required | | Electrode Impedance (↓) | Better signal quality | Skin prep (alcohol abrasion) required | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ECG Embedded System > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The Mermaid block diagram below traces the *complete signal flow* from the patient's body to the IoT cloud. Each block has a single, well-defined function, mirroring the **5-stage pipeline** discussed in Section 2. ```mermaid flowchart LR BODY["Human Body
Heart Electrical Activity"]:::body RA["RA Electrode
Right Arm"]:::elec LA["LA Electrode
Left Arm"]:::elec LL["LL Electrode
Left Leg"]:::elec RL["RL Electrode
Right Leg Drive"]:::elec IA["Instrumentation Amplifier
INA128 / AD620
Gain 100 to 1000"]:::afe HPF["High-Pass Filter
fc = 0.05 Hz
Removes DC Offset"]:::filt LPF["Low-Pass Filter
fc = 40 Hz
Removes High-Freq Noise"]:::filt NF["Notch Filter
fo = 50 Hz
Mains Rejection"]:::filt ADC["Analog to Digital Converter
12 bit or 16 bit
fs = 500 Hz"]:::adc MCU["Microcontroller
ARM Cortex M4 / ESP32
R Peak Detection BPM HRV"]:::dsp DISP["Local Display
OLED / LCD"]:::out IO["IoT Link
WiFi / BLE / MQTT"]:::out CLOUD["Cloud Server
Telemedicine Dashboard"]:::out BODY --> RA BODY --> LA BODY --> LL BODY --> RL RA --> IA LA --> IA LL --> IA RL --> IA IA --> HPF HPF --> LPF LPF --> NF NF --> ADC ADC --> MCU MCU --> DISP MCU --> IO IO --> CLOUD classDef body fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef elec fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef afe fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef filt fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000 classDef dsp fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000 classDef out fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram (left to right):** > 1. **Red block** = Biological source (the heart). > 2. **Yellow blocks** = Sensors (electrodes). > 3. **Blue block** = Amplification (IA). > 4. **Green blocks** = Filtering (HPF → LPF → NF). > 5. **Purple block** = Digitization (ADC). > 6. **Pink block** = Intelligence (MCU/DSP). > 7. **Light-blue blocks** = Outputs (Display + Cloud). --- ## 4.2 Sub-Block — Instrumentation Amplifier Internal Topology > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters:** KTU frequently asks students to *label the three op-amps* in an IA. The diagram below shows the canonical 3-op-amp topology with $R_G$ as the single gain-control element. ```mermaid flowchart TB V1["Input V1
LA Electrode"]:::in V2["Input V2
RA Electrode"]:::in A1["Op-Amp A1
Buffer + Gain"]:::op A2["Op-Amp A2
Buffer + Gain"]:::op A3["Op-Amp A3
Unity Difference"]:::op RG["R_G
Gain Set Resistor"]:::rg R1a["R1"]:::r R1b["R1"]:::r R2a["R2"]:::r R2b["R2"]:::r R3a["R3"]:::r R3b["R3"]:::r R4["R4"]:::r VOUT["Vout
Amplified ECG"]:::out V1 --> A1 V2 --> A2 A1 -- R1a --> RG A2 -- R1b --> RG A1 -- R2a --> A3 A2 -- R2b --> A3 A3 -- R3a --> VOUT A3 -- R3b --> R4 R4 -- R2b --> A3 classDef in fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef op fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef rg fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef r fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#6b7280,color:#000 classDef out fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 ECG Waveform Anatomy — P-QRS-T Mapping > [!NOTE] > **Diagram interpretation:** This is the *information* the embedded system is trying to preserve. Each segment carries clinical information; the AFE must not distort it. ```mermaid flowchart LR P["P Wave
Atrial Depolarization
0.08 to 0.10 s
Amplitude 0.25 mV"]:::p Q["Q Wave
Septal Depolarization
Small Negative"]:::qrs R["R Wave
Ventricular Depolarization
Peak 1.0 to 1.5 mV
Sharp Spike"]:::qrs S["S Wave
Ventricular End
Negative Dip"]:::qrs T["T Wave
Ventricular Repolarization
0.16 s
0.1 to 0.5 mV"]:::t U["U Wave
Sometimes Visible
Papillary Muscle Repolarization"]:::u P --> Q Q --> R R --> S S --> T T --> U classDef p fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000 classDef qrs fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef t fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef u fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Trap:** If your LPF cutoff is set *below 40 Hz*, the R-wave amplitude is attenuated. The MCU's R-peak detector then **misses beats** → wrong BPM → wrong diagnosis. This is a common source of validation failure in KTU lab projects. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the standard amplitude and frequency range of an ECG signal. Why is a dedicated front-end amplifier required for ECG acquisition?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The standard ECG signal has an amplitude of **0.5 mV to 5 mV** and a frequency content of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**. A dedicated front-end amplifier is required because: 1. **The signal amplitude is in the millivolt range**, far below the input range of a typical ADC (0–3.3 V or 0–5 V). The amplifier provides a gain of **100 to 1000** to bring the signal into the usable ADC input range. 2. **The ECG signal is differential and rides on a large common-mode voltage** (the electrode half-cell potential, ~300 mV). A simple op-amp would amplify this common-mode noise along with the signal. Therefore, an **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** with high **CMRR (> 80 dB)** is mandatory to reject the common-mode component while amplifying the differential signal. 3. **The IA also provides very high input impedance (> 10 GΩ)**, preventing loading of the body and ensuring signal integrity. *(1 mark for each point)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the role of a notch filter in an ECG acquisition system? State its centre frequency for Indian power-line conditions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **notch filter** (also called a band-stop filter) is used in the ECG acquisition chain to **reject a narrow band of unwanted frequencies** while passing all other frequencies unaffected. *(1 mark)* In ECG systems, the notch filter is specifically used to **remove power-line interference (mains hum)** that is picked up by the body and the electrode cables. The body acts as an antenna, and the 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) mains supply can easily couple into the ECG signal, corrupting it. *(1 mark)* For **Indian power-line conditions**, the supply frequency is **50 Hz**, so a notch filter with a centre frequency $f_o = 50$ Hz is used. This filter typically provides **> 40 dB attenuation** at 50 Hz while leaving the diagnostic ECG band (0.5–40 Hz) untouched. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) regulations, Module 1 carries either Q1 or Q2 (14 marks each), with internal choice. Solve ONE full question from the pair below.** --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q. A (a)** **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional stages of an ECG sensor interface in an embedded biomedical system. State the function of each stage. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** #### Model Solution: **Introduction (1 mark):** An ECG sensor interface is the analog front-end (AFE) that acquires, conditions, and digitizes the heart's electrical signal for processing by an embedded microcontroller. It bridges the gap between the biological source (body) and the digital domain (ADC/MCU). **Block Diagram (2 marks):** $$ \text{Electrode} \rightarrow \text{IA} \rightarrow \text{HPF} \rightarrow \text{LPF} \rightarrow \text{Notch} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{MCU} $$ **Stage-by-Stage Explanation (4 marks):** 1. **Electrodes (Sensors):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes (placed on RA, LA, LL, RL according to Einthoven's triangle) transduce ionic current from the body into electronic current. They provide a differential voltage typically 0.5–5 mV. 2. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** A 3-op-amp IA (e.g., INA128) provides high differential gain (100–1000), high CMRR (> 80 dB), and high input impedance. The gain is set by a single resistor $R_G$. 3. **High-Pass Filter (fc = 0.05 Hz):** Removes the DC electrode offset (~±300 mV half-cell potential) and baseline wander caused by respiration and motion. 4. **Low-Pass Filter (fc = 40 Hz):** Removes high-frequency muscle noise (EMG) and radio-frequency interference above the diagnostic band. 5. **Notch Filter (fo = 50 Hz):** Specifically rejects Indian mains hum (50 Hz) coupled into the body and cables. 6. **ADC (12-bit or 16-bit, fs = 500 Hz):** Converts the cleaned analog signal to digital codes at a sampling rate satisfying the Nyquist criterion. 7. **Microcontroller (ARM/ESP32):** Performs DSP — R-peak detection, BPM calculation, QRS analysis, HRV, and forwards to display/IoT. --- **Q. A (b)** **[7 Marks]** An instrumentation amplifier is built with $R_1 = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_3 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_G = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the differential gain. If a 2 mV ECG signal is applied, what is the output voltage? If the common-mode gain is 0.01, compute the CMRR in dB. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** #### Model Solution: **Step 1 — Compute the differential gain $A_d$ (3 marks):** $$ A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} $$ $$ A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25\text{k}}{2\text{k}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{50\text{k}}{50\text{k}} $$ $$ A_d = (1 + 25) \cdot 1 = 26 $$ $$ \boxed{A_d = 26 \text{ V/V}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute output voltage for 2 mV input (2 marks):** $$ V_{out} = A_d \times V_{in} = 26 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 52 \text{ mV} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{out} = 52 \text{ mV}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute CMRR in dB (2 marks):** $$ \text{CMRR} = \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \dfrac{26}{0.01} = 2600 $$ $$ \text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2600) = 20 \times 3.415 = 68.3 \text{ dB} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx 68.3 \text{ dB}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log(2600)$ but forget to *show the division by $A_{cm}$ first*. A common error is to take $\log$ of $A_d$ alone, which is wrong. CMRR is *always* a *ratio* — you must divide first, then convert to dB. Losing this step costs **2 marks**. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **Q. B (a)** **[7 Marks]** Design a first-order RC high-pass filter for an ECG system with a cutoff frequency of **0.5 Hz**. Choose $C = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and find $R$. Explain why a separate low-pass filter is also required in the chain. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]** #### Model Solution: **Step 1 — Recall the HPF cutoff formula (1 mark):** $$ f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C} $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for R (1 mark):** $$ R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $f_c = 0.5$ Hz, $C = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (2 marks):** $$ R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{1}{\pi \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{10^6}{3.1416} \approx 318{,}310 \text{ } \Omega $$ **Step 4 — Practical nearest E12 value (1 mark):** $$ R \approx 330 \text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ \boxed{R \approx 330 \text{ k}\Omega} $$ **Step 5 — Why an LPF is also required (2 marks):** A high-pass filter alone only blocks DC and very low frequencies. It does not remove high-frequency noise such as electromyographic (EMG) interference from muscles, radio-frequency interference, or aliasing effects. A **low-pass filter** with cutoff at 40 Hz (or 150 Hz for full clinical band) is required to limit the signal bandwidth to the diagnostic range. The combination of HPF + LPF forms a **band-pass response** that admits only the clinically useful ECG spectrum (0.5–40 Hz) and rejects everything else, maximizing SNR before the ADC. --- **Q. B (b)** **[7 Marks]** A 10-bit ADC with a 5 V reference is used in an ECG embedded system. Calculate (i) the resolution in mV, (ii) the digital output code for a 2.5 mV ECG signal, and (iii) the maximum quantization error. Comment on whether 10-bit resolution is sufficient for clinical ECG. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** #### Model Solution: **Step 1 — Resolution (LSB) (2 marks):** $$ \Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta V \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}} $$ **Step 2 — Digital code for 2.5 mV input (2 marks):** $$ D = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\dfrac{2.5}{4.88}\right) = \text{round}(0.512) $$ $$ \boxed{D = 1 \text{ (decimal)} = 0000000001_2} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum quantization error (1 mark):** $$ \varepsilon_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm 2.44 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 4 — Comment on adequacy (2 marks):** > **Not Sufficient for Clinical ECG.** > > A 10-bit ADC at 5 V reference has a quantization step of **4.88 mV**, which is **larger than the peak ECG amplitude (5 mV)**. This means the ADC will barely distinguish the QRS complex from the baseline — most samples will collapse to either 0 or 1 LSB, and the morphology of the P, QRS, and T waves will be completely lost. > > For clinical ECG, the typical resolution requirement is **at least 5 $\mu$V per LSB** (so that 5 mV = 1000 codes). This requires: > > $$ > n = \log_2\left(\dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ }\mu\text{V}}\right) = \log_2(10^6) \approx 20 \text{ bits} > $$ > > In practice, **12-bit (with front-end gain) or 16-bit ADCs** (e.g., ADS1115) are used. The front-end IA amplifies the ECG from 5 mV to ~4.8 V (gain ≈ 1000), so a 10-bit ADC's 4.88 mV LSB becomes an effective input resolution of **4.88 $\mu$V**, which is then acceptable. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B(b):** > The most common student error in this question is to *accept 10-bit as sufficient* without computing the actual input-referred resolution. Examiners award full marks **only** when you (a) compute the effective resolution after the IA gain, and (b) explicitly state that **12-bit ADCs are industry standard** for ECG. Skipping either point costs **1.5 marks**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)** ### Core Concepts (Memorize Verbatim) - **ECG Amplitude:** 0.5 mV to 5 mV *(most-tested value)*. - **ECG Bandwidth:** 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz (diagnostic), 0.5 Hz to 40 Hz (monitor). - **Standard Sampling Rate:** 500 Hz (satisfies Nyquist for 150 Hz). - **Power Line Notch:** 50 Hz for India, 60 Hz for USA. - **CMRR Required:** > 80 dB minimum, > 100 dB for clinical. - **Electrode Half-Cell Potential:** ± 300 mV (Ag/AgCl). - **Einthoven's Law:** $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$. - **IA Differential Gain:** $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$. - **CMRR in dB:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$. - **First-Order RC Cutoff:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. ### Signal Chain (Remember the Order) 1. Electrode → 2. IA → 3. HPF → 4. LPF → 5. Notch → 6. ADC → 7. MCU → 8. Display/IoT. ### Why Each Block Exists (Examiner Favourite) - **IA:** Boost mV → V range; reject common-mode noise. - **HPF:** Remove DC offset and baseline wander. - **LPF:** Remove EMG, RF, and aliasing risk. - **Notch:** Remove 50 Hz mains hum. - **ADC:** Convert analog → digital for MCU processing. ### KTU Numerical "Must-Do" Templates - **IA gain calculation** with given $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_G$. - **Output voltage** for a given ECG input. - **CMRR conversion** ratio → dB. - **Filter design** for given $f_c$ and component choice. - **ADC resolution / digital code** calculation. ### Common Pitfalls to Avoid 1. Confusing **CMRR ratio** with **CMRR in dB**. 2. Using **10-bit ADC** without front-end gain (insufficient). 3. Setting **LPF below 40 Hz** → loss of R-wave amplitude. 4. Forgetting **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** circuit in 3-lead ECG. 5. Not considering **electrode-skin contact impedance** in input-stage design. ### Clinical Waveform Anatomy (P-QRS-T) - **P wave** → Atrial depolarization. - **QRS complex** → Ventricular depolarization (R-peak is the detection target). - **T wave** → Ventricular repolarization. - **R-R interval** → used for **BPM = 60 / $T_{RR}$**. ### Two-Sentence Exam Strategy > When asked "Design an ECG system," **always** state: *(i)* amplitude 0.5–5 mV and bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz, *(ii)* the 5-stage pipeline (IA-HPF-LPF-Notch-ADC-MCU), and *(iii)* at least one numerical justification (gain, cutoff, or ADC bits). This single template has secured full-marks answers in KTU 2023 and 2024 papers. --- **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — ECG SENSOR INTERFACES**

topics.title: Pulse Oximeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Oximeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pulse Oximeter** is a non-invasive, photoelectric medical device that continuously and indirectly monitors the **functional oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** of a patient's arterial blood, along with the **pulse rate (PR)**, by analyzing the differential absorption of two specific wavelengths of light — Red (≈ **660 nm**) and Near-Infrared (≈ **940 nm**) — through a pulsating vascular bed (typically a fingertip, earlobe, or toe).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"A pulse oximeter is a spectrophotometric instrument that measures the ratio of oxygenated haemoglobin to total haemoglobin in arterial blood using the principles of Beer-Lambert law and photoplethysmography (PPG)."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Traffic Light for Your Blood"

Imagine a busy two-lane tunnel:
- **Red lane (660 nm light)** — represents **deoxygenated blood (Hb)** passing through
- **Infrared lane (940 nm light)** — represents **oxygenated blood (HbO₂)** passing through

When you shine two different colored flashlights through your fingertip, oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood cells absorb them differently. The pulse oximeter acts like a "traffic counter" that measures **how much of each color gets through**, then mathematically computes the percentage of oxygen-bound haemoglobin.

| Parameter | Standard Value | Clinical Significance |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Normal SpO₂ | **95% – 100%** | Healthy oxygenation |
| Mild Hypoxemia | **90% – 94%** | Needs monitoring |
| Severe Hypoxemia | **< 90%** | Emergency intervention |

### 1.3 Core Terminology

- **SpO₂** — Peripheral oxygen saturation (%)
- **PPG** — Photoplethysmograph (the waveform signal)
- **HbO₂** — Oxyhaemoglobin
- **Hb** — Reduced (deoxy) haemoglobin
- **AC Component** — Pulsatile arterial blood signal
- **DC Component** — Non-pulsatile venous + tissue signal

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> A pulse oximeter measures **FUNCTIONAL** saturation, not **FRACTIONAL** saturation. It cannot detect dyshaemoglobins (like COHb or MetHb) — this is a common viva question!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Dual-Wavelength Illumination**
Two Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs) — one **Red (660 nm)** and one **Infrared (940 nm)** — are placed on one side of the measurement site (typically fingertip). They are time-multiplexed (alternately switched ON/OFF at ~500 Hz).

**Step 2: Light Transmission / Reflection**
Light passes through the vascular tissue and is detected on the opposite side (transmission mode) or the same side (reflectance mode) by a photodetector (photodiode).

**Step 3: Photoplethysmographic (PPG) Signal Generation**
The detector output contains two superimposed signals:
- **DC component (I_DC)** — Constant baseline from skin, bone, muscle, venous blood, and non-pulsatile arterial blood.
- **AC component (I_AC)** — Time-varying pulsatile portion due to arterial blood volume changes during systole and diastole.

**Step 4: Beer-Lambert Absorption Analysis**
The Beer-Lambert Law governs the attenuation:

$$I_{out} = I_{in} \cdot e^{-\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$

Where the change in path length `l` (due to arterial pulsation) and concentration `c` (due to HbO₂/Hb ratio) determines the AC swing.

**Step 5: Ratio-of-Ratios Calculation (R value)**
The microprocessor computes the **normalised absorbance ratio**:

$$R = \frac{AC_{red} / DC_{red}}{AC_{IR} / DC_{IR}}$$

**Step 6: Empirical Calibration Curve**
SpO₂ is derived using a pre-calibrated empirical polynomial:

$$SpO_2 = a - b \cdot R$$

Where `a` and `b` are device-specific empirical constants (typically a ≈ 110, b ≈ 25).

### 2.2 Why Two Wavelengths? (High-Yield KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Two-LED Design:**
> - At **660 nm**, **deoxy-Hb absorbs ~10× more light** than oxy-Hb → red light tells us about Hb.
> - At **940 nm**, **oxy-Hb absorbs more** than deoxy-Hb → IR light tells us about HbO₂.
> - Using both wavelengths **cancels out non-blood variables** (tissue thickness, skin pigmentation, bone density).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (Transmittance) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Absorbance Equation | AU |
| 3 | $A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Absorbance in terms of intensity | AU |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{660}}{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{940}}$ | Ratio-of-Ratios | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SpO_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$ | Empirical SpO₂ Equation | % |
| 6 | $I_{total} = I_{DC} + I_{AC} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ | PPG Signal Model | arbitrary |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{Hb}^{660} \gg \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660}$ | Red wavelength selectivity | L/(mol·cm) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} > \varepsilon_{Hb}^{940}$ | IR wavelength selectivity | L/(mol·cm) |
| 9 | $HR = \frac{60}{T_{pulse}}$ | Heart Rate from PPG | bpm |
| 10 | $f_{LED} \approx 500 \text{ Hz}$ | LED multiplexing frequency | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU ANSWERS:**
> The ratio `R` is **dimensionless** (no units). Always report SpO₂ as a percentage (%), not as a decimal.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pulse oximeters are ubiquitous in:
- **ICU/CCU patient monitoring** (continuous SpO₂)
- **Anaesthesia and surgical recovery rooms**
- **Neonatal care** (incubator-based monitoring)
- **COVID-19 home monitoring** (post-pandemic IoT boom)
- **High-altitude aviation & mountaineering**
- **IoT-enabled wearable health devices** (smartwatches, fitness bands)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Beer-Lambert to SpO₂

**Starting Point — The Beer-Lambert Law:**

The intensity of monochromatic light passing through a medium decays exponentially:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

**Step 1: Define the DC and AC Components**

The transmitted light intensity at the photodetector for any wavelength can be modelled as:

$$I_{total}(t) = I_{DC} + I_{AC}(t)$$

Where:
- $I_{DC}$ = constant baseline (tissue, venous blood, non-pulsatile arterial)
- $I_{AC}(t)$ = time-varying arterial pulsation

**Step 2: Apply Beer-Lambert to Each Wavelength**

For the **Red LED (λ₁ = 660 nm)**, the absorbance at peak systole is:

$$A_{red} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{DC,red}}{I_{DC,red} - I_{AC,red}}\right)$$

For the **Infrared LED (λ₂ = 940 nm)**:

$$A_{IR} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{DC,IR}}{I_{DC,IR} - I_{AC,IR}}\right)$$

**Step 3: Linearisation Using Small AC Approximation**

Since $I_{AC} \ll I_{DC}$ (typically AC is only 1–5% of DC), we use the first-order Taylor expansion:

$$\ln(1 - x) \approx -x \quad \text{for} \quad x \ll 1$$

Therefore, the absorbance simplifies to:

$$A_{red} \approx \frac{I_{AC,red}}{I_{DC,red} \cdot \ln(10)}$$

$$A_{IR} \approx \frac{I_{AC,IR}}{I_{DC,IR} \cdot \ln(10)}$$

**Step 4: The Ratio-of-Ratios (R)**

The $\ln(10)$ cancels when we form the ratio:

$$R = \frac{A_{red}}{A_{IR}} = \frac{I_{AC,red} \cdot I_{DC,IR}}{I_{AC,IR} \cdot I_{DC,red}}$$

**Step 5: Substituting Two-Haemoglobin Absorption Model**

Since blood at each wavelength absorbs via both Hb and HbO₂:

$$A_{red} = (\varepsilon_{Hb}^{red} \cdot [Hb] + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{red} \cdot [HbO_2]) \cdot l_{pulse}$$

$$A_{IR} = (\varepsilon_{Hb}^{IR} \cdot [Hb] + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{IR} \cdot [HbO_2]) \cdot l_{pulse}$$

The path length $l_{pulse}$ cancels in the ratio R, leaving:

$$R = \frac{\varepsilon_{Hb}^{red} \cdot (1 - S) + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{red} \cdot S}{\varepsilon_{Hb}^{IR} \cdot (1 - S) + \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{IR} \cdot S}$$

Where $S$ is the fractional oxygen saturation $S = [HbO_2]/([Hb] + [HbO_2])$.

**Step 6: Solving for SpO₂**

Rearranging for S gives the empirical linear approximation used in clinical devices:

$$S \approx 1 - \frac{R - r_0}{k}$$

Which in clinical form is:

$$SpO_2 \text{ (\%)} = 110 - 25 \cdot R$$

This completes the full derivation chain from fundamental physics to the bedside display value.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Performance & Limitations

| Parameter | Specification / Value | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Red LED wavelength | **660 nm** | Peak Hb absorption |
| IR LED wavelength | **940 nm** | Peak HbO₂ absorption |
| Sampling rate | 100 – 1000 Hz | Real-time waveform fidelity |
| LED switching freq. | **~500 Hz** | Avoids motion artefacts |
| DC/AC ratio | 95:5 typical | Validates good probe placement |
| Accuracy | ±2% (above 70% SpO₂) | FDA clinical standard |
| Power consumption | < 50 mW | Battery-operated suitability |
| Response time | 5 – 10 seconds | Critical for emergency use |
| Operating temp. | 0 – 50 °C | Hospital-grade range |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly write that the pulse oximeter measures **partial pressure of oxygen (PaO₂)**. It does NOT! SpO₂ is **saturation (%)**, not pressure (mmHg).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pulse Oximeter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller Unit] --> B[LED Driver Circuit]
    B --> C1[Red LED 660 nm]
    B --> C2[Infrared LED 940 nm]
    C1 --> D[Fingertip Probe Sensor]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Photodetector Photodiode]
    E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    F --> G[Analog Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 5 Hz]
    G --> H[12 bit ADC]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    I --> J[SpO2 and HR Calculation]
    J --> K[LCD OLED Display]
    J --> L[Buzzer Alarm Module]
    J --> M[IoT WiFi or BLE Module]
    M --> N[Cloud Dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram — PPG Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[DC Component: Tissue and Venous Blood] --> ADD((Combine))
    S2[AC Component: Pulsatile Arterial Blood] --> ADD
    ADD --> DET[Photodetector Output]
    DET --> AMP[Amplification Stage]
    AMP --> FIL[Filtering Stage]
    FIL --> ADC[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    ADC --> CALC[Ratio of Ratios R Computation]
    CALC --> SPO2[SpO2 Lookup Table Lookup]
    CALC --> HR[Heart Rate Peak Detection]
    SPO2 --> OUT[Display Output]
    HR --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Measurement Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Power ON] --> P2[Initialise Microcontroller and Sensors]
    P2 --> P3[Place Finger in Probe]
    P3 --> P4[Check PPG Signal Quality]
    P4 --> P5{Signal Valid?}
    P5 -->|No| P6[Display Probe Error]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Illuminate Red LED]
    P7 --> P8[Capture Ired Intensity]
    P8 --> P9[Illuminate IR LED]
    P9 --> P10[Capture IIR Intensity]
    P10 --> P11[Compute R Ratio]
    P11 --> P12[Apply Calibration Equation]
    P12 --> P13[Display SpO2 and HR]
    P13 --> P14{Threshold Breach?}
    P14 -->|Yes| P15[Trigger Alarm]
    P14 -->|No| P13
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Probe Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopClamp
        L1[Red LED 660 nm]
        L2[IR LED 940 nm]
    end
    subgraph BottomClamp
        PD[Photodiode Detector]
    end
    L1 --> PD
    L2 --> PD
    PD --> S[Housing and Cable]
    S --> M[Main Processing Board]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU 2024:**
> The choice of **940 nm IR** is deliberate — this wavelength sits in the **"optical window"** of biological tissue (700 nm – 1100 nm) where melanin, water, and haemoglobin have minimal confounding absorption, maximising SNR.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define pulse oximeter. Mention any two clinical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pulse oximeter** is a non-invasive, photoelectric medical device used to measure the **functional oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** of arterial blood and the **pulse rate**, by analysing the differential absorption of red (**660 nm**) and infrared (**940 nm**) light through a pulsating vascular bed (typically a fingertip), based on the **Beer-Lambert law** and **photoplethysmography (PPG)**.

**Two clinical applications:**
1. Continuous patient monitoring in **ICU, CCU, and operation theatres** during anaesthesia.
2. Early detection of **hypoxemia** in patients with COPD, pneumonia, and post-COVID respiratory complications.
3. (Bonus) Home-based IoT health monitoring in chronic respiratory diseases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why are two different wavelengths of light used in a pulse oximeter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two wavelengths — **Red (660 nm)** and **Infrared (940 nm)** — are used because:

1. At **660 nm**, **deoxyhaemoglobin (Hb)** absorbs light strongly while **oxyhaemoglobin (HbO₂)** absorbs weakly → enables detection of Hb concentration.
2. At **940 nm**, **oxyhaemoglobin (HbO₂)** absorbs light strongly while deoxyhaemoglobin absorbs weakly → enables detection of HbO₂ concentration.
3. The **ratio of the two absorbance values** cancels out common variables like skin thickness, pigmentation, and path length, yielding an accurate SpO₂ value independent of tissue variation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice A]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **working principle of a pulse oximeter** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Beer-Lambert law in SpO₂ measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **ratio-of-ratios (R)** expression and explain how it is used to compute SpO₂ using an empirical calibration equation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Block Diagram (7 marks):**

A pulse oximeter operates on the principle of **spectrophotometry combined with photoplethysmography**. The major blocks are:

1. **Probe Assembly:** Two LEDs (Red 660 nm, IR 940 nm) on one side, photodetector on the other side of the fingertip.
2. **LED Driver Circuit:** Time-multiplexes the two LEDs at ~500 Hz so only one illuminates at a time.
3. **Photodetector & Transimpedance Amplifier:** Converts transmitted light into a proportional voltage signal.
4. **Bandpass Filter (0.5 – 5 Hz):** Isolates the AC pulsatile component from DC baseline.
5. **ADC & Microprocessor:** Digitises the signal and computes SpO₂ and heart rate.
6. **Display & Alarm:** Shows SpO₂%, pulse rate, and triggers alarms if thresholds are breached.

**Role of Beer-Lambert Law:**
The Beer-Lambert law states $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$. The pulse oximeter uses the **pulsatile change in path length (l)** and the **differential absorption coefficients (ε) of Hb and HbO₂ at two wavelengths** to compute the ratio of oxy- to deoxy-haemoglobin. The pulsatile (AC) signal isolates arterial blood from venous/tissue contributions, enabling selective measurement of arterial oxygenation. **(7 marks complete)**

**Part (b) — Derivation of R and SpO₂ Equation (7 marks):**

Let $I_{DC}$ be the baseline (non-pulsatile) intensity and $I_{AC}$ be the pulsatile component. From Beer-Lambert:

$$I_{total} = I_{DC} + I_{AC} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

The absorbance at each wavelength is:

$$A = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{DC}}{I_{DC} - I_{AC}}\right) \approx \frac{I_{AC}}{I_{DC} \cdot \ln(10)}$$

(The approximation holds because $I_{AC} \ll I_{DC}$.)

The **ratio-of-ratios** is defined as:

$$R = \frac{A_{red}}{A_{IR}} = \frac{I_{AC,red} / I_{DC,red}}{I_{AC,IR} / I_{DC,IR}}$$

This R is independent of path length and LED intensity drift. The SpO₂ is then computed using the empirical linear equation derived from volunteer studies:

$$SpO_2 \text{ (\%)} = 110 - 25 \cdot R$$

For example, if $R = 0.6$, then $SpO_2 = 110 - 15 = 95\%$.

The constants 110 and 25 are **device-specific empirical coefficients** determined by calibration against co-oximetry on healthy volunteers at varying oxygen levels. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Module Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **construction and components of a transmittance-type pulse oximeter probe**. Mention the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **photoplethysmographic (PPG) signal** in detail. How is the AC and DC component used to extract SpO₂ information? What are the **limitations of pulse oximetry**? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Probe Construction (7 marks):**

A **transmittance-type pulse oximeter probe** (used on fingertip) consists of:

1. **Upper Clamp Housing:** Contains two surface-mount LEDs:
   - **Red LED (660 nm):** Strongly absorbed by deoxyhaemoglobin.
   - **Infrared LED (940 nm):** Strongly absorbed by oxyhaemoglobin.
2. **Lower Clamp Housing:** Contains a **silicon photodiode** that detects the light transmitted through the tissue.
3. **Spring-loaded Hinge:** Ensures consistent, gentle pressure on the finger without occluding arterial flow.
4. **Shielding & Cable:** Electrical shielding minimises EMI from 50/60 Hz mains interference.
5. **Connector Cable:** Carries LED drive signals and photodiode output to the main processing unit.
6. **Optional Temperature Sensor:** Compensates for skin temperature variations affecting haemoglobin absorption spectra.

The probe is typically a **clip-on or adhesive wrap** design, optimised for finger, toe, or earlobe application. **(7 marks complete)**

**Part (b) — PPG Signal & Limitations (7 marks):**

**PPG Signal Explanation:**
The PPG waveform has two parts:
- **DC Component (≈ 95%):** Light absorbed by skin, bone, muscle, venous blood, and non-pulsatile arterial blood. It establishes the baseline intensity.
- **AC Component (≈ 5%):** Time-varying intensity caused by the **systolic increase in arterial blood volume**. The AC peak-to-peak amplitude is directly proportional to the arterial pulse pressure.

**Use in SpO₂ Extraction:**
The device measures $I_{AC}$ and $I_{DC}$ for both wavelengths, then forms the ratio:

$$R = \frac{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{660 \text{ nm}}}{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{940 \text{ nm}}}$$

This R is mapped to SpO₂ via the empirical calibration curve.

**Limitations of Pulse Oximetry:**
1. **Cannot detect dyshaemoglobins** (carboxyhaemoglobin COHb and methaemoglobin MetHb) — gives falsely normal readings in CO poisoning.
2. **Inaccurate during motion artefacts** (shivering, tremors).
3. **Affected by nail polish, henna, and skin pigmentation** (especially in dark-skinned patients).
4. **Poor peripheral perfusion** (cold extremities, shock) causes signal loss.
5. **Ambient light interference** (surgical lamps, sunlight) can corrupt the signal.
6. **Calibration drift** with venous pulsations or venous congestion. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 1. ❌ **Writing "pulse oximeter measures PaO₂"** — wrong, it measures **SpO₂ (saturation)**, not oxygen partial pressure.
> 2. ❌ **Confusing AC and DC components** — AC = pulsatile arterial blood; DC = tissue + venous + non-pulsatile arterial blood.
> 3. ❌ **Omitting the role of Beer-Lambert law** — examiners specifically look for the equation $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon c l}$.
> 4. ❌ **Forgetting the wavelength values** (660 nm and 940 nm) — these are mandatory.
> 5. ❌ **Not mentioning the empirical nature of the SpO₂ equation** — the constants 110 and 25 are empirical, not theoretical.
> 6. ❌ **Missing limitations of pulse oximetry** — always mention at least 3 limitations (motion, pigmentation, dyshaemoglobins).
> 7. ❌ **Drawing block diagrams without arrow labels** — KTU expects labelled arrows on every block.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Pulse oximeter = **non-invasive SpO₂ + pulse rate monitor**
- ✅ Operating principle: **Beer-Lambert law + Photoplethysmography (PPG)**
- ✅ Two wavelengths used: **Red 660 nm** and **Infrared 940 nm**
- ✅ At 660 nm → **deoxy-Hb absorbs more**; at 940 nm → **oxy-HbO₂ absorbs more**
- ✅ DC component → tissue + venous blood (baseline)
- ✅ AC component → pulsatile arterial blood (pulse-synchronous)
- ✅ Ratio-of-Ratios: $R = \dfrac{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{red}}{(I_{AC}/I_{DC})_{IR}}$
- ✅ Empirical SpO₂ equation: $SpO_2 = 110 - 25R$
- ✅ Two probe types: **Transmittance** (finger) and **Reflectance** (wrist, forehead)
- ✅ LED multiplexing frequency ≈ **500 Hz** to avoid motion artefacts
- ✅ Measures **functional** saturation (cannot detect COHb/MetHb)
- ✅ Normal SpO₂ range: **95% – 100%**; Hypoxemia: **< 90%**
- ✅ Heart Rate: $HR = 60 / T_{pulse}$ (in bpm)
- ✅ Optical window: **700 – 1100 nm** — why 940 nm is chosen
- ✅ **IoT integration:** BLE / WiFi module → cloud dashboard for remote monitoring
- ✅ Key components: LED, photodiode, TIA, BPF, ADC, DSP, display, alarm
- ✅ Limitations: motion artefacts, pigmentation, dyshaemoglobins, peripheral perfusion
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** Wearable IoT pulse oximeters, COVID-19 relevance, cloud-based SpO₂ dashboards
- ✅ Empirical constants (110, 25) are **device-specific**, not universal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:**
> When answering 14-mark questions, **always include**: (1) Block diagram, (2) Beer-Lambert equation, (3) Derivation of R, (4) SpO₂ formula, (5) At least 3 limitations, and (6) At least 1 IoT application. This structure typically fetches **full marks** in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Sensor:** A temperature sensor is a transducer that converts thermal energy (temperature variations) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, resistance, or current) in a deterministic, reproducible, and calibrated manner. In the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications**, temperature sensors are foundational sensing elements used to monitor ambient, surface, and physiological temperatures in real-time.

The electrical output of the sensor is a monotonic function of the absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin). The general form of this transduction relationship is:

$$Y = f(T)$$

where $Y$ is the electrical output (resistance, voltage, or current) and $f(T)$ represents the sensor's specific transfer function.

## 1.2 Principal Classification of Temperature Sensors

Temperature sensors are broadly classified into four families, each governed by a distinct physical transduction principle:

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Principal Families of Temperature Sensors**
> 1. **Thermistors** — Resistance varies non-linearly with temperature (NTC/PTC).
> 2. **RTDs (Resistance Temperature Detectors)** — Resistance varies nearly linearly with temperature (metallic conductors).
> 3. **Thermocouples** — EMF generated at the junction of two dissimilar metals (Seebeck Effect).
> 4. **Semiconductor / IC Sensors** — Bandgap-based voltage output proportional to absolute temperature.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **metal spoon** and a **wooden spoon** in a hot cup of tea. After a few seconds, the **metal spoon feels hot** because the free electrons in metals efficiently transfer thermal energy (high thermal conductivity), and the electrical resistance of the metal changes predictably with this energy. The **wooden spoon feels less hot** because wood is an insulator. 

A **temperature sensor works on the same principle**:
- In an **RTD**, the spoon is replaced with a fine platinum/nickel wire; we measure how its *electrical resistance* changes with heat.
- In a **thermocouple**, two different metals joined together act like a tiny "thermal battery," producing a *voltage* when heated.
- In a **thermistor**, a ceramic semiconductor is used; its resistance *drops dramatically* with heat (NTC) or *rises* with heat (PTC).
- In an **IC sensor** (e.g., **LM35**), the transistor's *base-emitter voltage* changes linearly with temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants (Bolded for Memory):**
> - Standard temperature for calibration: $T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$
> - Absolute zero: $0\,\text{K} = -273.15^\circ\text{C}$
> - Reference temperature for thermocouples (cold junction): $0^\circ\text{C}$
> - Platinum resistance at $0^\circ\text{C}$ (PT100): $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$

## 1.4 IoT & Biomedical Context

In the **PEEBT632 syllabus**, temperature sensors are pivotal for:
- **Wearable health monitoring** (continuous body temperature tracking).
- **Clinical thermometry** (digital fever thermometers using **NTC thermistors**).
- **Cold-chain logistics** (vaccine storage, blood bank monitoring).
- **Patient monitoring systems** in ICUs (multi-parameter IoT nodes).
- **Smart incubators** for neonatal care.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermistors

A **thermistor** (thermally sensitive resistor) is a semiconductor device whose resistance is a strong function of temperature.

### 2.1.1 NTC Thermistor (Negative Temperature Coefficient)

The resistance of an **NTC thermistor** *decreases* exponentially as temperature increases. The governing equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in Kelvin).
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically $298.15\,\text{K}$ or $25^\circ\text{C}$).
- $\beta$ = material constant (Beta value), typically between $3000\,\text{K}$ and $5000\,\text{K}$ for common NTCs.

**The 'Why' behind NTC behavior:** In semiconductors, increasing temperature excites more charge carriers from the valence band to the conduction band, thereby *reducing* resistance.

### 2.1.2 PTC Thermistor (Positive Temperature Coefficient)

The resistance of a **PTC thermistor** *increases* sharply once a *switching temperature* (Curie point) is crossed. PTC behavior is most commonly exhibited by doped **barium titanate ($\text{BaTiO}_3$)** ceramics.

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{A(T - T_0)}$$

where $A$ is the temperature coefficient constant of the PTC material.

## 2.2 Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs)

An **RTD** exploits the *linear* increase of electrical resistance in pure metals (typically platinum, nickel, or copper) with temperature. The Callendar-Van Dusen equation is the standard mathematical model:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

For temperatures $T \geq 0^\circ\text{C}$, the cubic term vanishes:

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$$

For $T < 0^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

**Standards:**
- **PT100:** $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$.
- **PT1000:** $R_0 = 1000\,\Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$.
- $\alpha$ for platinum: $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^\circ\text{C}$ (IEC 60751 standard).

## 2.3 Thermocouples

A **thermocouple** consists of two dissimilar metals joined at one end (hot junction). The open-circuit voltage is generated by the **Seebeck Effect**.

$$E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} \left(S_B(T) - S_A(T)\right) dT$$

For practical engineering analysis, a polynomial approximation is used:

$$E = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + \ldots + a_n T^n$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the Seebeck coefficients of metals A and B, $T_h$ is the hot junction temperature, and $T_c$ is the cold junction temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Thermocouple Types (ISA Standards):**
> - **Type K** (Chromel-Alumel): Range $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $1350^\circ\text{C}$, sensitivity $\approx 41\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
> - **Type J** (Iron-Constantan): Range $-40^\circ\text{C}$ to $750^\circ\text{C}$.
> - **Type T** (Copper-Constantan): Range $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $350^\circ\text{C}$ (used in cryogenics & biomedical labs).

## 2.4 Semiconductor / IC Temperature Sensors

The **LM35** is a precision IC temperature sensor with a linear output:

$$V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV}/^\circ\text{C} \cdot T \quad (\text{with } T \text{ in } ^\circ\text{C})$$

The underlying principle is the linear dependence of the base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ of a BJT on absolute temperature:

$$V_{BE} = V_{g0} - \frac{T}{T_0}(V_{g0} - V_{BE0})$$

where $V_{g0}$ is the bandgap voltage of silicon at $0\,\text{K}$ ($\approx 1.206\,\text{V}$) and $V_{BE0}$ is the base-emitter voltage at reference temperature $T_0$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Output Quantity | Sensitivity | Key Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NTC Thermistor** | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Resistance ($\Omega$) | Non-linear, high | $\beta \approx 3000\text{–}5000\,\text{K}$ |
| **PTC Thermistor** | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{A(T - T_0)}$ | Resistance ($\Omega$) | Sharp, non-linear | $A$ material-specific |
| **RTD (PT100)** | $R_T \approx R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Resistance ($\Omega$) | Linear, $\approx 0.385\,\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$ | $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Thermocouple (Type K)** | $E = \int (S_B - S_A) dT$ | EMF (mV) | $\approx 41\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ | $S_B - S_A$ differential Seebeck coeff. |
| **LM35 IC Sensor** | $V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV}/^\circ\text{C} \cdot T$ | Voltage (mV) | Linear, $10\,\text{mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ | Calibrated internally |
| **Body Temp Range** | $36.1^\circ\text{C}$ to $37.2^\circ\text{C}$ (normal) | N/A | N/A | Clinical reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For biomedical applications, the **NTC thermistor** and **LM35** are the most frequently tested sensors in KTU examinations because of their direct relevance to digital clinical thermometers and wearable IoT health monitors.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PT100 RTDs** are used in **industrial process control**, pharmaceutical reactors, and **calibration laboratories** due to their high accuracy ($\pm 0.1^\circ\text{C}$) and long-term stability.
- **NTC thermistors** dominate **consumer medical devices** (oral/axillary thermometers) because of their low cost, small size, and high sensitivity in the narrow biomedical range.
- **Thermocouples** are essential in **autoclaves**, **hyperthermia therapy equipment**, and **research-grade biomedical instrumentation** requiring wide measurement range.
- **LM35** is the workhorse of **Arduino/Raspberry Pi IoT prototypes** for body temperature monitoring wearables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: NTC Thermistor Resistance Equation

**Problem Setup:** Derive the resistance-temperature relationship for an NTC thermistor starting from semiconductor physics.

**Step 1 — Intrinsic Carrier Concentration:**
For a semiconductor, the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ depends on temperature as:

$$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \cdot e^{-E_g / k_B T}$$

**Step 2 — Conductivity Relation:**
The electrical conductivity $\sigma$ is proportional to $n_i$:

$$\sigma = q (n_i \mu_e + p_i \mu_h)$$

For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n_i = p_i$, so conductivity becomes:

$$\sigma = q n_i (\mu_e + \mu_h)$$

**Step 3 — Inverting to Get Resistance:**
Since $R = \rho L / A$ and $\rho = 1/\sigma$:

$$R = \frac{L}{A \cdot q \cdot n_i (\mu_e + \mu_h)}$$

**Step 4 — Combining with Carrier Equation:**
Substituting the expression for $n_i$ into the resistance equation yields:

$$R \propto e^{+E_g / 2k_B T}$$

**Step 5 — Defining the Beta Constant:**
Let $\beta = E_g / 2k_B$. Then:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic Verification (Reference Point):**
At $T = T_0$:

$$R_{T_0} = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T_0 - 1/T_0)} = R_0 \cdot e^0 = R_0 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms dimensional and physical consistency.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PT100 Resistance at 100°C

**Problem:** Find the resistance of a **PT100 RTD** at $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$.

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$
- $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^\circ\text{C}$ (PT100 standard)

**Step 1 — Apply the Linearized RTD Equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 100)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Product:**

$$R_{100} = 100 \times (1 + 0.385) = 100 \times 1.385$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$R_{100} = 138.5\,\Omega$$

**Engineering Insight:** A PT100 at $100^\circ\text{C}$ should read exactly $138.5\,\Omega$, which is the basis of the *international fixed point* in the **ITS-90** temperature scale.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LM35 Output Voltage for Human Body Temperature

**Problem:** Calculate the output voltage of an **LM35** sensor when measuring normal human body temperature ($T = 37^\circ\text{C}$).

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:**

$$V_{out} = 10\,\text{mV}/^\circ\text{C} \times T$$

**Step 2 — Substitute T:**

$$V_{out} = 10 \times 37 = 370\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Volts:**

$$V_{out} = 0.370\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — IoT Interface Interpretation:**
For an ESP32 / Arduino ADC (3.3 V reference, 12-bit):

$$\text{ADC Reading} = \frac{0.370}{3.3} \times 4095 = 459 \text{ (digital counts)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Decision Logic:** If ADC reading $> 410$ (corresponding to $\approx 37.5^\circ\text{C}$), the IoT node triggers a **fever alert** to the cloud dashboard.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Temperature from NTC Thermistor Voltage Divider

**Problem:** An NTC thermistor ($R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$, $\beta = 3950\,\text{K}$) is connected in series with a $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ reference resistor across a $5\,\text{V}$ supply. Find the temperature when the output voltage is $V_{out} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Voltage Divider Equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_{ref}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_T$:**

$$2.5 = 5 \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + 10000}$$

$$0.5 = \frac{R_T}{R_T + 10000}$$

$$0.5 R_T + 5000 = R_T$$

$$5000 = 0.5 R_T$$

$$R_T = 10000\,\Omega = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Equate to NTC Equation:**

$$10000 = 10000 \cdot e^{3950(1/T - 1/298.15)}$$

$$1 = e^{3950(1/T - 1/298.15)}$$

$$0 = 3950 \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$T = 298.15\,\text{K} = 25^\circ\text{C} \quad \checkmark$$

**Engineering Insight:** This symmetry occurs because the divider ratio is $0.5$ at the calibrated reference point. Any deviation indicates a temperature change.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection for Biomedical/IoT

| Application | Recommended Sensor | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Digital clinical thermometer | NTC thermistor | High sensitivity, small size, low cost |
| ICU patient skin probe | PT100 RTD | High accuracy, stability, biocompatible |
| Wearable IoT patch | LM35 / TMP36 | Linear output, ADC-friendly, low power |
| Cryogenic blood storage | Type T thermocouple | Wide range, low drift at low temps |
| Hyperthermia therapy | Type K thermocouple | High-temperature resilience, fast response |
| Neonatal incubator | PT1000 RTD | High accuracy, lead-wire error compensation |
| Wireless body area network (WBAN) | Digital sensor (DS18B20) | Direct digital output, IoT-ready |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: IoT Temperature Monitoring Node


## 4.4 Thermocouple Cold-Junction Compensation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Junction at Patient] --> B[Thermocouple Wire A and B]
    B --> C[Cold Junction at Terminal Block]
    C --> D[Reference IC Sensor Measures Tcold]
    D --> E[Amplifier INA128]
    B --> F[ADC reads EMF mV]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Software]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Add Cold Junction Voltage using NIST Tables]
    H --> I[Compute True Hot Temperature]

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f4e1ff

    style G fill:#f0e1ff
    style H fill:#ffe1f0
    style I fill:#f0ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The cold-junction compensation (CJC) step is the most critical part of thermocouple signal conditioning. Without it, a Type K thermocouple measuring $37^\circ\text{C}$ in a $25^\circ\text{C}$ ambient would read only $12^\circ\text{C}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors with suitable biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* with increasing temperature; governed by $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$; used in **digital clinical thermometers** for oral/axillary measurement.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* sharply beyond a switching temperature; used in **over-temperature protection** circuits of biomedical equipment like infant warmers and incubators.
- **Key contrast:** NTC has gradual exponential behavior; PTC exhibits a sharp non-linear transition.

**Q2. Why is PT100 preferred over a thermocouple in precision biomedical instrumentation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. PT100 offers **higher accuracy** ($\pm 0.1^\circ\text{C}$ vs $\pm 1^\circ\text{C}$ for Type K).
2. It has **excellent long-term stability** and linear output.
3. It does **not require cold-junction compensation**, simplifying signal conditioning in ICU monitors and laboratory equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **RTD** with the help of the Callendar-Van Dusen equation. Calculate the resistance of a **PT100** sensor at $50^\circ\text{C}$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the construction, working, and biomedical applications of an **NTC thermistor** with a neat labeled diagram. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — RTD Working Principle (7 Marks):**

The **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature. Platinum is the most widely used metal due to its chemical inertness, stability, and high repeatability.

The Callendar-Van Dusen equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

For $T \geq 0^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

**Calculation at $T = 50^\circ\text{C}$ for PT100:**

Given $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^\circ\text{C}$:

$$R_{50} = 100 \times (1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 50)$$

$$R_{50} = 100 \times (1 + 0.1925) = 100 \times 1.1925$$

$$\boxed{R_{50} = 119.25\,\Omega}$$

**Part (b) — NTC Thermistor (7 Marks):**
