```mermaid
    Start -->|Write Digital| WriteDigital{"Single or Multiple?"}
    WriteDigital -->|Single Coil| FC05["FC 05 - Write Single Coil"]
    WriteDigital -->|Multiple Coils| FC15["FC 15 - Write Multiple Coils"]
    Start -->|Write Analog 16 bit| WriteAnalog{"Single or Multiple?"}
    WriteAnalog -->|Single Register| FC06["FC 06 - Write Single Register"]
    WriteAnalog -->|Multiple Registers| FC16["FC 16 - Write Multiple Registers"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the Modbus protocol. List any three differences between Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP/IP.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Modbus is an open, **master-slave (client-server) communication protocol** developed by Modicon in 1979, operating at the application layer (OSI Layer 7) to exchange data between industrial / IoT devices over serial or Ethernet media.

**Three Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Modbus RTU | Modbus TCP/IP |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Layer | RS-232 / RS-485 (Serial) | Ethernet (TCP) |
| Encoding | Compact binary | Binary with MBAP header |
| Error Check | **CRC-16** | TCP built-in checksum |
| Port | Serial | **TCP Port 502** |
| Addressing | 1 byte (1–247) | 1 byte Unit ID + IP |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List the four primary data tables (data model) in Modbus. Specify which are read-only and their bit widths.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Data Table | Access | Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coils** | Read/Write | 1 bit |
| 2 | **Discrete Inputs** | Read-Only | 1 bit |
| 3 | **Holding Registers** | Read/Write | 16 bits |
| 4 | **Input Registers** | Read-Only | 16 bits |

(2 marks for correct listing, 1 mark for access + width columns)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Module Choice: Module 2 – Communication Protocols for IoT**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Modbus RTU frame structure**. Define each field and state the maximum ADU size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Modbus RTU master sends the following request to Slave 2:
`[02 03 00 00 00 0A C5 CD]`

**(i)** Decode the request and explain the operation requested.
**(ii)** Compute the CRC-16 for verification and confirm whether the frame is valid. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Modbus RTU Frame Structure (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):**

| Slave Addr | Function Code | Data | CRC-16 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 byte | 1 byte | N bytes | 2 bytes |
| (0x01–0xF7) | (0x01–0x10 etc.) | Payload | Error Check (Low, High) |

**Field Explanations (3 marks):**
- **Slave Address (1 byte):** Identifies the target device (1–247). Address 0 = broadcast.
- **Function Code (1 byte):** Indicates the operation (01=Read Coils, 03=Read Holding Registers, etc.).
- **Data (N bytes):** Contains sub-function information such as starting address, quantity, byte count, register values.
- **CRC-16 (2 bytes):** Cyclical Redundancy Check using polynomial $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$. The low byte is transmitted first.

**Max ADU Size (1 mark):** 256 bytes total (1 + 1 + 252 + 2).

#### Part (b) — Frame Decoding and CRC Verification (7 marks)

**(i) Decoding the request [02 03 00 00 00 0A C5 CD] (3 marks):**

| Byte | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0x02 | Slave 2 | Target device |
| 0x03 | FC 03 | Read Holding Registers |
| 0x00 0x00 | Start Address | Register 0 |
| 0x00 0x0A | Quantity | **10 registers** |
| 0xC5 0xCD | CRC-16 | Low byte = 0xC5, High byte = 0xCD |

**Interpretation:** *"Slave 2, read 10 holding registers starting at address 0."*

**(ii) CRC Verification (4 marks):**

Computing CRC-16 over the data bytes [02, 03, 00, 00, 00, 0A] using the standard Modbus algorithm:

**Step 1:** Initialize CRC = 0xFFFF.

**Step 2:** XOR first byte 0x02 → CRC = 0xFFFD.

**Step 3:** Process each of the 8 bits per byte. After processing all 6 bytes, the resulting 16-bit value is:

$$ \text{CRC}_{computed} = 0\text{xCDC5} $$

**Step 4:** Transmit low byte first → 0xC5, then 0xCD.

This matches the CRC bytes in the received frame, confirming **the frame is valid** (no transmission errors).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Modbus TCP/IP architecture** with the MBAP header. How does it differ from Modbus RTU at the transport and addressing levels? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biomedical gateway receives the following Modbus TCP frame from a remote ventilator (slave unit 5):
`[00 2A 00 00 00 06 05 04 00 10 00 04]`

**(i)** Decode all MBAP fields and the PDU.
**(ii)** If the ventilator responds with measured flow rate = 0x01F4 and tidal volume = 0x0320, construct the expected response frame. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Modbus TCP/IP Architecture (7 marks)

**MBAP Header (4 marks):** Modbus TCP uses an **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) header** of 7 bytes placed before the PDU:

| Field | Size | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | 0x002A (example) | Synchronization of request/response pairs |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | Always 0x0000 | Identifies Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | Remaining bytes (Unit ID + PDU) | Tells receiver where PDU ends |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | 1–247 (or 0xFF) | Slave ID; for serial gateway bridging |

**Differences from Modbus RTU (3 marks):**

| Layer | Modbus RTU | Modbus TCP/IP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | Asynchronous serial (RS-485) | TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) |
| Error Check | CRC-16 (mandatory) | TCP checksum (inherited) + no extra CRC |
| Framing | Inter-character timing (3.5 chars) | TCP byte stream (length field) |
| Addressing | Slave address only | IP + Transaction ID + Unit ID |
| Default Port | N/A (serial) | **TCP/502** |

#### Part (b) — Decoding TCP Frame and Constructing Response (7 marks)

**(i) Decoding [00 2A 00 00 00 06 05 04 00 10 00 04] (4 marks):**

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 0x002A | 42 |
| Protocol ID | 0x0000 | Modbus (always 0) |
| Length | 0x0006 | 6 bytes follow (Unit ID + FC + 4 data bytes) |
| Unit ID | 0x05 | Slave 5 (ventilator) |
| Function Code | 0x04 | **Read Input Registers** |
| Start Address | 0x0010 | Register 16 |
| Quantity | 0x0004 | **Read 4 input registers** |

**Interpretation:** *"Read 4 input registers from slave 5 starting at address 16 (e.g., flow rate, tidal volume, RR, PEEP)."*

**(ii) Expected response frame (3 marks):**

Response length = UnitID(1) + FC(1) + ByteCount(1) + 2·Quantity(8) = **11 bytes**.

Constructed frame:

| Field | Value | Bytes |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 0x002A | 00 2A |
| Protocol ID | 0x0000 | 00 00 |
| Length | 0x000B (11) | 00 0B |
| Unit ID | 0x05 | 05 |
| Function Code | 0x04 | 04 |
| Byte Count | 8 | 08 |
| Reg 16 (Flow Rate) | 0x01F4 = 500 | 01 F4 |
| Reg 17 (Tidal Volume) | 0x0320 = 800 | 03 20 |
| Reg 18 (RR) | not given | XX XX |
| Reg 19 (PEEP) | not given | XX XX |

**Complete response:** `00 2A 00 00 00 0B 05 04 08 01 F4 03 20 XX XX XX XX`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the byte count field** in the response of FC 03/04 — this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Transmitting CRC bytes in big-endian order** in RTU — must be **little-endian** (low byte first).
> 3. **Using address 0 as a valid slave** — address 0 is **broadcast** and slaves *do not reply*.
> 4. **Confusing Function Code 03 (Holding Registers) with 04 (Input Registers)** — FC 03 is read/write, FC 04 is read-only.
> 5. **Wrong Length field in TCP responses** — length counts *only* the bytes after itself, not the MBAP header.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Modbus** = open protocol, master-slave, **Layer 7 (Application)** of OSI.
- ✅ Three variants: **RTU (binary, CRC-16)**, **ASCII (text, LRC)**, **TCP/IP (Ethernet, port 502)**.
- ✅ Max ADU size (RTU) = **256 bytes**.
- ✅ **4 Data Tables:** Coils (1-bit RW), Discrete Inputs (1-bit RO), Holding Registers (16-bit RW), Input Registers (16-bit RO).
- ✅ **8 Most Common Function Codes:** 01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 15, 16.
- ✅ **Exception Response** = request FC OR-ed with **0x80** (MSB set).
- ✅ Address **0 = broadcast** (no reply), **1–247 = slaves**.
- ✅ **Big-Endian** byte order for register values; **Little-Endian** for CRC transmission.
- ✅ **MBAP Header** (7 bytes) precedes PDU in Modbus TCP; Protocol ID is always 0x0000.
- ✅ **CRC-16 Polynomial** for RTU: $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ (initial = 0xFFFF, poly = 0xA001).
- ✅ **LRC** for ASCII = 2's complement of sum of bytes (8-bit).
- ✅ Maximum **32 unit loads** per RS-485 segment (256 with 1/8 UL transceivers).
- ✅ **Inter-frame delimiter** in RTU = 3.5 character times of silence.
- ✅ Modbus in **biomedical IIoT**: bedside monitors, ventilators, infusion pumps, patient warmers commonly act as Modbus slaves for **centralized EMR/HIS integration**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zigbee. Network and Security Architecture. IoT Open Source Architecture (OIC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zigbee. Network and Security Architecture. IoT Open Source Architecture (OIC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Zigbee — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zigbee** is a specification for a suite of high-level communication protocols used to create **personal area networks (PANs)** built from small, low-power digital radios. It is based on the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard** for the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers and is maintained by the **Connectivity Standards Alliance (CSA)**, formerly known as the Zigbee Alliance.

Zigbee is specifically engineered for low-data-rate, low-power applications such as home automation, industrial control, healthcare monitoring, and sensor networks. The standard operates primarily in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** (worldwide), with additional regional bands at **868 MHz** (Europe) and **915 MHz** (North America), delivering a maximum over-the-air data rate of **250 kbps** in the 2.4 GHz band.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Post Office System"**
> Imagine a city with three types of postal workers: **Coordinators** (Head Postmaster), **Routers** (Branch Postmasters), and **End Devices** (Letter Boxes). The Head Postmaster (Coordinator) sets up the entire postal network and assigns addresses. Branch Postmasters (Routers) can forward letters, accept new letter boxes, and remain powered on 24/7. Letter Boxes (End Devices) only wake up briefly to send/receive mail, then go back to sleep — saving enormous amounts of battery power. Letters can be routed through multiple branches (mesh), ensuring that even if one branch is closed, the letter still reaches its destination. This is exactly how Zigbee works in the IoT world!

### 1.1.2 Key Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard Base** | IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY + MAC) |
| **Frequency Band (Global)** | **2.4 GHz ISM** |
| **Regional Bands** | **868 MHz** (EU), **915 MHz** (NA) |
| **Maximum Data Rate** | **250 kbps** (2.4 GHz) |
| **Range (Line of Sight)** | **10–100 m** |
| **Number of Channels (2.4 GHz)** | **16 channels** (channels 11–26) |
| **Channel Spacing** | **5 MHz** |
| **Modulation** | O-QPSK (2.4 GHz), BPSK (868/915 MHz) |
| **Encryption** | **AES-128 CCM** |
| **Max Nodes per Network** | **65,536** |
| **Encryption Block Size** | 128 bits |
| **MAC Address Length** | 64 bits (IEEE address) / 16 bits (short) |

---

## 1.2 Zigbee Network Architecture — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Zigbee Network Architecture** defines how devices are organized, addressed, and communicate. It comprises three device roles, three network topologies, and a layered protocol stack that allows scalability from a few nodes to large mesh networks.

### 1.2.1 Intuition for Network Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Corporate Office Hierarchy"**
> Think of a corporate office: the **CEO (Coordinator)** sets up the company, assigns floors, and holds the master key to the building. **Managers (Routers)** handle communication between floors and help new employees join. **Employees (End Devices)** simply do their work and report to their managers. They can communicate in a **star** (everyone reports to CEO), in a **mesh** (employees can talk to each other through managers), or in a **cluster-tree** (offices are grouped into wings).

### 1.2.2 Zigbee Device Types (The Three Roles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Zigbee Coordinator (ZC)** — Exactly **one** per network. Starts the network, assigns the **PAN ID (16-bit)**, selects the operating channel, and acts as the **Trust Center** for security.
> 2. **Zigbee Router (ZR)** — Optional intermediate nodes. Forwards packets, allows child devices to join, and supports routing. Cannot sleep.
> 3. **Zigbee End Device (ZED)** — Leaf nodes. Communicate only with their parent (Coordinator or Router). Can enter **sleep mode** for power saving.

---

## 1.3 Zigbee Security Architecture — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Zigbee Security Architecture** is built upon **AES-128 encryption**, providing **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** for transmitted frames. Security is handled primarily at the **MAC**, **NWK**, and **APS** layers, with the **Trust Center (TC)** acting as the central authority for key distribution and network access control.

### 1.3.1 Intuition for Security

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Secure Office Building"**
> The **Trust Center** is the security guard at the main gate. He has a **master key** to every room. When a new employee (device) joins, the guard checks their ID (**authentication**), gives them a special key to their floor (**link key**), and tells them a common code used by all employees for general communication (**network key**). Every message inside the building is sealed in a tamper-proof envelope (**AES-128 encryption**), and any unauthorized person is denied entry (**access control**).

---

## 1.4 IoT Open Source Architecture (OIC) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)** — now known as the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** — is an industry consortium that developed the **OCF Specification**, an open-source, royalty-free framework for **device-to-device communication** in IoT. The OIC architecture defines a **resource-oriented model** where every entity (sensor, actuator, or service) is represented as a **resource** with a **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** accessible via **RESTful APIs** running over **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**.

### 1.4.1 Intuition for OIC

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Universal Translator and Address Book"**
> Imagine you have a smart light bulb from company A, a thermostat from company B, and a door lock from company C. They all speak different "languages." The **OIC architecture** acts as a **universal translator** with a **global address book**: every device exposes its capabilities (on/off, temperature, lock state) as **web-like resources** with **URIs** (e.g., `/oic/p/light/1`). Any authorized device, regardless of brand, can `GET` (read) or `POST` (change) these resources. This enables true **interoperability** in IoT.

### 1.4.2 OIC Core Components

> [!NOTE]
> - **Resource Model** — Everything is a resource with properties.
> - **Resource Directory (RD)** — A lookup service for resources.
> - **Discovery** — Devices find each other's resources.
> - **Messaging** — Asynchronous notifications via CoAP Observe.
> - **Security** — Uses **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** with PSK or X.509 certificates.
> - **Bridging** — Translates between OIC and other protocols (Zigbee, BLE, Z-Wave).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zigbee Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

The Zigbee protocol stack is built in layers, where the **lower two layers (PHY + MAC)** are defined by **IEEE 802.15.4**, and the **upper layers (NWK, APS, APL)** are defined by the **Zigbee specification**.

### 2.1.1 Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

#### Layer 1: Physical Layer (PHY) — IEEE 802.15.4
- Performs **modulation/demodulation**
- Selects the **RF channel** (e.g., channel 11–26 in 2.4 GHz)
- Performs **channel scanning** (ED scan, active scan, passive scan)
- Provides **clear channel assessment (CCA)**
- Transmits/receives **PPDU (PHY Protocol Data Unit)**

#### Layer 2: MAC Layer — IEEE 802.15.4
- Implements **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)**
- Handles **beacon management** (for beacon-enabled mode)
- Provides **PAN association and disassociation**
- Generates **16-bit short addresses** dynamically
- Performs **acknowledgment (ACK)** of received frames
- Provides **frame security** (AES-128)

#### Layer 3: Network Layer (NWK)
- Responsible for **network formation, routing, and management**
- Three routing options: **Tree routing, Mesh routing (AODV), Table routing**
- Manages **device addressing** and **neighbor tables**
- Provides **NWK layer security** using the **Network Key**
- Handles **route discovery** and **route repair**
- Performs **PAN ID conflict resolution**

#### Layer 4: Application Support Sublayer (APS)
- Manages **binding tables** (linking clusters to endpoints)
- Provides **APS layer security** using **Link Keys**
- Handles **group addressing**
- Provides **acknowledgment services** at the application level
- Performs **fragmentation and reassembly** of long messages

#### Layer 5: Application Framework (AF)
- Hosts **application objects** (endpoints 1–240)
- Each endpoint exposes **clusters** (sets of attributes and commands)
- Zigbee **Cluster Library (ZCL)** defines standard clusters
- Supports **custom clusters** for proprietary applications

#### Zigbee Device Object (ZDO)
- Special endpoint (endpoint 0)
- Handles **device discovery, binding, and management**
- Implements **Zigbee Device Profile (ZDP)**
- Manages **network join/leave** procedures

### 2.1.2 Why This Layered Design?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:**
> - **Modularity:** IEEE 802.15.4 can be reused by other protocols (e.g., 6LoWPAN, Thread).
> - **Security flexibility:** Each layer can have its own security key.
> - **Scalability:** Mesh routing at NWK allows thousands of nodes.
> - **Application portability:** ZCL provides a common command set across vendors.

---

## 2.2 Zigbee Network Topologies

Zigbee supports **three fundamental topologies**:

| Topology | Structure | Use Case | Power Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All devices connect directly to the Coordinator | Small home networks | High (end devices sleep) |
| **Mesh** | Any device can communicate with any other | Industrial automation | Medium (routers always on) |
| **Cluster-Tree** | Hierarchical grouping of mesh clusters | Large buildings, smart cities | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** In a **mesh network**, if one router fails, traffic is automatically rerouted — this is called **self-healing**.

---

## 2.3 Zigbee Security Architecture — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Security Keys Hierarchy

Zigbee uses **three types of symmetric AES-128 keys**:

| Key Type | Scope | Purpose | Distribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Master Key** | Per-device | Long-term identity key (pre-installed or via out-of-band) | Out-of-band (e.g., entered manually) |
| **Link Key** | Per-pair of devices | Unicast communication between two devices | Trust Center or key exchange (SKKE) |
| **Network Key** | All devices in PAN | Broadcast/multicast and general traffic | Trust Center broadcasts encrypted with Master/Link key |

### 2.3.2 Security Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Security Mode:** Uses the **Network Key** for all communications. Simpler but less secure (any compromised device compromises the network).
> - **High Security Mode:** Uses **Link Keys** between each device and the Trust Center. More secure but requires more memory and processing.

### 2.3.3 Security Services Provided

- **Confidentiality** — AES-128-CTR encryption
- **Integrity** — AES-128-CBC-MAC (Message Integrity Code)
- **Authentication** — Verifying sender identity
- **Anti-replay** — Frame counter prevents replay attacks
- **Access Control** — Trust Center controls who joins

### 2.3.4 Trust Center Responsibilities

1. Authenticates joining devices
2. Distributes the Network Key securely
3. Maintains a list of authorized devices
4. Can **revoke** a device by updating the Network Key
5. Optionally maintains a **Trust Center Link Key** for each device

---

## 2.4 OIC (Open Interconnect Consortium) Architecture — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 OIC Layered Architecture (Bottom-Up)

| Layer | Function | Key Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **Device Layer** | Physical hardware, sensors, actuators | Sensors, MCUs, radios |
| **Connectivity Layer** | Communication hardware | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, Zigbee, NFC, Z-Wave |
| **Communication Layer** | Reliable message transport | **CoAP over UDP/DTLS** |
| **Framework Layer** | Resource modeling, discovery, security | **Resource Model, RD, Discovery, Security** |
| **Application/Vertical Layer** | Industry-specific use cases | Smart Home, Healthcare, Industrial |

### 2.4.2 OIC Resource Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every physical or logical entity in OIC is modeled as a **Resource** with:
> - A **URI** (e.g., `oic/p/light`, `oic/r/switch`)
> - **Properties** (attributes such as `value`, `dimming`)
> - **Resource Types** (e.g., `oic.r.light`, `oic.r.switch`)
> - **Interfaces** (e.g., `oic.if.a` for actuator, `oic.if.s` for sensor)

### 2.4.3 OIC Discovery and Messaging

- **Discovery:** Devices broadcast a multicast request (`/oic/res`) to find nearby resources.
- **Messaging:** Uses **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — a RESTful protocol for constrained devices.
  - `GET` — Read a resource
  - `POST` — Update a resource
  - `PUT` — Create a resource
  - `DELETE` — Remove a resource
  - `OBSERVE` — Subscribe to resource changes (similar to pub-sub)
- **Security:** All CoAP messages are secured using **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** with **pre-shared keys (PSK)** or **X.509 certificates**.

### 2.4.4 Why OIC Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> OIC solves the **fragmentation problem** in IoT. Without a common framework, every vendor creates isolated ecosystems. OIC provides:
> - **Interoperability** across vendors
> - **Open-source** royalty-free licensing
> - **Standardized security** via DTLS
> - **Cross-transport bridging** (Zigbee-to-Wi-Fi, BLE-to-Zigbee)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of 2.4 GHz channels | $N_{ch} = 16$ | Channels 11–26 |
| Channel center frequency | $f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11)$ MHz | $k = 11, 12, \ldots, 26$ |
| Maximum 2.4 GHz data rate | $R_{max} = 250$ kbps | O-QPSK modulation |
| Maximum PAN nodes | $N_{nodes} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | 16-bit short address |
| IEEE address length | $L_{addr} = 64$ bits | EUI-64 |
| Encryption key size | $K_{AES} = 128$ bits | AES-CCM |
| Frame security levels | $0$ (none) to $7$ (ENC-MIC-128) | 8 levels defined |
| CSMA/CA backoff | $BE_{min} = 3$, $BE_{max} = 5$ | macMinBE, macMaxBE |
| OIC transport | CoAP over UDP | Port 5683 (default) |
| OIC security | DTLS 1.2 | PSK or certificate mode |
| Zigbee payload max | $127$ bytes | MAC frame limit |
| APS payload max | $70$ bytes (typical) | After APS overhead |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Zigbee 2.4 GHz Channel Frequency Assignment

> [!NOTE]
> The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines the 2.4 GHz ISM band with **16 channels**, numbered **11 through 26**. The center frequency of each channel is derived from a linear equation.

**Given:**
- First channel: $k = 11$ → $f_c = 2405$ MHz
- Channel spacing: $\Delta f = 5$ MHz

**Derivation:**

The general linear relation between channel number $k$ and center frequency $f_c$ is:

$$
f_c = a \cdot k + b
$$

For $k = 11$ and $f_c = 2405$ MHz:

$$
2405 = 11a + b \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

For $k = 12$ and $f_c = 2410$ MHz:

$$
2410 = 12a + b \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Subtracting equation (1) from (2):

$$
(2410 - 2405) = (12a - 11a) + (b - b)
$$

$$
5 = a
$$

Substituting $a = 5$ into equation (1):

$$
2405 = 11(5) + b \Rightarrow 2405 = 55 + b \Rightarrow b = 2350
$$

Therefore, the final formula is:

$$
\boxed{f_c = 5k + 2350 \text{ MHz}, \quad k \in \lbrace 11, 12, \ldots, 26 \rbrace}
$$

**Verification:**
- $k = 11$: $f_c = 5(11) + 2350 = 55 + 2350 = 2405$ MHz ✓
- $k = 26$: $f_c = 5(26) + 2350 = 130 + 2350 = 2480$ MHz ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total occupied bandwidth spans from **2405 MHz to 2480 MHz**, which is **75 MHz wide** — exactly covering the global 2.4 GHz ISM band.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Zigbee Maximum Node Capacity

The 16-bit short address allocated by the Coordinator allows:

$$
N_{nodes} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

However, in practice:
- Address **0x0000** is reserved for the Coordinator
- Address **0xFFFE** is reserved for broadcast
- Address **0xFFFF** is reserved for "no address" (during joining)

So the **practical maximum** is:

$$
N_{practical} = 2^{16} - 3 = 65{,}533 \text{ usable short addresses}
$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Secure Network Join Procedure

The Zigbee secure join process involves a series of encrypted exchanges between a **joining device (Router/End Device)** and the **Trust Center**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step procedure:

**Step 1 — Channel Scan**
The joining device performs an **active scan** across all 16 channels to locate the nearest Coordinator or Router.

**Step 2 — Association Request**
The device sends an **Association Request** command (MAC layer) to the discovered Coordinator, providing its IEEE 64-bit address.

**Step 3 — Association Response**
The Coordinator allocates a **16-bit short address** and replies with an **Association Response**.

**Step 4 — Transport Key Request**
The joining device sends a **Transport Key Request** to the Trust Center (via the Coordinator), requesting the Network Key. This request is encrypted with the **Master Key** (or pre-configured Trust Center Link Key).

**Step 5 — Transport Key Delivery**
The Trust Center encrypts the **Network Key** with the **Master Key** and sends it to the joining device. The Network Key is now shared.

**Step 6 — Network Key Verification**
The joining device sends a **APS Update Device** command to confirm successful key receipt.

**Step 7 — Encrypted Communication Begins**
All subsequent communication uses the **Network Key** (or **Link Key** in High Security Mode) with **AES-128-CCM** providing encryption and integrity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Zigbee vs. OIC

| Parameter | Zigbee | OIC (OCF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Domain** | Low-power mesh networks | Cross-platform IoT interoperability |
| **Standardization Body** | CSA / IEEE 802.15.4 | Open Connectivity Foundation |
| **Transport Protocol** | IEEE 802.15.4 MAC | CoAP over UDP |
| **Security** | AES-128 CCM at MAC/NWK/APS | DTLS at transport layer |
| **Addressing** | 16-bit short / 64-bit IEEE | URI-based resource model |
| **Topology** | Star, Mesh, Cluster-Tree | Client-Server / Resource-oriented |
| **Data Model** | Clusters (ZCL) | Resources (JSON-like) |
| **Range** | 10–100 m | Depends on underlying transport |
| **Power** | Very low (sleep modes) | Application-dependent |
| **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | Application-dependent |
| **Discovery** | Device/Service Discovery (ZDO) | Multicast CoAP discovery |
| **Key Management** | Trust Center | PKI / PSK / X.509 |
| **Open Source** | Zigbee PRO spec (licensed) | Fully open-source |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: OIC Resource Access Example

**Scenario:** A smartphone app wants to turn on a smart light bulb.

**Step 1 — Discovery**
The smartphone sends a multicast GET request to `coap://224.0.1.187/oic/res` to discover all available resources in the local network.

**Step 2 — Response**
The light bulb responds with a JSON resource description:

```json
{
  "rt": ["oic.r.light"],
  "if": ["oic.if.a"],
  "uri": "/oic/p/light/1",
  "value": false
}
```

**Step 3 — DTLS Handshake**
The smartphone and light bulb perform a **DTLS handshake** to establish a secure session (using pre-shared keys or certificates).

**Step 4 — POST Request**
The smartphone sends a CoAP POST request to `coap://[device-ip]/oic/p/light/1` with payload:

```json
{"value": true}
```

**Step 5 — Execution**
The light bulb's resource handler activates the LED driver. A response is returned:

```json
{"value": true}
```

**Step 6 — Observation (Optional)**
The smartphone subscribes to the resource using **CoAP OBSERVE** to receive updates whenever the light state changes.

## 4.3 Zigbee Security Architecture — Trust Center Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    Start[New Device Wants to Join Network] --> Scan[Step 1: Active Channel Scan]
    Scan --> AssocReq[Step 2: Send Association Request to Coordinator]
    AssocReq --> AssocResp[Step 3: Receive 16-bit Short Address]
    AssocResp --> TransKeyReq[Step 4: Transport Key Request to Trust Center]
    TransKeyReq --> EncKey{Network Key Encrypted With Master or Link Key}
    EncKey --> KeyDeliver[Step 5: Trust Center Sends Encrypted Network Key]
    KeyDeliver --> AckKey[Step 6: Device Sends APS Update Device Acknowledgment]
    AckKey --> SecureComm[Step 7: Secure Communication Begins with AES-128-CCM]
    SecureComm --> Monitor{Trust Center Continuously Monitors}
    Monitor --> Normal[Normal Operation]
    Monitor --> Revoke[If Compromised: Update Network Key to Revoke Device]
    Revoke --> SecureComm
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
> **List any three characteristics of Zigbee technology. Also mention the IEEE standard on which Zigbee is based.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Zigbee is a low-power, low-data-rate wireless communication protocol. The three key characteristics are:

1. **Low Power Consumption:** Zigbee end devices can operate for **years on a single battery** (e.g., AA cells) due to aggressive sleep modes.
2. **Low Data Rate:** Maximum over-the-air data rate of **250 kbps** in the 2.4 GHz band, suitable for periodic sensor data, not for video/audio.
3. **Mesh Networking:** Supports **self-healing mesh topologies** allowing thousands of nodes (up to **65,536** per PAN) with multi-hop routing.

Zigbee is based on the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard**, which defines the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers. The upper layers (NWK, APS, APL) are defined by the Zigbee specification (CSA).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> **What is the Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)? Mention any two features of OIC architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)**, now known as the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)**, is an industry consortium that developed an **open-source, royalty-free specification** for **device-to-device interoperability** in the Internet of Things (IoT).

Two key features of OIC architecture are:

1. **Resource-Oriented Model:** Every IoT entity (sensor, actuator, or service) is represented as a **resource** with a unique **URI**, properties, and interfaces accessible via **RESTful APIs**.
2. **CoAP-Based Communication:** OIC uses the **Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP)** over **UDP** with **DTLS security**, making it lightweight and ideal for constrained IoT devices.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Zigbee protocol stack** in detail with a neat diagram. Mention the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **Zigbee security architecture**. Explain the roles of **Trust Center, Master Key, Link Key, and Network Key** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Zigbee Protocol Stack (7 marks)

The Zigbee protocol stack is a **5-layer architecture** built on top of IEEE 802.15.4. The layers and their functions are:

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| **Physical Layer (PHY)** | Performs **modulation/demodulation**, **channel selection** (2.4 GHz / 868 MHz / 915 MHz), **clear channel assessment (CCA)**, and **transmit/receive PPDU**. Uses **O-QPSK** in 2.4 GHz band. |
| **MAC Layer** | Implements **CSMA/CA** for channel access, manages **beacons** (beacon-enabled mode), handles **PAN association/disassociation**, generates **16-bit short addresses**, and provides **frame acknowledgment (ACK)**. |
| **Network Layer (NWK)** | Responsible for **network formation, routing, and management**. Supports **tree, mesh (AODV), and table routing**. Manages **neighbor tables, route tables, and routing table sizes**. Provides **NWK security** using Network Key. |
| **APS Layer** | Manages **binding tables** (link clusters to endpoints), **group addressing**, **fragmentation/reassembly**, and provides **APS security** using Link Keys. |
| **Application Layer (APL)** | Hosts **application objects** (endpoints 1–240), **Zigbee Device Object (ZDO) at endpoint 0**, and implements the **Zigbee Cluster Library (ZCL)** for standardized commands. |

*(Reference: Refer to the Zigbee Protocol Stack Block Diagram in SECTION 4.1.)*

#### Part (b) — Zigbee Security Architecture (7 marks)

The Zigbee security architecture is a **multi-layered, key-based system** centered around the **Trust Center (TC)**, which is typically the **Zigbee Coordinator**.

**Key Components:**

1. **Trust Center (TC):** Central security authority of the PAN. It:
   - Authenticates joining devices
   - Distributes the Network Key
   - Maintains a list of authorized devices
   - Can revoke compromised devices by updating the Network Key

2. **Master Key:** A **pre-installed or out-of-band** long-term identity key (128 bits) for a device. Used only during initial key exchange.

3. **Link Key:** A **per-pair symmetric key** used for unicast communication between two specific devices. In High Security Mode, every device-trust center pair has a unique link key.

4. **Network Key:** A **shared symmetric key** used by all devices in the PAN for general broadcast/unicast traffic. Distributed encrypted by the Trust Center.

**Security Modes:**
- **Standard Mode:** All devices share the **Network Key** for all traffic.
- **High Security Mode:** Each device uses a **unique Link Key** with the TC; separate keys for application data.

**Security Services:** Confidentiality (AES-CTR), Integrity (AES-CBC-MAC), Authentication, Anti-replay (frame counter), Access Control.

*(Reference: Refer to the Security Flow Diagram in SECTION 4.3.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** Explain the **OIC (Open Interconnect Consortium) architecture** in detail. Describe its layers and the **resource model** with an example. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare **Zigbee and OIC architecture** in terms of **transport, security, data model, and interoperability**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — OIC Architecture (7 marks)

The **OIC (now OCF) architecture** is a **5-layer open-source framework** designed for **interoperable IoT device-to-device communication**.

**Layers (Bottom to Top):**

1. **Device Layer:** Hardware — sensors, actuators, MCUs, memory.
2. **Connectivity Layer:** Wireless/wired transports — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, Zigbee, NFC, Z-Wave.
3. **Communication Layer:** **CoAP over UDP** with **DTLS 1.2** security.
4. **Framework Layer:** **Resource Model, Resource Directory (RD), Discovery, Messaging, Security**.
5. **Application / Vertical Layer:** Industry-specific use cases (Smart Home, Healthcare, Industrial, Automotive).

**Resource Model:**
Every IoT entity is a **Resource** identified by a **URI**. Example:

```
/oic/p/light/1
```

This resource has:
- `rt` (Resource Type): `oic.r.light`
- `if` (Interface): `oic.if.a` (actuator)
- `value` (Property): `true / false`

A client (e.g., smartphone) performs a `GET /oic/res` (multicast discovery) to find resources, then sends `POST /oic/p/light/1 {"value": true}` to control the light.

**Security:** All CoAP messages are secured with **DTLS** using **PSK** or **X.509 certificates**.

*(Reference: Refer to OIC Architecture Diagram in SECTION 4.4.)*

#### Part (b) — Zigbee vs. OIC Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | Zigbee | OIC (OCF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | IEEE 802.15.4 MAC | CoAP over UDP |
| **Security** | AES-128 CCM at MAC/NWK/APS | DTLS 1.2 at transport layer |
| **Data Model** | Zigbee Cluster Library (ZCL) | Resource Model with URI |
| **Addressing** | 16-bit short / 64-bit IEEE | URI-based (e.g., `/oic/p/light`) |
| **Topology** | Star, Mesh, Cluster-Tree | Client-Server / Resource-oriented |
| **Discovery** | Device/Service Discovery (ZDO) | Multicast CoAP `GET /oic/res` |
| **Interoperability** | Within Zigbee ecosystem only | Cross-vendor, cross-protocol |
| **Power** | Very low (sleep modes) | Application-dependent |
| **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | Application-dependent |
| **Key Management** | Trust Center | PKI / PSK / X.509 |

**Conclusion:** Zigbee is a **technology-specific protocol** optimized for low-power mesh, while OIC is an **interoperability framework** that works across multiple transports to enable vendor-agnostic IoT ecosystems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **omit the IEEE 802.15.4 base** when defining Zigbee — always mention it (carries **1 mark**).
> - Confusing **Link Key** and **Network Key** is a frequent mistake. Remember: **Network Key = shared, Link Key = per-pair**.
> - For OIC, students forget to mention **CoAP and DTLS** as the transport/security — both are mandatory.
> - Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** for the protocol stack and security flow (carries **2–3 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts).
> - Do not confuse OIC with **OSGi** or **oneM2M** — they are different frameworks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Zigbee is based on **IEEE 802.15.4** for PHY and MAC.
- ✅ Operates in **2.4 GHz (global), 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (NA)** with **16 channels** in 2.4 GHz.
- ✅ Maximum data rate is **250 kbps**; range is **10–100 m**.
- ✅ Three device types: **Coordinator, Router, End Device**.
- ✅ Three topologies: **Star, Mesh, Cluster-Tree**.
- ✅ Maximum nodes per PAN: **65,536** (16-bit short address).
- ✅ Zigbee security uses **AES-128-CCM** with three keys: **Master, Link, Network**.
- ✅ **Trust Center** (Coordinator) is the central security authority.
- ✅ **Standard Mode** uses Network Key; **High Security Mode** uses Link Keys.
- ✅ **OIC (OCF)** is an open-source IoT interoperability framework.
- ✅ OIC uses **CoAP over UDP** with **DTLS** security.
- ✅ OIC models everything as **resources** with **URIs** and **properties**.
- ✅ Discovery uses multicast `GET /oic/res`; messaging uses CoAP `GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/OBSERVE`.
- ✅ OIC supports **bridging** to Zigbee, BLE, Z-Wave, etc.
- ✅ The 2.4 GHz channel center frequency is given by $f_c = 5k + 2350$ MHz, $k = 11, \ldots, 26$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OIC Architecture and Design Principles. IoT Devices and Deployment Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** OIC Architecture and Design Principles. IoT Devices and Deployment Models # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC) The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)** was an industry group (founded in 2014, later rebranded as the **Connectivity Standards Alliance / Matter Alliance** in 2021) tasked with defining a unified, open-source framework for billions of IoT devices to discover, connect, and interoperate seamlessly — irrespective of manufacturer, OS, transport, or physical layer. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim-aligned):** > OIC (Open Interconnect Consortium) is a standardization body that provides a specification and framework for device-to-device communication in IoT. Its reference architecture is implemented primarily through the **IoTivity** open-source project, providing APIs in **C, C++, Java, and Arduino**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator" Imagine every IoT device in the world as a person speaking a different native language (Samsung speaks Korean, Philips speaks Dutch, Bosch speaks German). Without a **Universal Translator**, none of them can collaborate. **OIC is that Universal Translator.** It doesn't replace the native languages; instead, it installs a small *interpreter layer* on every device so that a Samsung refrigerator can ask a Philips light bulb, "Please dim yourself to 40%," and the bulb understands. ## 1.3 What is an IoT Device? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** An **IoT Device** is a physical or virtual entity (sensor, actuator, smart object, embedded system, or computational node) that is uniquely identifiable, capable of sensing/actuating the physical world, gathering data, communicating over a network, and triggering actions — typically without direct human intervention. ### Real-World Analogy — "The Five Senses of a Computer" A traditional computer has only a keyboard and mouse (manual senses). An IoT device adds **sight (camera), hearing (microphone), touch (temperature/pressure sensor), smell (gas sensor), and motion (accelerometer/gyroscope)** — turning dumb machines into perceptive, decision-making actors in a cyber-physical system. ## 1.4 What are IoT Deployment Models? A **Deployment Model** describes the *operational topology* — *where the data is processed, stored, and acted upon* — across the IoT stack (device, gateway, cloud, edge). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The four canonical IoT Deployment Models are: > 1. **IoT Device-based** (all processing on-device) > 2. **IoT Gateway-based** (gateway mediates device ↔ cloud) > 3. **IoT Cloud-based** (raw data streamed to cloud) > 4. **IoT Hybrid / Fog-based** (distributed between edge + cloud) > [!NOTE] > **Key Architectural Constants (must memorize):** > - **OIC Reference Architecture Layers:** **7 layers** (Business → Application → Service → Core → Resource → Connectivity → Device) > - **IoTivity Project Languages:** **C, C++, Java, Arduino** > - **IoTivity Tiers:** **Tizen, Android, Arduino, Linux** (deployment tiers) # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The OIC Reference Architecture (7-Layer Model) OIC defines a **layered, modular, and abstracted architecture** where each layer exposes services to the layer above and consumes services from the layer below. This separation of concerns is what enables cross-vendor interoperability. ### Layer-by-Layer Breakdown | Layer # | Layer Name | Functional Responsibility | Real-World Analogy | |:-------:|:-----------|:--------------------------|:-------------------| | **L1** | **Device (Resources) Layer** | Physical sensors/actuators, data acquisition, hardware abstraction | Human nerve endings & muscles | | **L2** | **Connectivity Layer** | Reliable transport (TCP/UDP, BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, Z-Wave, LoRaWAN) | Spinal cord & nervous system | | **L3** | **Resource Layer** | Resource modeling (RAML, JSON-LD), state observation, attribute discovery | Memory of sensations | | **L4** | **Core (Identity/Mgmt) Layer** | Device identity, provisioning, authentication, cryptography (PKI/DTLS) | Personal identity card & keys | | **L5** | **Service Layer** | Service discovery, pairing, group management, remote access | Phone contacts & services | | **L6** | **Application Layer** | End-user application logic, UI, analytics, business rules | Conscious thinking & decisions | | **L7** | **Business Layer** | Organizational goals, SLA, monetization, policy enforcement | Personal goals & ethics | > [!NOTE] > **Why 7 Layers?** OIC deliberately mirrors the spirit of the **OSI 7-Layer Model** for the IoT context. This makes the architecture teachable, vendor-neutral, and extensible — three properties the KTU examiner rewards heavily. ## 2.2 The Five Foundational OIC Design Principles ### Principle 1 — **Discoverability** - Every OIC-compliant device must announce its **presence**, **capabilities** (resources/services), and **metadata** to the network. - Protocol: **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** over UDP/DTLS. - Discovery query example (high-yield for viva): `GET /oic/res` ### Principle 2 — **Interoperability** - Heterogeneous devices from different vendors MUST communicate using **standard resource models** (defined in RAML/JSON-LD). - Achieved through **Common Resource Models** like `oic.r.switch.binary` (binary switch resource). ### Principle 3 — **Security & Privacy (End-to-End)** - Built-in cryptographic primitives: **DTLS 1.2**, **TLS 1.2**, **AES-128/256**, **X.509 certificates**. - Security services exposed: **Provisioning**, **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**. ### Principle 4 — **Scalability** - Architecture supports billions of devices through lightweight protocols (CoAP replaces HTTP for constrained nodes). - Hierarchical addressing via IPv6 + 6LoWPAN. ### Principle 5 — **Portability & Cross-Platform** - IoTivity SDK is available on **Tizen (Samsung), Android (Google), Arduino, Linux**. - This allows the same application code to deploy on phones, watches, refrigerators, and microcontrollers. ## 2.3 IoTivity Project — The Open-Source Implementation of OIC **IoTivity** is the de-facto open-source reference implementation of the OIC specification. | Property | Specification | |:---------|:--------------| | **Original Author** | Samsung + Intel + Broadcom + Dell | | **License** | Apache 2.0 | | **Supported Languages** | **C, C++, Java, Arduino** | | **Supported Platforms (Tiers)** | **Tizen, Android, Arduino, Linux** | | **Communication Protocols** | CoAP, DTLS, BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee (via adapters) | | **Resource Description** | RAML / JSON-LD | | **API Style** | RESTful (GET/PUT/POST/DELETE on /oic/res) | ## 2.4 IoT Devices — Formal Classification ### 2.4.1 By Function 1. **Sensor Devices** — *perceive* (temperature, humidity, motion, gas, light, biosensors) 2. **Actuator Devices** — *act* (relays, motors, valves, LEDs, robotic arms) 3. **Hybrid Devices** — both perceive and act (smart thermostat, smart lock) 4. **Gateway/Edge Devices** — aggregate, translate, pre-process (Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC) ### 2.4.2 By Computational Capability - **Class 0** — < 100 KB flash, severely constrained (passive RFID tags) - **Class 1** — ~100 KB flash, limited (sensor motes like TelosB) - **Class 2** — ~250 KB flash, moderate (smart wearables) - **Class 3** — > 1 MB flash, capable (smartphones, gateways) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** IoTivity primarily targets **Class 2 and Class 3** devices. Class 0/1 are addressed by lower-level protocols like 6LoWPAN + CoAP alone. ## 2.5 IoT Deployment Models — Detailed Anatomy ### Model 1 — IoT Device-Based (Standalone / On-Premise) - All sensing, computation, storage, and actuation happen **on the device itself**. - No cloud dependency. - **Pros:** Ultra-low latency, full offline operation, maximum privacy. - **Cons:** Limited compute, no global learning, expensive per-device. - **Example:** Industrial PLC with embedded ML inference. ### Model 2 — IoT Gateway-Based (Local Aggregation) - Devices → Local Gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi running IoTivity) → Optional Cloud. - Gateway performs **protocol translation** (BLE → MQTT), data filtering, local analytics. - **Pros:** Reduced bandwidth, faster local response, security choke-point. - **Cons:** Gateway becomes single point of failure. - **Example:** Smart home with ZigBee bulbs + BLE sensors → Home Hub → AWS IoT. ### Model 3 — IoT Cloud-Based (Pure Cloud) - Devices send raw telemetry directly to cloud over **MQTT/HTTPS/CoAP**. - Cloud performs analytics, ML, storage (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT). - **Pros:** Massive compute, global scale, advanced ML. - **Cons:** High latency (50–500 ms WAN), privacy concerns, dependency on connectivity. ### Model 4 — IoT Hybrid / Fog-Based - A **continuum of compute** across device → edge → fog → cloud. - Latency-critical inference at the edge; batch analytics in the cloud. - **Pros:** Best of all worlds — low latency + massive scale. - **Cons:** Complex orchestration, security across tiers. - **Example:** Autonomous vehicles, smart grids, remote surgery. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (For This Topic) | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Typical Use | |:-:|:--------|:---------------------|:------|:------------| | 1 | **OIC Layer Count** | $N_{OIC} = 7$ | integer | Architecture questions | | 2 | **IoTivity Tier Count** | $N_{Tiers} = 4$ (Tizen, Android, Arduino, Linux) | integer | SDK/Platform questions | | 3 | **Device Density per Gateway** | $D_g = \dfrac{N_{devices}}{N_{gateways}}$ | devices/gateway | Capacity planning | | 4 | **End-to-End Latency (Hybrid)** | $T_{E2E} = T_{device} + T_{edge} + T_{cloud} + T_{network}$ | ms | Deployment latency | | 5 | **Bandwidth Aggregation** | $B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (f_s \times b_i)$ | kbps | IoT gateway sizing | | 6 | **CoAP Header Size** | $H_{CoAP} = 4$ bytes (fixed) | bytes | Wireless efficiency | | 7 | **DTLS Handshake RTTs** | $N_{DTLS} = 1.5$ to $2$ RTT | round-trips | Security overhead | | 8 | **Power Budget (Battery)** | $P_{budget} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{T_{life}} \times V_{bat}$ | mW | Device deployment | | 9 | **Resource Discovery Latency** | $T_{disc} \approx 1$ to $3$ RTT | s | OIC discoverability | | 10 | **Maximum Hop Count (6LoWPAN)** | $H_{max} \leq 64$ | hops | Mesh deployment | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by **Smart City planners** to size gateways, by **biomedical IoT designers** to compute battery life of implanted sensors, and by **industrial automation engineers** to certify deterministic latency for safety-critical control loops. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Power Budget of an IoT Deployment When deploying thousands of IoT devices on battery, the **system-level power budget** determines replacement cycles and operational cost. Let us derive it rigorously. ### Given - Number of devices: $N_d$ - Average current draw per device: $I_{avg}$ (in mA) - Battery capacity: $C_{bat}$ (in mAh) - Battery voltage: $V_{bat}$ (in Volts) - Desired operational lifetime: $T_{life}$ (in hours) ### Step-by-Step Derivation The energy stored in a single battery is given by the fundamental battery equation: $$E_{bat} = C_{bat} \times V_{bat} \quad [\text{mAh} \times \text{V} = \text{mWh}]$$ The power drawn by a single IoT device is calculated from Ohm's law generalized: $$P_{device} = I_{avg} \times V_{bat} \quad [\text{mW}]$$ The operational lifetime of a single device, assuming ideal linear discharge, is: $$T_{life} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{device}} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \times V_{bat}}{I_{avg} \times V_{bat}} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} \quad [\text{hours}]$$ This simplifies beautifully — voltage cancels — leaving the classic *"milliamp-hours divided by milli-amps equals hours"* formula. For a deployment of $N_d$ homogeneous devices, the **aggregate energy demand per replacement cycle** is: $$E_{total} = N_d \times E_{bat} = N_d \times C_{bat} \times V_{bat} \quad [\text{mWh}]$$ Substituting a numerical example for clarity: $$E_{total} = 1000 \times 2400 \text{ mAh} \times 3.7 \text{ V} = 8{,}880{,}000 \text{ mWh} = 8.88 \text{ kWh}$$ ### Analytical Insight > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners love asking *"If 1000 wearable ECG sensors each draw 50 mA from a 3.7 V, 600 mAh Li-Po battery, how often must the hospital replace batteries?"* > The student is expected to compute: > $$T_{life} = \dfrac{600}{50} = 12 \text{ hours}$$ > Then conclude: *twice-daily replacement schedule — operationally infeasible for an ICU, hence duty-cycling is mandatory.* ## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth Sizing for an IoT Gateway A deployment must compute the **uplink bandwidth** required by the gateway to the cloud so as not to saturate the WAN link. ### Given - $N$ sensor streams aggregated by the gateway - Sampling frequency per stream: $f_s$ (in Hz) - Bits per sample: $b_i$ (after compression/encoding) ### Step-by-Step Derivation The data rate generated by a single sensor stream is: $$R_i = f_s \times b_i \quad [\text{bits per second}]$$ Summing across all $N$ streams (assuming independent sources, no inter-sample redundancy exploited): $$B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (f_s \times b_i) \quad [\text{bps}]$$ For the homogeneous case where every stream has identical $f_s$ and $b$: $$B_{total} = N \times f_s \times b \quad [\text{bps}]$$ Adding the cryptographic overhead of DTLS (approximate 5–10%): $$B_{DTLS} = B_{total} \times (1 + \alpha), \quad \alpha \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10$$ Adding the CoAP encapsulation overhead (header = 4 bytes, token = 1–8 bytes): $$B_{payload} = B_{DTLS} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{H_{overhead}}{L_{payload}}\right)$$ where $H_{overhead}$ is the protocol header and $L_{payload}$ is the typical payload size. ### Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A hospital gateway aggregates 500 ECG sensors. Each sensor samples at $f_s = 250$ Hz with $b = 16$ bits per sample. Compute the gateway uplink bandwidth, assuming 7% DTLS overhead. $$B_{total} = 500 \times 250 \times 16 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2 \text{ Mbps}$$ $$B_{DTLS} = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 1.07 = 2{,}140{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.14 \text{ Mbps}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The hospital needs at minimum a **3 Mbps uplinked** (with a 30% headroom for bursts). This computation directly justifies why hospitals use dedicated fiber/MPLS links rather than consumer broadband. ## 3.3 Derivation: End-to-End Latency for a Hybrid (Fog) Deployment In a hybrid deployment, latency must be analytically partitioned across tiers. $$T_{E2E} = T_{sensor}} + T_{gateway} + T_{fog} + T_{cloud} + T_{network}$$ ### Analytical Expansion - **Sensor acquisition latency:** $T_{sensor} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} \times N_{samples}$ (depends on sampling window) - **Gateway queuing latency:** $T_{gateway} = \dfrac{L_{queue}}{\mu}$ (M/M/1 queuing approximation), where $\mu$ is service rate. - **Fog compute latency:** $T_{fog} = \dfrac{F_{ops}}{P_{fog}}$ (FLOPS / processor power) - **Cloud round-trip latency:** $T_{cloud} = 2 \times \dfrac{D_{WAN}}{c_{fiber}}$ (propagation delay) - **Network jitter buffer:** $T_{jitter} \approx 3 \times \sigma_{jitter}$ (3-sigma rule for QoS) For a **remote patient monitoring** scenario: $$T_{sensor} = 0.004 \text{ s}, \quad T_{gateway} = 0.002 \text{ s}, \quad T_{fog} = 0.020 \text{ s}$$ $$T_{cloud} = 2 \times \dfrac{500 \text{ km}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.005 \text{ s}, \quad T_{jitter} = 0.003 \text{ s}$$ $$T_{E2E} = 0.004 + 0.002 + 0.020 + 0.005 + 0.003 = 0.034 \text{ s} = 34 \text{ ms}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** For **critical-care** biomedical IoT, latency must stay **below 100 ms** (ISO/IEEE 11073). Our 34 ms satisfies this easily — a candidate answer an examiner would award full marks. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Deployment Model | Criterion | IoT Device-Based | IoT Gateway-Based | IoT Cloud-Based | IoT Hybrid / Fog | |:----------|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:|:----------------:| | **Latency** | **< 1 ms (Best)** | 1–10 ms | 50–500 ms | 5–50 ms | | **Privacy** | **Excellent** | Good | Poor | Good | | **Scalability** | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | Excellent | | **Offline Resilience** | **Total** | Partial | None | Partial | | **Compute Power** | Low | Moderate | **Massive** | Distributed | | **Cost per Device** | High | Moderate | Low | Low-Moderate | | **Best Use Case** | Implanted sensors | Smart homes | Smart cities | Autonomous vehicles | | **Biomedical Fit** | Pacemakers | Wearables | Hospital analytics | Tele-surgery | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — OIC 7-Layer Reference Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7: BUSINESS LAYER
Policy, SLA, Monetization"]:::top L6["LAYER 6: APPLICATION LAYER
Apps, UI, Analytics"]:::mid L5["LAYER 5: SERVICE LAYER
Discovery, Pairing, Remote Access"]:::mid L4["LAYER 4: CORE LAYER
Identity, Security, Provisioning"]:::core L3["LAYER 3: RESOURCE LAYER
Resource Modeling, Attributes"]:::core L2["LAYER 2: CONNECTIVITY LAYER
TCP, UDP, BLE, WiFi, ZigBee"]:::low L1["LAYER 1: DEVICE LAYER
Sensors, Actuators, Hardware"]:::low L7 --> L6 L6 --> L5 L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef core fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef low fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IoTivity Stack and Supported Tiers ```mermaid flowchart LR APP["APPLICATION
Mobile Apps, Cloud Services"]:::app SDK["IoTivity SDK
C, C++, Java, Arduino"]:::sdk DISC["DISCOVERY
CoAP GET /oic/res"]:::mid SEC["SECURITY
DTLS 1.2, TLS 1.2, AES"]:::mid COMM["COMMUNICATION
WiFi, BLE, ZigBee, Ethernet"]:::low T1["TIER 1: Tizen
Samsung Smart Devices"]:::tier T2["TIER 2: Android
Phones and Tablets"]:::tier T3["TIER 3: Arduino
Microcontrollers"]:::tier T4["TIER 4: Linux
Servers and Gateways"]:::tier APP --> SDK SDK --> DISC SDK --> SEC SDK --> COMM SDK -.-> T1 SDK -.-> T2 SDK -.-> T3 SDK -.-> T4 classDef app fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef sdk fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef mid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef low fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef tier fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Four IoT Deployment Models Compared ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MODEL_A["MODEL 1: IoT DEVICE-BASED"] A1["Sensor"] --> A2["On-Device Processor"] --> A3["On-Device Storage"] --> A4["Actuator"] end subgraph MODEL_B["MODEL 2: IoT GATEWAY-BASED"] B1["Sensors"] --> B2["Local Gateway
Raspberry Pi / IoTivity"] --> B3{"Internet?"} B3 -->|Yes| B4["Cloud"] B3 -->|No| B5["Local Action"] end subgraph MODEL_C["MODEL 3: IoT CLOUD-BASED"] C1["Sensors"] -->|MQTT/HTTPS| C2["Cloud Platform
AWS IoT / Azure IoT"] --> C3["Analytics Engine"] --> C4["Dashboard / Action"] end subgraph MODEL_D["MODEL 4: IoT HYBRID / FOG-BASED"] D1["Sensors"] --> D2["Edge Node
Real-time ML"] --> D3["Fog Layer
Regional Compute"] --> D4["Cloud
Batch Analytics"] end classDef model fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class MODEL_A,MODEL_B,MODEL_C,MODEL_D model ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Device Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT["IoT DEVICES"]:::root FUNC["BY FUNCTION"]:::cat SENS["Sensors
Temp, Humidity, ECG, Gas"]:::leaf ACT["Actuators
Relays, Motors, Valves"]:::leaf HYB["Hybrid
Smart Lock, Thermostat"]:::leaf GTW["Gateways
RPi, Intel NUC"]:::leaf CLASS["BY COMPUTE CLASS"]:::cat C0["Class 0: Under 100KB
Passive RFID"]:::leaf C1["Class 1: ~100KB
TelosB Motes"]:::leaf C2["Class 2: ~250KB
Wearables"]:::leaf C3["Class 3: Above 1MB
Smartphones"]:::leaf ROOT --> FUNC FUNC --> SENS FUNC --> ACT FUNC --> HYB FUNC --> GTW ROOT --> CLASS CLASS --> C0 CLASS --> C1 CLASS --> C2 CLASS --> C3 classDef root fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef cat fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef leaf fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping (Diagram Fallback Justification):** > All four diagrams above are rendered as Mermaid `flowchart` constructs because the underlying concept is *logical layering* and *topological connectivity* — both of which Mermaid represents more clearly than any physical schematic could. Each box carries its functional identity and tier-color coding so the architecture is self-explanatory at a glance. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **List any five design principles of the OIC architecture.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full credit mapping):** The Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC) is built on five foundational design principles: 1. **Discoverability** — Every OIC-compliant device must announce its presence, capabilities (resources), and metadata to other devices on the network using the CoAP-based `GET /oic/res` query mechanism. *(1 mark)* 2. **Interoperability** — Heterogeneous devices from different vendors must communicate using standard, vendor-neutral resource models (e.g., `oic.r.switch.binary`), enabling true plug-and-play. *(1 mark)* 3. **Security and Privacy** — End-to-end security is mandated through DTLS 1.2, TLS 1.2, AES-128/256, and X.509 certificates, with provisioning handled at the Core Layer. *(0.5 marks)* 4. **Scalability** — The architecture must support billions of devices, achieved through lightweight protocols (CoAP over UDP), IPv6 + 6LoWPAN, and hierarchical addressing. *(0.5 marks)* 5. **Portability / Cross-Platform Support** — The IoTivity SDK is available on four tiers: Tizen, Android, Arduino, and Linux, allowing the same application code to deploy across mobile, embedded, and server platforms. *(0.5 marks)* *(Note: Award 0.5 mark per correctly stated principle, with concise one-line explanation. Total = 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Differentiate between IoT Gateway-based and IoT Cloud-based deployment models.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | IoT Gateway-Based | IoT Cloud-Based | |:-------|:------------------|:----------------| | **Data Processing Location** | Local gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi) | Remote cloud (AWS, Azure) | | **Latency** | Low (1–10 ms) | High (50–500 ms) | | **Internet Dependency** | Optional — can act offline | Mandatory | | **Security Choke-Point** | Single hardened gateway | Distributed cloud IAM | | **Use Case** | Smart homes, factory floors | Smart cities, fleet analytics | | **Bandwidth Optimization** | Local aggregation & filtering before upload | Raw data streaming — high WAN cost | *(Award 1.5 marks for each correctly differentiated pair, 3 marks total.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the seven-layer OIC reference architecture with a neat diagram. State the responsibility of each layer.** **Model Solution:** The OIC reference architecture is a **7-layer, modular, vendor-neutral framework** designed for IoT interoperability. The layers, from top to bottom, are: 1. **Business Layer (Layer 7)** — Defines organizational goals, SLAs, monetization models, and policy enforcement. It abstracts business intent (e.g., "reduce hospital readmission rate by 20%") into actionable technical requirements. 2. **Application Layer (Layer 6)** — Hosts end-user applications (mobile apps, dashboards, web portals). It implements domain logic — e.g., an alarm dashboard in a smart hospital. 3. **Service Layer (Layer 5)** — Implements service discovery (`/oic/res` lookup), device pairing, group management, and remote access control. It exposes high-level services such as "find all lights in room 302." 4. **Core Layer (Layer 4)** — The heart of security and identity. Manages device identity (UUIDs, X.509 certs), provisioning, authentication, and access control lists (ACLs). DTLS handshake originates here. 5. **Resource Layer (Layer 3)** — Models physical and virtual resources (sensors, actuators) using RAML or JSON-LD. Each resource exposes attributes (e.g., a temperature sensor exposes `currentTemperature`, `units`, `range`). 6. **Connectivity Layer (Layer 2)** — Manages reliable data transport across heterogeneous physical media: Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave, LoRaWAN, Ethernet. Adapters abstract the radio details from upper layers. 7. **Device Layer (Layer 1)** — The physical hardware: sensors (temperature, ECG, accelerometer), actuators (motors, relays), and embedded MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, ESP32). Provides the hardware abstraction layer (HAL). *(Award 1 mark per layer explanation, 7 marks total. Diagram in Section 4.1 may be reproduced for an additional 0–1 mark as a bonus.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the IoTivity open-source project: its supported languages, deployment tiers, and the role of CoAP in resource discovery.** **Model Solution:** **IoTivity** is the flagship open-source (Apache 2.0) implementation of the OIC specification, originally incubated by Samsung, Intel, Broadcom, and Dell. **Supported Languages (1.5 marks):** - **C** — for resource-constrained Class 1/2 MCUs (Arduino, ARM Mbed). - **C++** — for higher-performance gateways and Linux servers. - **Java** — for Android phones and Tizen smart devices. - **Arduino** — a domain-specific wrapper over C++ for rapid prototyping. **Supported Deployment Tiers (1.5 marks):** 1. **Tizen** — Samsung's smart-TVs, refrigerators, smartwatches. 2. **Android** — phones, tablets, Android Things (now replaced by Embedded OS). 3. **Arduino** — Uno, Mega, Nano, ESP8266/ESP32 (with port). 4. **Linux** — Raspberry Pi, Intel NUC, BeagleBone, x86 servers. **Role of CoAP in Resource Discovery (4 marks):** - **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is the *de facto* discovery protocol in IoTivity — a lightweight RESTful counterpart to HTTP designed for constrained devices. - It runs over **UDP** (not TCP), reducing handshake overhead from 1.5–2 RTTs to a single exchange. - **Header size is only 4 bytes** (vs. HTTP's hundreds), crucial for low-power radios. - Discovery is performed via: - `GET /oic/res` — query the network for all available resources. - The responding devices reply with a JSON-LD payload listing their resource URIs, types, and interfaces. - Once a resource is discovered, clients use `GET`, `PUT`, `POST`, `DELETE` to read/write state. - **DTLS** layer secures CoAP exchanges — providing the confidentiality and integrity IoT requires. *(Award marks as per breakdown above. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Classify IoT devices by function and by computational capability. Explain each class with biomedical examples.** **Model Solution:** **Classification by Function (3.5 marks):** 1. **Sensor Devices** — Devices that *perceive* the physical world. - *Biomedical Example:* Wearable ECG patch (e.g., iRhythm Zio) capturing cardiac electrical activity. - *Other:* Temperature sensor (LM35), SpO2 pulse oximeter, galvanic skin response sensor. 2. **Actuator Devices** — Devices that *act* upon the physical world. - *Biomedical Example:* Insulin pump actuator delivering bolus doses in response to CGM readings. - *Other:* Smart infusion valve, MRI gradient coil driver. 3. **Hybrid Devices** — Combine sensing and actuation. - *Biomedical Example:* Smart pacemaker — senses intrinsic cardiac rhythm (sensor) and delivers pacing pulses (actuator) when bradycardia is detected. 4. **Gateway / Edge Devices** — Aggregate, translate, and pre-process. - *Biomedical Example:* Raspberry Pi hub in a hospital ward collecting 200 patient vitals over BLE and forwarding to the EMR cloud over HTTPS. **Classification by Computational Capability (3.5 marks):** | Class | Flash Memory | Biomedical Example | |:-----:|:-------------|:-------------------| | **Class 0** | < 100 KB | Passive RFID patient wristband (read-only) | | **Class 1** | ~100 KB | TelosB-style body-area network mote | | **Class 2** | ~250 KB | Wearable fitness band with embedded ML | | **Class 3** | > 1 MB | Smartphone, tablet-based patient monitor | > IoTivity primarily targets **Class 2 and Class 3** devices; Class 0/1 typically use bare CoAP + 6LoWPAN without a full IoTivity stack. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Compare the four IoT deployment models with respect to latency, scalability, privacy, and a biomedical use case. Justify which model is best suited for a remote robotic surgery application.** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Criterion | Device-Based | Gateway-Based | Cloud-Based | Hybrid / Fog | |:----------|:------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:------------:| | **Latency** | < 1 ms | 1–10 ms | 50–500 ms | 5–50 ms | | **Scalability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | | **Privacy** | Excellent | Good | Poor | Good | | **Biomedical Use Case** | Implanted pacemaker | Wearable ECG patch | Hospital-wide analytics | Tele-surgery | **Justification for Remote Robotic Surgery (3 marks):** Remote robotic surgery (e.g., da Vinci over 5G) demands: - **Latency < 10 ms** — a 100 ms delay in haptic feedback is perceptible and dangerous. - **Local reliability** — the robot must NOT fail if the cloud link drops mid-suture. - **Distributed compute** — haptic rendering at the edge, AI vision enhancement at the fog, archival in the cloud. Therefore, the **IoT Hybrid / Fog Deployment Model** is the only acceptable choice. It provides: - Edge-side haptic control loops (sub-millisecond response). - Fog-side ML inference (e.g., real-time tissue segmentation) with 5–50 ms latency. - Cloud-side archival and cross-institution learning — without ever being on the critical-path of the robotic actuator. *(Award 2 marks for the table, 3 marks for the justification. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Examiners Reveal):** > > 1. **OIC vs IoTivity Confusion** — OIC is the *consortium and specification*; IoTivity is the *implementation*. Writing "OIC is an open-source project" loses 1 mark instantly. > > 2. **Layer Count Error** — Students often write "OIC has 5 layers" or "OSI has 7 layers for IoT." The correct answer is **OIC = 7 layers**, **OSI = 7 layers** (separate models), **TCP/IP = 4 layers**. > > 3. **CoAP Misnaming** — CoAP runs over **UDP**, NOT TCP. Saying "CoAP over TCP" loses 0.5 marks. > > 4. **Deployment Model Confusion** — The "Hybrid" model is sometimes called "Fog" model — both are acceptable in KTU. However, calling it "Edge Computing" alone is incorrect; edge is a *tier* within the hybrid model. > > 5. **Formula Unit Errors** — When computing bandwidth, students forget to convert bits ↔ bytes (÷ 8). The examiner *will* check this in 14-mark numerical problems. > > 6. **Skipping the Diagram** — A 7-mark question asking for an architecture diagram without a diagram loses 1–2 marks unless textually compensated with extreme precision. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ OIC = **Open Interconnect Consortium**; IoTivity = its open-source implementation. - ✅ OIC Reference Architecture = **7 layers** (Business → Application → Service → Core → Resource → Connectivity → Device). - ✅ IoTivity supports **4 languages**: C, C++, Java, Arduino. - ✅ IoTivity supports **4 tiers**: Tizen, Android, Arduino, Linux. - ✅ IoTivity uses **CoAP over UDP** for discovery (`GET /oic/res`) with **DTLS** for security. - ✅ **5 OIC design principles**: Discoverability, Interoperability, Security & Privacy, Scalability, Portability. - ✅ **4 IoT Deployment Models**: Device-based, Gateway-based, Cloud-based, Hybrid/Fog-based. - ✅ **Hybrid (Fog) model** offers the best balance for latency-sensitive biomedical applications like tele-surgery. - ✅ **CoAP header = 4 bytes** — far smaller than HTTP, suited for constrained radios. - ✅ **DTLS handshake = 1.5–2 RTTs** — must be accounted for in latency-sensitive deployments. - ✅ **Device Class 0/1/2/3** correspond to **<100 KB / ~100 KB / ~250 KB / >1 MB** flash memory. - ✅ KTU examiners reward **neat labeled diagrams** — always include one for 14-mark architecture questions. - ✅ **Memorize the formula sheet** in Section 2.6 — at least 2 numericals per KTU paper test these.

topics.title: IoTivity: An Open-Source IoT Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoTivity: An Open-Source IoT Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoTivity** is an **open-source software framework** initiated by Samsung Electronics and hosted by the Linux Foundation, designed to enable seamless **device-to-device connectivity** and interoperability across heterogeneous IoT (Internet of Things) ecosystems. It was originally developed under the stewardship of the **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)**, which was later rebranded as the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** in 2016. The framework provides a unified, **standards-based communication stack** that abstracts hardware, operating system, and transport-layer differences, allowing constrained and powerful devices alike to discover, connect, and exchange data over diverse physical media (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Bluetooth Low Energy, ZigBee, NFC, and Thread).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IoTivity is presented as the *de-facto* open-source reference implementation of the OCF specification. Students must remember that IoTivity ≠ a single protocol — it is an **entire middleware stack** spanning the Application, Service, and Adaptation layers of the OSI model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **universal translator device** sitting inside every smart device (light bulb, fitness band, refrigerator, heart-rate monitor). 

- Without IoTivity: A Samsung phone cannot directly talk to a Philips bulb because each vendor speaks a different "language" (Samsung uses SmartThings, Philips uses Hue, etc.). You need a vendor-specific app and a vendor-specific hub.
- With IoTivity: Every device ships with the **same translator chip (the IoTivity stack)**. Now any device can say *"I am a light with brightness control"* in a **standardized voice**, and any controller can understand and command it — irrespective of manufacturer.

This "standardized voice" is the **OCF Resource Model**, and the "translation rules" are built upon **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**, which is essentially a lightweight, UDP-based cousin of HTTP tailored for tiny, battery-powered devices.

### 1.3 Core Technical Metrics & Constants

The following are the **standardized parameters** enforced by the IoTivity specification:

- **Default Multicast Discovery Address:** `224.0.1.187` (IPv4) and `FF03::FD` / `FF05::FD` (IPv6) — used for device/resource discovery.
- **Default CoAP Port:** `5683` (unsecured) and `5684` (DTLS-secured).
- **Encoding Standard:** JSON / **CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation)** for payload serialization.
- **Security Suite:** **DTLS 1.2** with **AES-128-CCM** cipher suite.
- **Endianness:** Little-endian byte ordering on wire.
- **License:** Apache 2.0 (open-source).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** KTU examiners frequently test the **default CoAP port (5683)** and the **multicast discovery IP**. Memorize these as numerical constants — they are analogous to *port 80 for HTTP* in classical networking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IoTivity Architecture — A Layered Breakdown

IoTivity is structured as a **four-tier modular stack**. Each tier encapsulates a specific responsibility, enabling **portability across OS platforms** (Linux, Tizen, Android, iOS, RIOT, Zephyr, Contiki, Windows).

#### Tier 1 — Adaptation Layer (Bottom)
- Provides a uniform abstraction over physical transports.
- Implementations: Wi-Fi Adapter, Ethernet Adapter, BLE Adapter, ZigBee Adapter.
- **Why:** A developer writing an IoTivity application on a BLE link should not have to rewrite code for an IP-based device.

#### Tier 2 — Communication Layer
- Implements **CoAP** over UDP for IP-based connectivity.
- Handles **DTLS** session establishment, message confidentiality, integrity, and replay protection.
- For low-power mesh transports, the CoAP semantics are mapped onto the corresponding radio's native primitives (e.g., GATT for BLE).

#### Tier 3 — Service Layer (The "Brain")
- Manages **resource representation**, **discovery**, **group communication**, **device management**, and **security provisioning**.
- Implements the **OCF Core Specification**:
  - Resource Directory (RD)
  - Device Discovery (multicast + unicast)
  - Security Resource Manager (SRM)

#### Tier 4 — Application Layer (Top)
- Exposes the stack to user code via high-level C, C++, or Java APIs.
- Developers declare **Resource Handlers** (`OCRepPayload`, `OCEntityHandlerResult`).
- Cloud bridges (e.g., OneIoT cloud agent) extend reach to wide-area networks.

### 2.2 The OCF Resource Model

Every "thing" in IoTivity is modeled as a **Resource** — a virtual endpoint identified by a **URI** and possessing **Properties** (key-value pairs), **Actions** (invokable methods), and **Events** (asynchronous notifications).

$$\text{Resource} = \{\, \text{URI},\ \text{Resource Types},\ \text{Interfaces},\ \text{Properties},\ \text{Actions}\,\}$$

The URI follows a hierarchical structure analogous to REST:

$$\text{URI} = \text{coaps}://\langle\text{device\_IP}\rangle:\langle\text{port}\rangle/\langle\text{resource\_path}\rangle$$

Example: `coaps://192.168.1.42:5684/light/1/brightness`

### 2.3 Discovery Mechanism — The Heart of Interoperability

IoTivity supports two discovery modes:

| Mode | Trigger | Scope | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Multicast Discovery** | `OCF_DISCOVERY_MULTICAST` | All devices on local network | First-time pairing, "find my devices" |
| **Unicast Discovery** | Directed request to specific device/IP | Single target | Configuration, targeted queries |

The discovery flow uses **CoAP GET** requests with the special URI `/.well-known/ocf/res`:

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{GET coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res}} \text{Network}$$

Devices respond with their **Resource Directory** listing every exposed endpoint and resource type.

### 2.4 Security Architecture

IoTivity employs a **three-pronged security model** mapped to OCF's security tiers:

1. **Just-Works Provisioning** — Used for resource-constrained devices lacking keypads/screens; relies on DTLS handshake.
2. **Random PIN / Manufacturer Certificate** — Mid-tier security with shared secret.
3. **PKI / X.509 Mutual Authentication** — Enterprise/medical-grade deployments.

$$\text{Secure Session} = \text{DTLS Handshake} + \text{EDHOC (Optional)} + \text{CBOR Payload Encryption}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Parameter / Concept | Value or Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default CoAP UDP Port | **5683** | Decimal, unsecured |
| Default CoAP DTLS Port | **5684** | Decimal, secured |
| Multicast Discovery IPv4 | **224.0.1.187** | Class D address |
| Multicast Discovery IPv6 | **FF05::FD** | Site-scope / realm-scope variants exist |
| Resource Discovery URI | `/.well-known/ocf/res` | Standard well-known path |
| Encoding | CBOR (binary) / JSON | CBOR preferred on constrained |
| Security | DTLS 1.2 / AES-128-CCM | Mandatory cipher |
| Header (CoAP) | 4-byte base + TKL | Token Length $\in [0,8]$ bytes |
