| CoAP Method Code (GET) | 0x01 (1) | Confirmable (CON) or Non-CON |
| CoAP Method Code (POST) | 0x02 (2) | Resource creation/update |
| CoAP Method Code (PUT) | 0x03 (3) | Update |
| CoAP Method Code (DELETE) | 0x04 (4) | Resource removal |
| CoAP Method Code (OBSERVE) | 0x06 (6) | Subscribe for notifications |
| Stack License | Apache 2.0 | Open-source |
| Governing Body | OCF (formerly OIC) | Industry consortium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a biomedical deployment (e.g., wearable ECG + bedside monitor), the IoTivity stack guarantees that devices from different OEMs can share vitals data without proprietary gateways — a critical requirement for **interoperable Point-of-Care (PoC) systems** in hospitals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: URI Construction for a Discovered Resource

The OCF specification mandates that every resource be uniquely addressable. Given the following identifiers:

- **Device UUID (UDN):** `11111111-2222-3333-4444-555555555555`
- **Resource Type (`rt`):** `core.light`
- **Resource Instance (`id`):** `0`
- **Endpoint Host:** `2001:db8::a1`
- **Port:** `5684`

We construct the canonical OCF URI step-by-step.

#### Step 1 — Establish the Base URL
The base URL is the device's secure endpoint:

$$ \text{base} = \text{coaps://[2001:db8::a1]:5684} $$

Here the square brackets `[ ]` are mandatory in IPv6 literal addressing.

#### Step 2 — Append the Device Identifier Path
Per OCF Core Spec §9, the device's URI path begins with its UUID, URL-encoded as a single continuous string (hyphens retained or stripped depending on URI normalization).

$$ \text{device\_path} = \text{/11111111-2222-3333-4444-555555555555} $$

#### Step 3 — Append the Resource Type Tag
The resource-type descriptor is added using the `rt` virtual query parameter — but in URI path form, it is encoded as a relative segment:

$$ \text{rt\_path} = \text{/rt/core.light} $$

#### Step 4 — Append the Instance Identifier
The numeric instance follows the resource type:

$$ \text{id\_path} = \text{/id/0} $$

#### Step 5 — Combine via Path Concatenation
String concatenation via the `/` separator yields the final canonical URI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{final\_uri} &= \text{base} \Vert \text{device\_path} \Vert \text{rt\_path} \Vert \text{id\_path} \\
&= \text{coaps://[2001:db8::a1]:5684/11111111-2222-3333-4444-555555555555/rt/core.light/id/0}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Validate Against CoAP URI Grammar
Verify the resulting string satisfies the CoAP `coap-URI` ABNF production: scheme `coap` or `coaps`, followed by `//`, host, optional port, path-segments of non-zero length. The constructed URI passes this check and is now routable.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — IoTivity Operation Lifecycle

The full lifecycle of an IoTivity interaction can be decomposed into **seven ordered phases**. KTU examiners often ask students to "describe the flow from device boot-up to data exchange" — the matrix below is the canonical answer template.

| Phase | Action | Key Message | Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Bootstrap** | Device boots, loads stack, reads device identity (UUID), and provisioning credentials from secure storage. | N/A | Internal |
| **2. Network Attachment** | Adaptation Layer acquires IP/BLE/ZigBee connectivity. | N/A | Internal |
| **3. Discovery — Multicast** | Device multicasts `GET /.well-known/ocf/res` to `224.0.1.187:5683`. | CoAP GET (CON) | Client $\to$ Multicast |
| **4. Discovery — Response** | All listening devices reply with their resource manifests encoded in CBOR. | CoAP Response (2.05 Content) | Device $\to$ Client |
| **5. Resource Matching** | Client filters responses by `rt` (resource type) to find compatible targets. | Local computation | Client-side |
| **6. Secure Handshake** | Client initiates **DTLS 1.2 handshake** with chosen target on port **5684** using provisioning method. | ClientHello, ServerHello, Certificate Exchange | Bidirectional |
| **7. CRUDN Operations** | Client issues **Create/Read/Update/Delete/Notify** requests over the secured channel. | CoAP POST/GET/PUT/DELETE | Bidirectional |

### 3.3 Detailed Step-Walkthrough of a CoAP GET on a Resource

Consider the client (smartphone) reading the **battery level** of a wearable heart-rate monitor with URI `coaps://[fd00::1]:5684/sensor/hr/battery`.

**Step 1:** Client constructs CoAP packet:
- Version = 1, Type = CON (Confirmable), Token Length = 0
- Code = 0.01 (GET)
- Message ID = auto-generated 16-bit integer, say `0x4A2B`
- Options: URI-Path = `["sensor", "hr", "battery"]`, each encoded as a delta + length
- Payload: empty

**Step 2:** Client transmits the UDP datagram to port **5684** on host `fd00::1`.

**Step 3:** Server's IoTivity stack receives, parses, matches URI to a registered resource handler (`OCCoreMain`, `OCEntityHandler`), and executes the GET callback.

**Step 4:** Server constructs the response:
- Type = ACK
- Code = 2.05 (Content)
- Matches the Message ID `0x4A2B`
- Content-Format = `application/cbor`
- Payload: `{ "battery": 87 }` encoded as CBOR major type 0 (unsigned integer) with value 87

**Step 5:** Client receives ACK + payload, decodes CBOR, and updates the UI.

Total round-trip time on a healthy Wi-Fi network: **< 50 ms**; over BLE: **< 200 ms** including GATT notification latency.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level IoTivity Stack Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the four-tier IoTivity architecture along with the supported transports and the typical external touchpoints.

```mermaid
graph TD
    appA[Application Layer - User Apps, Cloud Bridges] --> svcA[Service Layer - Discovery, RD, Security, DM]
    svcA --> commA[Communication Layer - CoAP, DTLS, OBSERVE]
    commA --> adpA[Adaptation Layer - Transport Abstraction]
    adpA --> trA1[Wi-Fi Adapter]
    adpA --> trA2[Ethernet Adapter]
    adpA --> trA3[BLE Adapter]
    adpA --> trA4[ZigBee Adapter]
    adpA --> trA5[Thread / NFC Adapter]
    svcA --> resA[OCF Resource Model - URI, rt, id, if]
    commA --> secA[DTLS 1.2 - AES-128-CCM]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Device Discovery & Communication Flow

This diagram maps the message exchanges during a typical **client-server** interaction through the IoTivity stack, useful when the answer demands a **functional architecture** rather than a pictorial schematic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    dev1[Client Device App] --> api1[IoTivity C-API]
    api1 --> disc1[Discovery Engine - Multicast]
    disc1 -->|CoAP GET /.well-known/ocf/res| mcast1[UDP Multicast 224.0.1.187:5683]
    mcast1 --> rdir1[Server Resource Directory]
    rdir1 -->|Resource Manifest CBOR| api1
    api1 --> sec1[DTLS Handshake Engine]
    sec1 -->|Port 5684| hdl1[Server Entity Handler]
    hdl1 --> app2[Application Logic / Sensor Read]
    app2 --> hdl1
    hdl1 -->|CoAP Response 2.05| sec1
    sec1 -->|CBOR Payload| api1
    api1 --> dev1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Security Provisioning Tiers

```mermaid
graph TD
    sec0[OCF Security Tier Selection] --> t1[Tier 1 - Just Works - DTLS only]
    sec0 --> t2[Tier 2 - Random PIN or Manufacturer Cert]
    sec0 --> t3[Tier 3 - PKI X.509 Mutual Auth]
    t1 --> srm1[Security Resource Manager SRM]
    t2 --> srm1
    t3 --> srm1
    srm1 --> acl1[Access Control List ACL Storage]
    srm1 --> cred1[Credential Vault - Secure Storage]
    srm1 --> dtls1[DTLS 1.2 Session Manager]
    dtls1 --> app3[Protected CRUDN Communication]
```

### 4.4 Resource Hierarchy — A Light Bulb as an OCF Thing

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Device UUID 11111111-aaaa-bbbb-cccc-000000000001] --> rA1[/oic/d - Device Info]
    rootA --> rA2[/oic/p - Platform Info]
    rootA --> rA3[/light/1 - Resource Instance]
    rA3 --> propA1[Properties - dimming, onoff, brightness]
    rA3 --> actA1[Actions - start, stop, reset]
    rA3 --> evA1[Events - on change of brightness]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** Define IoTivity. Mention the default ports used for unsecured and secured CoAP communication in the IoTivity stack.

**Model Answer:**
IoTivity is an **open-source, vendor-neutral middleware framework** developed under the auspices of the Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF, formerly OIC) to enable seamless device-to-device communication and interoperability across heterogeneous IoT ecosystems. It encapsulates the OCF specifications and provides a layered stack for discovery, resource representation, and secure data exchange.

The default CoAP port for **unsecured** communication is **5683**, and the port for **DTLS-secured** communication is **5684**. These ports are standardized in IETF RFC 7252 and adopted by the IoTivity Core Specification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** State the role of the **well-known URI** `/.well-known/ocf/res` in the IoTivity stack. What transport-layer address is used for device discovery?

**Model Answer:**
The URI `/.well-known/ocf/res` is the standardized discovery endpoint defined by the OCF Core Specification. A client device issues a **CoAP GET request** to this URI to retrieve the resource manifest of all listening IoTivity devices on the local network. The manifest is returned in **CBOR-encoded format** containing each resource's URI, resource type, interface, and instance identifier.

Device discovery is performed using **UDP multicast**. The standard IPv4 multicast address is **`224.0.1.187`**, and the IPv6 equivalent is **`FF05::FD`**, both targeting UDP port **5683**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-tier architecture of the IoTivity stack**. Discuss the responsibilities of the Service Layer in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **OCF Resource Model**. Using a suitable example (e.g., a smart light bulb or a wearable heart-rate monitor), illustrate the construction of an OCF-compliant resource URI and its associated properties. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a)** The four-tier architecture of IoTivity is presented below:

**1. Adaptation Layer (Bottom-most):**
- Acts as a uniform abstraction over heterogeneous physical transports.
- Provides adapter implementations for Wi-Fi, Ethernet, BLE, ZigBee, NFC, and Thread.
- Hides transport-specific primitives from upper layers.

**2. Communication Layer:**
- Implements **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol) over UDP for IP transports.
- Manages **DTLS 1.2** sessions for confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
- Provides the OBSERVE option for asynchronous notification.

**3. Service Layer (Core of the stack):**
- Implements **Resource Directory (RD)** for managing local and remote resources.
- Handles **Discovery** via multicast and unicast.
- Implements the **Security Resource Manager (SRM)** for provisioning and ACL enforcement.
- Manages **Device Management (DM)** functions: factory reset, reboot, firmware update, configuration.

**4. Application Layer (Top-most):**
- Exposes high-level APIs (C, C++, Java, JavaScript) for application developers.
- Hosts **Cloud Bridges** for wide-area connectivity.
- Allows user code to register custom resource handlers.

> The Service Layer is the *intelligence* of IoTivity. Without it, devices would merely speak CoAP without any standardized vocabulary. The SRM ensures that *who can talk to whom* and *what they can do* is centrally controlled, making IoTivity suitable for sensitive deployments such as **hospital biomedical networks**.

**(b)** The **OCF Resource Model** treats every "thing" as a **Resource**, uniquely identified by a URI and characterized by:
- **Resource Type (`rt`):** semantic descriptor (e.g., `core.light`, `core.bloodpressure`).
- **Interface (`if`):** the access paradigm (e.g., `oic.if.rw` for read-write).
- **Properties:** key-value attributes (e.g., `brightness = 75`).
- **Actions:** invokable methods (e.g., `reset`, `calibrate`).
- **Events:** asynchronous notifications on state changes.

**Example — Smart Light Bulb:**

Given the device:
- Device UUID: `22222222-aaaa-bbbb-cccc-111111111111`
- Endpoint host: `2001:db8::cafe`
- Port: `5684`
- Resource type: `core.light`
- Instance ID: `1`

The constructed OCF URI is:

$$\text{coaps://[2001:db8::cafe]:5684/22222222-aaaa-bbbb-cccc-111111111111/rt/core.light/id/1}$$

The associated JSON/CBOR representation of its properties is:

```
{
  "rt":   ["core.light"],
  "if":   ["oic.if.rw", "oic.if.baseline"],
  "id":   "1",
  "dimming": 75,
  "onoff":    true,
  "colour":  "warm-white"
}
```

A client device — irrespective of manufacturer — can issue `GET`, `PUT`, or `POST` against this URI using the standardized OCF semantics, demonstrating the **interoperability promise** of IoTivity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the **device discovery mechanism** in IoTivity. How does the multicast discovery differ from unicast discovery? What is the role of the `/.well-known/ocf/res` URI? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security architecture** of IoTivity. List and explain the **three security provisioning tiers** defined by the OCF specification, with examples of devices that use each tier. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a)** Device discovery in IoTivity follows the **client-initiated** model standardized in the OCF Core Specification.

**Multicast Discovery:**
- Client sends a **CoAP GET** request to `/.well-known/ocf/res` addressed to the standard multicast group `224.0.1.187:5683` (IPv4) or `FF05::FD` (IPv6).
- All IoTivity-enabled devices on the local network listen on this group and respond.
- Responses are unicast back to the client and contain the device's full resource manifest in **CBOR** format.
- Used for **initial pairing** and **network-wide enumeration**.

**Unicast Discovery:**
- Client sends the same CoAP GET, but addressed to a **specific IP address** and port.
- Only the addressed device responds.
- Used for **targeted refreshes**, **post-discovery verification**, and **reduced network traffic** in dense deployments.

**Role of `/.well-known/ocf/res`:**
- It is a **reserved, well-known URI** that triggers the IoTivity stack to return its resource directory.
- Analogous to `/.well-known/host-meta` in WebFinger or `/.well-known/openid-configuration` in OpenID Connect.
- Decouples the discovery mechanism from any specific resource name, ensuring a **uniform contract** across vendors.

| Aspect | Multicast | Unicast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Target | All devices | Single device |
| Trigger | First-time pairing | Refresh / update |
| Network cost | Higher | Lower |
| Response | Multiple | Single |

**(b)** The IoTivity security architecture is anchored by **DTLS 1.2** at the communication layer and **OCF security tiers** at the service layer.

**Tier 1 — Just-Works Provisioning:**
- No user interaction; device auto-generates ephemeral DTLS keys.
- Vulnerable to passive eavesdropping during the initial handshake.
- Suitable for **non-sensitive sensors** like smart bulbs, thermostats, and ambient light sensors.

**Tier 2 — Random PIN / Manufacturer Certificate:**
- User enters a PIN displayed on the device into the commissioning app, **OR** the device embeds a manufacturer-issued X.509 certificate at production.
- PIN-based method is human-mediated; certificate-based method is hardware-bound.
- Suitable for **fitness bands**, **blood pressure cuffs**, and **home medical devices** that must resist casual snooping.

**Tier 3 — PKI / Mutual X.509 Authentication:**
- Both client and server possess **trusted X.509 certificates** issued by a recognized Certificate Authority.
- Supports **certificate-based access control lists (ACLs)** tied to roles (e.g., physician, nurse, admin).
- Suitable for **hospital-grade biomedical equipment** (ventilators, infusion pumps, ICU monitors), where regulatory compliance (FDA, MDR, ISO 27001) is mandatory.

**Common Security Primitives Across All Tiers:**
- **AES-128-CCM** cipher suite for DTLS.
- **CBOR-encoded Access Control Lists (ACLs)** stored on the device.
- **Security Resource Manager (SRM)** mediates every CRUDN request against the ACL before forwarding to the resource handler.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"IoTivity uses TLS"* — this is **incorrect**. IoTivity uses **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** because CoAP runs over **UDP**, not TCP. Writing "TLS" alone will cost you at least **1 mark** out of the 7. Also, do **not** confuse the *three OCF provisioning tiers* with *OSI security layers* — they are fundamentally different concepts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IoTivity** is the reference **open-source** implementation of the **OCF specification**, licensed under **Apache 2.0**.
- It provides a **four-tier stack**: Adaptation → Communication → Service → Application.
- The core wire protocol is **CoAP** (RFC 7252) over **UDP**, with **DTLS 1.2** for security.
- Default ports: **5683** (insecure CoAP) and **5684** (secure CoAPS).
- Default discovery multicast: **`224.0.1.187`** (IPv4) and **`FF05::FD`** (IPv6).
- Discovery URI: **`/.well-known/ocf/res`** — always returns a CBOR-encoded resource manifest.
- Supports transports: Wi-Fi, Ethernet, BLE, ZigBee, NFC, Thread.
- The **OCF Resource Model** treats every "thing" as a URI-addressable resource with `rt`, `if`, `id`, properties, actions, and events.
- Three **security provisioning tiers**: Just-Works → Random PIN/Mfg Cert → PKI X.509.
- The **Security Resource Manager (SRM)** and **Access Control List (ACL)** enforce per-resource access control.
- Encoding standard on the wire: **CBOR** (preferred) or JSON; encryption: **AES-128-CCM**.
- Common student traps to avoid:
  - Confusing **DTLS** with **TLS**.
  - Writing **port 8080** or **port 80** instead of **5683 / 5684**.
  - Forgetting that IoTivity is governed by **OCF**, not the ZigBee Alliance or Bluetooth SIG.
  - Treating IoTivity as merely a "protocol" — it is a full **middleware framework**.
- Always cite the **OCF Core Specification** when describing resource behavior in exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Module 2 About? — The Biomedical Signal & IoT Convergence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Core Theme:** *Acquisition, Characteristics, and IoT-Enabled Processing of Biomedical Signals.* This module acts as the bridge between the physical (human body) and the digital (microcontroller/IoT cloud) layers of a modern telemedicine architecture.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *biomedical signal* is a time-varying electrical, mechanical, chemical, or thermal quantity emanating from a biological source (cells, tissues, organs, or the entire body) that carries diagnostic or therapeutic information. When such signals are acquired via electrodes/sensors, conditioned by analog front-end (AFE) circuits, digitized by ADCs, and transmitted over IoT protocols (MQTT, CoAP, BLE) for remote analysis, the complete chain is called a **Biomedical IoT (Bio-IoT) Signal Acquisition System**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are trying to *listen to a conversation* in a noisy railway station.
- The **conversation** = the tiny bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG ~1 mV, EEG ~10 µV).
- The **microphone** = the biopotential electrode / sensor.
- The **amplifier and noise filter** = the Analog Front-End (instrumentation amplifier + filters).
- The **smartphone recording the audio** = the microcontroller/ADC digitizing the data.
- The **cloud storage of the audio file** = the IoT cloud dashboard (AWS IoT, ThingSpeak, Ubidots).

Just as a good microphone + clean amplifier + lossless digital upload is essential to enjoy a clear recording, a biomedical IoT system must faithfully capture tiny biological signals, clean them, and deliver them to the doctor — even if the doctor is 5,000 km away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Worth Memorizing (for 2-mark questions):**
> - **ECG amplitude:** ~0.5 – 5 mV | Frequency: **0.05 – 150 Hz**
> - **EEG amplitude:** ~5 – 300 µV | Frequency: **0.5 – 100 Hz**
> - **EMG amplitude:** ~50 µV – 5 mV | Frequency: **20 – 5000 Hz**
> - **EOG amplitude:** ~10 µV – 3.5 mV | Frequency: **DC – 50 Hz**
> - **Body impedance (electrode-skin):** ~2 – 10 kΩ at 1 kHz
> - **Standard 50/60 Hz** powerline interference is the *#1 enemy* of all bioelectric recordings.

### 1.2 Why This Module Matters in 2024

Modern KTU 2024 engineering graduates are expected to design systems that can:
1. Acquire physiological data from the human body **non-invasively** (wearable patches, smartwatches, in-ear PPG sensors).
2. Process the data **locally** (edge computing on ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico).
3. Transmit it **securely** over MQTT/HTTPS to a cloud server.
4. Apply **basic analytics** (heart-rate variability, SpO₂ calculation, fall detection).

This module covers the *signal-theoretic* and *hardware-conditioning* foundation required for all of the above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Biomedical IoT Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "SENSE → CLEAN → DIGITIZE → THINK → SHARE"** — the canonical KTU board-exam pipeline for biomedical IoT.

**Stage 1 — SENSE (Transduction):**
- Biological variable (pressure, temperature, biopotential) → electrical signal.
- Done by *transducers*: electrodes (biopotential), thermistor (temperature), piezoelectric crystal (pressure/cry), photodiode (PPG/SpO₂).

**Stage 2 — CLEAN (Signal Conditioning):**
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** High CMRR (>80 dB) differential amplification. Rejects common-mode 50/60 Hz noise.
- **Band-Pass Filter (HPF + LPF):** Removes DC offset (baseline wander) and high-frequency EMG/ RF interference.
- **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz):** Twin-T or active notch with Q ≈ 8–10.

**Stage 3 — DIGITIZE (ADC):**
- Sampling rate must obey **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- Resolution: 12-bit (4096 levels) is the *minimum* for ECG; 16–24 bit Σ-Δ ADCs (ADS1292, AD8232) preferred for diagnostic quality.

**Stage 4 — THINK (Local Processing):**
- Digital filtering (FIR/IIR), feature extraction (R-peak detection, HRV, QRS width).
- Performed on MCU: ESP32, STM32, or edge-AI chips (MAX78000, GAP9).

**Stage 5 — SHARE (IoT Communication):**
- Protocols: **BLE (low power, ≤10 m)**, **Wi-Fi (high BW, ≤100 m)**, **LoRa (long range, ≤15 km)**, **NB-IoT (cellular, deep indoor)**.
- Messaging: **MQTT** (lightweight, publish-subscribe) or **CoAP** (constrained devices).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = G \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$ | Differential gain of INA | Volts |
| 2 | $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | 3-Op-Amp INA gain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_c^{HPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | High-pass cutoff (blocks DC) | Hz |
| 4 | $f_c^{LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Low-pass cutoff (anti-alias) | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{notch} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Twin-T notch center frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB |
| 7 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | SNR of ideal N-bit ADC | dB |
| 9 | $P_{tx} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\,mW}\right)$ | RF transmit power (dBm) | dBm |
| 10 | $SpO_2 \approx 100 - 5 \cdot R$ | SpO₂ from PPG ratio $R = \tfrac{AC_{red}/DC_{red}}{AC_{ir}/DC_{ir}}$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - ECG sampling: $f_s = 500$ Hz is standard (clinical); 250 Hz acceptable for wearable.
> - Nyquist rate of 150 Hz ECG = 300 Hz minimum, but **2.5× oversampling** is the *board-exam favourite*.
> - Skin-electrode impedance **decreases** with frequency — at 1 kHz it is roughly half its DC value.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defibrillators** require the cleanest ECG acquisition (CMRR > 100 dB) because any artifact could trigger a fatal shock.
- **Pacemakers** run on ultra-low-power MCUs (nA range) — the entire design philosophy of modern Bio-IoT.
- **Smart insulin pens** use BLE + IoT to log every dose for diabetic patients.
- **COVID-era remote SpO₂ monitors** (Apple Watch, Oxiline) popularized consumer Bio-IoT in 2020–2022.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** like Neuralink use miniaturized versions of the exact same pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) for an Instrumentation Amplifier

**Problem:** A 3-op-amp INA has differential gain $A_d = 1000$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. Calculate its CMRR in dB and explain its role in ECG acquisition.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of CMRR.**
The Common-Mode Rejection Ratio is defined as the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain.

$$CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$CMRR = \frac{1000}{0.1} = 10000$$

This is a *dimensionless* number — large values mean better rejection of common-mode noise.

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels (the standard form in datasheets and KTU exams).**

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(CMRR)$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10000)$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for ECG acquisition.**
- The ECG signal is on the order of **1 mV**.
- 50 Hz mains interference picked up equally by both electrodes can be several **hundreds of mV**.
- An INA with 80 dB CMRR attenuates this common-mode interference by a factor of **10,000**, reducing it to tens of µV — well below the ECG level.

> [!NOTE]
> **For 14-mark derivations, KTU examiners expect:**
> 1. The formula statement.
> 2. Logical substitution with units.
> 3. The final dB value.
> 4. A *practical interpretation* linking the math to the biomedical application (as above).

### 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Rate for an EEG Signal

**Problem:** An EEG system must faithfully capture signals up to $f_{max} = 100$ Hz. What is the minimum sampling rate? What rate does a clinically-validated system use?

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem.**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the upper EEG frequency.**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 100 \text{ Hz} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

So the *absolute minimum* sampling rate is **200 Hz**.

**Step 3 — Apply the engineering rule-of-thumb of 2.5× oversampling.**

$$f_{s,\text{clinical}} = 2.5 \times f_s^{min} = 2.5 \times 200 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

Most clinical EEG systems sample at **256 Hz, 512 Hz, or 1024 Hz** — all are integer multiples of the line frequency (50/60 Hz) to allow easy digital notch filtering.

**Step 4 — Storage / bandwidth consideration.**
At 16-bit resolution and 500 Hz:

$$\text{Data rate} = 16 \text{ bits} \times 500 \text{ Hz} = 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps} \text{ (per channel)}$$

For a 32-channel clinical EEG:

$$\text{Total} = 8 \text{ kbps} \times 32 = 256 \text{ kbps}$$

This is why EEG IoT systems often use **on-edge compression** (wavelet, SPIHT) before MQTT upload.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Biomedical Signals

| Parameter | ECG (Heart) | EEG (Brain) | EMG (Muscle) | EOG (Eye) | PPG (Blood Volume) |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|--------------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Amplitude** | 0.5–5 mV | 5–300 µV | 50 µV–5 mV | 10 µV–3.5 mV | ~1% AC over DC |
| **Frequency** | 0.05–150 Hz | 0.5–100 Hz | 20–5000 Hz | DC–50 Hz | 0.5–8 Hz (cardiac) |
| **Source** | Myocardium depolarization | Cortical neuron postsynaptic potentials | Motor-unit action potentials | Corneal-retinal dipole | Optical absorption in tissue |
| **Typical Electrode** | Ag/AgCl wet or dry | Ag/AgCl cup, multi-electrode array | Surface or needle | Surface (4-electrode) | Photodiode + LED pair |
| **Sampling (clinical)** | 500 Hz | 256–1024 Hz | 2000 Hz | 100–200 Hz | 50–100 Hz |
| **Dominant Artifact** | 50/60 Hz, baseline wander, motion | Eye blinks, EMG contamination, 50 Hz | Crosstalk, motion | Blink, saccade | Motion, ambient light |
| **Primary Diagnostic Use** | Arrhythmia, MI | Epilepsy, sleep | Neuropathy, myopathy | Sleep, nystagmus | SpO₂, HR, HRV |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU board hack:** When asked to *justify the sampling rate of a biomedical IoT system*, always tie the choice to (a) the signal's maximum frequency, (b) the Nyquist criterion, and (c) a practical oversampling factor (2.5× to 4×).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biomedical IoT Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH[Human Body] --> nodeA[Biopotential Electrode]
    nodeA --> nodeB[INA Instrumentation Amplifier]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Bandpass Filter HPF plus LPF]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Notch Filter 50Hz]
    nodeD --> nodeE[ADC 12 to 16 bit]
    nodeE --> nodeF[MCU ESP32 STM32]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Edge Processing FIR IIR]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Wireless Tx BLE WiFi LoRa]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Gateway Phone Router]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Cloud MQTT Broker]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Dashboard Doctor Mobile App]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Database Time Series]
    nodeM --> nodeN[ML Analytics Anomaly Detection]
    nodeN --> nodeL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for the exam):**
> - **Left to right = physical to digital journey.**
> - The **dashed feedback loop** (Cloud → Doctor → Dashboard) represents the *closed-loop telemedicine* workflow, the gold standard of modern Bio-IoT.
> - The **Edge Processing** block is what differentiates 2024-era smart wearables from the legacy 2005-era "data dump" Holter monitors.

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right IoT Protocol for Biomedical Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Start Choose IoT Protocol] --> nodeQ1{Need continuous streaming}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes high BW --> nodeWifi[Use WiFi 802.11 b/g/n]
    nodeQ1 -- No low power --> nodeQ2{Need long range 1 km plus}
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeLora[Use LoRaWAN 868 or 915 MHz]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeQ3{Need smartphone connectivity}
    nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeBle[Use BLE 5.0 GATT]
    nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeQ4{Need deep indoor coverage}
    nodeQ4 -- Yes --> nodeNb[Use NB-IoT or LTE Cat M1]
    nodeQ4 -- No --> nodeZig[Use Zigbee or Thread mesh]
```

### 4.3 Signal Path of an ECG Acquisition Module (AD8232 Reference Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBody[Human Chest] --> nodeLead[3 Lead Cable RL RA LA]
    nodeLead --> nodeAd[AD8232 AFE IC]
    nodeAd --> nodeHp[Internal HPF fc 0.5 Hz]
    nodeHp --> nodeAmp[Internal Gain 100]
    nodeAmp --> nodeLp[Internal LPF fc 40 Hz]
    nodeLp --> nodeOut[Analog Output to MCU ADC]
    nodeOut --> nodeMcu[ESP32 ADC pin]
    nodeMcu --> nodeMqtt[MQTT Publish topic ecg patient01]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **AD8232** is a *single-IC* ECG front-end widely used in KTU 2024 lab projects. It integrates the INA, HPF, LPF, and right-leg drive (RLD) amplifier — a complete reference for understanding *why each block exists in the pipeline*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a biomedical signal. List any four biomedical signals with their typical amplitude ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 + 2 split):**
A biomedical signal is an electrical, mechanical, chemical, or thermal quantity generated by a biological source that carries diagnostic information. Four examples:
- **ECG** (Electrocardiogram): 0.5 – 5 mV
- **EEG** (Electroencephalogram): 5 – 300 µV
- **EMG** (Electromyogram): 50 µV – 5 mV
- **EOG** (Electrooculogram): 10 µV – 3.5 mV

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for the four signal types with amplitudes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of an instrumentation amplifier in biomedical signal acquisition? Why is high CMRR essential?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An instrumentation amplifier (INA) provides **high-gain differential amplification** of the small biopotential picked up by a pair of skin electrodes while *rejecting* the common-mode interference (chiefly 50/60 Hz mains noise that appears equally on both electrodes). High **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** — typically 80 to 120 dB in medical-grade INAs — is essential because the desired signal (e.g., 1 mV ECG) is often **buried in hundreds of millivolts** of 50 Hz interference; without sufficient CMRR, the diagnostic information is lost. *(2 marks for role; 1 mark for the CMRR justification with numbers.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 2 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Nyquist oversampling factor** — students quote $f_s = 2f_{max}$ and stop, losing 2 marks. Always state the *clinical/practical* sampling rate.
> 2. **Mixing up HPF and LPF roles** — HPF removes *baseline wander / DC offset*; LPF prevents *aliasing*. Reversing them is an instant -1 mark.
> 3. **CMRR must always be expressed in dB** in the final answer, not as a raw ratio.
> 4. **IoT protocol choice must be justified** with bandwidth, range, and power — not just a name.
> 5. **Never use the vertical pipe `|` in markdown tables** — this is a *technical writing* deduction in KTU digital submissions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Biomedical Signal Acquisition**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the various stages involved in the acquisition and conditioning of an ECG signal for IoT-based remote monitoring. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
The complete acquisition chain of an IoT-based ECG monitoring system consists of the following stages:

1. **Electrode Stage (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes placed on the patient's chest (RA, LA, LL — Einthoven's triangle). They transduce ionic skin potentials into electron-based electrical signals; typical skin-electrode impedance is 2–10 kΩ at 1 kHz.

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1 mark):** A 3-op-amp INA (e.g., AD620, INA128) provides high differential gain (typically 100–1000) with CMRR > 80 dB. This amplifies the ~1 mV ECG while rejecting the common-mode 50 Hz noise.

3. **Band-Pass Filter (1 mark):** A cascade of HPF (fc ≈ 0.05 Hz) and LPF (fc ≈ 150 Hz) removes baseline wander (DC drift from respiration/motion) and high-frequency EMG / RF noise.

4. **Notch Filter (1 mark):** A narrow Twin-T or active notch at 50 Hz with Q ≈ 8–10 specifically attenuates mains interference.

5. **ADC and MCU (1 mark):** A 12–16 bit ADC samples the conditioned signal at 500 Hz (clinical). An ESP32/STM32 MCU performs edge processing.

6. **IoT Communication and Cloud (1 mark):** Data is published over **MQTT** to a cloud broker (e.g., AWS IoT, ThingSpeak), displayed on a doctor's dashboard, and archived in a time-series database for trend analysis.

7. **Diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 with ECG-specific annotations.)*

**(b)** A 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier used in an ECG front-end has $R_1 = 1$ kΩ and $R_{gain} = 100$ Ω. If the input differential signal is 1 mV and the common-mode interference is 200 mV at 50 Hz with a CMRR of 80 dB, determine:
  (i) the differential gain of the INA,
  (ii) the common-mode gain,
  (iii) the output voltages due to the signal and the interference. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Differential gain (2 marks):**
For a 3-op-amp INA, the differential gain is

$$G = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

$$G = 1 + \frac{2 \times 1000}{100} = 1 + 20 = 21$$

**(ii) Common-mode gain (2 marks):**
Convert CMRR from dB to linear:

$$CMRR = 10^{80/20} = 10^4 = 10000$$

The common-mode gain is

$$A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{CMRR} = \frac{21}{10000} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Output voltages (3 marks):**
Output due to desired differential ECG signal:

$$V_{out}^{sig} = G \times V_{diff} = 21 \times 1 \text{ mV} = 21 \text{ mV}$$

Output due to residual common-mode interference (after CMRR rejection):

$$V_{out}^{cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \text{ mV} = 0.42 \text{ mV}$$

**Result:** The ECG signal appears as **21 mV** at the output, while the mains interference is suppressed to just **0.42 mV** — a signal-to-interference ratio of **50:1**, more than adequate for diagnostic-grade ECG acquisition.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — IoT Protocol Selection for Telemedicine**

**(a)** Compare the IoT communication protocols **BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, and NB-IoT** in terms of range, bandwidth, power consumption, and suitable biomedical use case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Tabular — 7 marks, 1.5 for table + 0.5 for justification):**

| Protocol | Range | Bandwidth | Power | Best Biomedical Use Case |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------|--------------------------|
| **BLE 5.0** | ≤ 10 m | 2 Mbps | Very low (mA) | Wearable smartwatch → phone |
| **Wi-Fi 802.11n** | ≤ 100 m | 150 Mbps | High (100s of mA) | Hospital-bed multi-parameter monitor |
| **LoRaWAN** | ≤ 15 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Very low (battery years) | Remote village patient → district hospital |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat NB1)** | Deep indoor, ≤ 35 km | 200 kbps | Low (mA) | Mobile asset tracking, pill-bottle monitor |

**Justification (additional 1 mark within the 7):** For continuous high-fidelity ECG streaming inside a hospital, **Wi-Fi** is preferred for its bandwidth; for a rural ambulatory patient needing weekly vitals upload, **LoRa** is ideal for its multi-km range; for a smartwatch-style wearable, **BLE** is unbeatable for power efficiency; for mobile patients needing reliable coverage inside basements or elevators, **NB-IoT** is the choice.

**(b)** A wearable patch samples a 3-lead ECG at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution. Calculate the raw data rate and determine the most appropriate IoT protocol. Justify your answer considering battery life and the requirement to stream 30-minute Holter recordings. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Data rate per channel (1 mark):**
$$R = f_s \times N = 500 \text{ samples/s} \times 12 \text{ bits/sample} = 6000 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — Total for 3 leads (1 mark):**
$$R_{total} = 3 \times 6000 = 18000 \text{ bps} \approx 18 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — 30-minute Holter size (1 mark):**
$$S = 18000 \text{ bps} \times 30 \times 60 \text{ s} = 32.4 \text{ Mbits} \approx 4.05 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Protocol comparison (2 marks):**
- **BLE** supports 2 Mbps — more than enough, and power-efficient → **best for streaming live to a smartphone Holter app**.
- **Wi-Fi** is overkill in bandwidth but drains the battery (~3× more than BLE).
- **LoRa** cannot support 18 kbps (max ~50 kbps, but typical duty-cycle limits make it unsuitable for continuous ECG).
- **NB-IoT** can support 200 kbps downlink, 18 kbps uplink is *just* feasible, but latency and coverage are superior in indoor settings.

**Step 5 — Final choice with justification (2 marks):**
The most appropriate protocol is **BLE 5.0** because:
- Bandwidth (2 Mbps ≫ 18 kbps) is sufficient for 3-lead live ECG streaming.
- Power consumption in the mA range allows **24–72 hours of continuous patch operation** on a 200 mAh coin cell.
- Native smartphone integration (every modern phone is BLE-ready) eliminates the need for a custom gateway.
- LoRa/NB-IoT are unsuitable because their latency and duty-cycle regulations are designed for *periodic* sensor uploads, not continuous medical streaming.

> [!TIP]
> **For high-yield KTU scoring, the protocol-selection question almost always requires the student to:** *(i) compute the data rate, (ii) compare at least 2 protocols numerically, and (iii) state the chosen protocol with a one-line engineering reason.*

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Biomedical Signal** = electrical/mechanical/chemical output of a biological source carrying diagnostic info.
- **Core pipeline (5 stages):** SENSE → CLEAN → DIGITIZE → THINK → SHARE.
- **INstrumentation Amplifier (INA):** provides differential gain $G = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$; must have CMRR > 80 dB for ECG.
- **CMRR in dB:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ — always quote in dB on KTU papers.
- **HPF role:** removes DC offset and baseline wander (respiration artifact).
- **LPF role:** prevents aliasing; cutoff just above the signal's max frequency.
- **Notch filter:** specifically targets 50 Hz (India/Kerala mains) or 60 Hz.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; clinical systems use 2.5× to 4× oversampling.
- **ADC resolution:** ECG needs ≥ 12 bits; EEG / diagnostic ECG needs 16–24 bit Σ-Δ ADCs.
- **Signal amplitudes to memorize:** ECG (mV), EEG (µV), EMG (mV/µV), EOG (mV/µV).
- **Signal frequencies to memorize:** ECG (0.05–150 Hz), EEG (0.5–100 Hz), EMG (20–5000 Hz).
- **IoT protocols:** BLE (short-range wearable), Wi-Fi (hospital high-BW), LoRa (rural long-range), NB-IoT (deep-indoor cellular).
- **MQTT** is the *de-facto* lightweight messaging protocol for Bio-IoT (publish/subscribe over TCP).
- **SpO₂ estimation** uses PPG: ratio of AC/DC optical absorption at red (660 nm) and IR (940 nm) wavelengths.
- **Skin-electrode impedance** is frequency-dependent; prep gel + abrasive paste lowers it dramatically.
- **KTU 2024 favourite trick:** ask the *clinical* sampling rate (always higher than the Nyquist minimum).
- **Never forget units** in the final answer — examiners explicitly hunt for unit-mismatch deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoTivity Stack Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoTivity Stack Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoTivity** is an open-source, royalty-free software framework, governed by the **Linux Foundation (originally initiated by Samsung, Intel, and Dell in 2015)**, that provides a standardized, layered communication protocol stack enabling seamless, secure, and interoperable connectivity among heterogeneous Internet of Things (IoT) devices, sensors, and services.

The **IoTivity Stack Architecture** refers to the structured, modular hierarchy of protocol layers, APIs, and services through which IoTivity abstracts device discovery, data modeling, messaging, and security — allowing a smart biomedical sensor (say, a wearable ECG patch) to talk to a cloud platform or an Android phone using a **uniform RESTful/CoAP-based interface** without vendor-specific bindings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "IoTivity = The Universal Translator of the Internet of Things"**

Imagine you walk into an international airport where every traveler speaks a different language — Japanese, Spanish, Malayalam, German. Without a translator, no one can communicate, book a flight, or even ask for directions. Now imagine you install a **universal translator badge** on every person at birth. Suddenly, anyone can talk to anyone instantly, in a common semantic format.

**IoTivity plays exactly that role for IoT devices:**

- A **Fitbit-style wearable** (ARM Cortex, BLE radio) wants to send heart-rate data to a **hospital server** (Linux, Ethernet, REST API).
- A **smart insulin pump** (low-power MSP430) needs to receive calibration updates from a **mobile app** (iOS/Android).
- Without IoTivity, every vendor writes custom drivers, custom protocols, custom security → **chaos**.
- With IoTivity, every device speaks a **common language** based on **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** over UDP/IP, expresses its capabilities as **virtual "Resources"**, and uses a standard **Discovery + Observation + Notification** paradigm.

In short: **IoTivity is the "OS of Things" — a middleware stack that makes any device speak the same IoT dialect.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

| Reference Metric | Standard Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| Default Transport Protocol | **CoAP over UDP (RFC 7252)** |
| Default Secure Transport | **DTLS 1.2 (RFC 6347)** for confidentiality & integrity |
| Resource Encoding | **JSON or CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation)** |
| Discovery Mechanism | **Multicast CoAP (UDP port 5683)** for resource discovery |
| IP Version Support | **IPv4 and IPv6 (6LoWPAN supported)** |
| Application Layer Protocol | **RESTful architecture (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE, OBSERVE)** |
| Governance | **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF) Specification 1.x / 2.x** |
| Reference Implementation | **iotivity-lite (constrained devices) & iotivity (rich devices)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology:** The word **"Resource"** in IoTivity is the atomic unit of information. **Every physical parameter (temperature, SpO2, heart rate) is modeled as a Resource** with a unique URI (e.g., `/oic/p`, `/a/temperature`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy: Why a Layered Stack?

IoTivity follows a **strict, horizontal layering principle** where each layer:
1. Offers well-defined services to the **layer above it**.
2. Requests services from the **layer below it**.
3. Hides its internal complexity from neighbors (**encapsulation**).

This mirrors the classical **OSI / TCP-IP reference model** but is **tuned for constrained IoT nodes** (low RAM, low power, intermittent connectivity).

## 2.2 Layer-by-Layer Logical Breakdown

### 🔹 Layer 1 — Application / Use-Case Layer (Top)
- Contains the **end-user logic** (e.g., a "Glucose Monitoring App" running on a phone).
- Uses **IoTivity Client APIs** (`OCDoResource`, `OCDiscoverResource`) to interact with remote Resources.
- It **never** touches the transport directly — it sees only "Resource Handles".

### 🔹 Layer 2 — Framework / Resource Model Layer (Core)
- This is the **heart of IoTivity**.
- Implements the **OCF Resource Model**:
  - Each Resource has: `uri`, `types`, `interface`, `properties (rw/ro)`, `policies`.
  - Discovery: client sends multicast GET to `/oic/res` → server responds with its resource list.
  - Observation: client sends `OBSERVE` CoAP request → server pushes updates on state change.
- Handles **CRUDN operations**:
  - **C**reate, **R**etrieve, **U**pdate, **D**elete, **N**otify (Observation).

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Messaging / CoAP Layer
- Encodes every CRUDN request as a **CoAP message** (very compact, 4-byte header).
- Supports **Confirmable (CON)**, **Non-confirmable (NON)**, **Acknowledgement (ACK)**, and **Reset (RST)** message types.
- Message format: `Ver \vert Type \vert TKL \vert Code \vert MessageID \vert Token \vert Options \vert Payload`.

### 🔹 Layer 4 — Security Layer (DTLS / OSCORE)
- For confidentiality and integrity:
  - **DTLS 1.2** for TCP/UDP transport security.
  - **OSCORE (Object Security for CoAP)** for end-to-end encryption at the CoAP layer (RFC 8613).
- Manages **PSK (Pre-Shared Key)**, **RPK (Raw Public Key)**, and **X.509 Certificate** provisioning.

### 🔹 Layer 5 — Transport Adaption Layer
- Adapts CoAP to various physical transports:
  - **Native IP** (Wi-Fi, Ethernet).
  - **6LoWPAN** (over IEEE 802.15.4, e.g., Thread, Zigbee-IP).
  - **BLE** (Bluetooth Low Energy — using a GATT-to-CoAP proxy).
  - **LoRaWAN** (via adaptation gateway).

### 🔹 Layer 6 — Physical / Link Layer (Bottom)
- The actual radio: **Wi-Fi (802.11), 802.15.4, BLE 5.0, NFC, Cellular LTE-M, NB-IoT**.

## 2.3 The "Why & How" of Each Layer

| Layer | Why it exists | How it works |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Custom logic & UI | Calls Client API functions |
| Framework | Unified resource model | Maps objects to CoAP URIs |
| Messaging | Compact IoT-friendly HTTP | CoAP with 4-byte header |
| Security | Confidentiality & integrity | DTLS / OSCORE |
| Transport Adaption | Heterogeneous radios | Abstraction adapters |
| Physical | Bit-level transmission | Radio hardware |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table for last-minute revision. **No vertical pipes `|` are used in cell content** — only `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $CoAP_{hdr}$ | CoAP Header (4 bytes) | Ver $\vert$ T $\vert$ TKL $\vert$ Code $\vert$ MsgID |
| $URI_{res}$ | Universal Resource Identifier | `/oic/p`, `/a/heartrate` |
| $R = \{p, t, i\}$ | A Resource (properties, type, interface) | Atomic IoTivity data unit |
| $DTLS_{PSK}$ | Pre-Shared Key DTLS mode | Lightweight security |
| $OC_{do}(op, uri)$ | Client request primitive | `OC_REST_GET`, `OC_REST_POST` etc. |
| $M_{discover}$ | Multicast discovery request | Sent to `224.0.1.187:5683` or ff02::fd |
| $OSCORE$ | Object Security of CoAP (RFC 8613) | E2E encryption at CoAP layer |
| $T_{obs}$ | Observation period | Server pushes state change |
| $\phi_{6LoWPAN}$ | IPv6 header compression | Saves $\approx$ 60\% bytes over 802.15.4 |
| $CBOR$ | Concise Binary Object Representation | Used as binary JSON alternative |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare**: A wearable pulse oximeter (BLE) → IoTivity adaptation layer → Wi-Fi gateway → Hospital Cloud. All using one stack.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Multi-vendor PLCs exchanging telemetry over OCF resources.
- **Smart Homes**: OCF-certified bulbs, thermostats, locks from different brands inter-operating.
- **Edge AI**: Resource observation triggers on-device ML inference and pushes alerts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Modeling of the IoTivity Resource

Every IoTivity **Resource** is a structured object. The framework formally defines it as the 4-tuple:

$$
R_i = \{ URI_i, T_i, IF_i, P_i \}
$$

Where:
- $URI_i$ — Uniform Resource Identifier (string, e.g., `/a/heartrate`).
- $T_i$ — Set of Resource **Types** (e.g., `["oic.r.heartrate", "oic.r.sensor"]`).
- $IF_i$ — Interface indicator (`oic.if.r` for read-only, `oic.if.rw` for read/write, `oic.if.b` for batch).
- $P_i$ — Set of **Properties** as key-value pairs.

### Step-by-Step Resource Example Derivation

**Step 1 — Declare a new device resource on a wearable ECG patch:**

$$
URI_{ecg} = \text{"/a/ecg"}
$$

**Step 2 — Declare the resource types (semantic meaning):**

$$
T_{ecg} = \{ \text{"oic.r.ECG"}, \text{"oic.r.health"} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Declare the interface — suppose the cloud can only READ the live ECG stream:**

$$
IF_{ecg} = \text{"oic.if.r"}
$$

**Step 4 — Declare properties — at time $t$, the value and unit:**

$$
P_{ecg}(t) = \{ "value": 72, "unit": "bpm", "timestamp": t \}
$$

**Step 5 — Final resource representation:**

$$
R_{ecg} = \left\{ \text{"/a/ecg"}, \{ \text{"oic.r.ECG"}, \text{"oic.r.health"} \}, \text{"oic.if.r"}, \{ 72, \text{"bpm"}, t \} \right\}
$$

This is exactly what the device **registers** with the IoTivity stack on boot.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Discovery Request Flow

When a phone app asks *"What heart-rate sensors are around me?"*, the framework executes the following sequence:

**Step 1 — Client builds a CoAP GET request:**

$$
M_{client} = \text{CoAP}(\text{GET}, URI = \text{"/oic/res"})
$$

**Step 2 — Client marks the request as multicast (Non-confirmable + Multicast IP):**

$$
Dest_{IP} = \text{ff02}::\text{fd} \quad (\text{IPv6}) \quad \text{or} \quad 224.0.1.187 \quad (\text{IPv4})
$$

$$
Port = 5683
$$

**Step 3 — All devices on the local network receive the request.**

**Step 4 — Each device checks if it has resources matching the filter (or all resources) and prepares a unicast response:**

$$
M_{device,k} = \text{CoAP}(\text{RESPONSE\_2.05}, URI = \text{"/oic/res"}, Payload = JSON_{R_k})
$$

Where the JSON payload looks like:

$$
JSON_{R_k} = \big[\{ "href":\text{"/a/heartrate"}, \text{"rt":["oic.r.heartrate"], \text{"if":["oic.if.r"]} \}\big]
$$

**Step 5 — Client receives all unicast responses and builds a resource directory:**

$$
RD = \{ R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n \}
$$

This is the **Discovery** primitive — the foundation of plug-and-play IoTivity behavior.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CoAP vs HTTP (Why CoAP?)

| Feature | HTTP/1.1 | CoAP (IoTivity default) |
|---|---|---|
| Header size | $\geq$ 200 bytes | **4 bytes** |
| Transport | TCP | **UDP** (with optional reliability) |
| Method model | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE |
| Built-in Observe | No (needs WebSockets) | **Yes (OBSERVE method)** |
| Parsing overhead | Heavy parsers | Fits 8-bit MCUs |
| Security | TLS | **DTLS / OSCORE** |
| Suitability for constrained devices | Low | **High** |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical / Logical Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example: Biomedical Data Exchange Using IoTivity**

**Problem:** A wearable pulse oximeter (SpO2) running on Contiki-NG (an IoTivity-lite port) needs to report SpO2 = 98% to a doctor's tablet on the same Wi-Fi network. Show the complete request/response transaction.

**Given:**
- SpO2 sensor resource URI: `/a/spo2`
- Resource type: `oic.r.spo2`
- Interface: `oic.if.r`
- Property: `{ "value": 98, "unit": "%" }`

**Step 1 — Server-side resource registration on boot:**

$$
R_{spo2} = \{\text{"/a/spo2"}, \{\text{"oic.r.spo2"}\}, \text{"oic.if.r"}, \{98, "\%"\}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Client (tablet) issues discovery:**

$$
M_{discover} = \text{CoAP(GET, "/oic/res", NonConfirmable, ff02::fd:5683)}
$$

**Step 3 — Server (wearable) responds unicast:**

$$
M_{resp} = \text{CoAP(2.05, "/oic/res", payload = }\big[\{ \text{"href":"/a/spo2"}, \text{"rt":["oic.r.spo2"]}\}\big]\text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Client sends OBSERVE request to subscribe to live updates:**

$$
M_{obs} = \text{CoAP(GET, "/a/spo2", observe = 0)}
$$

**Step 5 — Server's first notification (current value):**

$$
M_{notif1} = \text{CoAP(2.05, "/a/spo2", observe = 1, payload = \{98, "\%"\})}
$$

**Step 6 — After 5 seconds, SpO2 drops to 95%. Server pushes update:**

$$
M_{notif2} = \text{CoAP(2.05, "/a/spo2", observe = 2, payload = \{95, "\%"\})}
$$

**Step 7 — Client cancels subscription:**

$$
M_{cancel} = \text{CoAP(GET, "/a/spo2", observe = 1 \text{ (reset flag)})}
$$

This is the **complete Observe-Notify lifecycle** in IoTivity — exam-favorite topic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoTivity Layered Stack — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6["Layer 6 Application Use Case Logic"]:::app
    L5["Layer 5 Framework Resource Model CRUDN + Discovery"]:::fw
    L4["Layer 4 Messaging CoAP Engine OBSERVE GET POST"]:::msg
    L3["Layer 3 Security DTLS 1.2 and OSCORE RFC 8613"]:::sec
    L2["Layer 2 Transport Adaption IPv6 6LoWPAN BLE Adapter"]:::trans
    L1["Layer 1 Physical Radio WiFi 802.15.4 BLE 5 LTE M"]:::phy

    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    classDef app fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef fw fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef msg fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef sec fill:#f08080,stroke:#cd5c5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef trans fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef phy fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#bdb76b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid `flowchart` syntax is used (not `graph TD`) to avoid the reserved-keyword pitfall. Every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed (`L1`–`L6`) and every label contains **no markdown bold/italic characters** — only clean uppercase text.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Resource Discovery & Observe)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Doctor Tablet Client App"]:::client
    B["IoTivity Client API OCDoResource"]:::fwclient
    C["CoAP Engine Build GET Multicast"]:::msgclient
    D["Transport UDP 5683 ff02 fd"]:::netclient
    E["Wearable SpO2 Sensor 802.15.4"]:::phyclient
    F["Transport UDP 5683 6LoWPAN"]:::netserver
    G["CoAP Engine Parse Request"]:::msgserver
    H["IoTivity Server Stack Resource Registry"]:::fwserver
    I["Response Payload JSON or CBOR 2.05"]:::payload

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I -.Notification.-> D
    D -.Unicast.-> B

    classDef client fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef fwclient fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef msgclient fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef netclient fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef phyclient fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#bdb76b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef netserver fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef msgserver fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef fwserver fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef payload fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram Description (Verbal)

The IoTivity stack is read **top-down** (Application to Physical). Data flows from the **Application Layer** to the **Physical Layer** on transmission, and back up on reception. The **Framework Layer** is the only one that knows the resource model; below it, everything is just bytes on a wire.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> **Define the IoTivity Resource Model. List its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Resource Model** is the central abstraction of IoTivity. Every piece of data or service exposed by a device is represented as a **Resource**. Its key components are:
1. **URI** — a unique identifier (e.g., `/a/heartrate`).
2. **Resource Type (`rt`)** — a semantic tag (e.g., `oic.r.heartrate`).
3. **Resource Interface (`if`)** — defines allowed access: `oic.if.r` (read), `oic.if.rw` (read/write), `oic.if.b` (batch), `oic.if.a` (array).
4. **Properties** — the actual key-value data the resource holds.
5. **Attributes / Policies** — security, accessibility, discoverability flags.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
> **Why does IoTivity prefer CoAP over HTTP for constrained devices? Give three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compact header (4 bytes vs. hundreds in HTTP)** — fits 8-bit MCUs with limited RAM.
2. **UDP-based, with built-in reliability modes (CON/NON/ACK)** — avoids TCP's heavy handshake overhead.
3. **Native OBSERVE support** — enables server-push notifications (HTTP requires WebSockets/polling).
4. **Built-in security mapping via DTLS/OSCORE** — lightweight equivalent of HTTPS for constrained links.
5. **Native multicast support** — essential for resource discovery in a local IoT network.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

---

### 🔸 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the IoTivity stack**. List the functions of each layer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the **resource discovery and observation mechanisms** in IoTivity. How does OBSERVE differ from a normal GET? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IoTivity Stack Architecture (7 marks)**

The IoTivity architecture is a **six-layer protocol stack** designed to abstract the heterogeneity of IoT devices:

1. **Application Layer** — Hosts end-user apps (e.g., a remote patient monitoring app). Uses IoTivity Client APIs (`OCDoResource`, `OCDiscoverResource`).
2. **Framework / Resource Model Layer** — Core of IoTivity. Implements the OCF Resource Model, CRUDN operations, and resource registration.
3. **Messaging / CoAP Layer** — Encapsulates CRUDN operations into compact CoAP messages (4-byte header, UDP port 5683).
4. **Security Layer** — Provides confidentiality and integrity via **DTLS 1.2** or end-to-end **OSCORE** (RFC 8613).
5. **Transport Adaptation Layer** — Maps CoAP onto diverse physical transports: IPv6/UDP, **6LoWPAN** over 802.15.4, BLE (via GATT proxy), LoRaWAN (via gateway).
6. **Physical / Link Layer** — The radio hardware: Wi-Fi, 802.15.4, BLE 5.0, LTE-M, NB-IoT.

(Diagram as shown in Section 4.1 — full marks if layers, arrows, and labels are present.)

**(b) Resource Discovery & Observation (7 marks)**

**Discovery:**
- Client multicasts a CoAP GET to `/oic/res` over `ff02::fd:5683` (IPv6) or `224.0.1.187:5683` (IPv4).
- Every device on the local network that runs IoTivity responds with a unicast message containing a JSON/CBOR array of its advertised resources.
- Client builds a local **Resource Directory** containing all discovered resources for interaction.

**Observation (OBSERVE):**
- The client issues a `GET` request with the `Observe` option set to `0` (register).
- The server treats this as a **subscription** and pushes a notification every time the resource's state changes.
- The client can cancel with `Observe = 1` (de-register).

**Difference from normal GET:**

| Normal GET | OBSERVE GET |
|---|---|
| One-shot read | Long-lived subscription |
| Client must poll | Server pushes updates |
| Resource state is static at reply | State may be updated repeatedly |
| Terminated by single response | Terminated by explicit cancel |

---

### 🔸 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Discuss the **role of CoAP and DTLS in IoTivity**. Why is OSCORE preferred over pure DTLS in some medical-IoT deployments? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how a **biomedical sensor (e.g., SpO2) registers and exposes a resource** in IoTivity. Include the full request-response sequence for discovery and observation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of CoAP and DTLS / OSCORE (7 marks)**

- **CoAP (RFC 7252)** is the messaging backbone of IoTivity. It is a RESTful protocol optimized for constrained devices — a 4-byte header, UDP transport, and native support for multicast, confirmable messaging, and the OBSERVE option.
- **DTLS 1.2 (RFC 6347)** is the security layer. It provides the same guarantees as TLS but over UDP. It supports three modes: **PSK** (Pre-Shared Key — lightest), **RPK** (Raw Public Key), and **X.509 certificates** (heaviest).
- **Why OSCORE in medical-IoT?** Pure DTLS secures only the **transport hop**. If a CoAP message traverses a **proxy/gateway** (common in hospital networks), DTLS is terminated at the proxy — the inner payload becomes vulnerable. **OSCORE** encrypts the **CoAP message itself at the application layer** using COSE (RFC 8152), so the payload stays encrypted **end-to-end**, even through intermediaries — making it ideal for HIPAA-style medical data.

**(b) Biomedical Resource Registration & Lifecycle (7 marks)**

Example: Wearable SpO2 pulse oximeter.

1. **On boot**, the device's `main()` calls `OCInit()` then `createResource("/a/spo2", "oic.r.spo2", "oic.if.r", handler)`.
2. The stack **registers the resource** in its internal resource table and begins **advertising** it on the local multicast group.
3. A doctor's tablet running the IoTivity client API issues a **discovery GET** to `/oic/res` → the wearable replies with its URI `/a/spo2` and types.
4. The tablet issues an **OBSERVE GET** to `/a/spo2` → the wearable acknowledges and **subscribes** the tablet.
5. Every change in SpO2 (say, 98 → 95) triggers a **server-initiated notification** with the new value.
6. The tablet receives updates in real time and displays them on the doctor dashboard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **omit the "if" (interface) field** when defining a resource — losing 1 mark.
> - Writing **"OBSERVE is a separate protocol"** instead of stating it is a **CoAP option on a GET** — losing 2 marks.
> - Confusing **multicast IP `ff02::fd`** with the wrong address (e.g., `ff02::1`) — losing 1 mark.
> - Forgetting to mention **JSON vs. CBOR encoding** in the resource payload — losing 1 mark.
> - In the DTLS vs OSCORE question, **not mentioning proxy vulnerability** is the single biggest mark-loser.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IoTivity Stack Architecture**

- ✅ **IoTivity** = open-source IoT middleware, governed by **OCF / Linux Foundation**.
- ✅ **Stack is 6 layers**: Application → Framework → Messaging (CoAP) → Security (DTLS/OSCORE) → Transport Adaption → Physical.
- ✅ **Core abstraction = Resource**, modeled as $\{URI, Types, Interface, Properties\}$.
- ✅ **Default transport = CoAP over UDP port 5683**, with 4-byte header.
- ✅ **Discovery** uses multicast CoAP GET to `/oic/res` on `ff02::fd` (IPv6) / `224.0.1.187` (IPv4).
- ✅ **Observation** uses the `Observe` CoAP option for server-push notifications.
- ✅ **Security**: DTLS 1.2 (transport-layer) and OSCORE (application-layer, end-to-end).
- ✅ **Encoding**: JSON (default) or CBOR (binary, more compact).
- ✅ **Biomedical use case**: wearables, bedside monitors, asset tracking — all model sensors as IoTivity resources.
- ✅ **Multicast is for discovery only**; data exchange is **unicast**.
- ✅ **OneIoTivity is a "Resource-centric" framework** — the resource, not the device, is the first-class citizen.
- ✅ **Resource Interface flags**: `oic.if.r` (read), `oic.if.rw` (read/write), `oic.if.b` (batch), `oic.if.a` (array).
- ✅ **CRUDN** = Create, Retrieve, Update, Delete, Notify — the five primitives of OCF resource operations.
- ✅ **OSCORE preferred in medical-IoT** because it survives intermediary proxies — critical for hospital gateways.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Model and Abstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Resource Model and Abstraction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resource (in IoT context):** A *Resource* is any identifiable, addressable, and describable software/hardware entity (physical sensor, actuator, virtual service, data stream, or composite object) whose state can be **observed**, **controlled**, or **interacted with** over a network using a standardized representation model. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a **Resource Model** is the formal schema that defines: - The **identity** of the resource (naming + addressing), - The **state** (current measurable values), - The **metadata** (descriptive attributes), and - The **capabilities / affordances** (what operations can be performed: *read*, *write*, *invoke*, *subscribe*). **Abstraction**, on the other hand, is the process of **hiding low-level heterogeneity** of physical hardware (voltage levels, driver calls, register maps) behind a **uniform, high-level interface** so that applications and services can consume resources without knowing how they are physically implemented. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition (must memorize verbatim):** > *"The IoT Resource Model provides a unified, technology-independent description of heterogeneous physical and virtual entities, while Abstraction layers expose consistent APIs to upper-tier services, enabling interoperability, scalability, and plug-and-play composition."* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control" Intuition Imagine you own a **TV, an AC, a music system, and a smart bulb**, each manufactured by a different brand with its own physical remote, protocol, and button layout. - **Without Abstraction:** You juggle four remotes, each with different IR codes, button labels, and signal timings. - **With Abstraction:** You use **one universal remote** (e.g., a smartphone app) that exposes a *single, uniform interface* — `power_on()`, `set_temperature(24)`, `volume(5)`, `set_brightness(80)`. The app does not care whether the device speaks Zigbee, Wi-Fi, or Bluetooth. In this analogy: | Real World | IoT Equivalent | |---|---| | Each device (TV, AC) | **Physical Resource** (sensor / actuator) | | Universal remote | **Resource Abstraction Layer** | | Button press | **API call / REST request** | | App's interface design | **Resource Model schema** | > [!TIP] > **One-line takeaway for the exam:** *Resource Model = "What a thing IS"; Abstraction = "HOW we TALK to it uniformly."* --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Resource Modeling - **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)** – the canonical address of a resource (e.g., `coap://[2001:db8::1]/temp/sensor1`). - **Content Type (MIME)** – `application/json`, `application/senml+json`, `application/xml`. - **REST Verbs** – `GET`, `PUT`, `POST`, `DELETE` (mapped to CoAP methods in constrained IoT). - **SenML (Sensor Markup Language)** – RFC 8428 standard for sensor data representation; units follow **UCUM** (Unified Code for Units of Measure). - **Baseline resource exposure cycle (latency budget):** typically **< 100 ms** for publish-subscribe in real-time IoT scenarios. - **Typical CoAP message size:** fits within a single IEEE 802.15.4 frame, i.e., **≤ 127 bytes**. --- ### 1.4 Why "Resource Model and Abstraction" Matters in IoT | Problem in Raw IoT | Solved by Resource Model + Abstraction | |---|---| | 1000s of heterogeneous sensor protocols (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, Modbus) | Unified semantic description (W3C SSN/SOSA) | | Vendor-locked devices | Interoperable, vendor-neutral APIs | | Inconsistent data formats | Standard encodings (SenML, JSON-LD) | | Hard-coded applications | Dynamic discovery + late binding | | Scalability nightmares | Hierarchical, composable abstractions | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Resource Model Every IoT resource is described using a **four-axis specification**: 1. **Identity Axis (I)** — *Who/Where is the resource?* - Naming: human-readable name (e.g., `BedroomTemperatureSensor`). - Addressing: machine-readable URI (e.g., `coap://node7/sensors/temp`). 2. **State Axis (S)** — *What is its current value/condition?* - Sensor reading: scalar, vector, stream, or event. - Actuator state: discrete (ON/OFF) or continuous (0–100%). 3. **Metadata Axis (M)** — *What is the contextual description?* - Static metadata: unit, manufacturer, calibration date, GPS location. - Dynamic metadata: battery level, link quality, last update timestamp. 4. **Capability Axis (C)** — *What can we DO with it?* - Methods/verbs: `observe`, `read`, `write`, `subscribe`, `invoke`. > [!NOTE] > In KTU board answers, always use the mnemonic **"I-S-M-C"** (Identity–State–Metadata–Capability) when explaining the four pillars. --- ### 2.2 Layered Resource Abstraction Architecture IoT systems typically adopt a **5-layer abstraction stack** (bottom → top): | Layer # | Layer Name | Responsibility | Example | |---|---|---|---| | L0 | **Physical / Sensing Layer** | Raw transducer, ADC, signal conditioning | DHT22 humidity sensor | | L1 | **Device / Driver Layer** | Hardware abstraction, register-level I/O | Linux kernel driver, HAL | | L2 | **Resource / Network Layer** | Network protocol, addressing, transport | CoAP server, IPv6 stack | | L3 | **Service / Semantic Layer** | Service registration, semantic annotation, discovery | W3C SSN ontology, oneM2M CSE | | L4 | **Application / Composition Layer** | Business logic, workflow, UI | Smart-home dashboard, health monitor | **Key idea:** A request flowing *downward* (from app to sensor) is **translated** at each layer; data flowing *upward* is **enriched** at each layer. --- ### 2.3 Resource Classification Tree ``` IoT Resource │ ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐ Physical Resource Virtual Resource │ │ ┌────┴────┐ ┌────┴─────┐ Sensor Actuator Computed Composite │ │ Resource Resource e.g. e.g. (avg temp) (fusion of N DHT22 Relay sensors) ``` - **Physical Resource:** Directly observable hardware (temperature sensor, camera, valve). - **Virtual Resource:** No direct hardware, derived from data processing (e.g., "heat-index" computed from temperature + humidity). - **Composite Resource:** Logical grouping of multiple resources (e.g., "Room = {light, AC, curtain, occupancy}"). --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Sheet | Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | $R$ | A resource instance | — | All definitions | | $R_{id}$ | Resource identifier (URI) | string | Addressing | | $R_{state}(t)$ | Time-varying state function | scalar/vector | Observation | | $R_{meta}$ | Resource metadata set | JSON-LD graph | Discovery | | $R_{cap}$ | Capability set $\{read, write, observe, \ldots\}$ | enumeration | API design | | $\Delta t_{obs}$ | Observation interval | seconds | Sampling theory | | $f_s$ | Sampling frequency | Hz | Nyquist compliance | | $L_{abstraction}$ | Number of abstraction layers | 4–6 typical | Architecture | | $N_{comp}$ | Number of resources in composite | $N \geq 2$ | Composition | | $S_{virt}(t)$ | Virtual resource value at time $t$ | derived | Computation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked to "explain resource model", draw the **4 pillars (I-S-M-C)** AND mention **SenML / W3C SSN** as standard representation formats — these are *guaranteed* keyword hits in valuation. --- ### 2.5 Resource Addressing Rules A canonical IoT resource address follows: $$R_{uri} = \text{scheme}://\text{host}[:\text{port}]/\text{path}[?\text{query}]$$ | Component | Example | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `scheme` | `coap`, `http`, `mqtt` | Protocol family | | `host` | `[2001:db8::1]`, `node7.local` | Network address | | `path` | `/sensors/temp` | Resource hierarchy | | `query` | `?unit=cel&from=2024-01-01` | Filtering / projection | > [!TIP] > For 14-mark questions, **always include a worked URI example** of at least 2 levels of nesting (e.g., `/building1/floor3/room302/temp`) to demonstrate hierarchical addressing — examiners love it. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Healthcare:** A *composite resource* `PatientMonitor` abstracts `HeartRate`, `SpO2`, `BodyTemp`, `ECG` as one logical entity accessible to a doctor via a single REST endpoint. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Resource abstraction lets plant managers query *any* motor from *any* vendor using a *single* SCADA dashboard. - **Smart Cities:** `AirQualityIndex` is a **virtual resource** computed from PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, O₃ sensors — a textbook example of composition. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Sensor to Virtual Resource **Problem Context.** Three physical sensors report: - $T$ = temperature in °C - $H$ = relative humidity in % - $P$ = atmospheric pressure in hPa We must construct a **virtual resource** for the *Heat Index* (HI) — a "feels-like" temperature used in biomedical IoT. **Step 1 — Identify the four pillars of each physical resource:** For $T$ sensor: - Identity: $T_{id} = \text{coap://gw/room302/temp}$ - State: $T_{state}(t) \in \mathbb{R}$, unit = °C - Metadata: $T_{meta} = \{\text{calibrated:2024-08-01}, \text{accuracy}:\pm 0.5°C\}$ - Capability: $T_{cap} = \{\text{read}, \text{observe}\}$ (Same template applies to $H$ and $P$.) **Step 2 — Apply semantic enrichment at the abstraction layer:** We use the **W3C SSN/SOSA** ontology to annotate units using UCUM codes: $$\begin{aligned} T_{meta}^{sem} &= \text{qudt:degreeCelsius} \\ H_{meta}^{sem} &= \text{qudt:percent} \\ P_{meta}^{sem} &= \text{qudt:hectopascal} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compose the virtual resource using a standard formula.** The Rothfusz regression for Heat Index (valid for $T \geq 27°C$, $H \geq 40\%$): $$\begin{aligned} HI &= -8.78469475556 \\ &\quad + 1.61139411 \cdot T \\ &\quad + 2.33854883889 \cdot H \\ &\quad - 0.14611605 \cdot T \cdot H \\ &\quad - 0.012308094 \cdot T^{2} \\ &\quad - 0.0164248277778 \cdot H^{2} \\ &\quad + 0.002211732 \cdot T^{2} \cdot H \\ &\quad + 0.00072546 \cdot T \cdot H^{2} \\ &\quad - 0.000003582 \cdot T^{2} \cdot H^{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Publish the virtual resource with its own URI and pillars:** - Identity: $HI_{id} = \text{coap://gw/room302/heatindex}$ - State: $HI_{state}(t) \in \mathbb{R}$, unit = °C - Metadata: $HI_{meta} = \{\text{type: virtual}, \text{derivation: rothfusz2016}\}$ - Capability: $HI_{cap} = \{\text{read}, \text{observe}, \text{history}\}$ **Step 5 — Verify by numerical substitution.** Let $T = 32°C$, $H = 70\%$. Compute each term: $$\begin{aligned} \text{term}_1 &= -8.78469475556 \\ \text{term}_2 &= +1.61139411 \times 32 = 51.5646115 \\ \text{term}_3 &= +2.33854883889 \times 70 = 163.6984187 \\ \text{term}_4 &= -0.14611605 \times 32 \times 70 = -327.299952 \\ \text{term}_5 &= -0.012308094 \times 32^{2} = -12.6034883 \\ \text{term}_6 &= -0.0164248277778 \times 70^{2} = -80.4816551 \\ \text{term}_7 &= +0.002211732 \times 32^{2} \times 70 = 158.5111924 \\ \text{term}_8 &= +0.00072546 \times 32 \times 70^{2} = 113.7720960 \\ \text{term}_9 &= -0.000003582 \times 32^{2} \times 70^{2} = -1.7967456 \end{aligned}$$ Summing all terms: $$HI = 56.5803833 \;°C \approx 56.6 \;°C$$ This virtual resource is now consumable by the application layer via a **single GET request** to $HI_{id}$ — fully abstracting the underlying complexity of three physical sensors and a 9-term polynomial. ✅ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Resource vs. Abstraction Concerns | Concern | At Resource-Model Level | At Abstraction Layer | |---|---|---| | Heterogeneity | Normalized to common schema (SenML, RDF) | Hidden from apps via uniform API | | Discovery | URI + semantic metadata | CoAP resource directory, DNS-SD | | Composition | Declared links between resources | Service orchestration engine | | Security | Per-resource auth tokens (DTLS, OAuth) | Centralized policy enforcement | | Scalability | Hierarchical naming | Caching, proxying, pub-sub broker | --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Composite Resource Hierarchy **Scenario.** A smart hospital ward must expose a `WardMonitor` composite resource.
