The two circuits share **no electrical connection** — only light travels between them. This guarantees **5000 V RMS** of isolation, preventing ground loops and protecting patients/users from mains-side faults. Common optocouplers: **PC817, 4N35, MOC3021** (the last one is specifically designed for triac triggering). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Relay coil current | $I_{coil} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$ | Amperes (A) | | 2 | Coil resistance (Songle 5V) | $R_{coil} \approx 70\ \Omega$ | Ohms (Ω) | | 3 | Coil power dissipation | $P_{coil} = I_{coil}^{2} \cdot R_{coil}$ | Watts (W) | | 4 | Base resistor (transistor driver) | $R_{b} = \frac{V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}}{I_{B}}$, where $I_{B} = \frac{I_{coil}}{h_{FE}}$ | Ohms (Ω) | | 5 | Flyback diode reverse voltage | $V_{R} \geq V_{cc}$ (at minimum) | Volts (V) | | 6 | Back-EMF (inductor kick) | $V_{L} = -L \frac{di}{dt}$ | Volts (V) | | 7 | AC RMS voltage to peak | $V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \cdot V_{RMS}$ | Volts (V) | | 8 | AC load power (resistive) | $P = V_{RMS} \cdot I_{RMS} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) | | 9 | Triac latching current | $I_{L}$ (must exceed to fire, typically 5–25 mA) | Milliamperes (mA) | | 10 | Triac holding current | $I_{H}$ (must remain above to stay ON) | Milliamperes (mA) | > [!NOTE] > **Important Boundary Condition:** Always select a relay with a contact rating of at least **1.5× to 2×** the expected load current to provide a safety margin for inrush currents (especially inductive loads like motors, which can draw **5–10×** the rated current at startup). ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility In **biomedical IoT systems**, relay-controlled AC power devices appear in: - **Infant Incubators** — Relay switches the heating element and the circulation fan. - **Ventilators** — Relay controls solenoid valves for oxygen/air blending. - **Autoclaves/Sterilizers** — Relays switch the heating coils (3 kW–6 kW). - **MRI Cooling Systems** — Relays control the chiller compressor. - **Patient Monitoring Room** — Smart switching of bedside lamps, alarms, and signaling. In **general IoT (Home Automation)**, relay modules (like the 1-channel, 2-channel, 4-channel, 8-channel modules used with Arduino/ESP32) switch: - Living room lights, ceiling fans, table lamps. - Air-conditioner compressors. - Water-pump motors. - Garden sprinklers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Base Resistor Value for a Relay Driver Transistor **Problem Statement:** A 5 V relay coil (Songle SRD-05VDC-SL-C) has a coil resistance of **70 Ω** and a rated operating current of **~71 mA**. The relay is driven by a BC547 NPN transistor from an Arduino UNO GPIO pin (HIGH = 5 V). The BC547 has $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7\ V$ and $h_{FE(\beta)} = 100$ (minimum). Calculate the required base resistor $R_B$. **Step 1 — Identify the Collector Current (which equals the coil current):** $$I_{C} = I_{coil} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$$ Substituting $V_{CC} = 5\ V$, $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\ V$, $R_{coil} = 70\ \Omega$: $$I_{C} = \frac{5 - 0.2}{70} = \frac{4.8}{70} \approx 0.0686\ A$$ So $I_C \approx 68.6\ mA$. (We will design for saturation, so we need slightly more base current.) **Step 2 — Compute the Required Minimum Base Current:** To drive the transistor into saturation, we must satisfy: $$I_{B} \geq \frac{I_{C}}{h_{FE(\min)}}$$ Substituting $I_C = 68.6\ mA$ and $h_{FE(\min)} = 100$: $$I_{B} \geq \frac{68.6\ mA}{100} = 0.686\ mA$$ **Step 3 — Design with a Safety Overdrive Factor of 2×:** To ensure hard saturation under all conditions (low temperature, aging, part-to-part variation), we use an overdrive factor $k = 2$: $$I_{B(\text{design})} = k \cdot \frac{I_{C}}{h_{FE}} = 2 \cdot 0.686\ mA = 1.372\ mA$$ **Step 4 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) across the Base-Emitter Loop:** The GPIO pin drives the base resistor $R_B$, which connects to the base-emitter junction. $$V_{GPIO} = I_{B} \cdot R_{B} + V_{BE(sat)}$$ Rearranging: $$R_{B} = \frac{V_{GPIO} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(\text{design})}}$$ Substituting $V_{GPIO} = 5\ V$, $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7\ V$, $I_B = 1.372\ mA = 1.372 \times 10^{-3}\ A$: $$R_{B} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{1.372 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.3}{1.372 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$R_{B} \approx 3134\ \Omega$$ **Step 5 — Choose the Nearest Standard Resistor Value:** We round UP to the next E24 standard value for guaranteed saturation: $$\boxed{R_{B} = 3.3\ k\Omega\ (\text{standard E24 value})}$$ **Step 6 — Verify:** $$I_{B(\text{actual})} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{3300} = 1.30\ mA$$ $$\beta_{\text{required}} = \frac{I_{C}}{I_{B}} = \frac{68.6}{1.30} \approx 52.8$$ Since $52.8 \ll 100 = h_{FE}$, the transistor is comfortably in saturation. ✅ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: AC Load Power and Peak Voltage (230 V Indian Mains) **Problem Statement:** A 230 V AC, 50 Hz incandescent bulb rated at **100 W** is connected through a relay contact. Compute the RMS current, peak voltage, and verify that a 10 A relay contact can handle it. **Step 1 — Compute the RMS Current:** For a resistive load ($\cos\phi = 1$): $$P = V_{RMS} \cdot I_{RMS} \cdot \cos\phi$$ $$I_{RMS} = \frac{P}{V_{RMS} \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{100}{230 \cdot 1} \approx 0.435\ A$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Peak Voltage:** $$V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \cdot 1.414 \approx 325.27\ V$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Peak Current:** $$I_{peak} = I_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 0.435 \cdot 1.414 \approx 0.615\ A$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Angular Frequency:** $$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \cdot 50 = 314.16\ rad/s$$ **Step 5 — Inrush Multiplier for Cold Tungsten Filament:** > [!WARNING] > A cold tungsten filament has a resistance that is approximately **1/10th to 1/15th** of its hot resistance. Therefore, the inrush current at switch-on can be **10× to 15×** the steady-state RMS current. $$I_{inrush(peak)} = 10 \cdot I_{peak} = 10 \cdot 0.615 \approx 6.15\ A$$ Since the relay contact rating is **10 A**, the inrush is within the safe limit, but only barely. For a robust design, a relay rated at **16 A** would be preferred. **Step 6 — Final Selection Recommendation:** $$\boxed{\text{Use a 16 A, 250 V AC relay for a 100 W incandescent bulb.}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — EMR vs SSR for AC Control | Evaluation Criterion | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | |---|---|---| | **Switching speed** | Slow (10–15 ms operate, 5–10 ms release) | Fast (<1 ms turn-on, zero-cross turn-off) | | **Acoustic noise** | Audible "click" | Silent (no moving parts) | | **Contact wear** | Mechanical wear, finite life (~100,000 cycles) | No wear, semiconductor only (~billion cycles) | | **Inrush current handling** | Excellent (massive silver alloy contacts) | Limited by $I^2 t$ of the triac | | **Off-state leakage** | True zero (open contact) | Small leakage (~1–10 mA) | | **Voltage drop when ON** | ~0 V (closed metal contacts) | ~1.5–2 V across triac → heat dissipation | | **EMI generation** | Low (clean mechanical switching) | High (dV/dt switching transients) | | **Cost** | Very low (₹15–₹40 for 5 V module) | Higher (₹120–₹400) | | **Isolation** | 5 kV RMS (galvanic via air gap) | 5 kV RMS (optical via optocoupler) | | **Best application** | General-purpose ON/OFF, low cycle count | Heater control, fast PWM, high cycle count | | **Biomedical preference** | Used in alarm and power switching | Preferred for heater and proportional control | --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Triac Latching Current Requirement **Problem Statement:** A MOC3021 optotriac is used to drive a BTA16-600B triac for switching a 2 kW room heater from a 230 V AC mains. Determine whether the MOC3021's trigger current $I_{FT} = 15\ mA$ is sufficient to fire the BTA16. **Step 1 — Triac Latching Current $I_{L}$:** From the BTA16 datasheet, $I_{L} = 50\ mA$ (typical at 25 °C). **Step 2 — Verify Trigger Condition:** The MOC3021 must deliver a trigger current that, when converted through the gate, exceeds $I_{L}$: $$I_{GT} \geq I_{L}/k$$ where $k$ is a safety factor. For BTA16, $I_{GT(\max)} = 50\ mA$ in the **QIV quadrant** (most sensitive quadrant). **Step 3 — Compute Required LED Current:** $$I_{FT} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{F(LED)}}{R_{LED}}$$ Given $V_{F(LED)} = 1.2\ V$ and $V_{in} = 5\ V$: $$R_{LED} = \frac{5 - 1.2}{15\ mA} = \frac{3.8}{0.015} \approx 253\ \Omega$$ Use standard **270 Ω**. **Step 4 — Heat-Sink Sizing for the Triac:** $$P_{triac} = I_{RMS} \cdot V_{T(rms)} \approx 8.7\ A \cdot 1.5\ V \approx 13\ W$$ With a thermal resistance requirement $\theta_{JA} < (T_{J(\max)} - T_{A})/P = (125 - 50)/13 \approx 5.77\ °C/W$, a heat-sink of at least **5 °C/W** must be attached. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Microcontroller to AC Load via Relay ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["Microcontroller
GPIO 3.3V or 5V"] OPTO["Optocoupler
PC817 Isolation"] DRV["Transistor Driver
BC547 / MOSFET"] COIL["Relay Coil
5V DC 70mA"] CONTACTS["Relay Contacts
COM NO NC"] ACLOAD["AC Load
Bulb / Fan / Motor"] MAINS["230V AC Mains
50 Hz"] MCU -->|Logic Signal| OPTO OPTO -->|Isolated Drive| DRV DRV -->|Switching Current| COIL COIL -->|Magnetic Field| CONTACTS MAINS --> CONTACTS CONTACTS --> ACLOAD ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Circuit — Single-Channel Relay Module (Industry-Standard Topology) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CTRL["Control Side - Low Voltage DC"] VCC5V["+5V DC Supply"] GND1["GND DC"] GPIOPIN["MCU GPIO Pin"] R1["R1 = 1k ohm
Current Limiter"] LED1["LED1 = Indicator
Red 3mm"] D1["D1 = 1N4007
Flyback Diode"] end subgraph RELAY["Relay Element"] COILW["Coil L1
70 ohm 5V"] COM["COM Terminal"] NO["NO Terminal"] NC["NC Terminal"] end subgraph LOAD["Load Side - Mains Voltage AC"] LIVE["230V AC Live"] NEUTRAL["230V AC Neutral"] BULB["LAMP / Bulb"] end VCC5V --- COILW COILW --- D1 D1 --- GND1 GPIOPIN --> R1 R1 --> LED1 LED1 --> GND1 LIVE --> COM NO --> BULB BULB --> NEUTRAL ``` ### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Relay Switching Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as Microcontroller participant DRV as Transistor Driver participant COIL as Relay Coil participant ARM as Armature and Contacts participant LOAD as AC Load Note over MCU: GPIO Pin set LOW (Idle) MCU->>DRV: Base voltage = 0V DRV->>COIL: No current COIL->>ARM: No magnetic field ARM->>LOAD: COM at NC, Load OFF Note over MCU: User sends ON command MCU->>DRV: GPIO HIGH, 5V applied DRV->>COIL: Transistor saturates, ~70 mA flows COIL->>ARM: Magnetic field builds ~10 ms ARM->>LOAD: Armature snaps to NO, Load receives 230V AC Note over LOAD: Bulb glows, Fan rotates Note over MCU: User sends OFF command MCU->>DRV: GPIO LOW DRV->>COIL: Transistor cutoff, current stops COIL->>ARM: Field collapses, spring returns armature ARM->>LOAD: COM returns to NC, Load OFF ``` ### 4.4 Architecture — Multi-Channel Relay Module for Smart Home / ICU ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PI["Raspberry Pi / ESP32 Controller"] GPIO17["GPIO 17"] GPIO27["GPIO 27"] GPIO22["GPIO 22"] GPIO23["GPIO 23"] end subgraph MOD8["8-Channel Relay Module"] RLY1["Relay 1
Bedside Lamp"] RLY2["Relay 2
Ceiling Fan"] RLY3["Relay 3
Oxygen Valve"] RLY4["Relay 4
Heater Coil"] RLY5["Relay 5
Alarm Buzzer"] RLY6["Relay 6
Pump Motor"] RLY7["Relay 7
Spare 1"] RLY8["Relay 8
Spare 2"] end GPIO17 --> RLY1 GPIO27 --> RLY2 GPIO22 --> RLY3 GPIO23 --> RLY4 GPIO17 -.shared bus.-> RLY5 GPIO27 -.shared bus.-> RLY6 GPIO22 -.shared bus.-> RLY7 GPIO23 -.shared bus.-> RLY8 ``` ### 4.5 SSR vs EMR — Internal Schematic Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EMR["Electromechanical Relay EMR"] EMRCOIL["Coil L"] EMRCORE["Iron Core"] EMRARM["Movable Armature"] EMRCOM["COM Contact"] EMRNO["NO Contact"] EMRNC["NC Contact"] EMRCOIL --> EMRCORE EMRCORE --> EMRARM EMRARM --> EMRCOM EMRCOM --> EMRNO EMRCOM --> EMRNC end subgraph SSR["Solid State Relay SSR"] SSROPTO["Optocoupler LED Input"] SSRPHT["Photo-Triac Detector"] SSRTRG["Trigger Circuit"] SSRTRIAC["Power Triac
BTA16 or similar"] SSROUT["Output Terminals"] SSROPTO --> SSRPHT SSRPHT --> SSRTRG SSRTRG --> SSRTRIAC SSRTRIAC --> SSROUT end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define a relay. With a neat sketch, explain the function of COM, NO, and NC terminals of an SPDT relay used for AC appliance control.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A relay is an **electromechanically operated switch** that allows a low-power electrical signal to control a higher-power circuit while maintaining complete galvanic isolation between the two. **Pin Functions of an SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) Relay:** - **COM (Common):** This is the pole or the moving terminal that carries the load current. It is the input terminal from the AC source (or DC load positive). In an SPDT relay, COM is connected to exactly one of the two output terminals at any given time. - **NO (Normally Open):** When the relay coil is **de-energized** (no current in the coil), the COM terminal is **not connected** to the NO terminal. The circuit is OPEN. When the coil is energized, COM connects to NO, closing the circuit and switching ON the load. - **NC (Normally Closed):** When the relay coil is **de-energized**, the COM terminal is **already connected** to the NC terminal, so the load is ON by default. When the coil is energized, COM disconnects from NC, opening the circuit and switching OFF the load. **Sketch:** ``` COIL CONTACTS +---o o---COM | | o---+ +---NO | | +----[ L ]----+ +---NC | o (coil pin 2) ``` For **AC appliance control**, the **COM-NO** combination is typically used: COM receives the Live wire from mains, and the NO terminal feeds the appliance. When the relay is activated, the appliance gets mains voltage and turns ON. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why is a flyback diode connected across a relay coil? What happens if it is omitted?** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A relay coil is fundamentally an **inductor** (a long winding of copper wire wound around an iron core). When current flows through an inductor, it stores energy in its magnetic field: $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L \cdot I^{2}$$ When the driving transistor is switched OFF, the current through the coil tries to instantaneously drop to zero. However, an inductor **opposes any sudden change in current**, and by Faraday's law it generates a large reverse voltage given by: $$V_{L} = -L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$ If the current falls from 70 mA to 0 A in **1 microsecond** through an inductance of, say, 100 mH, the induced voltage would be: $$V_{L} = -0.1 \cdot \frac{0.070}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = -7000\ V$$ This **negative voltage spike** (with the collector terminal becoming massively negative) will: 1. **Destroy the driving transistor** by reverse-bias breakdown of the B-C junction. 2. **Reset/reboot the microcontroller** by injecting noise into the supply rails. 3. Cause **EMI** that can corrupt nearby analog signals (critical in biomedical instrumentation). The **flyback diode** (1N4007 for general use, 1N5819 for fast switching) is connected with its **cathode to the positive supply** and its **anode to the switching transistor's collector**. When the transistor turns OFF, the diode becomes forward-biased by the induced EMF and provides a safe circulating loop for the coil current, dissipating the stored energy as heat. The reverse voltage across the transistor is clamped to approximately: $$V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CC} + V_{F(\text{diode})} \approx 5 + 0.7 = 5.7\ V$$ This is well within the transistor's safe operating area. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — Electromechanical Relay Design and Interfacing [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Possible Question Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a **5 V SPDT relay** with an Arduino UNO to control a **230 V AC incandescent bulb**. Label all components and explain the role of the transistor driver, base resistor, and flyback diode. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A 5 V relay coil has a resistance of **70 Ω** and is driven by a BC547 transistor ($V_{BE} = 0.7\ V$, $h_{FE} = 100$) from an Arduino pin (HIGH = 5 V). Design the base resistor using a **2× overdrive factor** for saturation. If the bulb is rated **60 W at 230 V AC**, compute the RMS current, peak voltage, and recommend a suitable relay contact rating. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Circuit Diagram (described verbally for ASCII clarity):** - **Arduino Pin D7** → Base resistor $R_B = 3.3\ k\Omega$ → Base of BC547 NPN transistor. - **Emitter of BC547** → Arduino GND. - **Collector of BC547** → Pin 1 of relay coil. - **Pin 2 of relay coil** → +5 V DC supply (Arduino 5V pin). - **Flyback diode 1N4007** across the coil: Cathode to +5 V, Anode to Collector. - **Indicator LED + 1 kΩ** from Arduino D7 to GND (shows relay state visually). - **COM terminal of relay** → 230 V AC Live wire. - **NO terminal of relay** → One terminal of the 60 W bulb. - **Other terminal of the bulb** → 230 V AC Neutral wire. **Component Roles:** | Component | Function | |---|---| | **BC547 Transistor** | Current amplifier. The Arduino GPIO can source only ~40 mA, but the relay coil needs ~70 mA. The transistor switches the heavier coil current using a small base current (~1 mA). | | **Base Resistor $R_B$** | Limits the base current to a safe value to prevent damage to the Arduino pin and to ensure transistor saturation. | | **Flyback Diode 1N4007** | Clamps the back-EMF generated when the relay coil is de-energized, protecting the BC547 from reverse-voltage breakdown. | | **Indicator LED** | Provides a visual confirmation of the relay's ON/OFF state without needing to measure voltage. | **Operating Sequence:** 1. `digitalWrite(7, HIGH)` → Arduino outputs 5 V on pin D7. 2. Current flows through $R_B$ into the base of BC547. 3. Transistor enters saturation, collector voltage drops to ~0.2 V. 4. Relay coil sees ~4.8 V and draws ~70 mA, generating a magnetic field. 5. Armature snaps to the NO contact. COM is now connected to NO. 6. 230 V AC flows through the relay contacts to the bulb. Bulb glows. 7. `digitalWrite(7, LOW)` → Base current stops, transistor cuts off, coil de-energizes. 8. Spring returns armature to NC. Bulb turns OFF. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Coil Current:** $$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5 - 0.2}{70} = 0.0686\ A \approx 68.6\ mA$$ **Step 2 — Minimum Base Current for Saturation:** $$I_{B(\min)} = \frac{I_C}{h_{FE}} = \frac{68.6}{100} = 0.686\ mA$$ **Step 3 — Design Base Current with 2× Overdrive:** $$I_{B(\text{design})} = 2 \times 0.686 = 1.372\ mA$$ **Step 4 — Base Resistor Calculation:** $$R_B = \frac{V_{GPIO} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(\text{design})}} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{1.372 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{4.3}{1.372 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3134\ \Omega$$ **Nearest standard value (E24): $R_B = 3.3\ k\Omega$** ✅ **Step 5 — Bulb RMS Current:** $$I_{RMS} = \frac{P}{V_{RMS}} = \frac{60}{230} \approx 0.261\ A = 261\ mA$$ **Step 6 — Peak Voltage Across Bulb:** $$V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 \approx 325\ V$$ **Step 7 — Peak Current:** $$I_{peak} = I_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 0.369\ A$$ **Step 8 — Inrush Factor (Cold Filament):** $$I_{inrush} = 10 \times I_{peak} \approx 3.69\ A$$ **Step 9 — Recommended Relay Contact Rating:** $$\boxed{\text{Relay rating: 5 A at 250 V AC (minimum); Recommended: 10 A at 250 V AC.}}$$ A standard **Songle SRD-05VDC-SL-C** relay module (10 A, 250 V AC) is ideal. --- #### **Question B — Solid-State Relay, Triac, and Optoisolation [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Anticipated Pattern]** **(a)** Compare **Electromechanical Relays (EMR)** and **Solid-State Relays (SSR)**. Explain with a block diagram how a zero-crossing SSR using an optotriac (MOC3041) provides optical isolation and silent switching of an AC heater load. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A **2 kW, 230 V AC heater** is controlled by a BTA16 triac through a MOC3021 optotriac. Compute the RMS load current, the peak voltage across the triac, and the approximate heat-sink thermal resistance required if the ambient temperature is 40 °C and the junction temperature must not exceed 110 °C. Assume $V_T = 1.5\ V$ across the triac when conducting. *(7 Marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | EMR | SSR | |---|---|---| | Switching Element | Mechanical contacts + armature | Semiconductor (Triac / MOSFET) | | Moving Parts | Yes | No | | Switching Speed | 10–15 ms | <1 ms | | Acoustic Noise | Audible click | Silent | | Lifetime | ~100,000 cycles | >1 billion cycles | | On-state Voltage Drop | ~0 V (metallic contact) | ~1.5–2 V (semiconductor) | | Off-state Leakage | Zero | 1–10 mA | | Galvanic Isolation | Via air gap (5 kV) | Via optocoupler (5 kV) | | EMI | Low | Can be high | | Cost | Low | Higher | **Block Diagram of Zero-Crossing SSR (MOC3041):** ``` +----------------+ +-------------------+ +------------+ +-----------+ | Logic Input |-->| MOC3041 LED |-->| Zero-Cross |-->| Triac |--> AC Load | (5V from MCU) | | (Infrared Emitter)| | Detector | | (BTA16) | +----------------+ +-------------------+ +------------+ +-----------+ | | | | | v v v GND Optical Barrier Firing Circuit Heat-Sink (5000V Isolation) ``` **Explanation:** 1. **Input Stage:** The microcontroller sends a 5 V logic HIGH through a current-limiting resistor (typically 470 Ω) to the LED of the MOC3041 optotriac. 2. **Optical Isolation:** The LED emits infrared light, which is detected by a photosensitive triac inside the MOC3041. There is **no electrical connection** between the MCU side and the AC side — only light passes through, providing 5000 V of galvanic isolation. 3. **Zero-Cross Detection:** The MOC3041 contains an internal zero-crossing detector that ensures the output triac fires **only when the AC mains voltage is at or near 0 V**. This eliminates the high inrush currents and EMI associated with random-phase switching. 4. **Power Stage:** The triggered photo-triac then drives the gate of the main power triac (BTA16), which switches the AC load. 5. **Silent Operation:** Since there are no mechanical contacts, no audible click is produced. This is essential in **ICU and biomedical applications** where patient rest must not be disturbed. **Advantages for AC Heater Control:** - Proportional control via phase-angle firing (using MOC3021 instead of MOC3041). - Silent operation — no click to disturb patients. - Long life — heater cycles can be millions of operations per year. - Bounce-free switching — no contact arcing. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — RMS Load Current:** For a resistive heater ($\cos\phi = 1$): $$I_{RMS} = \frac{P}{V_{RMS}} = \frac{2000}{230} \approx 8.696\ A \approx 8.7\ A$$ **Step 2 — Peak Voltage:** $$V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 \approx 325\ V$$ **Step 3 — Voltage Across the Triac When ON:** The triac, when fully conducting, drops approximately $V_T = 1.5\ V$. This is the instantaneous voltage across the triac during conduction. **Step 4 — Power Dissipated by the Triac:** For a fully-phase-controlled resistive load, the average power dissipated by the triac is approximated as: $$P_{triac} \approx V_T \cdot I_{RMS} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$$ But for full-wave conduction (heater fully ON), a simpler approximation suffices: $$P_{triac} \approx V_T \cdot I_{RMS} = 1.5 \times 8.7 \approx 13.05\ W$$ **Step 5 — Required Thermal Resistance (Junction-to-Ambient):** The maximum allowable junction-to-ambient thermal resistance is given by: $$\theta_{JA(\max)} = \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_{A}}{P_{triac}} = \frac{110 - 40}{13.05} = \frac{70}{13.05} \approx 5.36\ \frac{^\circ C}{W}$$ **Step 6 — Heat-Sink Selection:** A BTA16 in a TO-220 package has a junction-to-case thermal resistance of $\theta_{JC} \approx 1.5\ ^\circ C/W$. After accounting for the thermal interface material (TIM) with $\theta_{CS} \approx 0.5\ ^\circ C/W$, the heat-sink must provide: $$\theta_{SA} \leq \theta_{JA(\max)} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = 5.36 - 1.5 - 0.5 = 3.36\ \frac{^\circ C}{W}$$ **Recommended Heat-Sink:** An extruded aluminum heat-sink of **≤ 3 °C/W** with a small fan for forced-air cooling. $$\boxed{\text{Use a heat-sink rated } \leq 3\ ^\circ C/W \text{ with a 12 V DC fan for the BTA16 triac.}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Relay-Based Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Flyback Diode (–2 marks):** Many students draw the relay circuit but omit the 1N4007 across the coil. Examiners specifically test for this. > 2. **Confusing NO and NC (–2 marks):** In an SPDT relay, the default state (coil OFF) has COM connected to NC, not NO. Students often incorrectly say "NO is closed when relay is OFF" — it is the opposite. > 3. **Using $V_{RMS}$ instead of $V_{peak}$ (–1 mark):** For triac voltage calculations, always use $V_{peak} = 1.414 \times V_{RMS}$. > 4. **Ignoring Inrush Current (–1 mark):** Always recommend a relay with **2× the steady-state current rating** to handle the inrush of cold filaments or motor starting transients. > 5. **Drawing Mains and DC without separation (–2 marks):** Examiners look for a clear visual or conceptual gap between the low-voltage DC side (left) and the mains AC side (right) of the schematic. Mixing them without labels loses isolation marks. > 6. **Wrong Base Resistor Direction of Bias (–1 mark):** The flyback diode's cathode MUST be at the higher potential (Vcc), with anode at the collector. Reversing it will short-circuit the supply through the diode. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Relay Control of AC Power"** - [x] A **relay** is an electrically operated switch that isolates low-voltage control from high-voltage load. - [x] An **SPDT relay** has **COM, NO, NC** terminals. COM is the pole; NO is open when coil is OFF; NC is closed when coil is OFF. - [x] The **coil** is energized by the microcontroller via a **transistor driver** (BC547 / 2N2222 / MOSFET). - [x] A **base resistor $R_B$** (typically 1–10 kΩ) limits base current to ensure transistor saturation. - [x] A **flyback diode** (1N4007) across the coil protects the driver from back-EMF. - [x] An **optocoupler** (PC817 / MOC3021) provides **galvanic isolation** of up to 5 kV. - [x] An **EMR** is mechanical, slow, has audible click, but handles high inrush well. - [x] An **SSR** is semiconductor-based, silent, fast, and has long life but generates heat. - [x] A **Triac** (BTA16, BTA41) is the switching element of an AC SSR. - [x] **MOC3041** = zero-cross SSR; **MOC3021** = random-phase SSR. - [x] A **heat-sink** is mandatory for triacs carrying >2 A; thermal resistance calculation is essential. - [x] Always size the relay to **at least 1.5× to 2× the steady-state load current**. - [x] For inductive AC loads (motors, transformers), use **RC snubbers** across the relay contacts to suppress arcing. - [x] In **biomedical IoT**, always use **optically isolated** relay modules to protect patients. - [x] Standard coil voltage = **5 V DC or 12 V DC**; typical coil current = **70 mA**. - [x] Contact rating format = **"10 A at 250 V AC / 30 V DC"**. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Pearl:** If a question asks "How does a relay provide isolation?" — answer in three crisp points: **(1) Physical air gap between coil and contacts, (2) No shared electrical conductor, (3) Only magnetic field crosses the barrier.** This phrasing matches the KTU model answer key exactly.

topics.title: Controlling Servo Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Controlling Servo Motor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition A **Servo Motor** (or **Servomechanism**) is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for **precise control of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration** in a closed-loop feedback system. In the context of IoT and embedded systems, the term *servo motor* predominantly refers to the **hobby-grade RC Servo Motor** (e.g., SG90, MG996R), which integrates a DC motor, a gearbox, a potentiometer (position sensor), and a control circuit into a single compact package. The **control signal** for a standard hobby servo is a **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM)** waveform with a fixed **period of 20 ms (50 Hz frequency)**. The **width of the pulse** (typically between **1 ms and 2 ms**) dictates the target angular position of the output shaft. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The PWM signal for a standard servo operates at a **fixed frequency of 50 Hz (Period T = 20 ms)**. The pulse width is the *only* variable parameter that controls position. Students often confuse this with variable-frequency PWM used in motor speed control — **for servos, frequency is FIXED; pulse width is VARIABLE**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Steering Wheel of a Car** > Imagine a car steering wheel. You turn the wheel to a specific angle (say, 30° to the left), and the wheels faithfully hold that exact position. If a gust of wind pushes the wheels, a feedback mechanism corrects them back to your commanded angle. > A servo motor works exactly the same way: > - You **command** an angle via a pulse width (1 ms → 0°, 1.5 ms → 90°, 2 ms → 180°). > - The internal **feedback loop** (potentiometer + control circuit) continuously compares the *actual* shaft position to the *commanded* position and applies corrective torque. > - The shaft **holds** that position against external disturbances (like a load pushing the robotic arm). ### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | PWM Frequency | **50 Hz** | Hertz (Hz) | | PWM Period (T) | **20 ms** | milliseconds | | Pulse Width Range | **1.0 ms to 2.0 ms** | milliseconds | | Angular Range (SG90) | **0° to 180°** | degrees | | Operating Voltage | **4.8 V to 6.0 V** | Volts DC | | Stall Torque (SG90) | **1.8 kg·cm** (at 4.8 V) | kg·cm | | Neutral Pulse Width | **1.5 ms** | milliseconds | ### The Three Wires of a Servo 1. **Orange/White (Signal):** Receives the PWM control pulse from the microcontroller. 2. **Red (Power – VCC):** Connects to +5V (or +6V for higher torque servos). 3. **Brown/Black (Ground):** Common ground with the microcontroller. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The PWM Control Principle A servo motor does **not** interpret PWM as a *power level* (unlike LED dimming or DC motor speed control). Instead, it interprets the **pulse duration** as a **position command**. The internal control board: 1. **Measures** the incoming pulse width using a timer circuit. 2. **Reads** the current shaft angle from the internal potentiometer (voltage divider). 3. **Computes the error**: $\text{Error} = \theta_{\text{commanded}} - \theta_{\text{actual}}$. 4. **Drives the DC motor** in the appropriate direction (via an H-bridge) to minimize the error. 5. **Stops** the motor (PWM-off internally) when the error is zero — this is the *holding torque*. ### 2.2 Mathematical Relationship: Pulse Width vs. Angular Position The mapping between pulse width and shaft angle is **linear** for standard hobby servos: $$\theta = \frac{(t_{\text{pulse}} - 1.0\,\text{ms}) \times 180°}{1.0\,\text{ms}}$$ where: - $\theta$ = Output shaft angle in degrees (0° ≤ θ ≤ 180°) - $t_{\text{pulse}}$ = Pulse width in milliseconds (1.0 ms ≤ $t_{\text{pulse}}$ ≤ 2.0 ms) > [!NOTE] > **Why the "Dead-band"?** > Sending a pulse width of 1.0 ms reliably commands **0°**, and 2.0 ms reliably commands **180°**. The servo ignores tiny fluctuations near the neutral point (≈1.5 ms) within a **dead-band of ~5–10 µs** to prevent jitter. ### 2.3 Duty Cycle Calculation Even though frequency is fixed, the **duty cycle** is often reported in datasheets: $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100\%$$ For a **neutral (90°)** position: $$\text{Duty Cycle}_{\text{neutral}} = \frac{1.5\,\text{ms}}{20\,\text{ms}} \times 100\% = 7.5\%$$ For **0°**: $$\text{Duty Cycle}_{0°} = \frac{1.0\,\text{ms}}{20\,\text{ms}} \times 100\% = 5.0\%$$ For **180°**: $$\text{Duty Cycle}_{180°} = \frac{2.0\,\text{ms}}{20\,\text{ms}} \times 100\% = 10.0\%$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period from frequency | ms | | 2 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency from period | Hz | | 3 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100\%$ | PWM duty cycle | % | | 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{(t_{\text{pulse}} - 1.0) \times 180°}{1.0}$ | Angle from pulse width | degrees | | 5 | $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$ | Inverse: pulse width from angle | ms | | 6 | $\text{Angular Velocity} = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ | Slew rate of the servo shaft | °/s | | 7 | $V_{\text{supply}} = 4.8\text{V to }6.0\text{V}$ | Operating voltage range | Volts | | 8 | $\text{Resolution}_{\text{Arduino}} = \dfrac{180°}{1024} \approx 0.176°$ | Minimum angular step (10-bit timer) | degrees | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > Servo motors are the **backbone of robotics, CNC machines, biomedical prosthetics, and IoT-based automation** — from a robotic arm picking-and-placing components in a smart factory to a **prosthetic hand controlled via EMG signals** in a biomedical application (directly linking to Module 3 of your IoT & Biomedical course). ### 2.5 Microcontroller Interface (Arduino UNO Reference) On an **Arduino UNO**, the ATmega328P's **16-bit Timer1** is conventionally used because hobby servos require **microsecond-level precision**: - Timer1 has a prescaler of **8**, so with a 16 MHz clock, one timer tick = **0.5 µs**. - For a 20 ms period: $\text{ICR1} = \dfrac{20{,}000\,\mu s}{0.5\,\mu s} = 40{,}000$ (decimal). - For a 1.5 ms neutral pulse: $\text{OCR1A} = \dfrac{1{,}500\,\mu s}{0.5\,\mu s} = 3{,}000$ (decimal). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pulse-Width-to-Angle Linear Mapping **Given:** - At $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0$ ms, the servo angle is $\theta = 0°$. - At $t_{\text{pulse}} = 2.0$ ms, the servo angle is $\theta = 180°$. - The relationship is **linear** (standard hobby servo assumption). **Step 1 — General form of a linear equation:** $$y = m \cdot x + c$$ Substituting servo variables: $$\theta = m \cdot t_{\text{pulse}} + c$$ **Step 2 — Compute the slope (m):** The slope is the change in angle divided by the change in pulse width: $$m = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t_{\text{pulse}}} = \frac{180° - 0°}{2.0\,\text{ms} - 1.0\,\text{ms}} = \frac{180°}{1.0\,\text{ms}} = 180°/\text{ms}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the intercept (c):** Apply the known data point $(t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0\,\text{ms}, \theta = 0°)$: $$0 = 180°/\text{ms} \times 1.0\,\text{ms} + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = -180°$$ **Step 4 — Final linear mapping equation:** $$\boxed{\theta = 180 \cdot t_{\text{pulse}} - 180 = 180 \cdot (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1.0)}$$ where $t_{\text{pulse}}$ is in milliseconds and $\theta$ is in degrees. **Step 5 — Verification with the three canonical points:** | $t_{\text{pulse}}$ (ms) | Calculation | $\theta$ (°) | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1.0 | $180 \times (1.0 - 1.0)$ | **0°** | Full counter-clockwise | | 1.5 | $180 \times (1.5 - 1.0)$ | **90°** | Neutral / center | | 2.0 | $180 \times (2.0 - 1.0)$ | **180°** | Full clockwise | --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pulse Width for 45° **Problem:** Determine the required PWM pulse width to position a standard SG90 servo at 45°. **Solution:** Starting from the inverse mapping: $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{\theta}{180°}$$ Substitute $\theta = 45°$: $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{45}{180} = 1.0 + 0.25 = 1.25\,\text{ms}$$ **Corresponding duty cycle:** $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{1.25}{20} \times 100\% = 6.25\%$$ > [!NOTE] > The neutral (1.5 ms / 90°) duty cycle of 7.5% is the **centerpoint**; values below command anticlockwise rotation, and values above command clockwise rotation. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Arduino Timer Register Calculation **Problem:** Configure **Timer1** of an Arduino UNO (16 MHz, 16-bit, prescaler = 8) to produce a **1.6 ms pulse** with a **20 ms period**. **Step 1 — Timer tick duration:** $$T_{\text{tick}} = \frac{\text{Prescaler}}{f_{\text{CPU}}} = \frac{8}{16 \times 10^6} = 0.5\,\mu s$$ **Step 2 — ICR1 (TOP value for 20 ms period):** $$\text{ICR1} = \frac{T}{T_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{20{,}000\,\mu s}{0.5\,\mu s} = 40{,}000$$ **Step 3 — OCR1A (compare value for 1.6 ms pulse):** $$\text{OCR1A} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{1{,}600\,\mu s}{0.5\,\mu s} = 3{,}200$$ **Step 4 — Resulting angle:** $$\theta = 180 \times (1.6 - 1.0) = 180 \times 0.6 = 108°$$ **Corresponding Arduino snippet (C/C++ for the IoT module):** ```c #include // Use the built-in Servo library Servo myServo; int servoPin = 9; void setup() { myServo.attach(servoPin); // Attach servo to PWM-capable pin 9 myServo.write(108); // Command 108° → pulse width ≈ 1.6 ms } void loop() { // Sweep demonstration for (int angle = 0; angle <= 180; angle++) { myServo.write(angle); delay(15); // Allow 15 ms for servo to reach position } for (int angle = 180; angle >= 0; angle--) { myServo.write(angle); delay(15); } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on `Servo.h` Library:** The Arduino's `Servo.h` library internally configures Timer1 and translates the `write(angle)` call into the correct `OCR1A` value using the linear mapping derived above. This is a *practical implementation* of the theory covered in this module. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Sweep Timing Calculation **Problem:** A servo sweeps from 0° to 180° in 1° steps with a 15 ms delay per step. Calculate the total time to complete one full sweep. **Solution:** $$\text{Steps} = 180° - 0° = 180$$ $$\text{Time per step} = 15\,\text{ms}$$ $$T_{\text{sweep}} = 180 \times 15\,\text{ms} = 2{,}700\,\text{ms} = 2.7\,\text{seconds}$$ For a full **round-trip** (0°→180°→0°): $$T_{\text{round-trip}} = 2 \times 2.7 = 5.4\,\text{seconds}$$ > [!NOTE] > Servo slew rate is limited by the **internal DC motor's RPM and gearbox ratio**. The SG90 has a typical operating speed of **0.1 s / 60° at 4.8 V**, so the 15 ms delay in code is conservative and safe. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing PWM Control Method | Control Method | Frequency Control | Pulse Width Control | Best Use Case | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---|---| | `analogWrite()` | Fixed (~490 Hz / 980 Hz) | Variable duty cycle | **LED dimming**, DC motor speed | **NOT** suitable for servos | | `Servo.h` library | Auto (50 Hz) | Variable pulse width | **Servo motors** | Correct choice for this topic | | Manual Timer1 | Configurable | Configurable | High-precision servos / multiple servos | Used for advanced KTU numericals | | PCA9685 (I2C) | 50–1600 Hz | 12-bit resolution | **16+ servos** (e.g., robotic arms) | Common in IoT-based designs | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["Microcontroller
Arduino UNO
16 MHz"] -->|PWM Signal

Control Board

Motor Driver"] HBR -->|Bidirectional Drive| DCM["DC Motor
with Gearbox"] DCM -->|Mechanical Output| SHAFT["Output Shaft
0 to 180 degrees"] SHAFT -->|Mechanical Coupling| POT["Potentiometer
Position Sensor"] POT -->|Analog Voltage
0 to Vcc| CTRL CTRL -.->|Holds position
when error = 0| SHAFT style MCU fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style CTRL fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style HBR fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style DCM fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style SHAFT fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style POT fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 ``` ### 4.2 Signal Timing Diagram — PWM Pulse Width vs. Servo Angle ```mermaid flowchart TB A0["Pulse Width 1.0 ms
Duty Cycle 5 percent
Servo Angle 0 degrees"]:::a A1["Pulse Width 1.5 ms
Duty Cycle 7.5 percent
Servo Angle 90 degrees"]:::b A2["Pulse Width 2.0 ms
Duty Cycle 10 percent

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Model Question]** **Q1. What is a servo motor? Explain the role of PWM in controlling a hobby servo motor. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A **servo motor** is a rotary actuator that uses a **closed-loop feedback mechanism** to precisely control its angular position. It consists of a small DC motor coupled to a gearbox, a potentiometer for position sensing, and a control circuit, all integrated into a single unit. A hobby servo is controlled using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** at a **fixed frequency of 50 Hz (period 20 ms)**. The **pulse width** of the PWM signal determines the target angle of the output shaft: - A pulse width of **1.0 ms** commands **0°** - A pulse width of **1.5 ms** commands **90°** (neutral) - A pulse width of **2.0 ms** commands **180°** The internal feedback loop continuously compares the commanded angle (from the pulse width) with the actual angle (from the potentiometer) and drives the motor to eliminate any error, thereby **holding the commanded position**. --- **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Model Question]** **Q2. List the three wires of a standard hobby servo and state the operating voltage range. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A standard hobby servo (e.g., SG90) has **three wires**: | Wire Color | Function | Connection | |---|---|---| | **Orange (or White)** | Signal | PWM output pin of the microcontroller | | **Red** | Power (VCC) | +5V (or external 5–6V supply) | | **Brown (or Black)** | Ground | Common GND with the microcontroller | **Operating Voltage Range:** **4.8 V to 6.0 V DC** (typically 5V from an Arduino UNO). The **signal wire** carries the 50 Hz PWM control pulse; the **VCC and GND wires** provide power to the internal DC motor and control electronics. --- ### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Servo PWM Control Theory + Calculation** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop operation of a hobby servo motor**. Identify the role of the potentiometer in the feedback path. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For an SG90 servo operating at the standard 50 Hz PWM frequency, calculate: (i) The **period T** of the control signal. (ii) The **duty cycle** required to position the shaft at **135°**. (iii) The **pulse width** required to position the shaft at **45°**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Closed-Loop Operation of a Hobby Servo Motor (7 Marks):** The block diagram is shown in Section 4.1. The operation proceeds as follows: 1. **Command Input:** The microcontroller generates a PWM signal with a fixed **50 Hz frequency (T = 20 ms)** and a variable pulse width between **1.0 ms and 2.0 ms**. This pulse width is the position command. 2. **Control Board / Comparator:** The internal control board measures the incoming pulse width using a precision timer (e.g., a monostable multivibrator or microcontroller-equivalent circuit) and converts it to a reference voltage. 3. **Position Feedback via Potentiometer:** The output shaft is mechanically coupled to a **potentiometer** (variable resistor). As the shaft rotates, the wiper of the potentiometer moves, producing an **analog voltage proportional to the actual shaft angle** (0 V at 0°, Vcc at 180°). 4. **Error Detection:** An **error amplifier (comparator)** computes the difference: $\text{Error} = V_{\text{commanded}} - V_{\text{actual}}$. 5. **H-Bridge Motor Driver:** Based on the sign of the error, the H-bridge drives the internal DC motor in the appropriate direction (clockwise or anticlockwise) to reduce the error. 6. **Shaft Movement:** The DC motor, via the gearbox, rotates the output shaft, which in turn rotates the potentiometer — closing the loop. 7. **Steady State:** When $V_{\text{commanded}} = V_{\text{actual}}$, the error is zero, the motor stops, and the shaft **holds** its position. The servo continues to apply holding torque to resist any external disturbance. **Role of the Potentiometer:** The potentiometer acts as the **position sensor (transducer)** in the feedback loop, converting the mechanical shaft angle into an electrical voltage that can be compared with the commanded position voltage. Without it, the servo would have no way of "knowing" its actual position and the closed-loop control would be impossible. **(b) Numerical Calculations (7 Marks):** **(i) Period T of the control signal:** $$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50\,\text{Hz}} = 0.02\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms}$$ **(ii) Duty cycle for 135°:** Using the inverse mapping $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$: $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{135}{180} = 1.0 + 0.75 = 1.75\,\text{ms}$$ Duty cycle: $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{1.75}{20} \times 100\% = 8.75\%$$ **(iii) Pulse width for 45°:** $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{45}{180} = 1.0 + 0.25 = 1.25\,\text{ms}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Arduino Implementation + Biomedical Application** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain how the **Arduino `Servo.h` library** internally generates the required PWM signal for a servo. Why is **Timer1** of the ATmega328P used instead of `analogWrite()`? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design an **IoT-based biomedical application** using a servo motor (e.g., an **automatic medicine dispenser** or a **prosthetic hand prototype**). Explain the system architecture, components, and the role of the servo in the design. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Arduino `Servo.h` Library Internals (7 Marks):** The Arduino `Servo.h` library uses the **ATmega328P's 16-bit Timer1** to generate a precise 50 Hz PWM signal. The reason for choosing Timer1 (and not `analogWrite()`) is as follows: **Why `analogWrite()` is unsuitable:** - `analogWrite()` generates PWM at a **fixed frequency of ~490 Hz (or ~980 Hz on pins 5 and 6)**. - Its duty cycle resolution is only **8-bit (0 to 255)**. - It does **not** produce the 50 Hz signal with 1–2 ms pulse widths that a hobby servo requires. **How `Servo.h` works internally:** 1. On the first call to `myservo.attach(pin)`, the library **stops Timer1's default PWM function** and reconfigures it in **Fast PWM mode with ICR1 as TOP**. 2. It sets the **prescaler to 8**, giving a timer tick of $T_{\text{tick}} = 0.5\,\mu s$. 3. It loads **ICR1 = 40,000** to define a 20 ms period: $T = 40{,}000 \times 0.5\,\mu s = 20{,}000\,\mu s = 20\,\text{ms}$. 4. When the user calls `myservo.write(angle)`, the library computes the required pulse width using the linear mapping: $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \frac{\text{angle}}{180°}\,\text{ms}$$ and converts it to the corresponding **OCR1A compare value**: $$\text{OCR1A} = \frac{t_{\text{pulse}}}{0.5\,\mu s}$$ 5. The hardware toggles the output pin **HIGH at the start of each 20 ms cycle** and **LOW when the timer count matches OCR1A**, producing the precise pulse. For example, for `myservo.write(90)`: $$t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.5\,\text{ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{OCR1A} = \frac{1{,}500}{0.5} = 3{,}000$$ **(b) IoT-Based Biomedical Application — Automatic Medicine Dispenser (7 Marks):** **System Architecture:** ``` [Patient / Caregiver] → [Mobile App / Web Dashboard] ↓ (MQTT over Wi-Fi) [NodeMCU / ESP8266] ↓ (I2C) [RTC Module DS3231] ← Real-time clock for schedule ↓ [LCD 16x2 Display] ← User interface ↓ (PWM 50 Hz) [Servo Motor SG90] ← Actuator ↓ [Medicine Compartment] ← Releases pill on rotation ↓ [Buzzer + LED] ← Alert / confirmation ``` **Components:** 1. **NodeMCU (ESP8266)** — Acts as the IoT gateway, connects to Wi-Fi, runs the MQTT client. 2. **SG90 Servo Motor** — Mounted on a rotating disc/lever that releases one pill per 90° rotation. This is the **actuator** of the system. 3. **DS3231 RTC Module** — Maintains accurate time even during power loss; triggers the dispense schedule. 4. **LCD 16x2 (I2C)** — Displays the current time, next dose, and status messages. 5. **Buzzer + LED** — Provides audible and visual feedback when a pill is dispensed. 6. **Cloud Platform (ThingSpeak / Blynk)** — Logs dose history and sends reminders to the patient's phone. **Role of the Servo Motor:** The servo is the **physical actuator** that performs the medicine-release action. On receiving a command from the NodeMCU (e.g., `myservo.write(90)`), the servo rotates a lever by 90°, pushing one pill out of the compartment. After a 1-second delay, it returns to its original position (`myservo.write(0)`), ready for the next dose. The servo's **precise angular control** and **holding torque** ensure that exactly one pill is dispensed per cycle — critical for patient safety. **KTU Context:** This application directly links to the **biomedical theme of the course**, demonstrating how IoT (Wi-Fi, MQTT, cloud) integrates with **embedded actuators (servo)** to solve real-world healthcare challenges such as **medication adherence in elderly patients**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing PWM frequency for servos vs. DC motors:** Students often write that PWM frequency is *variable* for servos. **No — for servos, the frequency is FIXED at 50 Hz; only the pulse WIDTH varies.** Deduct 1–2 marks for this error. > 2. **Mixing up the angle ↔ pulse-width mapping:** The 1.0 ms / 1.5 ms / 2.0 ms corresponds to 0° / 90° / 180° **only** — not the reverse. A common mistake is to map 0° → 2.0 ms. > 3. **Forgetting the unit conversions in duty cycle calculations:** Always write the period in **ms (not seconds)** when computing duty cycle for a servo, since the pulse width is naturally in ms. Mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical errors. > 4. **Saying `analogWrite()` can drive a servo:** This is **wrong**. `analogWrite()` produces 490 Hz PWM; the servo needs 50 Hz. The correct approach is the `Servo.h` library or manual Timer1 configuration. > 5. **Omitting the role of the potentiometer:** In any block-diagram question, explicitly state that the potentiometer is the **position feedback sensor** — examiners expect this term specifically. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Controlling Servo Motor** > - [x] A **servo motor** is a **closed-loop** actuator integrating a DC motor, gearbox, potentiometer, and control circuit. > - [x] Control is via **PWM at 50 Hz (T = 20 ms)** — **frequency is FIXED, pulse width is VARIABLE**. > - [x] Canonical pulse widths: **1.0 ms → 0°**, **1.5 ms → 90°**, **2.0 ms → 180°**. > - [x] **Linear mapping:** $\theta = 180 \times (t_{\text{pulse}} - 1.0)$ where $t_{\text{pulse}}$ is in **ms** and $\theta$ in **degrees**. > - [x] **Inverse mapping:** $t_{\text{pulse}} = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180°}$ (ms). > - [x] **Duty cycle range:** **5% (0°) to 10% (180°)** with a neutral of **7.5% (90°)**. > - [x] **Three wires:** Orange (Signal/PWM), Red (VCC/+5V), Brown (GND). > - [x] **Operating voltage:** 4.8 V to 6.0 V DC. > - [x] **`analogWrite()` is NOT suitable** for servos; use the **`Servo.h` library** or **manual Timer1** configuration. > - [x] **Timer1 (16-bit)** is used with prescaler 8; tick = **0.5 µs**; ICR1 = **40,000** for 20 ms. > - [x] **Holding torque** = the servo maintains its position against external load once the error becomes zero. > - [x] **Dead-band** of ~5–10 µs near the neutral point prevents jitter. > - [x] **IoT/Biomedical applications:** automatic medicine dispensers, prosthetic hands, robotic surgical tools, IoT-based valve control, camera gimbals, and rehabilitation devices.

topics.title: DC Motor. Sensors: Light Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Motor. Sensors: Light Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DC Motor — Definition

A **Direct Current (DC) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy supplied in the form of direct current into mechanical energy in the form of continuous rotational motion (torque), based on the principle of the **Lorentz force** experienced by a current-carrying conductor placed within a magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Actuators & Interfacing):**
> The DC motor is treated as the *primary rotary actuator* used in IoT edge devices for biomedical automation — e.g., infusion pumps, prosthetics, wheelchairs, and surgical-tool positioners. The syllabus emphasizes construction, EMF/torque equations, and PWM speed-control interfacing with microcontrollers (Arduino/ESP32/NodeMCU).

### Formal Working Principle
When a current-carrying armature conductor is placed in the uniform magnetic field produced by the field poles (stator), a force **F** acts on the conductor. The direction is given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** and the magnitude by:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

where **B** is the flux density (in Tesla), **I** is the current through the conductor (in Amperes), and **L** is the length of the conductor within the field (in meters). The cumulative tangential force across all armature conductors produces a net **torque**, causing the rotor (armature) to rotate.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pedal Boat:**
> Think of the DC motor like a paddle wheel boat. The water current (electric current `I`) pushes against paddles (conductors of length `L`) placed inside a river channel lined with magnets (magnetic field `B`). The harder the current flows, the more force on the paddles, the faster the wheel turns. The permanent river flow (supply voltage `V`) keeps the wheel moving — exactly like voltage keeps the motor spinning.

---

## 1.2 Light Sensor — Definition

A **Light Sensor** (also called a **photosensor** or **optical sensor**) is a transducer that detects the intensity, presence, or absence of visible, infrared (IR), or ultraviolet (UV) light in its surroundings and converts the optical energy into an electrical signal (voltage, current, or resistance change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Sensing Layer of IoT):**
> Light sensors form the *perception layer* of any IoT–biomedical system. They are used in pulse-oximeters (SpO₂), bilirubin monitors, sleep-cycle tracking, smart wound-care dressings, and ambient-light correction in medical imaging equipment.

### Formal Working Principle
Light sensors operate on the **photoconductive** or **photovoltaic** effect. Incident photons strike a semiconductor junction; if the photon energy exceeds the semiconductor bandgap, electron–hole pairs are generated, altering the device's electrical characteristics. The fundamental photoelectric equation is:

$$E_{photon} = h \cdot \nu = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$

where **h** is Planck's constant (**6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**), **c** is the speed of light (**3 × 10⁸ m/s**), and **λ** is the wavelength of incident light (in meters).

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Automatic Streetlamp:**
> Imagine a person standing at a street corner wearing photochromic sunglasses. As evening approaches and light intensity drops, the glasses darken (resistance changes) and the person switches on the streetlight (electrical signal is generated). A light sensor does exactly this — it "wears" a photon-sensitive material and "decides" to trigger an electrical response based on ambient brightness.

### Common Types of Light Sensors Used in KTU Curriculum

| S.No. | Sensor Type | Principle | Output |
|-------|-------------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) | Photoconductivity | Resistance change (Ω) |
| 2 | Photodiode | Photovoltaic / Photoconductive | Current (μA – mA) |
| 3 | Phototransistor | Phototransistor action | Current (mA) |
| 4 | Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell) | Photovoltaic effect | Voltage (V) |
| 5 | Ambient Light Sensor (ALS / BH1750 / TSL2561) | Spectral response | Digital I²C output (lux) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DC Motor — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Constructional Anatomy
A DC motor is composed of two principal sub-assemblies:

- **Stator (Field System):** Produces the magnetic field. May be a permanent magnet (PMDC) or an electromagnet (wound field — shunt, series, or compound).
- **Rotor (Armature):** The rotating part, comprising a laminated silicon-steel core, slots, armature winding, and a commutator with carbon brushes for unidirectional torque.

### 2.1.2 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Voltage Application** — DC supply `V` is applied across the armature terminals. Current `I_a` flows through the armature winding.
2. **Magnetic Interaction** — The field winding (or permanent magnet) establishes a uniform magnetic flux `Φ` per pole in the air gap.
3. **Force Generation** — Each conductor of the armature experiences force `F = BIL` (Lorentz force), oriented tangentially via Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.
4. **Torque Production** — The cumulative tangential forces across the armature radius `r` produce a net electromagnetic torque `T`.
5. **Rotation and Back-EMF** — As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, an EMF is induced *opposing* the supply (Lenz's Law). This is the **Back-EMF (E_b)**.
6. **Steady-State Equilibrium** — The motor reaches a stable speed when the Back-EMF plus armature resistance drop equals the applied voltage, balancing the electromagnetic and load torques.

### 2.1.3 The Two Governing Equations (Core to KTU)

**Equation A — Back-EMF / Speed Equation:**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = k \cdot \Phi \cdot N$$

In SI units with `A` parallel paths, `P` poles, `Z` conductors, and `N` rpm:

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

where `k_e` is the **voltage constant** (V·s/rad) and `ω` is angular velocity in rad/s.

**Equation B — Torque Equation:**

$$T = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot A} = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

where `k_t` is the **torque constant** (N·m/A). In SI, `k_t = k_e`.

### 2.1.4 Armature Voltage Equation (KVL of the Armature Circuit)

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

$$\Rightarrow I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V - k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}{R_a}$$

### 2.1.5 Power Flow Hierarchy

$$P_{input} = V \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a + I_a^2 \cdot R_a = P_{developed} + P_{copper\_loss}$$

$$P_{developed} = T \cdot \omega$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.6 Real-World IoT–Biomedical Utility
- **Infusion Pumps** — PMDC motor controls the peristaltic flow of IV fluids.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci-like)** — Brushless DC (BLDC) motors for precise tool positioning.
- **Prosthetic Limbs & Exoskeletons** — Geared DC motors for joint actuation.
- **Smart Wheelchairs** — Differential-drive DC motors for navigation.
- **Ventilators & CPAP Machines** — BLDC motors for blower impeller control.

---

## 2.2 Light Sensor — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Photoelectric Effect Foundation
The photoelectric effect, formalized by **Einstein (1905)**, states that incident photons liberate electrons from a metal surface only if:

$$E_{photon} = h \cdot \nu \geq W_0 \quad (\text{work function})$$

This threshold-based quantum interaction is the foundational mechanism for all semiconductor light sensors.

### 2.2.2 LDR (Photoresistor) — Working Logic

1. **Dark State** — In low light, the cadmium sulfide (CdS) lattice has very few free electrons → **high resistance (MΩ range)**.
2. **Illumination** — Photons excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, increasing charge carrier density.
3. **Conductivity Increase** — Resistance drops dramatically (kΩ range in bright light).

**Conductance vs. Illuminance relationship:**

$$G = G_{dark} + m \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

where `L` is illuminance in lux, `G` is conductance, and `m` is a material constant.

### 2.2.3 Photodiode — Working Logic

A photodiode is a reverse-biased PN-junction. Incident photons generate electron–hole pairs in the depletion region, swept apart by the electric field, producing a **photocurrent** proportional to light intensity.

$$I_{photo} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi_{photon}$$

where `R_λ` is the spectral responsivity (A/W) and `Φ_photon` is the optical power (W).

**Total current through photodiode:**

$$I = I_{photo} - I_{dark} \left( e^{\frac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right)$$

### 2.2.4 Phototransistor — Working Logic
A phototransistor amplifies the photocurrent generated at the base–collector junction. The effective responsivity is the photodiode responsivity multiplied by the transistor's DC current gain `h_fe`:

$$I_{C} = h_{fe} \cdot I_{photo}$$

This makes phototransistors typically **100×–1000× more sensitive** than bare photodiodes.

### 2.2.5 Real-World IoT–Biomedical Utility
- **Pulse Oximetry (SpO₂)** — Red (660 nm) and IR (940 nm) light passes through the fingertip; photodiode detects transmitted intensity.
- **Bilirubin Measurement (Jaundice Meter)** — Blue–green light absorption by bilirubin detected via photodiode.
- **Sleep Stage Monitoring** — Ambient light + IR sensors detect circadian cycles.
- **Smart Insulin Pens** — Optical encoders detect plunger position via slotted wheels + phototransistors.
- **Endoscopy Imaging** — CMOS/CCD image sensors capture internal visuals.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### DC Motor Master Table

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Force on conductor | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | N | Lorentz force per conductor |
| Back-EMF (constant flux) | $E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | V | Opposes applied voltage |
| Armature voltage eqn. | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V | KVL across armature |
| Electromagnetic torque | $T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | N·m | Mechanical output torque |
| Speed equation | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k_e \Phi}$ | rpm | Steady-state speed |
| Input power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$ | W | Electrical input |
| Developed power | $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical power developed |
| Copper loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$ | W | Armature heating loss |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Overall efficiency |
| Mechanical output | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | W | At shaft |
| Torque–speed slope | $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} = -\dfrac{(k_t \Phi)^2}{R_a}$ | N·m·s | Linear slope of characteristic |

### Light Sensor Master Table

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Photon energy | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J | Energy of single photon |
| Work function threshold | $\nu_0 = \dfrac{W_0}{h}$ | Hz | Threshold frequency |
| Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi$ | A | Generated by photodiode |
| Total photodiode current | $I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left(e^{qV/kT} - 1\right)$ | A | Shockley-type with light |
| LDR resistance (approx.) | $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ | Ω | γ ≈ 0.7–0.9 for CdS |
| Illuminance (lux) | $E_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{A}$ | lx | Luminous flux per unit area |
| Phototransistor gain | $I_C = h_{fe} \cdot I_{ph}$ | A | Amplified output |
| Spectral responsivity | $R_\lambda = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | Quantum efficiency based |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Back-EMF Equation of DC Motor

### Step 1: Consider one armature conductor

When an armature conductor of length `L` (m) moves with a velocity `v` (m/s) perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density `B` (T), the EMF induced in the conductor is given by:

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

### Step 2: Relate velocity to rotational speed

If the conductor is at a distance `r` (armature radius) from the rotational axis, the linear velocity is:

$$v = \omega \cdot r = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot r$$

where `N` is the rotational speed in **rpm** and `ω` is the angular velocity in **rad/s**.

### Step 3: Substitute and combine

$$e = B \cdot L \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot r$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator to introduce flux per pole `Φ` (Weber), we use the relationship:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A_{pole} = B \cdot \pi r L / \text{(pole fraction)}$$

For a `P`-pole machine with `A` parallel paths and `Z` total conductors, the total back-EMF is the sum of EMFs in series conductors per path:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A}$$

Define the machine constant:

$$k_e = \frac{Z \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

Then:

$$\boxed{E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Back-EMF is directly proportional to both the field flux `Φ` and the angular speed `ω`. This is the *physical foundation* of all DC motor speed-control techniques (flux control, armature voltage control).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Torque Equation of DC Motor

### Step 1: Force on a single conductor

The force on one armature conductor carrying current `I_a/A` in a magnetic field of flux density `B` is:

$$F = B \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L$$

### Step 2: Torque on a single conductor

The torque contribution of one conductor at radius `r` is:

$$T_{one} = F \cdot r = B \cdot \left(\frac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot r$$

### Step 3: Total torque (all conductors)

Summing over all `Z` conductors:

$$T = \frac{B \cdot L \cdot r \cdot I_a \cdot Z}{A}$$

Substituting `Φ = B × (effective pole area)` and simplifying via the machine constant:

$$T = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

Define:

$$k_t = \frac{Z \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot A} = k_e$$

Then:

$$\boxed{T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Speed vs. Torque Characteristic

### Step 1: Start from the armature voltage equation

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### Step 2: Express armature current in terms of torque

$$I_a = \frac{T}{k_t \cdot \Phi} = \frac{T}{k_e \cdot \Phi}$$

### Step 3: Express back-EMF in terms of speed

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

### Step 4: Substitute back

$$V = k_e \Phi \omega + \frac{T}{k_e \Phi} R_a$$

### Step 5: Solve for angular speed `ω`

$$k_e \Phi \omega = V - \frac{T \cdot R_a}{k_e \Phi}$$

$$\omega = \frac{V}{k_e \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(k_e \Phi)^2} T$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 - m \cdot T}$$

where:

- **No-load speed:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{k_e \Phi}$
- **Slope:** $m = \dfrac{R_a}{(k_e \Phi)^2}$
- **Stall torque** (at `ω = 0`): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V \cdot k_e \Phi}{R_a}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The torque–speed relationship is **linear** with a negative slope. This predictable behaviour is the key reason DC motors are preferred in position-control IoT applications — the controller (PID) can compute the required PWM duty cycle from a target speed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — DC Motor

**Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field resistance of 220 Ω. At no-load, it draws 5 A from the supply and runs at 1500 rpm. Compute (a) the back-EMF, (b) the armature current, and (c) the developed torque at full-load if the armature current doubles.

### Given
- `V = 220 V`
- `R_a = 0.5 Ω`
- `R_sh = 220 Ω`
- `I_L (no-load) = 5 A`
- `N_no-load = 1500 rpm`

### Solution

**Part (a) — No-Load Back-EMF**

Shunt field current:

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1 \text{ A}$$

Armature current at no-load:

$$I_{a,NL} = I_L - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ A}$$

Back-EMF:

$$E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 220 - (4)(0.5) = 220 - 2 = 218 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b) — Armature Current at Full-Load**

Given: armature current doubles →

$$I_{a,FL} = 2 \times I_{a,NL} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ A}$$

**Part (c) — Developed Torque at Full-Load**

Using the proportionality `T ∝ I_a` (since `Φ` is constant for shunt motor at fixed `V`):

$$\frac{T_{FL}}{T_{NL}} = \frac{I_{a,FL}}{I_{a,NL}}$$

Developed torque at no-load:

$$T_{NL} = \frac{E_{b,NL} \cdot I_{a,NL}}{\omega_{NL}} = \frac{218 \times 4}{2\pi \times 1500/60} = \frac{872}{157.08} = 5.55 \text{ N·m}$$

Developed torque at full-load:

$$T_{FL} = T_{NL} \times \frac{I_{a,FL}}{I_{a,NL}} = 5.55 \times 2 = 11.10 \text{ N·m}$$

### Result Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| No-load back-EMF $E_{b,NL}$ | **218 V** |
| Full-load armature current $I_{a,FL}$ | **8 A** |
| Full-load developed torque $T_{FL}$ | **11.10 N·m** |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Light Sensor (LDR Voltage Divider)

**Problem:** An LDR has a resistance of 1 MΩ in darkness and 1 kΩ in bright light. It is connected in a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ resistor. The supply is 5 V (typical Arduino/NodeMCU logic level). Compute the output voltage in (a) darkness and (b) bright light.

### Given
- `R_LDR (dark) = 1 MΩ = 10⁶ Ω`
- `R_LDR (light) = 1 kΩ = 10³ Ω`
- `R_fixed = 10 kΩ = 10⁴ Ω`
- `V_in = 5 V`

### Solution — Voltage Divider Formula

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$$

**Part (a) — Darkness**

$$V_{out,dark} = 5 \cdot \frac{10^4}{10^4 + 10^6} = 5 \cdot \frac{10^4}{1.01 \times 10^6} = 5 \times 0.0099 = 0.0495 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b) — Bright Light**

$$V_{out,light} = 5 \cdot \frac{10^4}{10^4 + 10^3} = 5 \cdot \frac{10^4}{1.1 \times 10^4} = 5 \times 0.9091 = 4.545 \text{ V}$$

### Interpretation

| Condition | $R_{LDR}$ | $V_{out}$ | ADC Reading (10-bit) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Dark | 1 MΩ | **0.0495 V** | **~10** |
| Bright | 1 kΩ | **4.545 V** | **~930** |

This wide digital swing is why the LDR is the *go-to choice* for hobby-grade IoT light sensing in KTU lab experiments.
