# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Back-EMF in a DC motor. Why is it considered the "electrical mirror" of mechanical speed?

**Model Answer:**

Back-EMF (denoted `E_b`) is the electromotive force induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor, opposing the applied supply voltage as per **Lenz's Law**. Mathematically:

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

It is called the "electrical mirror" of mechanical speed because it is **directly proportional** to the angular velocity `ω` and the field flux `Φ`. Any change in mechanical speed instantly reflects as a proportional change in `E_b`. At standstill, `ω = 0` so `E_b = 0`; at no-load, `E_b ≈ V` and the motor reaches maximum speed. This coupling is what enables **regenerative braking** in electric vehicles — turning the motor into a generator.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner Tip: Always mention Lenz's Law and the proportionality with both Φ and ω to score full marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between LDR and photodiode in terms of principle, response time, and typical biomedical application.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) | Photodiode |
|-----------|--------------------------------|------------|
| Principle | Photoconductivity (resistance change) | Photovoltaic / Photoconductive (current generation at PN-junction) |
| Response Time | Slow (50–200 ms) | Fast (nanoseconds) |
| Output | Resistance change (Ω) | Current change (μA–mA) |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | High (linear with light) |
| Biomedical Use | Ambient light detection in smart wards | Pulse oximetry (SpO₂), bilirubin meters |

LDR is preferred for slow, ambient monitoring (e.g., automatic room lighting in a hospital ward). Photodiode is preferred for fast, precision biomedical measurements (e.g., fingertip pulse oximeter).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt one full question (either OR choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**With neat diagrams and the necessary equations, explain the construction and working principle of a DC motor. Derive the expressions for back-EMF and developed torque.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - **Stator (Yoke, Pole Core, Field Winding):** Provides the magnetic field.
   - **Rotor (Armature Core, Armature Winding, Commutator, Brushes):** Carries current, rotates, and delivers mechanical power.
   - Label a Mermaid-style or hand-drawn cross-section showing the field poles, armature slots, and commutator-brush arrangement.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):**
   - Supply `V` drives armature current `I_a` through brushes and commutator.
   - The field system creates flux `Φ` per pole.
   - Force `F = BIL` acts on each conductor (Fleming's Left-Hand Rule).
   - Cumulative tangential force produces torque `T`, rotating the armature.

3. **Derivation – Back-EMF (1.5 marks):**
   - EMF per conductor: $e = BLv$
   - With $v = \omega r$ and summing for `Z` conductors, `A` paths:
   
   $$E_b = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N \cdot P}{60 \cdot A} = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

4. **Derivation – Torque (1.5 marks):**
   - Force per conductor: $F = BIL$
   - Torque per conductor: $F \cdot r$
   - Total torque:
   
   $$T = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot A} = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A 250 V DC shunt motor takes 30 A total current at no-load. The armature resistance is 0.3 Ω and the field winding resistance is 250 Ω. If the full-load armature current is 90 A, calculate: (i) No-load speed, assuming full-load speed is 1200 rpm and flux is reduced by 4% at full load. (ii) Developed torque at full-load.**

**Given:**
- `V = 250 V`
- `I_L (NL) = 30 A`
- `R_a = 0.3 Ω`
- `R_sh = 250 Ω`
- `I_a (FL) = 90 A`
- `N_FL = 1200 rpm`
- Flux reduction at FL: 4% → `Φ_FL = 0.96 Φ_NL`

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — No-Load Armature Current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{a,NL} = I_{L,NL} - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — No-Load Back-EMF**

$$E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 250 - (29)(0.3) = 250 - 8.7 = 241.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Full-Load Back-EMF**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (90)(0.3) = 250 - 27 = 223 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — No-Load Speed (using $N \propto E_b / \Phi$)**

$$\frac{N_{NL}}{N_{FL}} = \frac{E_{b,NL} / \Phi_{NL}}{E_{b,FL} / \Phi_{FL}}$$

$$N_{NL} = N_{FL} \times \frac{E_{b,NL}}{E_{b,FL}} \times \frac{\Phi_{FL}}{\Phi_{NL}} = 1200 \times \frac{241.3}{223} \times 0.96$$

$$N_{NL} = 1200 \times 1.082 \times 0.96 = 1246.5 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Developed Torque at Full-Load**

Angular speed at FL:

$$\omega_{FL} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

Developed power:

$$P_d = E_{b,FL} \times I_{a,FL} = 223 \times 90 = 20070 \text{ W}$$

Developed torque:

$$T_{FL} = \frac{P_d}{\omega_{FL}} = \frac{20070}{125.66} = 159.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| No-load speed $N_{NL}$ | **1246.5 rpm** |
| Full-load developed torque $T_{FL}$ | **159.7 N·m** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Explain the construction, working, and characteristics of a Light Dependent Resistor (LDR). Discuss its biomedical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - LDR is made of a high-resistance semiconductor material — typically **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** or Cadmium Selenide (CdSe).
   - The active surface is deposited in a zigzag pattern on a ceramic substrate to maximize the light-absorbing area.
   - Two metal contacts on either side connect to external leads.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):**
   - In darkness, the valence band is nearly full, and very few electrons are in the conduction band → resistance is in the **MΩ range**.
   - When photons strike the surface with energy $\geq$ bandgap, electrons jump to the conduction band.
   - The increased free electron density reduces the resistance to the **kΩ range** in bright light.
   - Relationship: $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ where γ ≈ 0.7–0.9.

3. **Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
   - Non-linear, inverse relationship between resistance and illuminance.
   - Slow response (50–200 ms) — not suitable for high-speed communication.
   - Spectral peak around **540 nm** (green light) for CdS.

4. **Biomedical Applications (1.5 marks):**
   - **Automatic infant incubator lighting** — adjusts brightness based on ambient light.
   - **Phototherapy units for neonatal jaundice** — light intensity feedback control.
   - **Smart ward curtains** — auto-open/close based on sunlight.
   - **Wearable sleep trackers** — detect ambient light for circadian rhythm analysis.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**A photodiode is used in a transimpedance amplifier (TIA) circuit for a pulse oximeter. The photodiode produces a photocurrent of 2.5 μA in response to red LED light (660 nm) transmitted through a fingertip. Calculate: (i) the incident optical power, assuming a responsivity of 0.35 A/W, and (ii) the output voltage of the TIA if the feedback resistor is 100 kΩ.**

**Given:**
- `I_ph = 2.5 μA = 2.5 × 10⁻⁶ A`
- `R_λ = 0.35 A/W`
- `R_f (TIA feedback) = 100 kΩ = 10⁵ Ω`

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Optical Power**

The responsivity relation:

$$I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi_{optical}$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi_{optical} = \frac{I_{ph}}{R_\lambda} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.35} = 7.143 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 7.14 \text{ μW}$$

**Part (ii) — TIA Output Voltage**

For a transimpedance amplifier, the output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = -I_{ph} \cdot R_f$$

The negative sign indicates inverting configuration; magnitude:

$$V_{out} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^5 = 0.25 \text{ V} = 250 \text{ mV}$$

**Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Incident optical power $\Phi$ | **7.14 μW** |
| TIA output voltage $V_{out}$ | **−250 mV** (inverting) or **+250 mV** magnitude |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a real pulse oximeter, the ratio of this red signal to the IR signal (940 nm) is used to compute SpO₂. The TIA amplifies the tiny photodiode current to a usable voltage for the MCU's ADC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `E_b` and `V`:** Students often write $E_b = V$ instead of $E_b = V - I_a R_a$. Always subtract the armature drop.
> 2. **Units of `k_e` and `k_t`:** Examiner will deduct 1 mark if you write $k_e = k_t$ without specifying SI units (V·s/rad = N·m/A).
> 3. **LDR vs. Photodiode confusion:** LDR changes *resistance*; photodiode generates *current*. Don't mix the outputs.
> 4. **Speed equation direction:** In a generator, $E_b$ *adds* to `V`; in a motor, `E_b` *opposes* `V`. Sign convention is critical.
> 5. **Spectral peak omission:** Writing "LDR detects light" without specifying the spectral peak wavelength will cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **PWM ≠ DC voltage:** Some students describe PWM as a variable DC supply. Explain that PWM is a fixed-amplitude, variable-duty-cycle square wave, and the effective average is what controls speed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DC Motor Principle:** Conversion of electrical energy to mechanical energy via Lorentz force on current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field.
- ✅ **Back-EMF Equation:** $E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ — directly proportional to flux and speed.
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ — directly proportional to flux and armature current.
- ✅ **Armature Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — foundational KVL relationship.
- ✅ **In SI units:** $k_e = k_t$ (both have same numerical value, different units).
- ✅ **DC Motor Types:** PMDC, Shunt, Series, Compound — each with distinct speed-torque characteristics.
- ✅ **Speed Control Methods:** (1) Armature voltage control, (2) Flux (field) control, (3) Series-parallel control.
- ✅ **PWM Control:** The IoT-preferred method using L298N/DRV8871 H-bridge drivers with microcontroller PWM pins.
- ✅ **Light Sensor Principle:** Photoconductive (LDR) or Photovoltaic (photodiode/phototransistor) conversion of optical energy.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** Photon energy $E = hc/\lambda$ — relates wavelength to quantum energy.
- ✅ **LDR Spectral Peak:** ~540 nm (CdS); high dark resistance (MΩ), low bright resistance (kΩ).
- ✅ **Photodiode Spectral Range:** 400–1100 nm (silicon), peak ~900 nm — ideal for IR biomedical use.
- ✅ **IoT–Biomedical Linkage:** DC motors actuate (pumps, prosthetics); Light sensors perceive (oximetry, phototherapy, ambient awareness).
- ✅ **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):** Converts tiny photodiode currents to usable voltages for MCU ADC input.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Questions:** "Derive back-EMF and torque equations", "Compare LDR and photodiode", "Solve numerical on shunt motor at NL/FL".
- ✅ **Standard Constants to Memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Detection Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Detection Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MOTION DETECTION SENSORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Detection Sensor:** An electronic device that converts physical movement of an object, person, or animal within its field of view (FoV) into a corresponding electrical signal. The sensor detects a change in position, velocity, acceleration, or thermal/electromagnetic signature of a target and produces a measurable output (analog voltage, digital HIGH/LOW, or data packet in IOT nodes).

In the context of **PEEBT632 — IOT & Biomedical Applications**, motion detection sensors form a critical sub-system of the **perception layer** of the IOT stack. They enable context-aware systems such as patient fall detection, gait analysis, occupancy-driven HVAC control in hospitals, intruder alarms, and activity monitoring in Ambient Assisted Living (AAL) environments.

### 1.2 Standard Classification (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

| Category | Operating Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Infrared (PIR)** | Detects change in IR radiation emitted by warm bodies | HC-SR501, AMN31111 |
| **Microwave (Doppler Radar)** | Reflects microwave (GHz) signals; uses Doppler shift | HB100, RCWL-0516 |
| **Ultrasonic** | Emits acoustic waves above 20 kHz; measures time-of-flight | HC-SR04 |
| **Tomographic** | Detects disturbances in radio frequency field | Xandem, Siemens HMS |
| **Vibration / Accelerometer** | Detects mechanical acceleration on a structure | ADXL345, MPU6050 |
| **Optical / Camera-based** | Computer-vision frame differencing | OpenCV + Raspberry Pi |
| **PIR + Microwave hybrid** | Dual-technology with AND-logic to reduce false alarms | Bosch Blue Line |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Watchman"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a watchman standing at the door of a building at night. He cannot see in the dark, but he can *feel* a person walk past because (a) he senses body heat on his skin, (b) hears footsteps, and (c) the air pushes against him. A motion detection sensor behaves identically:
> 1. **PIR** = sensing body heat (temperature change)
> 2. **Ultrasonic** = listening to echoes of chirps
> 3. **Microwave** = feeling the radio-wave "air push" (Doppler)
> 4. **Vibration** = feeling footsteps through the floor
>
> Just as the watchman converts these sensations into a bell ring, the sensor converts them into an **electrical trigger** that an IOT microcontroller (e.g., ESP32) can process and forward to the cloud via MQTT/HTTP.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Detection Range ($R$):** Maximum distance at which motion is reliably detected — typically **3 m to 12 m** for indoor sensors.
> - **Field of View (FoV):** Angular coverage in degrees — commonly **90°, 110°, 180°, or 360°**.
> - **Response Time ($t_r$):** Time delay between physical motion and output signal — typically **< 0.5 s**.
> - **False-Trigger Rate:** Unintended detections due to noise, pets, sunlight, or HVAC drafts.
> - **Power Consumption ($P$):** Critical for battery-powered IOT nodes — PIR ≈ **< 1 mW**, Microwave ≈ **< 50 mW**.
> - **Operating Frequency ($f$):** Microwave sensors operate at **2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz, 10.525 GHz**; Ultrasonic at **40 kHz** standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — One Concept, Three Technologies

### 2.1.1 Passive Infrared (PIR) Sensor

A PIR sensor contains **two pyroelectric slots** behind a Fresnel lens. The human body emits infrared radiation in the wavelength band of approximately **8 µm to 14 µm** (peak ≈ 9.5 µm, by Planck's law at skin temperature 305 K). When a warm body crosses from one slot to the other, the **differential IR flux** generates a voltage across the pyroelectric crystal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Passive"?** The sensor does NOT emit any signal — it only *receives* IR radiation. This makes it ultra-low-power and ideal for battery IOT nodes.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**
1. Ambient IR from static background cancels out across the two slots.
2. A moving warm object creates an **asymmetric IR exposure** between the two slots.
3. The difference is amplified by a low-noise BISS0001 / AS312 op-amp stage.
4. Output is a digital HIGH pulse of configurable duration (typically 3 s to 300 s).
5. Microcontroller (e.g., ESP32) detects rising edge and publishes MQTT message.

### 2.1.2 Microwave Doppler Sensor (HB100)

The HB100 module contains a **Gunn diode oscillator** transmitting a Continuous Wave (CW) at **10.525 GHz** (X-band). When this wave reflects off a moving target, the **Doppler frequency shift** appears on the reflected signal. A mixer inside the module multiplies transmitted and reflected signals to extract the **difference frequency (beat note)**.

The fundamental Doppler shift equation is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0 \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$$

where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler shift frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = target velocity (m/s)
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency (Hz)
- $\theta$ = angle between target motion and the radar line-of-sight
- $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)

### 2.1.3 Ultrasonic Sensor (HC-SR04)

The HC-SR04 emits a **40 kHz burst** of 8 pulses. The wave travels, reflects off an object, and returns. The sensor measures the **Time of Flight (ToF)**:

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = distance to object (m)
- $v_{sound}$ = speed of sound in air ($\approx 343$ m/s at 20°C)
- $t_{echo}$ = round-trip echo time (s)

The speed of sound varies with temperature as:

$$v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

where $T$ is air temperature in degrees Celsius.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-ready formula reference** students should memorize for ESE questions on motion detection.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift for monostatic radar (microwave motion sensor) | Hz |
| 2 | $v_{rel} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_d}{2 \cos\theta}$ | Velocity from observed Doppler shift | m/s |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ | Distance from ultrasonic time-of-flight | m |
| 4 | $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 T$ | Temperature correction for speed of sound | m/s |
| 5 | $P_{rx} = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Monostatic radar received power (Radar range equation) | W |
| 6 | $R_{max} = \left(\dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}}\right)^{1/4}$ | Maximum detection range of a motion sensor | m |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{p \cdot D \cdot \lambda}{2 \cdot v_{sound}}$ | Quality factor of ultrasonic transducer (bandwidth relation) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Wavelength of EM/acoustic wave | m |
| 9 | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio for detection threshold | dB |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{NEP}{\eta \cdot A}$ | Minimum resolvable temperature difference (PIR, NEP = noise-equivalent power) | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always include **$\cos\theta = 1$** for the maximum Doppler case (target moving directly toward/away from the radar). Examiners award full marks only when the angular dependence is explicitly stated.

### 2.3 Real-World Biomedical / IOT Utility

- **Patient fall detection** in geriatric wards using PIR ceiling arrays (e.g., Siemens HMS).
- **Sleep-stage monitoring** using Doppler radar placed under the mattress (Soliom S100).
- **Apnea detection** via chest-wall motion via mmWave FMCW radar (IWR6843, 60 GHz).
- **Smart wheelchair** obstacle-avoidance using HC-SR04 ultrasonic array.
- **Hospital hand-hygiene compliance** using PIR-triggered dispensers.
- **Smart-pillow** apnea monitor with integrated accelerometer (MPU6050).
- **Intrusion detection** in hospital pharmacy (drug storage) using dual-tech PIR + microwave.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Doppler Frequency from Reflected Microwave Signal

We start with a microwave oscillator radiating a Continuous Wave (CW) of frequency $f_0$. The transmitted electric field at the antenna is:

$$E_{tx}(t) = E_0 \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$$

**Step 1 — Field at the moving target.**
Assume the target is moving with radial velocity $v$ directly toward the radar. By the relativistic (and for $v \ll c$, classical) Doppler effect, the wave received by the target is frequency-shifted to:

$$f' = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right)$$

The target reflects this wave as a re-radiated field.

**Step 2 — Field received back at the radar.**
The moving target now acts as a moving source. The reflected signal reaching the radar experiences a second Doppler shift:

$$f_{rx} = f' \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right) = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — First-order approximation.**
Since $v \ll c$, we expand $\left(1 + \dfrac{v}{c}\right)^2 \approx 1 + \dfrac{2v}{c}$, neglecting higher-order terms in $(v/c)^2$:

$$f_{rx} \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2v}{c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Doppler beat frequency.**
The mixer in the HB100 multiplies the transmitted and received signals, and after low-pass filtering retains only the difference frequency:

$$f_d = f_{rx} - f_0 = f_0 \cdot \frac{2v}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Angular generalization.**
When the velocity vector is not along the radar line of sight but at an angle $\theta$, only the radial component $v \cos\theta$ contributes. Therefore:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$

This is the **canonical Doppler equation** students must derive on the board.

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution check:** With $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz, $v = 1$ m/s (typical human walking speed), $\theta = 0$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot 10.525 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 70.17 \text{ Hz}$$

The HB100 outputs a low-frequency audio signal of ~70 Hz when a person walks toward the sensor. The ESP32 can sample this via its ADC and trigger a motion event.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ultrasonic Distance with Temperature Compensation

**Step 1 — Round-trip time of flight.**
A 40 kHz burst is emitted at $t = 0$. It travels distance $d$ to the object, reflects, and returns. Total path is $2d$. At sound speed $v_{sound}$:

$$2d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate distance $d$:**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature correction.**
Substitute $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 T$:

$$d(T) = \frac{(331 + 0.6 T) \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**
At $T = 30$°C, the HC-SR04 reports $t_{echo} = 5.83$ ms. Compute $d$:

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

$$d = \frac{349 \cdot 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.03467}{2} \approx 1.017 \text{ m}$$

Without temperature compensation (using 343 m/s), the error would be:

$$\Delta d = \frac{(349 - 343) \cdot 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \approx 0.0175 \text{ m} = 1.75 \text{ cm}$$

This is the **error magnitude** typically asked in KTU numerical questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Radar Maximum Range (Detection Threshold)

We begin with the **monostatic radar range equation**, which describes the received signal power as a function of transmitted power, antenna gains, wavelength, target Radar Cross Section (RCS), and range $R$:

$$P_{rx} = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4}$$

For a monostatic radar (single antenna for Tx/Rx) with $G_t = G_r = G$:

$$P_{rx} = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4}$$

**Step 1 — Detection condition.**
Detection is possible only if the received power exceeds the receiver's minimum sensitivity $S_{min}$:

$$\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot R^4} \geq S_{min}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R$:**

$$R^4 \leq \frac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the fourth root:**

$$R_{max} = \left(\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min}}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation (a KTU favorite):**
$R_{max}$ scales as $\lambda^{1/2}$ (better range with longer wavelength) and as $P_{tx}^{1/4}$ (so quadrupling the transmit power only doubles the range). This explains why 10 GHz X-band sensors are preferred for short-range indoor motion detection (a few meters), while S-band (2-4 GHz) is used for perimeter radar (100s of meters).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Motion Sensor

| Application Scenario | Recommended Sensor | Justification | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Room occupancy detection (home) | PIR (HC-SR501) | Low cost, low power, simple digital output | 3–7 m |
| Bathroom fall detection (elderly) | mmWave FMCW radar | Penetrates steam, detects micro-movements | 2–5 m |
| Hospital corridor counting | PIR array + ESP32-CAM | Wide FoV, low false trigger | 8–12 m |
| Smart wheelchair obstacle | Ultrasonic HC-SR04 | Precise distance, immune to lighting | 0.02–4 m |
| Outdoor perimeter (farm, gate) | Microwave HB100 + PIR | Weather-resistant, weather-independent | 8–15 m |
| Bed-exit alarm (sleep lab) | Triboelectric / accelerometer | Body-coupled vibration detection | Contact |
| Pharmacy cold-room intrusion | Dual-tech PIR + microwave | AND-logic eliminates HVAC false triggers | 6–10 m |
| Heart-rate / respiration radar | 24 GHz / 60 GHz FMCW | Sub-mm chest-wall displacement detection | 0.5–2 m |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic IOT Motion Detection Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Target Object Human Animal] -->|Emits Reflects IR Microwave Acoustic| nodeB[Motion Sensor PIR Microwave Ultrasonic]
    nodeB -->|Analog Differential Signal| nodeC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    nodeC -->|Cleaned Analog| nodeD[Threshold Comparator ADC]
    nodeD -->|Digital HIGH LOW| nodeE[Microcontroller ESP32 STM32]
    nodeE -->|UART I2C SPI| nodeF[Wireless Module WiFi BLE LoRa]
    nodeF -->|MQTT HTTP| nodeG[Cloud Server AWS Azure]
    nodeG -->|Push Notification| nodeH[Smartphone Dashboard]

```

## 4.2 PIR Sensor Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Warm Object IR Emission 8 to 14 um] --> node2[Fresnel Lens Segmented Array]
    node2 --> node3[Two Pyroelectric Slots Slot A and Slot B]

    node4 -->|Amplified Signal| node5[Window Comparator + Threshold]
    node5 -->|Pulse Output 3 to 300 s| node6[Digital Output Pin HIGH LOW]

```

    nodeD -->|Reflected Wave with Doppler Shift| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Receives Echo| nodeE[Mixer Multiplies Tx and Rx]
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeE -->|IF Beat Note fd| nodeF[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    nodeF -->|Audio Band Signal| nodeG[Comparator Threshold]

```

    node2 -->|Point Cloud XYZ Doppler| node3[Edge Processor ESP32 S3]
    node3 -->|Feature Extraction Gait Speed Trajectory| node4[Local ML Classifier TFLite Micro]
    node4 -->|Fall Detected| node5[WiFi Module]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEEBT632]**
Differentiate between **active** and **passive** motion detection sensors. Give one biomedical example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Signal emission | Emits its own energy (microwave / ultrasonic / IR pulse) | Does not emit; only detects ambient energy |
| Power consumption | Higher (drives Tx stage) | Very low (micro-watts typical) |
| Example | Ultrasonic HC-SR04 in smart wheelchair | PIR HC-SR501 for patient occupancy in ward |
| Range metric | Determined by transmit power and antenna gain | Limited by thermal contrast and FoV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key line for full marks:** "Active sensors contain a transmitter; passive sensors contain only a receiver. PIR is passive; HC-SR04 is active." (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contrast, 1 mark for biomedical examples.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEBT632]**
A PIR sensor has a detection range of **7 m** and a Field of View of **110°**. State two limitations of PIR sensors in a hospital environment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Cannot detect stationary patients** — PIR requires *change* in IR flux. A sleeping patient produces no trigger even if they need attention. (1.5 marks)
2. **False triggers from HVAC drafts and sunlight** through windows — warm air movement or direct sunlight creates IR flux variation. (1.5 marks)
3. (Bonus acceptable answer) — Line-of-sight requirement; cannot sense through walls or glass.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEEBT632, Module 3, Q8(a) or 8(b)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Passive Infrared (PIR) motion sensor**. Discuss its role in an IOT-based hospital occupancy monitoring system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microwave Doppler motion sensor operates at **10.525 GHz**. A patient walks toward the sensor at a speed of **1.2 m/s** along the line of sight ($\theta = 0°$). Calculate the **Doppler shift frequency** and discuss how the ESP32 can be interfaced to it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

The diagram below shows the components (already provided in SECTION 4.2). The student must draw: Warm Body → Fresnel Lens → Two Pyroelectric Elements → Differential Amplifier (BISS0001) → Window Comparator → Output → ESP32.

**Working explanation (3 marks):**
1. All objects above absolute zero emit infrared radiation; the human body at 305 K peaks at 9.5 µm.
2. The Fresnel lens focuses IR onto two opposing pyroelectric slots. Static background cancels.
3. Crossing motion produces a positive then negative differential pulse — the BISS0001 converts this to a clean digital HIGH for a preset duration.
4. The ESP32 GPIO interrupt wakes the MCU from deep sleep, publishes an MQTT message, and returns to sleep.

**IOT occupancy monitoring role (2 marks):**
- Patient in/out counts in wards.
- Energy-saving HVAC control (lights/fan OFF when room empty).
- Combined with BLE beacons for individual patient tracking.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz $= 10.525 \times 10^9$ Hz, $v = 1.2$ m/s, $\theta = 0°$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Doppler shift formula:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Substitution:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 10.525 \times 10^9 \cdot 1}{3 \times 10^8}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$f_d = \frac{2.4 \cdot 10.525 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$= \frac{2.4 \cdot 10.525}{3} \times 10^{9-8}$$

$$= 0.8 \cdot 10.525 \times 10^{1}$$

$$= 8.42 \times 10^{1}$$

$$\boxed{f_d \approx 84.2 \text{ Hz}}$$

**ESP32 interfacing (3 marks):**
1. HB100 IF output → ESP32 GPIO 34 (ADC1_CH6, input only, no internal pull-up).
2. Use `analogRead()` at 1 kHz sampling; compute FFT with `arduinoFFT` library to extract peak frequency.
3. If $f_d$ exceeds threshold (e.g., 30 Hz) for >100 ms, declare motion.
4. Publish JSON payload `{"sensor":"HB100","fd":84.2,"motion":true}` to MQTT broker.
5. Return ESP32 to deep sleep between detections for battery efficiency.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEBT632, Module 3, Q9(a) or 9(b)]**

**(a)** Derive the **Doppler range equation** for a microwave motion sensor and explain its significance in IOT design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor** is used in a smart wheelchair to detect obstacles. The temperature of the operating environment is **35°C** and the echo time is **8.5 ms**. Calculate the distance to the obstacle, the percentage error if temperature compensation is omitted, and discuss the role of ultrasonic sensing in biomedical IOT. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Derivation (5 marks):** as presented in Section 3.3 of these notes, the student should write:
1. State the monostatic radar equation: $P_{rx} = \dfrac{P_{tx} G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$.
2. Apply detection condition $P_{rx} \geq S_{min}$.
3. Solve for $R_{max}$:

$$R_{max} = \left(\frac{P_{tx} \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min}}\right)^{1/4}$$

**Significance in IOT design (2 marks):**
- Predicts sensor range *before deployment* — critical for IOT planning.
- Shows $R \propto P_{tx}^{1/4}$ — energy-efficient IOT design favours higher antenna gain over raw power.
- Determines node density in smart hospital layouts (e.g., one sensor per 5 m corridor).

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $T = 35°C$, $t_{echo} = 8.5$ ms $= 8.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute sound speed at 35°C:**

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute corrected distance:**

$$d_{corrected} = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2} = \frac{352 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.992}{2} = 1.496 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Uncorrected distance (using 343 m/s, the 20°C reference):**

$$d_{uncorrected} = \frac{343 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.9155}{2} = 1.4578 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Absolute error:**

$$\Delta d = 1.496 - 1.4578 = 0.0382 \text{ m} \approx 3.82 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{0.0382}{1.496} \times 100 \approx 2.55\%$$

**Role in biomedical IOT (2 marks):**
- Smart wheelchair obstacle avoidance.
- Patient bed-exit detection (toe-zone ultrasonic).
- Drug-dispensing bin level sensing.
- Non-contact gesture recognition in sterile surgical environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in Doppler problems** — KTU examiners explicitly test the angular dependence. If the question says "at an angle of 30°", the answer is zero if you write 84.2 Hz.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion** — Frequency in GHz must be converted to Hz before plugging into formulas. $10.525$ GHz = $10.525 \times 10^9$ Hz, NOT $10.525 \times 10^6$ Hz. A single missing factor of 1000 = **0/7 marks**.
> 3. **Ultrasonic: dividing by 2 once, not twice** — Round-trip distance is $2d$. Students frequently write $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}$ (no division by 2). **Lose 1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Not mentioning temperature compensation** in ultrasonic numerical — always state the temperature explicitly and compute $v_{sound}$ from it.
> 5. **Block diagrams without labels** — every block must be labeled (Fresnel lens, BISS0001, ESP32, MQTT, etc.). Unlabeled boxes get **zero marks** in 2D diagrams.
> 6. **Confusing PIR and Ultrasonic** — PIR is passive and works on heat; Ultrasonic is active and works on sound. Mixing them up = 0 for the comparison question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**
>
> ✅ Motion Detection Sensors convert **physical movement** into **electrical signals** for IOT systems.
>
> ✅ Three dominant technologies in the syllabus: **PIR (passive, IR), Microwave (active, Doppler), Ultrasonic (active, ToF)**.
>
> ✅ **PIR** uses pyroelectric effect + Fresnel lens; cannot detect stationary warm objects; ultra-low power.
>
> ✅ **Microwave Doppler** uses $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$; works through non-metallic walls; weather-resistant.
>
> ✅ **Ultrasonic** uses $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$; immune to light/smoke; speed of sound is temperature-dependent.
>
> ✅ Doppler shift for 10.525 GHz at 1 m/s ≈ **70.17 Hz** — memorize this benchmark value.
>
> ✅ Speed of sound at 20°C = **343 m/s**; use $v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6T$ for any other temperature.
>
> ✅ Radar range equation: $R_{max} \propto (P_{tx})^{1/4}$ — quadrupling power only doubles the range.
>
> ✅ **Hybrid dual-tech sensors** (PIR + microwave) use AND-logic to suppress false triggers in hospital HVAC environments.
>
> ✅ Biomedical applications: **fall detection, sleep apnea, gait analysis, wheelchair obstacle avoidance, occupancy-based HVAC**.
>
> ✅ IOT pipeline: Sensor → Signal Conditioning → MCU → Wireless (WiFi/BLE/LoRa) → Cloud → Dashboard/Alert.
>
> ✅ Always state **units** for every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
>
> ✅ For angular Doppler problems, the maximum case is at $\theta = 0°$ (cos θ = 1).
>
> ✅ mmWave FMCW radar (60 GHz) is the **modern trend** for non-contact vital sign monitoring in biomedical IOT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Bluetooth Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wireless Bluetooth Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Bluetooth Sensors** are short-range, low-power, radio-frequency (RF) transducer nodes that utilize the **Bluetooth protocol stack** (governed by the **IEEE 802.15.1** standard) to wirelessly transmit physiological, biochemical, or environmental data from a sensor front-end to a central aggregator (smartphone, gateway, or hub) in **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Biomedical** ecosystems.

The Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) defines the physical (PHY) and media access control (MAC) layers, while upper-stack profiles (GATT, GAP, etc.) define the semantic meaning of the transmitted data. For biomedical applications, **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — Bluetooth 4.0/5.0/5.2** is the dominant variant due to its coin-cell battery compatibility and duty-cycled transmission paradigm.

| Bluetooth Class | Max Power Output | Typical Range | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Class 1 | **100 mW (20 dBm)** | ~**100 m** | Industrial / Long-range |
| Class 2 | **2.5 mW (4 dBm)** | ~**10 m** | Wearables / Biomedical |
| Class 3 | **1 mW (0 dBm)** | ~**1 m** | Coin-cell sensors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie of the Body"

Imagine a **hospital ward** where every patient wears a tiny thermometer that needs to send its reading to the nurse's station without any wires. A **wireless Bluetooth sensor** is essentially a **walkie-talkie** strapped to the patient's skin — but unlike a walkie-talkie, it:
1. **Hops between 79 different frequencies** 1,600 times per second (FHSS), so two devices never "talk over" each other.
2. **Sleeps most of the time** (BLE duty cycle ≈ 0.01%–1%) to save the tiny coin-cell battery.
3. **Pairs only with a trusted device** (the nurse's tablet) using a cryptographic handshake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Bluetooth is *not* Wi-Fi. Wi-Fi is for high-bandwidth, always-on streaming (e.g., video). Bluetooth is for **low-bandwidth, event-driven, battery-critical** telemetry — which is exactly what biomedical sensors need.

## 1.3 Operating Frequency & Channel Architecture

- **ISM Band:** 2.4 GHz (Industrial, Scientific, Medical — license-free worldwide)
- **Classic Bluetooth:** 79 channels, each **1 MHz** wide, spanning **2402 MHz to 2480 MHz**.
- **BLE:** 40 channels, each **2 MHz** wide (37 data + 3 advertising).
- **Hop Rate:** **1600 hops/second** (Classic) — frequency-hopping spread spectrum (FHSS).

> [!TIP]
> The frequency hopping is what makes Bluetooth robust against the **2.4 GHz interference** caused by Wi-Fi routers and microwave ovens sharing the same band.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack — The "Layer Cake"

Bluetooth is organized in a **layered protocol architecture**, similar to the OSI model. Each layer has a specific job:

| Layer | Function | Biomedical Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Radio (PHY)** | 2.4 GHz modulation (GFSK), frequency hopping | Physical transmission of ECG/SpO2 data |
| **Baseband (MAC)** | Time-division duplexing, packet framing | Manages channel access; avoids collision |
| **Link Manager (LMP)** | Pairing, authentication, link supervision | Ensures only authorized devices connect |
| **HCI (Host-Controller Interface)** | Standardized bus (UART/USB) between chip and MCU | Decouples radio hardware from application |
| **L2CAP** | Logical Link Control & Adaptation — segmentation/reassembly | Multiplexes multiple sensor data streams |
| **RFCOMM** | Serial port emulation over Bluetooth | Legacy medical device compatibility |
| **SDP / GATT** | Service discovery (Classic) / Attribute Protocol (BLE) | Discovers "Heart Rate Service" etc. |
| **Application Profiles** | HDP (Health Device Profile), HRP, GLP, BLP | Defines semantic meaning of medical data |

## 2.2 Piconet vs. Scatternet — The Topology of Trust

- **Piconet:** A master device + up to **7 active slaves** (255 parked) sharing the same hopping sequence.
- **Scatternet:** Multiple interconnected piconets, where a slave in one piconet acts as the master (or slave) in another.

```
Piconet (1 Master + ≤7 Slaves)
        Master
       /  |  \
    S1   S2   S3 ... S7

Scatternet = Piconet_A ∪ Piconet_B  (shared node)
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear in Part A and Part B of KTU ESE papers.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)** | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_t, P_r$ in W; $\lambda$ in m |
| **Symbol Energy / Noise (BLE)** | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \text{SNR} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $R_b$ = bit rate (bps) |
| **BLE Data Throughput** | $R_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\text{Useful payload}}{\text{Connection interval} \cdot \text{MTU size}}$ | bits per second |
| **Average Current Draw (BLE)** | $I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1-D) + I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D$ | $D$ = duty cycle (0 to 1) |
| **Battery Life** | $T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}} \cdot V_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}} \cdot V_{\text{load}}}$ | $C$ in mAh |
| **Hop Period (Classic)** | $T_{\text{hop}} = \dfrac{1}{1600} = 625\ \mu s$ | — |
| **Channel Spacing (BLE)** | $\Delta f = 2\ \text{MHz}$ | — |
| **Bit Period (BLE 1 Mbps)** | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{1 \times 10^6} = 1\ \mu s$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for KTU Valuation:** Inside markdown tables, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid`, never as the raw `|` pipe character, to avoid breaking the table rendering engine.

## 2.4 BLE — The Biomedical Workhorse

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** was introduced in Bluetooth **4.0** (2010) and is the *de-facto* standard for modern IoT-biomedical sensors. Its distinguishing features:

1. **40 channels** (vs. 79) → faster discovery, lower power.
2. **Connection intervals** of 7.5 ms up to 4 s → sensor sleeps in between.
3. **Peak current ≈ 15 mA** for < 3 ms per event.
4. **GATT-based services** (e.g., Heart Rate Service 0x180D, Glucose Service 0x1808).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGM):** Dexcom G7, Abbott FreeStyle Libre 3 → BLE to phone.
- **ECG patches:** iRhythm Zio → BLE telemetry.
- **Pulse oximeters:** Nonin 3230 → BLE Health Thermometer Profile.
- **Smart inhalers, smart pill bottles, fall-detection pendants.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss (Receiving-End Voltage at a Bluetooth Sensor)

**Problem:** A Class-2 Bluetooth transmitter (Pt = 2.5 mW, 2.45 GHz) is located **5 m** from a wearable BLE receiver with antenna gains Gt = Gr = 1.8 dBi. Compute the received power in dBm.

**Step 1 — Convert transmit power to dBm:**

$$
P_t(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}}{1\ \text{mW}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.5) = 3.98\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert antenna gains to linear scale:**

$$
G_t = G_r = 10^{1.8/10} = 1.514
$$

**Step 3 — Wavelength of the 2.45 GHz carrier:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.45 \times 10^9} = 0.1224\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Friis equation in dB form:**

$$
P_r(\text{dBm}) = P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) + G_r(\text{dBi}) - \text{FSPL}(\text{dB})
$$

**Step 5 — Compute FSPL using d = 0.005 km, f = 2450 MHz:**

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(0.005) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.44
$$

Substitute the logarithms:

$$
= 20(-2.301) + 20(3.389) + 32.44 = -46.02 + 67.78 + 32.44
$$

$$
\text{FSPL} = 54.20\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Friis dB equation:**

$$
P_r = 3.98 + 1.8 + 1.8 - 54.20 = -46.62\ \text{dBm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Notice that a Class-2 transmitter (≈ 4 dBm) loses more than **54 dB** over just 5 m of free space. This shows why Bluetooth receivers must have a sensitivity of **−70 dBm to −90 dBm** to function reliably.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Current & Battery Life of a BLE Pulse Oximeter

**Problem:** A pulse-oximeter BLE sensor has I_sleep = 1 µA, I_tx (during a 3 ms transmission) = 15 mA, and transmits once every **1 s**. The coin-cell battery is **CR2032, 3 V, 220 mAh**. Compute average current and battery life.

**Step 1 — Compute the duty cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{period}}} = \frac{3\ \text{ms}}{1000\ \text{ms}} = 0.003
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the duty-cycled current equation:**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - D) + I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D
$$

$$
= (1 \times 10^{-6})(0.997) + (15 \times 10^{-3})(0.003)
$$

$$
= 9.97 \times 10^{-7} + 4.5 \times 10^{-5} = 4.60 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \approx 46\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the battery-life equation:**

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{220\ \text{mAh}}{0.046\ \text{mA}} = 4782.6\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:**

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \frac{4782.6}{24} \approx 199.3\ \text{days}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU viva:** A pulse-oximeter that "puffs" only **3 ms per second** lasts nearly **7 months** on a coin cell — this is precisely why BLE dominates medical wearables.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classic Bluetooth vs. BLE vs. Wi-Fi (Sensor Use-Case Comparison)

| Parameter | Classic BT (BR/EDR) | BLE (4.0/5.x) | Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Frequency Band | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz |
| Channels | 79 | 40 | 11–13 / 25+ |
| Max Data Rate | **1–3 Mbps** | **1–2 Mbps** (LE 2M PHY) | **54–600 Mbps** |
| Peak Current | 30–40 mA | **5–15 mA** | 200–500 mA |
| Sleep Current | ~50 µA | **< 1 µA** | ~100 µA |
| Connection Setup | 1–3 s | **< 3 ms** (advertising) | > 5 s |
| Range (typical) | 10 m | 10–100 m (LE Coded) | 30–100 m |
| Ideal for Sensor? | ✗ | **✓ (Best fit)** | ✗ (power-hungry) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "Bluetooth is faster than Wi-Fi." It is *not*. Bluetooth is *lower power*; Wi-Fi is *higher bandwidth*. The exam expects this distinction.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Throughput of a BLE Heart-Rate Service

**Given:** Connection interval = 100 ms, MTU = 23 bytes (default), HR data packet = 20 bytes (payload). Compute effective throughput.

**Step 1 — Useful bits per connection event:**

$$
\text{Data} = 20\ \text{bytes} \times 8 = 160\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per second:**

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{160\ \text{bits}}{0.1\ \text{s}} = 1600\ \text{bps} = 1.6\ \text{kbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 72 bpm heart rate requires only ≈ 1 bit/beat — a 1.6 kbps link is **massively over-provisioned** for a wearable HR sensor, leaving room for SpO2, temperature, accelerometer, and battery-level telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wireless Bluetooth Sensor Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSE["Physical Sensor\nECG, SpO2, Temp, Accelerometer"] --> AFE["Analog Front End\nAmplifier, Filter, ADC"]
    AFE --> MCU["Microcontroller Unit\nARM Cortex-M0/M4"]
    MCU --> RADIO["BT Radio SoC\nCC2640, nRF52840, DA14580"]
    RADIO --> ANT["2.4 GHz Antenna\nCeramic Chip, F-antenna"]
    MCU -.Power Mgmt.-> BATT["Coin Cell / Li-Po\nCR2032, 3 V"]
    BATT --> LDO["LDO Regulator\n1.8 V / 3.3 V"]
    LDO --> MCU
    LDO --> RADIO
    RADIO <-- "BLE Advertising" --> GATEWAY["Smartphone / Hub Gateway"]
    GATEWAY --> CLOUD["Cloud / Hospital Server"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **physical sensor** captures a biomedical signal → **AFE** conditions it → **MCU** runs the application → **BT radio** transmits via the **antenna** → the **battery + LDO** powers the whole stack. The dotted arrow indicates a *control relationship* (the MCU manages power modes of the radio).

---

## 4.2 BLE Protocol Stack — Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application Layer\nHeart Rate, Glucose, SpO2 Profile"] --> GATT["GATT\nGeneric Attribute Profile"]
    APP --> GAP["GAP\nGeneric Access Profile"]
    GATT --> ATT["ATT\nAttribute Protocol"]
    ATT --> L2CAP2["L2CAP\nLogical Link Control"]
    GAP --> L2CAP2
    L2CAP2 --> HCI2["HCI\nHost Controller Interface"]
    HCI2 --> LL["Link Layer\nConnection Events, Channel Map"]
    LL --> PHY2["Physical Layer\n2.4 GHz GFSK Modulation, 40 Channels"]
```

---

## 4.3 Piconet and Scatternet Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PICONETA["Piconet A"]
        M1["Master A"] --- SA1["Slave 1A"]
        M1 --- SA2["Slave 2A"]
        M1 --- SA3["Slave 3A"]
    end
    subgraph PICONETB["Piconet B"]
        M2["Master B"] --- SB1["Slave 1B"]
        M2 --- SB2["Slave 2B"]
    end
    M1 -.Shared Node.-> SB1
    SA2 -.Shared Node.-> M2
```

> [!TIP]
> In the diagram, **Master A** simultaneously acts as a slave in **Piconet B**, forming a *scatternet*. This is rare in biomedical IoT (which usually uses star topology) but is a frequent KTU exam question.

---

## 4.4 BLE Connection Event — Timing Diagram (Block-Level)

```mermaid
gantt
    title BLE Connection Event Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Master TX
    Master Sends Packet  :a1, 0, 2
    section Slave RX
    Slave Listens        :a2, 0, 2
    section Slave TX
    Slave Responds       :b1, 2, 2
    section Master RX
    Master Listens       :b2, 2, 2
    section Sleep
    Both Sleep Until CI  :crit, 4, 6
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shaded "Both Sleep" block is the **inter-connection-interval** (e.g., 100 ms). During this time, current draw falls to ≈ 1 µA — the foundation of BLE's low average power consumption.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). List any four features that make BLE suitable for biomedical sensor applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** BLE is a wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology standardized in **IEEE 802.15.1** and introduced in Bluetooth 4.0 (2010). It is designed for short-burst, low-power, duty-cycled communication between a sensor node and a host device (smartphone/gateway).

> **Four features (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Ultra-low sleep current (< 1 µA)** → coin-cell operation for months.
> 2. **Fast connection setup (< 3 ms)** → ideal for periodic sensor "puffs."
> 3. **GATT-based profile architecture** → standardized medical services (Heart Rate 0x180D, Glucose 0x1808, Health Thermometer 0x1809).
> 4. **40 dedicated channels (2 MHz spacing)** → co-existence with Wi-Fi and robust against 2.4 GHz interference.
> 5. (Any one extra for bonus) **LE Coded PHY (Bluetooth 5.0)** → range up to 1 km for remote patient monitoring.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a piconet? How is it different from a scatternet?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Piconet (1½ marks):** A *piconet* is the fundamental Bluetooth topology consisting of **one master** device and up to **7 active slaves** (255 parked) that all share the same frequency-hopping sequence. The master controls channel access using time-division duplexing.

> **Scatternet (1½ marks):** A *scatternet* is formed when two or more piconets are linked, and at least one device acts as a **bridge** (a slave in one piconet and master in another, or vice-versa). This allows more than 7 devices to inter-operate, at the cost of increased protocol complexity and synchronization overhead.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### `Question A` — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of a wireless Bluetooth sensor node used for biomedical applications. List any four commonly used BLE-based medical profiles. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> **Architecture (4 marks):** A wireless Bluetooth biomedical sensor node consists of the following functional blocks:
>
> 1. **Physical / Biosensor Layer:** Transducer (e.g., Ag/AgCl electrodes for ECG, photodiode for SpO2, thermistor for skin temperature, MEMS accelerometer for fall detection).
> 2. **Analog Front End (AFE):** Instrumentation amplifier (gain ≈ 1000), band-pass filter (0.5–40 Hz for ECG), and a 12-/16-bit ADC sampling at 100–500 Hz.
> 3. **Microcontroller Unit (MCU):** Low-power ARM Cortex-M0/M4 (e.g., Nordic nRF52840, TI CC2640) that runs the BLE stack and applies digital signal processing (e.g., Pan-Tompkins QRS detection for ECG).
> 4. **Bluetooth Radio SoC:** 2.4 GHz GFSK modulator with a 50 Ω-matched ceramic-chip antenna. Sensitivity ≈ −90 dBm.
> 5. **Power Management:** CR2032 coin cell (220 mAh, 3 V) with an LDO regulator and a load switch that disconnects the radio between transmissions.
> 6. **Encapsulation:** Biocompatible, IP67-rated silicone or polyurethane housing.
>
> **Four BLE Medical Profiles (3 marks — ¾ mark each):**
> | Profile | UUID | Function |
> | :--- | :---: | :--- |
> | Heart Rate | **0x180D** | BPM, RR interval, energy expended |
> | Glucose | **0x1808** | Continuous glucose monitoring |
> | Health Thermometer | **0x1809** | Body temperature |
> | Blood Pressure | **0x1810** | Systolic, diastolic, MAP |
> | Pulse Oximeter | **0x1822** | SpO2 % and pulse rate |

---

**(b) A Class-2 Bluetooth transmitter operates at 2.45 GHz with 2.5 mW output. The receiving wearable antenna has Gt = Gr = 2 dBi. Compute the received power in dBm at a distance of 8 m in free space. Comment on the suitability of this link for an in-clinic patient monitor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pt in dBm:**

$$
P_t = 10\log_{10}(2.5) = 3.98\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 2 — FSPL (d = 0.008 km, f = 2450 MHz):**

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(0.008) + 20\log_{10}(2450) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 20(-2.097) + 20(3.389) + 32.44 = -41.94 + 67.78 + 32.44 = 58.28\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Total path gain:**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} = 2 + 2 - 58.28 = -54.28\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Received power:**

$$
P_r = 3.98 + (-54.28) = -50.30\ \text{dBm}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (2 marks):**
A typical BLE receiver sensitivity is **−70 dBm to −90 dBm**. The received power of **−50.30 dBm** provides a link margin of ≈ 20–40 dB, which is more than sufficient for a 10 m in-clinic link. The link is **highly suitable**, even with body-shadowing and multipath losses inside a hospital room.

---

### `Question B` — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the BLE protocol stack. Compare BLE with Classic Bluetooth in terms of power, channels, and connection time. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

> **BLE Protocol Stack (4 marks):**
>
> | Layer | Function |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | Application | Medical profiles (HR, Glucose, SpO2) |
> | GATT | Defines Services and Characteristics; client-server model |
> | ATT | Attribute protocol — read, write, notify, indicate |
> | GAP | Roles: Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central |
> | L2CAP | Packet segmentation, channel multiplexing |
> | HCI | Host–controller interface (UART/SPI) |
> | Link Layer | Connection events, channel map, encryption |
> | PHY | 2.4 GHz GFSK, 40 channels × 2 MHz, LE 1M/2M/Coded PHY |
>
> **Comparison (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**
>
> | Parameter | Classic BT | BLE |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: |
> | Channels | 79 | **40** |
> | Peak Current | 30 mA | **15 mA** |
> | Connection Setup | 1–3 s | **< 3 ms** |
> | Sleep Current | 50 µA | **< 1 µA** |

---

**(b) A wireless Bluetooth pulse-oximeter sleeps at 1 µA, transmits at 12 mA for 2 ms every 2 s, and uses a CR2032 coin cell (3 V, 220 mAh). Calculate (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the average current, and (iii) the battery life in days. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Duty Cycle (2 marks):**

$$
D = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{period}}} = \frac{2\ \text{ms}}{2000\ \text{ms}} = 0.001
$$

**(ii) Average Current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - D) + I_{\text{tx}} \cdot D
$$

$$
= (1 \times 10^{-6})(0.999) + (12 \times 10^{-3})(0.001) = 9.99 \times 10^{-7} + 1.2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A} = 13\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**(iii) Battery Life (3 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{220\ \text{mAh}}{0.013\ \text{mA}} = 16923\ \text{h}
$$

Converting to days:

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \frac{16923}{24} \approx 705\ \text{days} \approx 1.93\ \text{years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common student error is to **omit** the I_sleep term and compute only I_tx·D. This gives a falsely low current of 12 µA and an inflated battery life. **Always include both sleep and active terms** unless the question explicitly says "ignore sleep current."

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Common Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Friis equation.** Using d in metres but f in MHz gives a wrong FSPL. *Convert d to km, or use the alternative form with c and λ.*
> 2. **Confusing Classic BT and BLE specifications.** BLE has **40 channels**, Classic has **79**. BLE peak current is **≈ 15 mA**, not 30 mA.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract FSPL from the Pt+Gt+Gr term** in the Friis dB calculation. The minus sign is the most-skipped step in valuation.
> 4. **Listing IEEE 802.15.4 for Bluetooth.** That is **ZigBee/6LoWPAN**, not Bluetooth. Bluetooth is **IEEE 802.15.1**.
> 5. **Writing "Bluetooth uses CDMA."** It does not. It uses **FHSS (Frequency-Hopping Spread Spectrum)** with TDD.
> 6. **Skipping the comment / interpretation step** in numerical answers. KTU awards 1–2 marks for "engineering judgement" beyond pure calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bluetooth** → **IEEE 802.15.1** → **2.4 GHz ISM band** → license-free worldwide.
- **Classic BT:** 79 channels, 1 MHz spacing, **1600 hops/s**, GFSK modulation, peak 30 mA.
- **BLE:** 40 channels (37 data + 3 adv), 2 MHz spacing, peak **15 mA**, sleep **< 1 µA**, **< 3 ms** connection.
- **Piconet:** 1 master + ≤ 7 active slaves (255 parked) sharing the same hopping sequence.
- **Scatternet:** Interconnected piconets for > 7 devices (rare in medical IoT).
- **Power classes:** Class 1 = 100 mW / 100 m; Class 2 = 2.5 mW / 10 m; Class 3 = 1 mW / 1 m.
- **Friis equation (dB form):** $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44$.
- **BLE duty-cycled current:** $I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{sleep}}(1-D) + I_{\text{tx}} D$.
- **Battery life:** $T_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Standard medical GATT services:** Heart Rate (0x180D), Glucose (0x1808), Health Thermometer (0x1809), Blood Pressure (0x1810), Pulse Oximeter (0x1822).
- **Bluetooth 5.0 LE Coded PHY** → range up to **1 km** (used in remote patient monitoring).
- **Bluetooth ≠ Wi-Fi:** Wi-Fi = high bandwidth, high power; BLE = low bandwidth, ultra-low power.
- **Modulation:** **GFSK** (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying), index = 0.5 for Classic, 0.5 for BLE.
- **Hop period:** $T_{\text{hop}} = 625\ \mu s$ in Classic Bluetooth.
- **Key biomedical use cases:** CGM, ECG patch, SpO2 fingertip, smart inhaler, fall detector, smart pill bottle.
- **Total link budget for Class 2 link at 5–10 m** ≈ 50–60 dB loss; BLE sensitivity ≈ −70 to −90 dBm → plenty of margin.
- **Always** include both sleep and active current terms when computing battery life.
- **Always** convert units **before** substituting into logarithmic Friis equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling and Analytics: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Data Handling and Analytics: Introduction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **Data Handling and Analytics** refers to the systematic end-to-end process of acquiring, ingesting, storing, processing, transforming, and extracting meaningful, actionable insights from the massive volumes of heterogeneous data generated by networked sensors, actuators, and smart devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Data Handling* is the disciplined management of data across its lifecycle — from **acquisition → transmission → storage → retrieval → archival**. *Analytics* is the application of statistical, mathematical, and machine-learning techniques on this data to produce **descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, or prescriptive** intelligence that supports decision-making in IoT systems. ### 1.2 The "5 V's" of IoT Data (Big Data Framework) Modern IoT ecosystems are characterized by the **5 V's of Big Data**, which define the engineering challenges of data handling: | Dimension | Meaning | IoT Example | |-----------|---------|-------------| | **Volume** | Scale of data (Terabytes → Petabytes/day) | ECG data from 10,000 wearable patients | | **Velocity** | Speed of generation & processing | 1000 sensor readings/second in a smart factory | | **Variety** | Different formats & structures | Structured (DB tables), semi-structured (JSON), unstructured (images, audio) | | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / quality of data | Noisy biosensor data, dropped MQTT packets | | **Value** | Business/clinical insight extracted | Predicting cardiac arrest 6 hours in advance | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Aquarium" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine an **IoT-enabled smart hospital** as a giant digital aquarium. Thousands of tiny fish (sensors) swim around constantly, each producing bubbles (data) at different rates, colors, and sizes. > > - **Data Handling** is the plumbing + filtration system that captures every bubble, pipes it to a holding tank (storage), filters the dirt (cleaning), and organizes it on shelves (databases). > - **Data Analytics** is the marine biologist who walks in, looks at the patterns of bubbles, and concludes: *"The water temperature is rising, the fish are stressed — add ice to the tank!"* — that conclusion is the **actionable insight**. > > Without the plumbing, the bubbles vanish. Without the biologist, the bubbles are useless noise. **Both are mandatory for an intelligent IoT system.** ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Biomedical IoT In biomedical applications, data handling and analytics are **mission-critical**: - **Patient Monitoring**: Continuous vitals (SpO₂, ECG, BP) demand **real-time analytics** to trigger alarms. - **Clinical Decision Support**: Predictive analytics can flag sepsis hours before clinical symptoms. - **Regulatory Compliance**: Patient data must follow **HL7/FHIR** and **HIPAA/GDPR** handling rules. - **Edge Analytics**: Wearable insulin pumps cannot wait for cloud round-trips — analytics must run **on-device**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The IoT Data Lifecycle (Six Stages) The handling of IoT data follows a rigorous six-stage pipeline: 1. **Data Acquisition (Sensing)** - Analog signals from physical/biological phenomena are sampled by sensors. - Governed by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**: minimum sampling rate must be **≥ 2 × f_max** of the signal to avoid aliasing. - Biomedical example: ECG signal (bandwidth ~0.05–150 Hz) must be sampled at **≥ 300 Hz** (typical: 500 Hz). 2. **Data Preprocessing (Cleaning & Normalization)** - Handles missing values, outliers, noise (e.g., motion artifacts in wearable ECG). - Techniques: **Z-score filtering**, **moving average**, **median filtering**. - **Normalization** to a common range [0,1] or standard score: $$x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$$ 3. **Data Transmission** - Secure transport via **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP**. - Lightweight protocols (MQTT/CoAP) preferred for low-power biomedical wearables. 4. **Data Storage** - Selection between **SQL (relational)**, **NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra)**, **Time-Series DBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)**, and **Data Lakes (HDFS, AWS S3)**. - Biomedical preference: **HL7 FHIR-compliant** relational or hybrid stores. 5. **Data Processing & Analytics** - Batch, real-time (stream), or hybrid (Lambda/Kappa architecture). - Edge vs Fog vs Cloud trade-off. 6. **Data Visualization & Action** - Dashboards (Grafana, PowerBI), alerts, automated actuator control. ### 2.2 Four Pillars of Analytics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Classification — Analytics Hierarchy:** | Analytics Type | Question Answered | Technique | Biomedical Example | |----------------|-------------------|-----------|---------------------| | **Descriptive** | *What happened?* | Dashboards, summary stats, histograms | "Avg heart rate today = 78 bpm" | | **Diagnostic** | *Why did it happen?* | Correlation, drill-down, root-cause | "Arrhythmia correlated with skipped medication" | | **Predictive** | *What will happen?* | Regression, Neural Networks, LSTM | "Patient has 82% probability of hypoglycemia in 4 hrs" | | **Prescriptive** | *What should we do?* | Optimization, Reinforcement Learning | "Deliver 2 units insulin now via closed-loop pump" | ### 2.3 Edge vs Fog vs Cloud Analytics > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU board exams:** The choice of analytics layer directly determines **latency, bandwidth cost, privacy, and power consumption**. - **Edge Analytics**: Data is analyzed **on/near the sensor device itself** (e.g., microcontroller on a wearable). → Ultra-low latency (<10 ms), preserves privacy, limited compute. - **Fog Analytics**: Intermediate layer (gateway, local server). → Balances latency and compute. Suitable for hospital ward-level aggregation. - **Cloud Analytics**: Performed in remote data centers (AWS, Azure). → High compute, scalable, but introduces network latency & privacy risk. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Note | |------------------|----------------------|-------------| | Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Hz | | Min Storage per sample | $B = n \cdot f_s$ bits/sec | bps (n = bit-depth) | | Daily data volume | $V = B \cdot 86400$ | bits/day | | Min-Max Normalization | $x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | dimensionless ∈ [0,1] | | Z-Score Standardization | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | | SNR (Signal Quality) | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | ≥ 1 | | Latency Budget | $L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{transmit} + L_{process} + L_{actuate}$ | seconds (ms target in clinical) | | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | unit² of variable | | R² (Model Fit) | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | dimensionless ∈ [0,1] | > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas govern every layer of an IoT pipeline — from selecting an ADC sampling rate for a pulse oximeter, to sizing cloud storage for a 1000-bed hospital's patient telemetry, to evaluating the accuracy of a sepsis-prediction model. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Storage Sizing for an IoT Biomedical Stream **Problem:** A wearable ECG patch records at $f_s = 500$ Hz with a 16-bit ADC. Estimate the **daily data volume** in MB. **Step 1 — Identify the bit-rate.** The bit-rate (data generated per second) is the product of sampling frequency and bit-depth: $$B = n \cdot f_s = 16 \text{ bits} \times 500 \text{ Hz} = 8000 \text{ bits/sec}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to bits per day.** One day contains $86400$ seconds: $$V_{bits} = B \cdot t = 8000 \times 86400 = 691{,}200{,}000 \text{ bits/day}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Megabytes (MB).** Since $1 \text{ Byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$ and $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \times 1024 \text{ Bytes} = 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits}$: $$V_{MB} = \frac{691{,}200{,}000}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \approx 82.4 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A single patient generates **~82 MB/day** of raw ECG. For a 500-patient cardiology ward, raw data ≈ **40 GB/day**, justifying the need for compression and edge filtering. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Score Outlier Detection on SpO₂ Readings **Problem:** A pulse oximeter reports the following SpO₂ readings (%): **{96, 95, 97, 94, 96, 95, 99, 60, 96, 95}**. The reading "60" is suspected to be an artifact. Use the Z-score method to confirm. **Step 1 — Compute Mean (μ).** $$\mu = \frac{96+95+97+94+96+95+99+60+96+95}{10} = \frac{923}{10} = 92.3$$ **Step 2 — Compute Standard Deviation (σ).** First, squared deviations from the mean: $$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = (96-92.3)^2 + (95-92.3)^2 + (97-92.3)^2 + (94-92.3)^2 + (96-92.3)^2 + (95-92.3)^2 + (99-92.3)^2 + (60-92.3)^2 + (96-92.3)^2 + (95-92.3)^2$$ $$= 13.69 + 7.29 + 22.09 + 2.89 + 13.69 + 7.29 + 44.89 + 1043.29 + 13.69 + 7.29 = 1176.10$$ $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{1176.10}{10}} = \sqrt{117.61} \approx 10.84$$ **Step 3 — Compute Z-score for the suspect value x = 60.** $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{60 - 92.3}{10.84} = \frac{-32.3}{10.84} \approx -2.98$$ **Step 4 — Decision.** Since $\vert z \vert = 2.98 > 2.5$ (typical threshold), the reading is statistically a **strong outlier** and should be rejected as a sensor artifact. ### 3.3 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Analytics Layer > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam topic. **Memorize the trade-offs.** | Criterion | Edge | Fog | Cloud | |-----------|:----:|:---:|:-----:| | Latency | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | | Compute Power | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | | Privacy / HIPAA | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | | Bandwidth Cost | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | | Power Budget | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ (utility) | | Scalability | ★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Best For** | Wearables, implants | Hospital ward gateways | Population-scale ML training | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The IoT Data Analytics Pipeline (End-to-End) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IoT Sensors
ECG BP Temp] --> B[Edge Gateway
Preprocessing] B --> C{Data Type?} C -->|Critical Alarm| D[Edge Analytics
Real Time] C -->|Routine Vitals| E[Fog Layer
Ward Aggregation] C -->|Historical| F[Cloud Storage
Data Lake] D --> G[Actuator / Alarm] E --> H[Clinical Dashboard] F --> I[Cloud Analytics
ML Training] I --> J[Predictive Model] J --> K[Decision Support
to Clinician] H --> K G --> K ``` ### 4.2 The Four Levels of Analytics Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Prescriptive Analytics
What should we do] L2[Predictive Analytics
What will happen] L3[Diagnostic Analytics
Why did it happen] L4[Descriptive Analytics
What happened] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L1 -.uses.-> M1[Optimization RL] L2 -.uses.-> M2[Regression LSTM] L3 -.uses.-> M3[Correlation Drilldown] L4 -.uses.-> M4[Stats Dashboards Histograms] ``` ### 4.3 Lambda vs Kappa Architecture (Data Processing Paradigms) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Lambda_Architecture L1[Stream Layer
Real Time Speed] --> L3[Serving Layer
Merged Query] L2[Batch Layer
Full Accuracy] --> L3 end ``` ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Kappa_Architecture K1[All Data as Stream] --> K2[Single Processing Engine] K2 --> K3[Serving Layer] end ``` ### 4.4 Edge-to-Cloud Analytics Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph EdgeTier[Edge Tier] E1[Sensor Acquisition] E2[Local Filtering] E3[Threshold Alarm] end subgraph FogTier[Fog Tier] F1[Ward Gateway] F2[Local ML Inference] end subgraph CloudTier[Cloud Tier] C1[Data Lake Storage] C2[Batch Training] C3[Population Analytics] end E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> F1 --> F2 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *List and briefly define the four types of analytics. Give one biomedical IoT example for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Descriptive Analytics** — Summarizes past data. *Example:* Daily average heart rate of a patient displayed on a ward dashboard. 2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — Identifies causes of past events. *Example:* Correlating a drop in SpO₂ with patient movement using activity-log cross-analysis. 3. **Predictive Analytics** — Forecasts future events using ML. *Example:* LSTM model predicting hypoglycemia 30 minutes in advance from CGM data. 4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — Recommends optimal action. *Example:* Closed-loop insulin pump adjusting dosage automatically based on predicted glucose. *(1 mark for listing + 1 mark for brief definition + 1 mark for biomedical example. Total = 3)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define the "5 V's of Big Data" with reference to a biomedical IoT scenario.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Volume** — Terabytes of patient telemetry stored per hospital per year. - **Velocity** — Real-time vitals streamed at 500 Hz from ICU monitors. - **Variety** — Mix of ECG waveforms (unstructured), HL7 records (structured), doctor notes (text). - **Veracity** — Motion artifacts in wearable ECG reduce data trustworthiness. - **Value** — Extracted insight: early sepsis warning saves lives. *(0.5 mark per V × 5 = 2.5, rounded to 3 with one-line biomedical reference)* --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete IoT data lifecycle with a neat block diagram. For each stage, state one key engineering challenge. **(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable SpO₂ sensor transmits readings every 1 second (8-bit resolution) to a gateway. Calculate the **daily data volume in KB**, and discuss why edge analytics is preferred over cloud analytics for wearable SpO₂ monitoring in a critical-care scenario. --- ### **Model Answer A:** **(a) IoT Data Lifecycle [7 marks]:** The IoT data lifecycle consists of six stages: 1. **Acquisition** [1 mark]: Sensors sample physical signals (e.g., SpO₂ photodiode, 1 Hz). *Challenge:* Sampling rate vs battery trade-off. 2. **Preprocessing** [1 mark]: Noise filtering, outlier removal, normalization. *Challenge:* Distinguishing motion artifact from genuine desaturation. 3. **Transmission** [1 mark]: Encrypted wireless transport (BLE, MQTT). *Challenge:* Reliable delivery in crowded ISM band. 4. **Storage** [1 mark]: Time-series DB, in-memory buffers, cloud data lake. *Challenge:* Balancing cost vs query latency. 5. **Processing & Analytics** [1 mark]: Stream processing (Apache Kafka/Flink), ML inference. *Challenge:* Real-time SLAs under variable load. 6. **Visualization & Action** [1 mark]: Dashboards, alerts, actuator control. *Challenge:* Avoiding alert fatigue in clinicians. **Neat Block Diagram** [1 mark]: ``` Sensors → Preprocessing → Transmission → Storage → Analytics → Visualization/Action ``` --- **(b) Storage Calculation & Edge Justification [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Bit-rate:** $$B = n \cdot f_s = 8 \text{ bits} \times 1 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ bps}$$ **Step 2 — Daily bits:** $$V_{bits} = 8 \times 86400 = 691{,}200 \text{ bits/day}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to KB:** $$V_{KB} = \frac{691{,}200}{8 \times 1024} = 84.375 \text{ KB/day}$$ **Step 4 — Why edge analytics is preferred [4 marks]:** - **Latency**: Hypoxemia alarms must fire within 1–2 seconds. Cloud round-trip = 100–500 ms network + processing = unacceptable delay. - **Reliability**: Hospital WiFi may fail; edge works offline. - **Privacy**: Raw SpO₂ data never leaves the device, satisfying HIPAA. - **Bandwidth**: 84 KB/patient/day × 500 patients = 42 MB/day — small but multiplied by 1000s of wards, bandwidth becomes significant. - **Battery**: Edge filtering reduces BLE transmission duty cycle, extending wearable battery from 1 day to 5+ days. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Edge, Fog, and Cloud Analytics** using a comparison table. State one real-world biomedical use case appropriate to each layer. **(b) [7 Marks]** A continuous glucose monitor (CGM) generates one reading every 5 minutes. Using the **Z-score method**, demonstrate how a reading of 40 mg/dL would be classified as a hypoglycemia alarm, given a patient's recent 10-reading window: **{110, 115, 108, 112, 109, 113, 111, 114, 110, 112} mg/dL** with μ = 111.2 and σ = 2.15. --- ### **Model Answer B:** **(a) Comparison Table + Use Cases [7 marks]:** | Feature | Edge | Fog | Cloud | |---|---|---|---| | Location | On sensor/wearable | Local gateway/server | Remote data center | | Latency | < 10 ms | 10–100 ms | 100 ms – seconds | | Compute | Microcontroller | Edge server / Raspberry Pi class | High-end servers / GPU clusters | | Use Case | Hypoglycemia alarm on insulin pump [1] | Ward-level vitals aggregation at ICU gateway [1] | Population-scale diabetes risk modeling across hospital network [1] | | Privacy | Highest | Medium | Lowest (but encryptable) | | **Subtotal marks** | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Additional discussion [1 mark each, total 4]:** - Edge is best for closed-loop medical devices. - Fog balances latency and analytics richness for ward-level decisions. - Cloud is ideal for training ML models on millions of historical records. --- **(b) Z-Score Hypoglycemia Detection [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Threshold Selection [1 mark]:** We use a strict threshold $\vert z \vert > 2$ for clinical alarms (false positives are acceptable but missing a true event is dangerous). **Step 2 — Apply Z-Score Formula [2 marks]:** $$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$ $$z = \frac{40 - 111.2}{2.15} = \frac{-71.2}{2.15} \approx -33.12$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation [2 marks]:** Since $\vert z \vert = 33.12 \gg 2$, the reading is **33 standard deviations** below the mean. This is a **statistical impossibility** under normal physiological conditions. **Step 4 — Clinical Decision [2 marks]:** - This is almost certainly a **true hypoglycemic event** (or a sensor failure, which itself requires alarm). - The system must **trigger an immediate hypoglycemia alarm**, notify the patient/clinician, and (if part of a closed-loop system) suspend insulin delivery. - This demonstrates the power of simple statistical analytics at the **edge** for life-critical biomedical IoT. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - ❌ **Confusing Data Handling with Data Analytics** — Handling = storage/transport/cleaning; Analytics = insight extraction.
