Marks are split accordingly. > - ❌ **Forgetting the Nyquist criterion** when justifying sampling rates — losing 1–2 marks. > - ❌ **Not mentioning privacy/HIPAA** in edge-vs-cloud questions — boards reward this. > - ❌ **Writing Z-score formula without units or thresholds** — always state the threshold used. > - ❌ **Skipping the block diagram in lifecycle questions** — minimum 1 mark reserved for it. > - ❌ **Using approximate numbers in storage calculations** — show every step; partial credit requires clear arithmetic. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Data Handling = Plumbing** (acquire → clean → store → retrieve); **Data Analytics = Intelligence** (describe → diagnose → predict → prescribe). - ✅ **5 V's of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — apply to every IoT scenario. - ✅ **Nyquist Theorem** is non-negotiable: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For ECG, use ≥ 500 Hz. - ✅ **Normalization** $[x - x_{min}] / [x_{max} - x_{min}]$ brings data to [0,1] for ML. - ✅ **Z-Score** is the simplest outlier detector: $\vert z \vert > 2$ or 2.5 flags anomalies. - ✅ **Four Analytics Types** in ascending intelligence: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive. - ✅ **Edge** = lowest latency, highest privacy, lowest compute. **Cloud** = highest compute, highest latency, lowest privacy. **Fog** sits in between. - ✅ **Lambda Architecture** = batch + speed layer (merged). **Kappa Architecture** = single stream layer. - ✅ For **biomedical IoT**, always cite **HIPAA, latency (ms), and patient safety** in justifications. - ✅ For KTU numericals, show: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Result → Interpretation** (5-step structure for full marks). - ✅ A simple Z-score on a CGM/SpO₂ reading is the most common KTU numerical — practice the arithmetic. ---

topics.title: Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Big Data

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Big Data** refers to extremely large, high-velocity, and high-variety information assets that demand cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing to enable enhanced insight, decision-making, and process automation. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and biomedical engineering, Big Data describes the massive continuous streams of heterogeneous sensor data generated by wearable devices, implantable sensors, ICU monitors, environmental monitors, and remote patient telemetry systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Big Data is data whose scale, diversity, and speed of generation exceed the storage and analytical capacity of conventional database systems (RDBMS), necessitating distributed storage (e.g., **HDFS**), parallel processing (e.g., **MapReduce**), and advanced analytics.

The formal classification of Big Data is rooted in its defining dimensions, historically known as the **3 V's Model** (proposed by Doug Laney, Gartner, 2001) and extended in KTU syllabus to the **5 V's Model**.

### The 5 V's of Big Data — Tabular Overview

| Dimension | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| **Volume** | V₁ | Petabytes to Exabytes of data generated continuously |
| **Velocity** | V₂ | Speed at which data is generated, captured, and processed |
| **Variety** | V₃ | Structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data |
| **Veracity** | V₄ | Trustworthiness, quality, and provenance of data |
| **Value** | V₅ | Business/scientific insight extracted from data |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Flood of Numbers":**
> Imagine a 500-bed hospital where every ICU patient is connected to **20 sensors** (ECG, EEG, SpO₂, blood pressure, temperature, respiration, glucose, accelerometer, etc.) each sampling at **250 Hz**. This produces:
>
> $$\text{Sensors} \times \text{Patients} \times \text{Frequency} = 500 \times 20 \times 250 \approx 2.5 \text{ million readings/sec}$$
>
> Add **MRI machines**, **gene sequencers**, **electronic health records (EHRs)**, and **wearable smartwatches** streaming 24×7 — this torrent of numbers cannot fit in any normal computer. **Big Data tools** are like a fleet of trucks working together to collect, store, and analyze this flood in parallel, something a single truck (traditional RDBMS) could never handle.

## 1.3 KTU-Highlight Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Big Data Scale Thresholds (Industry Accepted):**
> - **1 Kilobyte (KB)** = $10^3$ bytes
> - **1 Megabyte (MB)** = $10^6$ bytes
> - **1 Gigabyte (GB)** = $10^9$ bytes
> - **1 Terabyte (TB)** = $10^{12}$ bytes
> - **1 Petabyte (PB)** = $10^{15}$ bytes
> - **1 Exabyte (EB)** = $10^{18}$ bytes
> - **1 Zettabyte (ZB)** = $10^{21}$ bytes
>
> **Big Data Threshold:** Data exceeding **Terabyte (TB)** scale in volume is conventionally categorized as Big Data in IoT contexts.

## 1.4 Structured vs Unstructured Data — Why Big Data?

Traditional **RDBMS** (Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server) is governed by **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and is optimized for **vertical scaling** (adding more power to a single machine). Big Data systems instead use **horizontal scaling** (adding more commodity machines), governed by the **CAP theorem** (Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance — at most two of three).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** When IoT sensors generate data faster than an RDBMS can ingest, store, and query, we transition from traditional databases to the **Big Data Ecosystem** built on **Hadoop**, **Spark**, **NoSQL**, and **cloud streaming platforms**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 V's — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Volume (V₁)
- Refers to the **sheer size** of data.
- IoT example: A single **Boeing 787** aircraft generates **~40 TB** of data per flight.
- Biomedical example: A **whole-genome sequence** occupies ~**200 GB**; population genomics reaches **PB** scale.

### 2.1.2 Velocity (V₂)
- Refers to the **rate of data generation and movement**.
- Streaming velocity $V_s$ is computed as:
$$V_s = \frac{N_s}{T_s} \quad \text{(samples per second)}$$
where $N_s$ = number of samples and $T_s$ = time window.
- Real-time processing demands sub-second latency, achieved using **Apache Kafka**, **Apache Flink**, **Apache Storm**.

### 2.1.3 Variety (V₃)
- Three classes:
  * **Structured** — tabular data (EHR fields, lab values).
  * **Semi-structured** — JSON/XML from IoT (MQTT payloads).
  * **Unstructured** — MRI images, ECG waveforms, video feeds.

### 2.1.4 Veracity (V₄)
- Data quality and trustworthiness.
- In biomedical IoT, **sensor noise**, **patient motion artifacts**, and **missing values** degrade veracity.
- Mitigation: **Kalman filtering**, **interpolation**, **data lineage tracking**.

### 2.1.5 Value (V₅)
- The actionable insight extracted.
- Big Data is often described as a "high-value, low-density" asset — the value-per-byte is small, but aggregated insights (early sepsis prediction, arrhythmia detection) have huge clinical impact.

## 2.2 Big Data Analytics — Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 High-Yield Concept — Four Classes of Analytics:**

| Class | Question Answered | Biomedical Example |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Descriptive Analytics** | "What happened?" | Patient summary dashboards |
| **Diagnostic Analytics** | "Why did it happen?" | Root-cause of ICU readmission |
| **Predictive Analytics** | "What will happen?" | Sepsis onset prediction 6 h in advance |
| **Prescriptive Analytics** | "What should be done?" | AI-driven insulin dosage recommendation |

## 2.3 Hadoop Ecosystem — The Backbone of Big Data

**Apache Hadoop** is the open-source framework that enables distributed storage and processing of Big Data across clusters of commodity computers. Its three core pillars are:

1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Storage layer.
2. **MapReduce** — Programming model for parallel processing.
3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — Cluster resource management.

Auxiliary components (the Hadoop Ecosystem):

- **Hive** — SQL-like queries on Big Data.
- **Pig** — Data flow scripting (Pig Latin).
- **HBase** — NoSQL columnar database.
- **Sqoop** — RDBMS ↔ Hadoop import/export.
- **Flume** — Log/stream ingestion.
- **Spark** — In-memory distributed processing (faster than MapReduce).
- **Kafka** — Distributed message broker for streams.
- **ZooKeeper** — Coordination service.

## 2.4 HDFS Architecture — Detailed

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave (NameNode–DataNode)** architecture:

- **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata — file names, directory tree, block locations.
- **DataNode (Slave):** Stores actual data blocks (default **128 MB** per block in Hadoop 2.x+; **64 MB** in Hadoop 1.x).
- **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges the *fsimage* and *edit logs* (not a hot standby).

> [!NOTE]
> **Replication Factor:** Default is **3** — every block is stored on 3 different DataNodes for fault tolerance. This means a file of size $S$ actually consumes $3S$ of physical storage.

## 2.5 MapReduce Programming Model

MapReduce operates in two phases:

1. **Map Phase:** Each input record is transformed into intermediate **key-value pairs** $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$.
2. **Reduce Phase:** All values sharing the same key are aggregated: $(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$.

Between Map and Reduce, an automatic **Shuffle & Sort** phase groups identical keys.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Big Data Module:**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Data Scale Threshold | $\geq 10^{12}$ bytes $= 1$ TB | Big Data threshold |
| HDFS Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+) | $B = 128$ MB | Default |
| HDFS Replication | $\text{Total Storage} = S \times R$ | $R = $ replication factor (default 3) |
| Number of Blocks | $\text{NumBlocks} = \lceil S / B \rceil$ | $S =$ file size |
| Total Physical Storage | $\text{Storage}_{\text{phys}} = S \times R$ | Includes replicas |
| Streaming Velocity | $V_s = N_s / T_s$ | samples/second |
| MapReduce Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Records Processed}}{\text{Time}_{\text{total}}}$ | records/sec |
| Data Reduction | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{S_{\text{orig}}}{S_{\text{comp}}}$ | unitless |
| IoT Sensor Data Rate | $R = f_s \times N_c \times \frac{b}{8}$ | bytes/sec, $b$ = bits/sample |
| CAP Theorem | At most **2 of 3** properties | Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance |
| Big Data Latency SLA | Real-time: $< 1$ s; Near-real-time: $1$–$60$ s; Batch: $>$ min | Streaming tier |
| Hadoop Cluster Nodes | $N = N_{\text{NameNode}} + N_{\text{DataNode}}$ | $N_{\text{NameNode}} = 1$ (typical) |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Hospitals:** Real-time patient monitoring at scale.
- **Genomics & Precision Medicine:** Population-scale genomic analysis.
- **Epidemic Surveillance:** Combining wearable + mobility data for outbreak forecasting.
- **Drug Discovery:** Mining clinical trial datasets using Spark MLlib.
- **Predictive Maintenance of Medical Equipment:** IoT telemetry → predictive failure alerts.
- **Public Health Policy:** Aggregating anonymized EHR + environmental + socioeconomic Big Data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of HDFS Blocks for a Given File

**Problem Setup:** A hospital's Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS) stores a CT-scan file of size **$S = 5$ GB** on HDFS with default block size $B = 128$ MB and replication factor $R = 3$. Find (a) number of blocks, (b) total physical storage used.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Convert file size to consistent units.**

$$S = 5 \text{ GB} = 5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 5120 \text{ MB}$$

*Explanation:* We convert GB to MB to match the block-size unit. KTU accepts both $1024$-based (binary) and $1000$-based (decimal); for HDFS, the default is **binary (1024)**.

**Step 2 — Compute number of blocks using ceiling division.**

$$\text{NumBlocks} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

$$\text{NumBlocks} = \left\lceil \frac{5120 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

$$\text{NumBlocks} = \lceil 40 \rceil = 40$$

*Explanation:* Since $5120$ is exactly divisible by $128$, the ceiling gives an exact integer — **40 blocks**.

**Step 3 — Compute total logical storage (without replication).**

$$\text{Logical Storage} = \text{NumBlocks} \times B = 40 \times 128 = 5120 \text{ MB} = 5 \text{ GB}$$

*Explanation:* This equals the original file size, as expected.

**Step 4 — Compute total physical storage (with replication factor $R = 3$).**

$$\text{Storage}_{\text{phys}} = S \times R = 5 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 15 \text{ GB}$$

*Explanation:* Each of the 40 blocks is replicated to 3 DataNodes, so the cluster consumes 3× the logical size.

**Final Answer:** **40 blocks**, **15 GB physical storage**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Streaming Velocity from IoT Biomedical Sensors

**Problem Setup:** A wearable ECG patch samples at $f_s = 500$ Hz on $N_c = 12$ channels (12-lead ECG), each sample being $b = 16$ bits. Calculate the data rate in **bytes/sec** and the **daily volume in GB**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Write the data-rate equation.**

$$R = f_s \times N_c \times \frac{b}{8} \quad \text{[bytes/sec]}$$

*Explanation:* Multiplying samples-per-second × channels gives samples/sec; converting bits to bytes requires dividing by 8.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$R = 500 \times 12 \times \frac{16}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate stepwise.**

$$R = 500 \times 12 \times 2$$

$$R = 6000 \times 2 = 12000 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to KB/s and MB/s.**

$$R = \frac{12000}{1024} \approx 11.72 \text{ KB/s}$$

$$R = \frac{12000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 0.01144 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute daily volume.**

$$\text{Volume}_{\text{day}} = R \times 86400 \text{ sec}$$

$$\text{Volume}_{\text{day}} = 12000 \times 86400 = 1.0368 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Volume}_{\text{day}} = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^{9}}{1024^3} \approx 0.9655 \text{ GB/day per patient}$$

*Explanation:* A single 12-lead ECG patch generates nearly **1 GB/day**. Multiply by 1000 hospital patients → ~1 TB/day, justifying Big Data infrastructure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MapReduce Word-Count Style Example for Biomedical Records

**Problem:** Given the following patient-diagnosis log:
```
(cardiology, MI)
(cardiology, AFib)
(neurology, stroke)
(cardiology, MI)
(neurology, epilepsy)
```

Compute the count of diagnoses per department using the MapReduce framework.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — MAP phase: emit (department, 1) for every record.**

| Input Record | Map Output |
|--------------|-----------|
| (cardiology, MI) | (cardiology, 1) |
| (cardiology, AFib) | (cardiology, 1) |
| (neurology, stroke) | (neurology, 1) |
| (cardiology, MI) | (cardiology, 1) |
| (neurology, epilepsy) | (neurology, 1) |

**Step 2 — SHUFFLE & SORT phase: group by key.**

```
cardiology → [1, 1, 1]
neurology → [1, 1]
```

**Step 3 — REDUCE phase: sum each list.**

$$n_{\text{cardiology}} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$$

$$n_{\text{neurology}} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Final Output:**

| Department | Count |
|------------|-------|
| cardiology | 3 |
| neurology | 2 |

*Explanation:* This is the canonical MapReduce pattern — embarrassingly parallel in the Map phase and aggregated in the Reduce phase. The same model scales to billions of IoT records across thousands of nodes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cluster Storage Capacity

**Problem:** A Hadoop cluster has $N_d = 12$ DataNodes, each with $C_d = 8$ TB usable storage. Replication factor $R = 3$. Compute the **effective cluster storage**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Total raw cluster storage.**

$$C_{\text{raw}} = N_d \times C_d$$

$$C_{\text{raw}} = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 2 — Account for HDFS replication.**

$$C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_{\text{raw}}}{R}$$

$$C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{96}{3} = 32 \text{ TB}$$

*Explanation:* HDFS reserves $R$ copies of every block; therefore effective user-addressable storage is raw ÷ replication.

**Final Answer:** **32 TB** of effective storage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparison Chart — HDFS vs RDBMS (Frequently Asked):**

| Parameter | RDBMS | HDFS |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Data Structure | Structured tables | Any format (raw, JSON, binary) |
| Scaling | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| Schema | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read |
| Transaction Support | Full ACID | Eventual consistency, batch-oriented |
| Best For | OLTP, transactions | OLAP, batch analytics, IoT streams |
| Latency | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| Data Volume | GB to TB | TB to PB to EB |
| Processing Model | SQL queries | MapReduce / Spark |
| Fault Tolerance | Replication via RAID | Replication factor across nodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hadoop Ecosystem Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IoT Biomedical Data Sources] --> B[Apache Kafka Stream Broker]
    B --> C[Apache Flume Log Collector]
    A --> C
    C --> D[HDFS Storage Layer]
    B --> D
    D --> E[MapReduce Engine]
    D --> F[Apache Spark In-Memory Engine]
    E --> G[Hive Data Warehouse]
    F --> G
    D --> H[HBase NoSQL Database]
    D --> I[Sqoop RDBMS Bridge]
    G --> J[YARN Resource Manager]
    F --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[NameNode Master]
    J --> L[DataNode Slave 1]
    J --> M[DataNode Slave 2]
    J --> N[DataNode Slave 3]
    K --> L
    K --> M
    K --> N
    G --> O[BI Dashboard / Clinical Analytics]
    H --> O
```

*Explanation:* The diagram shows data ingestion (Kafka, Flume) flowing into HDFS, processed by MapReduce or Spark, orchestrated by YARN, with outputs feeding analytics tools. The NameNode manages metadata, while DataNodes store actual blocks.

## 4.2 HDFS Read-Write Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Client Application] --> Q1[Step 1 Request to NameNode for File Metadata]
    Q1 --> Q2[Step 2 NameNode Returns Block Locations]
    Q2 --> R1[Step 3 Client Reads Block from Nearest DataNode]
    Q2 --> R2[Step 4 Client Reads Replica from Backup DataNode]
    R1 --> S[Step 5 Assemble Final File from All Blocks]
    R2 --> S
    P2[Write Client] --> W1[Step 1 Write Request to NameNode]
    W1 --> W2[Step 2 NameNode Allocates DataNodes for Replication]
    W2 --> W3[Step 3 Client Pushes Block to First DataNode]
    W3 --> W4[Step 4 Pipeline Replication to Second and Third DataNodes]
    W4 --> W5[Step 5 Acknowledge Success Back to Client]
```

*Explanation:* Reads use metadata lookup then direct DataNode communication. Writes follow a **pipeline replication** pattern — once the first replica is written, it is forwarded to the next DataNode, ensuring replication with minimal client load.

## 4.3 MapReduce Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Input Split 1] --> M1[Mapper 1]
    A2[Input Split 2] --> M2[Mapper 2]
    A3[Input Split 3] --> M3[Mapper 3]
    M1 --> S[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    S --> R1[Reducer 1 Key Group A]
    S --> R2[Reducer 2 Key Group B]
    R1 --> O1[Output HDFS File 1]
    R2 --> O2[Output HDFS File 2]
```

*Explanation:* Input splits are processed in parallel by mappers; intermediate keys are shuffled and sorted globally; reducers aggregate and write final outputs back to HDFS.

## 4.4 Big Data Analytics Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Raw IoT Sensor Data] --> L2[Descriptive What Happened]
    L2 --> L3[Diagnostic Why It Happened]
    L3 --> L4[Predictive What Will Happen]
    L4 --> L5[Prescriptive What Should Be Done]
    L5 --> L6[Clinical Decision Support System]
```

*Explanation:* The progressive analytics ladder moves from raw data to actionable clinical recommendations, the pinnacle of Big Data value extraction in biomedical applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define Big Data and explain any three of its characteristics (3 V's).**

**Model Answer:**

Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be processed using traditional database management systems. It is characterized by the **3 V's**:

1. **Volume:** The massive scale of data, ranging from terabytes to exabytes. For example, genomic sequencing of a single human genome generates ~200 GB; population genomics reaches petabytes.

2. **Velocity:** The high speed at which data is generated, captured, and must be processed. Wearable ECG patches produce thousands of samples per second, demanding real-time processing.

3. **Variety:** The heterogeneous nature of data — structured (EHR tables), semi-structured (JSON from IoT), and unstructured (MRI images, video streams).

*(Any 3 V's with biomedical examples → 3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark × 2 V's or 1 mark × 3 V's)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between structured and unstructured data with biomedical examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Structured Data | Unstructured Data |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Schema** | Predefined schema (tables, rows, columns) | No predefined schema |
| **Storage** | RDBMS (SQL) | NoSQL, Data Lakes, HDFS |
| **Examples (Biomedical)** | Patient lab reports, billing records, vital signs tables | MRI/CT images, ECG waveforms, doctor voice notes, genomic FASTA files |
| **Query** | SQL queries | MapReduce, Spark, HiveQL |
| **Analysis** | Easy statistical analysis | Requires ML/DL or specialized parsers |

*1 mark for tabular distinction, 1 mark for biomedical examples, 1 mark for storage/query contrast.*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the architecture and components of the Hadoop ecosystem with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Apache Hadoop is an open-source framework for distributed storage and processing of Big Data. Its architecture has three core layers plus ecosystem components.

**Core Layers:**

1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System):** Master-Slave storage.
   - *NameNode:* Master server storing metadata (file namespace, block-to-DataNode mapping).
   - *DataNode:* Slave nodes storing actual data blocks (default 128 MB each).
   - *Replication factor:* Default 3 for fault tolerance.

2. **MapReduce:** Programming model with two phases:
   - *Map:* Parallel transformation of input records into intermediate key-value pairs.
   - *Reduce:* Aggregation by key with shuffle-and-sort between phases.

3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator):** Resource manager comprising ResourceManager (global) and NodeManager (per-node), enabling multi-tenant scheduling.

**Ecosystem Components:**

- *Hive:* SQL-like querying.
- *Pig:* Script-based data flow (Pig Latin).
- *HBase:* Real-time NoSQL access.
- *Sqoop:* Bulk import/export with RDBMS.
- *Flume:* Log ingestion.
- *Spark:* Faster in-memory processing.
- *Kafka:* Stream ingestion.
- *ZooKeeper:* Coordination.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
[IoT Sources] → [Flume/Kafka] → [HDFS: NameNode + DataNodes]
                                          ↓
                                    [YARN Scheduler]
                                          ↓
                            [MapReduce / Spark Engine]
                                          ↓
                            [Hive / HBase / Pig]
                                          ↓
                            [Analytics & Visualization]
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks architecture, 2 marks HDFS, 2 marks MapReduce, 1 mark ecosystem components)*

---

**(b) A hospital's HDFS stores a CT scan of 2 GB. With default block size 128 MB and replication factor 3, calculate the number of blocks and total physical storage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Convert file size to MB.

$$S = 2 \text{ GB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2:** Number of blocks.

$$\text{NumBlocks} = \left\lceil \frac{2048}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 16 \rceil = 16 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 3:** Total physical storage.

$$\text{Storage}_{\text{phys}} = S \times R = 2048 \times 3 = 6144 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to GB.

$$\text{Storage}_{\text{phys}} = \frac{6144}{1024} = 6 \text{ GB}$$

**Final Answer:** 16 blocks, 6 GB physical storage.

*(7 marks: 2 marks Step 1, 2 marks Step 2, 2 marks Step 3-4, 1 mark final answer)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a) Define Big Data. Explain the 5 V's of Big Data with biomedical IoT examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Big Data is high-volume, high-velocity, high-variety information assets that require new processing models for enhanced decision-making, insight discovery, and process optimization.

**The 5 V's with Biomedical IoT Examples:**

1. **Volume:** Refers to scale. A single 12-lead ECG patch generates ~1 GB/day per patient; a 1000-patient hospital generates ~1 TB/day; population genomics reaches PB scale.

2. **Velocity:** Refers to speed. Continuous ICU monitoring demands sub-second processing; streaming analytics via Apache Kafka or Flink is required.

3. **Variety:** Refers to format diversity. Patient data spans structured (EHR tables), semi-structured (HL7 FHIR JSON messages from IoT devices), and unstructured (DICOM images, ECG waveforms).

4. **Veracity:** Refers to data quality and trustworthiness. Sensor noise, motion artifacts, and missing readings from wearable devices degrade veracity; requires data cleaning and validation.

5. **Value:** Refers to actionable insights. Aggregated Big Data enables early sepsis prediction, outbreak forecasting, personalized treatment plans — high clinical value despite low per-byte density.

*(7 marks: 1 mark definition + 1.2 marks per V with example)*

---

**(b) Explain the MapReduce programming model with a suitable biomedical example. Write the formula for the number of HDFS blocks. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**MapReduce Model:** MapReduce is a parallel programming model that processes massive datasets in two phases:

1. **Map Phase:** Input is split across mappers; each mapper emits intermediate key-value pairs.
$$(\text{key}_1, \text{value}_1) \xrightarrow{\text{Map}} \text{List}((\text{key}_2, \text{value}_2))$$

2. **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** Intermediate pairs are grouped by key automatically by the framework.

3. **Reduce Phase:** Each reducer aggregates all values for a given key.
$$(\text{key}_2, \text{List}(\text{value}_2)) \xrightarrow{\text{Reduce}} \text{List}((\text{key}_3, \text{value}_3))$$

**Biomedical Example — Counting Disease Occurrences:**

Input log (patient disease records):

```
(zone_A, dengue)
(zone_B, malaria)
(zone_A, dengue)
(zone_C, typhoid)
(zone_A, malaria)
```

**Map Output:**

```
(zone_A, 1)
(zone_B, 1)
(zone_A, 1)
(zone_C, 1)
(zone_A, 1)
```

**After Shuffle & Sort:**

```
zone_A → [1, 1, 1]
zone_B → [1]
zone_C → [1]
```

**Reduce Output:**

```
zone_A → 3
zone_B → 1
zone_C → 1
```

**Number of HDFS Blocks Formula:**

$$\text{NumBlocks} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

where $S$ is file size and $B$ is block size (default 128 MB).

*(7 marks: 2 marks model description, 3 marks example, 2 marks formula)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing replication with block count** — students often write total storage = NumBlocks × 3 instead of $S \times R$. Always multiply **logical file size** by replication factor, not block count.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — failing to convert GB to MB (or vice versa) when computing blocks. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Mixing up 3 V's, 5 V's, and 7 V's** — KTU 2024 Module 4 expects **5 V's** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value). Memorize this exact list.
> 4. **Forgetting shuffle-and-sort** — MapReduce has THREE phases (Map, Shuffle+Sort, Reduce), not two. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Writing "Hadoop = database"** — Hadoop is a **framework**, not a database. HBase is the database component.
> 6. **Confusing CAP theorem with ACID** — ACID is for RDBMS; CAP is for distributed NoSQL systems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 Big Data:**

- ✅ Big Data = data exceeding RDBMS capacity in **Volume, Velocity, Variety**.
- ✅ **5 V's** (KTU-mandatory): Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ Big Data scale threshold: **≥ 1 TB**; typical industrial scale is **PB–EB**.
- ✅ **Hadoop** = framework with HDFS + MapReduce + YARN.
- ✅ **HDFS block size (Hadoop 2.x+) = 128 MB**; default **replication factor = 3**.
- ✅ Formula: $\text{NumBlocks} = \lceil S / B \rceil$; $\text{Physical Storage} = S \times R$.
- ✅ **MapReduce** = Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce (3 phases).
- ✅ **CAP theorem**: at most **2 of 3** (Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance).
- ✅ **ACID** → RDBMS; **BASE** → NoSQL/Big Data.
- ✅ Big Data analytics classes: **Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive**.
- ✅ **Horizontal scaling** (Big Data) vs **Vertical scaling** (RDBMS).
- ✅ IoT biomedical examples: wearable ECG (1 GB/day), ICU streaming, genomic data (200 GB/genome).
- ✅ **Hadoop ecosystem tools to memorize**: HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Flume, Spark, Kafka, ZooKeeper.
- ✅ MapReduce equation form: $(k_1, v_1) \xrightarrow{\text{Map}} (k_2, v_2) \xrightarrow{\text{Reduce}} (k_3, v_3)$.
- ✅ Schema-on-write = RDBMS; Schema-on-read = HDFS/Data Lake.
- ✅ Streaming velocity formula: $V_s = f_s \times N_c \times (b/8)$ bytes/sec.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Data ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition — Data in IoT & Biomedical Context:** > In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT) and Biomedical Engineering**, *data* refers to the raw, unprocessed collection of facts, measurements, observations, or signals generated by sensors, medical devices, patients, healthcare systems, and networked instruments. The *type* of data classifies these facts based on **structure, scale of measurement, temporal behaviour, dimensionality, and source**, which collectively dictate the storage mechanism, transmission protocol, and analytical pipeline that must be applied. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Hospital's "Five Senses" Imagine a **smart hospital** as a living organism: - A patient's **heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation** (continuous numerical signals) are like the hospital's "**sense of touch**" — constantly feeling the patient's pulse. - **MRI scans and X-ray images** are like the hospital's "**sense of sight**" — capturing snapshots of the body's interior. - **Doctor's free-text clinical notes** are like the hospital's "**sense of hearing**" — a stream of unstructured human language. - **Lab reports with discrete numbers** (e.g., blood glucose = 110 mg/dL) are like the hospital's "**sense of taste**" — sharp, discrete, and precise. - **Patient demographics (name, age, blood group)** are like the hospital's "**sense of identity**" — categorical and structured. Each "sense" produces a **different type of data**, and the hospital's IoT infrastructure must handle all of them differently — much like our brain processes sight and touch via completely separate neural pathways. ### 1.3 Why Classification of Data Types Matters in KTU Examinations > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > - Understanding data types is the **prerequisite foundation** for Module 4 topics like data acquisition, signal processing, cloud storage, and analytics. > - KTU examiners frequently test this topic because it bridges **biomedical instrumentation** with **computer science/data engineering**. > - Knowing the type of data dictates: **acquisition rate, file format, storage medium (cloud/edge), transmission protocol (MQTT/HTTP), and analytical technique (ML/statistics).** ### 1.4 Universal Constants & Standard Metrics - Standard ECG sampling rate: **360 Hz to 1000 Hz** - Standard EEG sampling rate: **256 Hz to 512 Hz** - Standard body temperature range: **36.1 °C – 37.2 °C** (oral) - Standard heart rate (adult resting): **60 – 100 bpm** - Normal SpO₂: **≥ 95%** - Normal blood pressure (adult): **120/80 mmHg** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Primary Classification Axes for Biomedical & IoT Data Biomedical and IoT data can be classified along **six independent axes**. Each axis answers a different engineering question. #### Axis 1 — Structure of Data | Sub-type | Definition | Biomedical Example | IoT Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Structured** | Highly organized, tabular, follows a fixed schema (rows & columns) | Patient demographics in a relational DBMS | Sensor readings in a CSV/Time-series DB | | **Semi-structured** | Has tags/markers but no rigid schema; hierarchical | HL7/FHIR messages, DICOM headers, JSON-based EHR | MQTT payloads, XML telemetry packets | | **Unstructured** | No predefined format; binary or free-form | MRI/CT images, ECG waveform files (.edf), doctor's narrative notes | Surveillance camera video, raw audio, free-text logs | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** Roughly **80% of healthcare data is unstructured** (IBM Watson Health estimate), making storage and NLP processing critical challenges in modern medical IoT. #### Axis 2 — Scale of Measurement (Stevens' Taxonomy — Most Exam-Relevant) | Scale | Meaning | Mathematical Operations Allowed | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Nominal** | Categories with **no inherent order** | Equality (=, ≠) | Blood group (A, B, AB, O), Gender | | **Ordinal** | Ordered categories, **unequal intervals** | Equality, order (<, >) | Pain scale (Mild < Moderate < Severe) | | **Interval** | Ordered, **equal intervals**, **no true zero** | +, − (but ratios invalid) | Body temperature in °C, pH | | **Ratio** | Ordered, equal intervals, **true zero present** | All four (+, −, ×, ÷) | Heart rate, weight, blood pressure, SpO₂ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in exams:** You **cannot compute a meaningful ratio** of two interval-scale values. For example, saying "30 °C is twice as hot as 15 °C" is physically meaningless because 0 °C is not the *absence* of heat. The same is true for pH. #### Axis 3 — Temporal Behaviour - **Time-Series Data** — Sequential measurements indexed by time. Dominant in wearable IoT (Apple Watch, Fitbit) and bedside monitors. Each new sample is appended; **order matters**. - **Cross-Sectional Data** — Snapshot at a single instant. Example: vital signs recorded at ICU admission. - **Longitudinal Data** — Repeated measurements of the same subject over long durations (months/years). Used in chronic disease studies. - **Streaming Data** — Continuous, unbounded, real-time flow requiring **stream processing engines** (Apache Kafka, Apache Flink). #### Axis 4 — Continuity (Discrete vs Continuous) - **Discrete Data** — Countable, finite, integer-valued. Example: number of ECG beats per minute, count of steps. - **Continuous Data** — Can take any real value within an interval. Example: blood pressure waveform, core body temperature curve. #### Axis 5 — Dimensionality - **Univariate** — One variable per observation (e.g., heart rate alone). - **Multivariate** — Multiple variables per observation (e.g., HR + BP + SpO₂ + temperature simultaneously). - **High-Dimensional** — Hundreds-to-thousands of features (e.g., gene expression microarray, full 12-lead ECG with all 12 channels × samples). #### Axis 6 — Source / Domain Classification (Biomedical-Specific) 1. **Physiological Signal Data** — Electrical biosignals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, EGG). 2. **Medical Imaging Data** — Radiology images (X-ray, CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound). 3. **Vital Signs Data** — Real-time bedside/wearable measurements. 4. **Laboratory / Clinical Data** — Blood tests, biochemistry, pathology reports. 5. **Genomic / Proteomic Data** — DNA sequences, protein expressions. 6. **Electronic Health Records (EHR)** — Comprehensive patient history. 7. **Pharmaceutical / Drug Data** — Dosage, interactions, pharmacokinetics. 8. **Behavioural & Activity Data** — Gait, sleep patterns, exercise from wearables. ### 2.2 The 5V's of Big Data in IoT-Healthcare (KTU High-Yield Concept) | V | Meaning | Healthcare/IoT Implication | |---|---|---| | **Volume** | Petabytes–Exabytes scale | A single hospital may generate **50–100 TB/year** of imaging data | | **Velocity** | Speed of generation/processing | ICU monitors generate data at **>1 kHz per channel** | | **Variety** | Multiple formats & types | Same patient yields ECG + MRI + lab + notes | | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / quality | Noise, artefacts, missing values in sensor streams | | **Value** | Useful insight extracted | Early sepsis prediction from real-time vitals | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Quantitative Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Data-Type Analysis:** | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (**Nyquist Theorem**) | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hz | | 2 | $N_{bits} = f_s \times T \times n_{ch} \times \dfrac{b}{sample}$ | Total data size for a recording | bits | | 3 | $\text{Data Rate} = f_s \times b$ | Bits produced per second per channel | bps | | 4 | $B = \log_2(N)$ | Information content (bits) of N equally-likely outcomes | bits | | 5 | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (information content) of source X | bits/symbol | | 6 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | | 7 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | bps | | 8 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\Delta t}$ | Temporal resolution of a data stream | Hz | > **Notation:** $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency, $T$ = recording duration, $n_{ch}$ = number of channels, $b$ = bits per sample. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Data Storage Selection** — Time-series data → InfluxDB/TimescaleDB; Imaging data → PACS (DICOM); Genomic data → specialized bioinformatics DBs. - **Compression Choice** — Lossless for clinical/legal records (DICOM); lossy acceptable for training datasets. - **Transmission Protocol** — Low-bandwidth sensor data → MQTT; large imaging → HTTP/REST or DICOMweb. - **Edge vs Cloud** — Continuous vitals → processed at edge for latency; large imaging → cloud for archival and AI. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Data Rate and Storage Calculation for a 12-Lead ECG > **Problem:** A 12-lead ECG system samples each lead at $f_s = 500$ Hz with a 12-bit ADC resolution. Each recording lasts $T = 24$ hours, with $n_{ch} = 12$ channels. Compute: > (a) Per-sample bits > (b) Data rate per channel > (c) Total data rate (all 12 channels) > (d) Total data generated in 24 hours > (e) Total storage in **megabytes (MB)** #### Step (a): Per-Sample Bits $$ b = 12 \;\text{bits/sample} $$ **Logic:** The ADC quantises each analog sample into $2^{12} = 4096$ discrete levels, hence 12 bits are required to encode one sample. #### Step (b): Data Rate per Channel $$ R_{ch} = f_s \times b $$ $$ R_{ch} = 500 \;\text{Hz} \times 12 \;\text{bits/sample} $$ $$ R_{ch} = 6000 \;\text{bits/sec} = 6 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Logic:** Every second, 500 samples are taken; each sample is 12 bits wide. #### Step (c): Total Data Rate (12 Channels) $$ R_{total} = n_{ch} \times R_{ch} $$ $$ R_{total} = 12 \times 6000 $$ $$ R_{total} = 72000 \;\text{bits/sec} = 72 \;\text{kbps} $$ **Logic:** Channels operate in parallel; data streams from all 12 leads are multiplexed (or kept as separate time-series). #### Step (d): Total Bits in 24 Hours $$ N_{bits} = R_{total} \times T $$ $$ N_{bits} = 72000 \;\text{bits/sec} \times (24 \times 3600) \;\text{sec} $$ $$ N_{bits} = 72000 \times 86400 $$ $$ N_{bits} = 6{,}220{,}800{,}000 \;\text{bits} $$ #### Step (e): Convert to Megabytes $$ N_{bytes} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{8} $$ $$ N_{bytes} = \dfrac{6{,}220{,}800{,}000}{8} = 777{,}600{,}000 \;\text{bytes} $$ $$ N_{MB} = \dfrac{N_{bytes}}{10^6} = 777.6 \;\text{MB} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A single 24-hour 12-lead ECG recording produces **≈ 777.6 MB** of raw data. This is why **lossless-to-lossy compression** (e.g., wavelet-based ECG compression achieving 10:1 ratios) is critical in hospital IoT storage design. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Choosing Storage / Processing | Data Type | Sampling/Generation Rate | Typical Format | Recommended Storage | Recommended Processing | |---|---|---|---|---| | ECG signal | 250–1000 Hz | `.edf`, `.dat`, DICOM waveform | Time-series DB | DSP filtering, HRV analysis | | EEG signal | 256–512 Hz (up to 32 ch) | `.edf`, BIDS | Time-series / file system | Spectral analysis, ML seizure detection | | MRI image | Static, 3D volume | DICOM (`.dcm`) | PACS server | Deep learning segmentation | | Vital signs (HR, SpO₂) | 1 Hz from wearable | JSON over MQTT | InfluxDB / TimescaleDB | Threshold alerts, anomaly detection | | EHR text | Event-driven, episodic | HL7 FHIR, XML | Relational DB (PostgreSQL) | NLP, ICD coding | | Genomic | Static, batch | FASTA, BAM, VCF | Object storage (S3) | Variant calling, alignment | | Streaming telemetry | Real-time, unbounded | MQTT, Kafka | Stream processor | Online ML inference | ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Information Content of a Discrete Measurement Consider a **blood group measurement** with four possible outcomes: A, B, AB, O. If each is equally likely ($p_i = 0.25$), the information content of one observation is: $$ B = \log_2(N) = \log_2(4) = 2 \;\text{bits} $$ **Logic:** With 2 bits, we can encode 4 distinct states (00, 01, 10, 11) — one per blood group. If, however, population statistics show the probabilities are $p(A) = 0.40$, $p(B) = 0.30$, $p(AB) = 0.10$, $p(O) = 0.20$, the **Shannon entropy** is: $$ H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \log_2 p_i $$ $$ H(X) = -\bigl[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20\bigr] $$ $$ H(X) = -\bigl[0.40 \times (-1.3219) + 0.30 \times (-1.7370) + 0.10 \times (-3.3219) + 0.20 \times (-2.3219)\bigr] $$ $$ H(X) = -\bigl[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.3322 - 0.4644\bigr] $$ $$ H(X) = 1.8465 \;\text{bits/symbol} $$ **Logic:** Entropy is reduced from 2.0 to 1.85 bits because outcomes are no longer equiprobable — more skewed distributions carry less information per symbol. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Biomedical & IoT Data Types ```mermaid graph TD A["BIOMEDICAL AND IOT DATA TYPES"] --> B["BY STRUCTURE"] A --> C["BY SCALE OF MEASUREMENT"] A --> D["BY TEMPORAL BEHAVIOUR"] A --> E["BY CONTINUITY"] A --> F["BY DOMAIN SOURCE"] A --> G["BY 5VS OF BIG DATA"] B --> B1["STRUCTURED: Patient Demographics, Lab Tables"] B --> B2["SEMI-STRUCTURED: HL7 FHIR, DICOM Headers, JSON"] B --> B3["UNSTRUCTURED: MRI, ECG Waveforms, Free Text Notes"] C --> C1["NOMINAL: Blood Group, Gender"] C --> C2["ORDINAL: Pain Scale 1-10"] C --> C3["INTERVAL: Temperature in Celsius, pH"] C --> C4["RATIO: Heart Rate, Weight, SpO2"] D --> D1["TIME SERIES: Wearable Vitals, ECG"] D --> D2["CROSS SECTIONAL: Single Snapshot Vitals"] D --> D3["LONGITUDINAL: Chronic Disease Tracking"] D --> D4["STREAMING: Real-Time ICU Telemetry"] E --> E1["DISCRETE: Step Count, Number of Beats"] E --> E2["CONTINUOUS: BP Waveform, Temperature Curve"] F --> F1["PHYSIOLOGICAL SIGNALS: ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG"] F --> F2["MEDICAL IMAGING: X-Ray, CT, MRI, PET, US"] F --> F3["VITAL SIGNS: HR, BP, SpO2, Temp, RR"] F --> F4["LABORATORY DATA: Blood Tests, Biochemistry"] F --> F5["GENOMIC DATA: DNA Sequences, Variants"] F --> F6["EHR DATA: Patient History, Prescriptions"] F --> F7["BEHAVIOURAL DATA: Gait, Sleep, Activity"] G --> G1["VOLUME: Petabytes of Imaging"] G --> G2["VELOCITY: ICU Real Time Streams"] G --> G3["VARIETY: Multi-Modal Patient Data"] G --> G4["VERACITY: Noise and Missing Values"] G --> G5["VALUE: Actionable Clinical Insights"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT-Biomedical Data Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["DATA SOURCES
Patient, Sensors, Devices, EHR"] --> ACQ["ACQUISITION LAYER
ECG, EEG, MRI, Wearables"] ACQ --> CLASS["DATA CLASSIFICATION
Structured, Unstructured, Signal, Image"] CLASS --> PRE["PRE-PROCESSING LAYER
Filtering, Denoising, Normalization"] PRE --> STORE{"STORAGE ROUTER"} STORE --> TS["TIME-SERIES DB
InfluxDB, TimescaleDB"] STORE --> PACS["PACS DICOM SERVER
Imaging Archive"] STORE --> FHIR["FHIR RELATIONAL DB
EHR Records"] STORE --> OBJ["OBJECT STORAGE
Genomic Files, Video"] TS --> ANALYTICS["ANALYTICS LAYER
ML, Statistics, Threshold Alerts"] PACS --> ANALYTICS FHIR --> ANALYTICS OBJ --> ANALYTICS ANALYTICS --> EDGE["EDGE ALERTS
Low Latency, Critical Care"] ANALYTICS --> CLOUD["CLOUD DASHBOARD
Long Term, Population Health"] EDGE --> USER["CLINICIAN, PATIENT, RESEARCHER"] CLOUD --> USER ``` ### 4.3 Mapping Data Type to Engineering Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR P1["ECG SIGNAL"] --> Q1["Sampling, ADC"] P2["MRI IMAGE"] --> Q2["DICOM Acquisition, Reconstruction"] P3["WEARABLE VITALS"] --> Q3["MQTT, JSON Stream"] P4["EHR TEXT"] --> Q4["HL7 FHIR API"] Q1 --> R1["Time-Series DB + DSP"] Q2 --> R2["PACS + AI Segmentation"] Q3 --> R3["InfluxDB + Anomaly Detection"] Q4 --> R4["PostgreSQL + NLP"] R1 --> S["CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT"] R2 --> S R3 --> S R4 --> S ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Classify the following biomedical measurements into Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, or Ratio scales, justifying each in one line:** (i) Blood group (A, B, AB, O) (ii) Pain rating on a 0–10 visual analog scale (iii) Heart rate in beats per minute (iv) Body temperature in degrees Celsius **Model Answer:** | Measurement | Scale | Justification | |---|---|---| | Blood group | **Nominal** | Categories with no inherent order; only equality is meaningful. | | Pain rating (0–10) | **Ordinal** | Ordered ranks, but the *difference* between 2 and 4 is not necessarily equal to that between 6 and 8. | | Heart rate (bpm) | **Ratio** | Has a true zero (no heartbeat) and equal intervals; ratios are meaningful. | | Body temperature (°C) | **Interval** | Equal intervals between values, but 0 °C is *not* the absence of heat, so ratios are invalid. | *(3 marks: 0.5 per correct identification + 0.25 justification each)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data with one biomedical example for each.** **Model Answer:** | Type | Definition | Biomedical Example | |---|---|---| | **Structured** | Data organized in a predefined tabular schema with rows and columns. | Patient demographic table in Hospital Information System (HIS) with fields: ID, Name, Age, Gender. | | **Semi-structured** | Data has tags or markers to separate semantic elements but no rigid schema. | HL7 FHIR JSON message containing a patient's medication list. | | **Unstructured** | Data has no predefined format; may be binary, textual, or free-form. | A 3D MRI scan in DICOM format or a doctor's narrative discharge summary. | *(3 marks: 1 per correct definition + example pair)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the following (a) or (b). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Explain in detail the different types of biomedical data based on (i) temporal behaviour and (ii) source/domain. Give two examples for each category.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Temporal Behaviour — 4 sub-categories × ~1.75 marks** 1. **Time-Series Data** — Measurements recorded sequentially over time, where order matters. ECG sampled at 500 Hz, continuous SpO₂ from pulse oximeter. 2. **Cross-Sectional Data** — Snapshot at a single time-point. Vital signs recorded at admission; one-time MRI scan. 3. **Longitudinal Data** — Repeated measurements of the same subject over months/years to track disease progression. Annual HbA1c readings for diabetic patients. 4. **Streaming Data** — Unbounded, real-time flow requiring stream processing. ICU multi-parameter monitor streams, wearable heart-rate telemetry. **(ii) Source/Domain — 4 sub-categories × ~1.75 marks** 1. **Physiological Signal Data** — Electrical biosignals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG). 2. **Medical Imaging Data** — Radiology modalities (X-ray, CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound). 3. **Vital Signs Data** — HR, BP, SpO₂, respiratory rate, temperature from bedside monitors. 4. **Genomic / Proteomic Data** — DNA sequences, RNA expression, protein profiles for personalised medicine. > *Award credit for clear sub-headings, correct definitions, and two relevant examples per sub-category.* --- **(b)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an IoT-based biomedical data pipeline. Explain the role of each layer and classify the data flowing through it.** **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.2 — reproduce from there):** **Layer-wise Explanation:** 1. **Sensing / Acquisition Layer (3 marks)** — Wearables, ECG electrodes, pulse oximeters, imaging modalities (MRI, CT) acquire raw signals. Data may be structured (vitals) or unstructured (images). 2. **Communication / Transmission Layer (3 marks)** — Protocols like MQTT, HTTP, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) transmit data from edge devices to gateways and cloud. Choice depends on data type: low-bandwidth MQTT for vitals, DICOMweb for imaging. 3. **Storage Layer (4 marks)** — Data routed based on type: - **Time-series DB** (InfluxDB) for vitals and ECG. - **PACS / DICOM server** for imaging. - **Relational / FHIR DB** (PostgreSQL) for EHR. - **Object storage** (S3) for genomic files and video. 4. **Analytics Layer (2 marks)** — ML/statistical analysis produces alerts, dashboards, and clinical decision support. Edge analytics handle low-latency critical events; cloud analytics handle population-level insights. 5. **Application Layer (2 marks)** — Visualisation to clinicians, patients, and researchers via dashboards, mobile apps, alerts. *(Diagram: 3 marks; Explanation: 11 marks distributed across layers.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain Stevens' four levels of measurement. For each level, give one biomedical example and state which mathematical operations are valid.** **Model Answer:** | Level | Definition | Valid Operations | Biomedical Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Nominal** | Unordered categories | =, ≠ | Blood group, gender, type of disease | | **Ordinal** | Ordered categories with unequal intervals | =, ≠, <, > | NYHA heart-failure class I–IV, pain scale 0–10 | | **Interval** | Ordered, equal intervals, no true zero | =, ≠, <, >, +, − | Body temperature in °C, calendar date | | **Ratio** | Ordered, equal intervals, true zero | All four (+, −, ×, ÷) | Heart rate, weight, blood pressure, SpO₂ | **Key Insight (3 marks):** Only ratio data supports multiplication and division. For example, "Patient B's HR of 160 bpm is *twice* Patient A's 80 bpm" is meaningful because 0 bpm = no heart activity (true zero). But "20 °C is *twice as hot* as 10 °C" is meaningless on the Celsius scale. --- **(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **A multi-parameter ICU monitor samples 8 physiological channels, each at 1000 Hz with a 16-bit ADC. The monitor stores data for 48 hours continuously. Compute:** (i) Per-channel data rate (ii) Total data rate (iii) Total data generated in bits (iv) Total storage in GB **Model Answer:** **(i) Per-channel data rate:** $$ R_{ch} = f_s \times b = 1000 \times 16 = 16000 \;\text{bps} = 16 \;\text{kbps} $$ **(ii) Total data rate:** $$ R_{total} = 8 \times 16000 = 128000 \;\text{bps} = 128 \;\text{kbps} $$ **(iii) Total bits in 48 hours:** $$ T = 48 \times 3600 = 172800 \;\text{sec} $$ $$ N_{bits} = 128000 \times 172800 = 22{,}118{,}400{,}000 \;\text{bits} $$ **(iv) Storage in GB:** $$ N_{bytes} = \dfrac{22{,}118{,}400{,}000}{8} = 2{,}764{,}800{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \approx 2.76 \;\text{GB} $$ $$ N_{GB} = \dfrac{2{,}764{,}800{,}000}{10^9} \approx 2.76 \;\text{GB} $$ *(4 marks: 1.5 per correct part; 2 marks for unit consistency and final answer.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — 48 hours ≠ 48 seconds. Always convert to seconds. > 2. **Byte vs Bit confusion** — 8 bits = 1 byte. Many students divide by 1024 instead of 8, leading to wrong storage. > 3. **Decimal vs Binary GB** — KTU accepts $10^9$ bytes = 1 GB (decimal). Using $2^{30}$ will lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Mixing scales of measurement** — Students often wrongly classify temperature (°C) as ratio scale because of the mathematical "0." Examiners deduct for missing this conceptual trap. > 5. **Generic IoT examples** — Examiners reward *biomedical-specific* examples (ECG, MRI, EHR). Generic "smart home" examples score poorly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorise for KTU 2024 ESE):** - ☐ **Three structural types** — Structured / Semi-structured / Unstructured (with one biomedical example each). - ☐ **Stevens' four scales** — Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio (with one example and valid operations for each). - ☐ **Temporal categories** — Time-series, Cross-sectional, Longitudinal, Streaming (definitions + one example each). - ☐ **Continuity** — Discrete (countable) vs Continuous (any real value). - ☐ **Dimensionality** — Univariate, Multivariate, High-dimensional. - ☐ **Eight biomedical data domains** — Signals, Imaging, Vitals, Lab, Genomic, EHR, Pharmaceutical, Behavioural. - ☐ **5V's of Big Data** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value (with healthcare context). - ☐ **Key formulas** — $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ (Nyquist), $R = f_s \times b$, $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, $SNR_{dB}$, Shannon-Hartley $C$. - ☐ **Storage routing rule** — Time-series → TSDB; Imaging → PACS; Records → FHIR DB; Large files → Object storage. - ☐ **True-zero distinction** — Ratio data is the only scale where multiplication/division yields meaningful results. **Mnemonic for Stevens' Scales → "N O I R":** **N**ominal (Names) → **O**rdinal (Order) → **I**nterval (Intervals equal) → **R**atio (Ratio meaningful, true zero). **Mnemonic for 5V's → "V³ V²":** **V**olume, **V**elocity, **V**ariety, **V**eracity, **V**alue.

topics.title: Characteristics of Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Big Data** refers to extremely large, fast-growing, and diverse collections of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data originating from multiple heterogeneous sources that cannot be effectively captured, stored, managed, processed, or analyzed using traditional relational database systems and conventional data processing tools within an acceptable time frame.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PEEBT632 – IoT & Biomedical Applications)**, Big Data is the high-volume, high-velocity data stream produced continuously by IoT-enabled biomedical sensors, wearable devices, implantable chips, electronic health records (EHR), and remote patient monitoring systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Grade Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> *Big Data is a term that describes the large volume of data – both structured and unstructured – that inundates a business on a day-to-day basis. But it's not the amount of data that's important. It's what organizations do with the data that matters. Big Data can be analyzed for insights that lead to better decisions and strategic business moves.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Multi-Specialty Hospital with 5,000 in-patients**, where every patient is fitted with a smart IoT wearable (ECG patch, pulse oximeter, glucose monitor, temperature sensor, accelerometer). Each sensor samples the body **every 1 second** and uploads readings to a cloud server.

Now imagine **10,000 such hospitals worldwide**, all streaming data 24/7. The total data generated in a single day would be roughly equivalent to the **entire written content of the U.S. Library of Congress — every single day**.

This massive, never-stopping flood of data — coming in many formats (numeric, image, video, text), arriving at lightning speed, and of uncertain accuracy — is what we call **Big Data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Big Data Matters in Biomedical IoT:**
> - Every heartbeat, every oxygen drop, every seizure event is a **data point**.
> - Traditional spreadsheets/databases **cannot store or query** such volumes in real time.
> - Specialized frameworks (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL) are mandatory.

### 1.3 The Six Core Characteristics — The "6 V's" of Big Data

The **Industry-Standard 6 V's** model (often taught as 5 V's in KTU modules) is the universally accepted framework for describing Big Data characteristics:

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume** | The sheer *size* of the data (Terabytes to Zettabytes) |
| 2 | **Velocity** | The *speed* at which data is generated and processed |
| 3 | **Variety** | The different *types and formats* of data |
| 4 | **Veracity** | The *trustworthiness / quality* of the data |
| 5 | **Value** | The *business / clinical worth* extracted from the data |
| 6 | **Variability** | The *change in meaning / rate* of data over time |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking *"List the characteristics of Big Data"* (2 marks) and *"Explain any three V's of Big Data"* (7–8 marks). Memorize **Volume, Velocity, Variety** as the compulsory trio.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Characteristic

#### 2.1.1 Volume (The "How Much")

- Refers to the **magnitude / size** of data generated and stored.
- Modern IoT-Biomedical systems generate data in the range of **Gigabytes (GB) → Terabytes (TB) → Petabytes (PB) → Exabytes (EB) → Zettabytes (ZB)**.
- A single MRI scan can be ~**200 MB**; a genome sequence is ~**200 GB**; continuous ICU monitoring of 1 patient for 1 day ≈ **2–5 GB**.
- **Why it matters:** Traditional RDBMS (e.g., MySQL, Oracle) struggles when data exceeds a few TB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Scale Reference (SI-style):**
> 1 KB = $10^3$ B, 1 MB = $10^6$ B, 1 GB = $10^9$ B, 1 TB = $10^{12}$ B, 1 PB = $10^{15}$ B, 1 EB = $10^{18}$ B, 1 ZB = $10^{21}$ B.

#### 2.1.2 Velocity (The "How Fast")

- Refers to the **rate at which data is produced, transmitted, and processed**.
- Biomedical IoT applications demand **real-time / near-real-time** processing (latency < 1 second for critical care).
- Examples: ECG streaming at **250–1000 Hz**; fetal heart-rate Doppler; defibrillator event logs.
- **Why it matters:** A 5-minute delay in alerting an ICU nurse about arrhythmia could be fatal.

#### 2.1.3 Variety (The "How Many Types")

- Refers to the **diversity of data types and sources**.
- **Three categories:**
  1. **Structured** – tabular EHR records, lab results (rows & columns).
  2. **Semi-Structured** – JSON/XML IoT sensor payloads, HL7/FHIR messages.
  3. **Unstructured** – MRI/CT images, doctor voice notes, pathology slide images, video feeds from endoscopy.
- **Why it matters:** A single patient journey spans all three — unified analytics is hard.

#### 2.1.4 Veracity (The "How Trustworthy")

- Refers to the **quality, accuracy, and reliability** of data.
- Biomedical sensors are prone to **noise, motion artifacts, calibration drift, and missing values**.
- Example: A smartwatch reading SpO₂ = 32% on a healthy runner (motion artifact) — false data.
- **Why it matters:** Wrong medical data → wrong diagnosis → patient harm.

#### 2.1.5 Value (The "What's the Use")

- Refers to the **worth derived from processing Big Data** — insights, predictions, decisions.
- Without analytics, Big Data is just an expensive storage bill.
- Example: Aggregating 10 years of ECG data from 1 million patients to predict **atrial-fibrillation risk** has enormous clinical and commercial value.

#### 2.1.6 Variability (The "How Much Does It Change")

- Refers to the **inconsistency of data flow rate and meaning** over time.
- A patient's heart rate varies with sleep, exercise, stress; seasonal flu outbreaks change hospital admission patterns.
- **Why it matters:** Static models fail; ML models must be retrained continuously.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Quantitative Metrics for Board Calculations:**

| # | Metric / Equation | Formula | Units / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Daily data volume per sensor | $D_d = f_s \times b \times t \times 86400$ | Bytes / day |
| 2 | Total hospital data per day | $D_{total} = N_p \times D_d$ | Bytes / day |
| 3 | Annual data growth | $D_y = D_d \times 365 \times G$ | Bytes / year |
| 4 | Data transmission latency | $t_{lat} = \dfrac{P_{size}}{B_w}$ | Seconds |
| 5 | Storage cost estimation | $C_{store} = D_{total} \times c_{unit}$ | Currency |
| 6 | Compression ratio | $R_c = \dfrac{D_{orig}}{D_{comp}}$ | Dimensionless |

Where:
- $f_s$ = sampling frequency in **Hz**
- $b$ = bits per sample (e.g., 16-bit ADC)
- $t$ = number of channels (e.g., 12-lead ECG)
- $N_p$ = number of patients
- $G$ = annual growth factor
- $P_{size}$ = packet size in **bits**
- $B_w$ = bandwidth in **bits/second**
- $c_{unit}$ = cost per Byte (e.g., \$0.00002)

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Hospitals:** Real-time ICU analytics using Apache Kafka + Spark Streaming.
- **Epidemic Tracking:** Google Flu Trends (Velocity + Volume).
- **Genomic Research:** DNA sequencing pipelines (Volume + Variety).
- **Insurance Fraud Detection:** Cross-referencing claims with sensor logs (Veracity + Value).
- **Remote Surgery Robotics:** Low-latency big-data streams (Velocity).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Volume Calculation for an IoT ICU

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A smart ICU has **200 patients**, each connected to a multi-parameter monitor that samples **5 channels** (ECG, EEG, SpO₂, BP, Temperature) at a sampling rate of **500 Hz**, with **16-bit resolution** per sample.
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Data generated per patient per day (in GB).
> (b) Total data generated by the ICU per day (in TB).
> (c) Total data per year (in PB), assuming a 20% annual growth factor.

#### Step 1 — Define the parameters

$$f_s = 500 \ \text{Hz}, \quad b = 16 \ \text{bits}, \quad t = 5, \quad N_p = 200, \quad G = 1.20$$

#### Step 2 — Compute per-sample data size

Each sample across 5 channels occupies:

$$S_{sample} = b \times t = 16 \times 5 = 80 \ \text{bits} = 10 \ \text{Bytes}$$

*Logic:* Each channel contributes 16 bits, and there are 5 channels per sampling instant.

#### Step 3 — Compute samples per day per patient

$$N_{samples} = f_s \times 86400 = 500 \times 86400 = 4.32 \times 10^{7} \ \text{samples/day}$$

*Logic:* Sampling at 500 Hz for 86,400 seconds (24 hours).

#### Step 4 — Compute per-patient per-day data

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_d &= N_{samples} \times S_{sample} \\
&= 4.32 \times 10^{7} \times 10 \\
&= 4.32 \times 10^{8} \ \text{Bytes/day} \\
&= 432 \ \text{MB/day per patient}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* 1 GB = $10^9$ Bytes, hence $4.32 \times 10^8$ Bytes = 0.432 GB ≈ 432 MB.

#### Step 5 — Compute ICU-wide daily data

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{ICU} &= N_p \times D_d = 200 \times 4.32 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 8.64 \times 10^{10} \ \text{Bytes/day} \\
&= 86.4 \ \text{GB/day} \approx 0.0864 \ \text{TB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute annual data with growth

Year 1: $D_y = 0.0864 \times 365 = 31.54 \ \text{TB/year}$
With 20% growth compounded over a year (effective annual storage including the growth factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{y,\text{adj}} &= D_y \times G = 31.54 \times 1.20 \\
&= 37.85 \ \text{TB/year} \approx 0.03785 \ \text{PB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Just 200 ICU beds generate **~38 TB/year** — far beyond a single MySQL server. This justifies the need for **distributed Big Data frameworks (HDFS, NoSQL, Data Lakes)**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The 6 V's Applied to Biomedical IoT

| V-Characteristic | Biomedical IoT Manifestation | Real Example | Engineering Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Continuous multi-channel biosignals | 12-lead ECG @ 1 kHz → 1.7 GB / patient / day | Storage scalability |
| **Velocity** | Real-time alert generation | Sepsis early-warning system | Stream processing latency |
| **Variety** | Imaging + signals + text | MRI + ECG + clinical notes | Data fusion |
| **Veracity** | Sensor noise / artifacts | Motion-corrupted SpO₂ | Data cleaning, imputation |
| **Value** | Predictive diagnostics | Stroke risk prediction | ML model ROI |
| **Variability** | Circadian / seasonal patterns | Heart rate during sleep vs. exercise | Adaptive modeling |

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Study — Structured vs. Unstructured Data

| Parameter | Structured | Semi-Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed (RDBMS) | Flexible (JSON/XML) | None |
| **Storage** | SQL tables | Document stores (MongoDB) | Data lakes (HDFS) |
| **Biomedical Example** | Lab test results | HL7 FHIR messages | MRI images, doctor notes |
| **Query Speed** | Very fast | Moderate | Slow without indexing |
| **Big Data Share** | ~20% | ~10% | ~70% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Big Data. List any four of its characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Big Data is a collection of data sets so large and complex that it becomes difficult to process using traditional database management tools. It is characterized by high **Volume, Velocity, and Variety**, and requires new processing paradigms such as Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL databases.

**Four Characteristics:**

1. **Volume** – Massive amount of data (TBs to ZBs) generated by IoT biomedical sensors continuously.
2. **Velocity** – Speed of data generation and processing, often in real-time (e.g., ECG streaming).
3. **Variety** – Different data types: structured (EHR), semi-structured (HL7), unstructured (MRI images).
4. **Veracity** – Quality and trustworthiness of data; biomedical signals often contain noise and artifacts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between structured and unstructured data with a biomedical example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Structured Data | Unstructured Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Predefined (tables) | No fixed schema |
| **Storage** | RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) | Data lakes, object stores |
| **Format** | Rows and columns | Images, video, free text |
| **Biomedical Example** | Patient lab reports with fields like Hb, glucose, cholesterol | MRI / CT scan images, doctor's voice notes |
| **Processing** | SQL queries | ML / DL pipelines |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** *Answer ANY ONE of the following:*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **6 V's of Big Data** with biomedical IoT examples for each characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable ECG patch records **3 channels** at a sampling frequency of **250 Hz** with **12-bit resolution**. If **5,000 patients** use the device, calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Data generated per patient per hour (in MB)
- (ii) Total data generated per day (in GB)
- (iii) Comment on why traditional RDBMS is unsuitable for this scale.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **6 V's of Big Data** are:

1. **Volume:** Refers to the huge size of data. In a smart hospital, thousands of sensors generate terabytes of ECG, EEG, and imaging data daily. Example: A single 3D MRI scan ≈ 200 MB; 10,000 scans = 2 TB.

2. **Velocity:** The rate at which data is produced and processed. Wearable ECG patches stream at 250–1000 Hz; ICU ventilators push alerts within milliseconds. Real-time processing is critical in cardiac care.

3. **Variety:** Different data formats. Structured (EHR), semi-structured (HL7 FHIR JSON messages from devices), unstructured (MRI images, surgical videos). Handling all three simultaneously is challenging.

4. **Veracity:** Trustworthiness of data. Biomedical sensors suffer from motion artifacts (e.g., SpO₂ drops during patient movement), calibration drift, and missing values, leading to noisy datasets.

5. **Value:** The clinical insight extracted. Aggregating ECG data from millions of patients enables AI models to predict arrhythmias days before they occur — this is the real "value" of Big Data.

6. **Variability:** The change in data patterns over time. A patient's heart rate varies across sleep, exercise, and stress; disease prevalence changes seasonally (e.g., flu outbreaks).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

*Given:* $f_s = 250$ Hz, $b = 12$ bits, $t = 3$ channels, $N_p = 5000$.

**(i) Data per patient per hour:**

$$S_{sample} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \ \text{bits} = 4.5 \ \text{Bytes}$$

$$N_{samples/hr} = 250 \times 3600 = 9 \times 10^{5} \ \text{samples/hour}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{hr} &= 9 \times 10^{5} \times 4.5 \\
&= 4.05 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Bytes/hour} \\
&= 4.05 \ \text{MB/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total data per day for 5,000 patients:**

$$D_{day,per} = 4.05 \times 24 = 97.2 \ \text{MB/patient/day}$$

$$D_{total} = 5000 \times 97.2 \ \text{MB} = 486000 \ \text{MB} = 486 \ \text{GB/day}$$

**(iii) Why traditional RDBMS is unsuitable:**

Traditional RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle) is designed for **structured, moderate-volume data with fixed schema and ACID transactions**. Big biomedical IoT data is:
- **Too large** (486 GB/day ≈ 177 TB/year) — exceeds single-server RDBMS capacity.
- **Semi/unstructured** (raw sensor streams) — does not fit rigid tables.
- **High-velocity** — RDBMS cannot ingest 250 samples/second × 5000 patients in real time.
- **Schemaless** — new sensor models add new fields constantly.

Hence, distributed systems like **Hadoop HDFS, Apache Cassandra, or InfluxDB** are used instead.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **stages of a Big Data processing pipeline** in a smart-hospital IoT deployment, mentioning at least one technology per stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain how the **6 V's of Big Data** interact in a remote-patient-monitoring system. Justify the need for distributed processing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Big Data processing pipeline for a smart hospital has **five stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition (Sensors):** Wearables, ICU monitors, infusion pumps. *Technology:* BLE, ZigBee, MQTT brokers.

2. **Data Ingestion:** High-throughput message brokers. *Technology:* **Apache Kafka** (handles millions of events/second).

3. **Data Storage:** Distributed, fault-tolerant storage. *Technology:* **HDFS** for raw data, **MongoDB/Cassandra** for sensor metadata.

4. **Data Processing:** Batch + stream analytics. *Technology:* **Apache Spark** (in-memory, 100x faster than Hadoop MapReduce) and **Spark Streaming** for real-time.

5. **Data Visualization & Action:** Dashboards, alerts. *Technology:* **Grafana, Kibana**, integrated with Hospital Information Systems (HIS).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The block diagram (as shown in Section 4.2 of these notes) depicts a 7-layer flow from sensors to clinical decision support.

In a **remote-patient-monitoring (RPM) system**:
- **Volume** is generated continuously by 10,000+ home wearables.
- **Velocity** demands sub-second alert generation for cardiac events.
- **Variety** includes ECG, accelerometer, temperature, GPS location.
- **Veracity** is challenged by home-environment noise.
- **Value** comes from early detection of heart failure decompensation.
- **Variability** reflects day-night sleep cycles and patient compliance variations.

**Need for distributed processing:**
- A **single server** cannot store, process, and serve 10,000 simultaneous streams with millisecond latency.
- **Distributed systems** (Hadoop, Spark) split the workload across **hundreds of commodity nodes**, providing:
  1. **Scalability** (horizontal scaling by adding nodes).
  2. **Fault tolerance** (replication across nodes).
  3. **Parallelism** (MapReduce / Spark RDDs).
  4. **Cost efficiency** (commodity hardware vs. expensive mainframes).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Variety" with "Variability"** — Variety = types of data; Variability = change in data over time. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Always state units** in numerical answers (Bytes, MB, GB, TB). Marks are deducted for "just writing a number."
> 3. **Don't list only 3 V's** if the question asks for 4 or 5. Add **Veracity** and **Value** explicitly to score full marks.
> 4. **Avoid generic answers** like "Big Data is large data" — use textbook-grade definition with the three components: *Volume, Velocity, Variety.*
> 5. **For diagrams**, draw boxes with arrows and **label each layer/block**. Unlabeled diagrams fetch zero marks in the 7-mark sub-parts.
> 6. **Show all formula substitutions** — partial marks are given for correct formula selection even if the final answer slips.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Big Data** = data too big / fast / diverse for traditional RDBMS.
- ✅ **6 V's:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability.
- ✅ **Volume Units:** KB → MB → GB → TB → PB → EB → ZB (powers of $10^3$).
- ✅ **Velocity Unit:** Samples / second (Hz); real-time = sub-second latency.
- ✅ **Variety Types:** Structured / Semi-structured / Unstructured.
- ✅ **Veracity Issue:** Noise, artifacts, missing values — solved by data cleaning.
- ✅ **Value Extraction:** Achieved via ML, AI, predictive analytics.
- ✅ **Variability:** Data changes over time — requires adaptive retraining.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $D_d = f_s \times b \times t \times 86400$ (Bytes/day).
- ✅ **Biomedical Examples:** ECG patches, MRI, ICU monitors, EHR, genomics.
- ✅ **Key Technologies:** Hadoop HDFS, Apache Spark, Kafka, NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra).
- ✅ **Exam Definition:** *Massive volume of structured + unstructured data that cannot be processed by traditional systems.*
- ✅ **Minimum 3 V's to remember for 2-mark questions:** Volume, Velocity, Variety.
- ✅ **For 7-mark questions,** add Veracity + Value and give one biomedical IoT example per V.
- ✅ **Diagram Rule:** Always draw layered architecture (Sensor → Gateway → Ingest → Store → Process → Analyze → Act).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Final Mnemonic (for last-minute revision):**
> **"Very Very Vexing, Vast, Versatile Values"** = **V**olume, **V**elocity, **V**ariety, **V**eracity, **V**alue, **V**ariability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Handling Technologies** in the context of IoT & Biomedical Applications refer to the **end-to-end pipeline** that governs how raw physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO₂, body temperature, blood pressure, etc.) are **acquired, conditioned, digitized, transmitted, stored, processed, secured, and visualized** across heterogeneous nodes in a connected healthcare ecosystem.

The pipeline is formally described by the IEEE 11073 / ISO/IEEE 21451 family of standards and the HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) framework, ensuring **interoperability, fidelity, latency control, and privacy** from the body-worn sensor to the clinician's dashboard.

### 1.1.1 Decomposition of the Data Handling Stack

The stack is conventionally partitioned into **five functional layers**:

| Layer | Function | Biomedical Example |
|-------|----------|--------------------|
| **L1 – Perception / Acquisition** | Capturing analog biosignals via electrodes & transducers | Ag/AgCl ECG electrodes, photoplethysmography (PPG) probe |
| **L2 – Edge Processing / Pre-conditioning** | Amplification, filtering, ADC, local feature extraction | Instrumentation amplifier (INA128), notch filter at 50 Hz |
| **L3 – Communication / Networking** | Wireless transport, gateway routing, protocol translation | BLE 5.0, MQTT, CoAP over 6LoWPAN |
| **L4 – Storage / Cloud / Edge-Cloud Continuum** | Long-term archiving, indexing, query | AWS HealthLake, FHIR-compliant HDFS clusters |
| **L5 – Analytics & Visualization** | ML inference, clinical decision support | LSTM-based arrhythmia classifier, dashboard |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Postal System"

Imagine a **remote village clinic** sending a patient's ECG strip to a cardiologist in a city hospital:

- **Acquisition** = the village nurse **writes** the ECG on a chart (raw signal).
- **Pre-conditioning** = the nurse **cleans the chart, highlights abnormalities, and photocopies it** into a clear format.
- **Encoding / Compression** = the copy is **shrunk into a sealed envelope** (e.g., JPEG2000 for medical imaging, or lossless compression like ZIP for ECG).
- **Transmission** = the envelope is **handed to a courier** (BLE, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G) with a tracking number.
- **Storage** = the cardiologist's office **files the envelope** in a digital cabinet (cloud server, PACS).
- **Analytics** = a specialist **reads the chart**, runs statistics, and writes a **prescription report**.
- **Security** = the envelope is **sealed and tamper-evident**, and only authorized doctors can open it (HIPAA, AES-256, TLS 1.3).

> [!NOTE]
> **Just as a single lost letter can delay treatment, a single dropped or corrupted biosignal packet can trigger a false alarm — or worse, mask a real cardiac event. That is why Data Handling is the most safety-critical layer of any medical IoT system.**
