<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Define Local Analytics in IoT. List any two advantages over cloud-centric analytics.*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Local Analytics refers to the processing, analysis, and decision-making on IoT-generated data at or near the source device (sensor, edge gateway, or fog node), rather than transmitting raw data to a remote cloud server.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Reduced Latency:** Decisions are made within **1–100 ms**, enabling real-time response in safety-critical biomedical applications like defibrillators and insulin pumps.
2. **Bandwidth Efficiency:** Only summarized insights, not raw waveforms, are transmitted, reducing network load by up to **95%** in continuous monitoring scenarios.

*(Additional acceptable points: improved privacy, offline resilience, lower OpEx.)*

---

### **Q2.** *What is a sliding window in stream processing? How does it differ from a tumbling window?*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **sliding window** is a bounded view of an unbounded data stream of fixed size $W$ that advances by a step $S < W$, producing **overlapping** windows. It enables continuous, real-time feature computation with smooth transitions.

A **tumbling window** is a fixed-size window of size $W$ that advances by exactly $W$, producing **non-overlapping**, disjoint segments. It is used for periodic batch-like summaries (e.g., hourly averages).

**Key difference:** Sliding windows overlap (more responsive, higher compute); tumbling windows do not (more efficient, coarser granularity).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Local Analytics Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often write "we send data to cloud and process" — for a local analytics question, this is *off-topic* and loses 4+ marks. Always emphasize **on-device / on-gateway processing**.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Numerical problems are rarely given for this topic, but if asked, students forget to use **Bessel's correction (N-1)** in variance. This loses 1 mark easily.
> - **Pitfall 3:** When asked to compare with cloud, students list only disadvantages of cloud without quantifying local advantages. Examiners want **numbers** (latency in ms, bandwidth in %).
> - **Pitfall 4:** Diagrams without labels fetch partial credit. Always label **arrows, blocks, and units** (e.g., $f_s = 500$ Hz).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

#### (a) *Explain the architecture of a Local Analytics system for a wearable ECG monitor. Include the data flow from sensor acquisition to local arrhythmia detection. (7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sensor Acquisition (1 mark):**
The wearable has a 3-lead ECG dry-electrode front-end. The analog signal is bandpass-filtered (0.5–40 Hz) and digitized by a 12-bit ADC at $f_s = 500$ Hz, satisfying the Nyquist criterion $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ where $f_{\max} \approx 40$ Hz for ECG bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Pre-processing (1 mark):**
A 50/60 Hz notch filter removes mains interference. A 0.5 Hz high-pass filter removes baseline wander. A moving-average low-pass filter (n = 5) smooths high-frequency noise.

**Step 3 — Feature Extraction (2 marks):**
The Pan-Tompkins algorithm runs locally on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 MCU to detect QRS complexes. It computes:
- **Derivative:** $\frac{dx}{dt}$ (slope detector)
- **Squaring function:** $x^2$ (emphasizes high slopes)
- **Moving window integrator:** $MA_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=t-N+1}^{t} x_k$ with $N = 30$ samples (75 ms window)

R-peaks are identified via adaptive thresholding.

**Step 4 — Local Inference (2 marks):**
The R-R interval sequence $\{RR_1, RR_2, \ldots\}$ is fed to a rule-based arrhythmia classifier:
- **Bradycardia:** if $\overline{RR} > 1.5$ s (HR < 40 bpm)
- **Tachycardia:** if $\overline{RR} < 0.5$ s (HR > 120 bpm)
- **Atrial Fibrillation:** if RMSSD of RR intervals > some threshold AND sample entropy indicates irregularity

A TinyML TFLite Micro model (8 KB) can additionally run for higher-order classification (PVC, VT).

**Step 5 — Action and Communication (1 mark):**
On detection of a critical event, the local MCU triggers a haptic buzzer alarm and writes the event to a circular log. Over BLE, a summary (event type, timestamp, 10-sec pre-event waveform) is sent to the paired smartphone, which relays it to the cloud EHR.

#### (b) *With a suitable example, derive the formula for computing a Z-score anomaly score on a streaming sensor window. A temperature sensor records {36.5, 36.7, 36.6, 36.8, 36.5} °C. A new reading of 38.9 °C arrives. Determine if it is anomalous at the 3-sigma level. (7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Z-score formula (1 mark):**

$$
z = \frac{x_{new} - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

where $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are computed over the sliding window of $N$ recent samples.

**Step 2 — Compute the window mean (1 mark):**

$$
\mu = \frac{36.5 + 36.7 + 36.6 + 36.8 + 36.5}{5} = \frac{183.1}{5} = 36.62 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the window variance (Bessel-corrected) (2 marks):**

$$
M_2 = (36.5-36.62)^2 + (36.7-36.62)^2 + (36.6-36.62)^2 + (36.8-36.62)^2 + (36.5-36.62)^2
$$

$$
M_2 = 0.0144 + 0.0064 + 0.0004 + 0.0324 + 0.0144 = 0.068
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{M_2}{N-1} = \frac{0.068}{4} = 0.017, \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{0.017} \approx 0.1304 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Z-score (1 mark):**

$$
z = \frac{38.9 - 36.62}{0.1304} = \frac{2.28}{0.1304} \approx 17.48
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (2 marks):**
Since $\lvert z \rvert = 17.48 \gg 3$, the new reading is **anomalous at a level far exceeding the 3-sigma threshold**. The local analytics module should raise a **fever alarm** and log the event.

**Example utility:** This is the exact logic used in wearable thermometers (e.g., TempTraq, FeverScout) to detect pediatric fever onset.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

#### (a) *Compare and contrast Edge Analytics and Fog Analytics in IoT. Discuss with neat diagrams where each layer sits in the IoT reference architecture. (7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Edge Analytics** is performed on the **device itself or its immediate gateway** (one hop from the sensor). It is characterized by extremely low latency (1–10 ms) and very tight resource constraints (KB of RAM, mW of power).
- **Fog Analytics** is performed on an **intermediate compute node within the LAN** (multi-hop, e.g., a hospital server room or a factory-floor mini-server). It aggregates data from many edge nodes and supports heavier workloads (10–100 ms latency, GB of RAM).

**Step 2 — Architectural Position (2 marks):**
In the standard IoT three-tier reference model (Perception → Network → Application), Edge Analytics sits at the **Perception/Network boundary** on the device side, whereas Fog Analytics sits at the **Network tier** as a compute-augmented middle layer between edge and cloud.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Edge Analytics | Fog Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| Location | On sensor / gateway | LAN-level node |
| Latency | 1–10 ms | 10–100 ms |
| Compute | MCU class | Industrial PC class |
| Power | Battery | Mains |
| Role | Real-time decision | Aggregation, heavy ML |
| Failure Impact | Local only | Affects multiple edges |

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 mark):** *(Student must draw a three-tier vertical stack: Sensors → Edge → Fog → Cloud, with arrows showing data and command flow.)*

#### (b) *Explain the concept of windowing in stream analytics. Derive the moving average filter formula and compute a 3-point MA for the input series {12, 15, 14, 18, 20, 16}. Discuss its use in smoothing biomedical signals. (7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Windowing (1 mark):**
Windowing is the technique of applying analytical functions (mean, RMS, FFT) over a *bounded subset* of an otherwise infinite data stream, thereby converting an unbounded stream into a sequence of finite, computable statistics.

**Step 2 — Derive Moving Average (2 marks):**
For an $n$-point moving average at time $t$:

$$
MA_t = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x_{t-k}
$$

For $n=3$, expanding:

$$
MA_t = \frac{x_t + x_{t-1} + x_{t-2}}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the given series (2 marks):**

| $t$ | $x_t$ | Computation | $MA_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 14 | $(12+15+14)/3$ | **13.67** |
| 4 | 18 | $(15+14+18)/3$ | **15.67** |
| 5 | 20 | $(14+18+20)/3$ | **17.33** |
| 6 | 16 | $(18+20+16)/3$ | **18.00** |

**Step 4 — Biomedical Application (2 marks):**
The simple moving average is widely used to:
- Smooth **EMG signals** to remove high-frequency motor noise.
- Compute **beat-to-beat heart rate** from a sliding window of R-R intervals.
- Provide **baseline estimation** for SpO$_2$ plethysmographic waveforms.
- Implement **low-pass filtering** in resource-constrained MCUs that cannot run a full IIR/FIR filter.

However, a pure MA has a **lag of $(n-1)/2$ samples**, which can be problematic for sharp transients (e.g., QRS detection). The **Exponential Moving Average (EMA)** is preferred when lower lag is needed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This for Last-Minute Review):**

- **Definition (Memorize):** Local Analytics = on-device / on-gateway data analysis and decision-making.
- **Three Layers:** **On-Device → Edge → Fog**, each with progressively more compute and higher latency.
- **Three Pillars Justification:** **Latency, Bandwidth, Privacy** — use this exact trio in any "why local?" question.
- **Pipeline:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Inference → Action.
- **Windowing Types:** **Tumbling** (non-overlapping), **Sliding** (overlapping), **Session** (gap-based).
- **Key Formulas (Must-know cold):**
  - Mean: $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$
  - Variance (Bessel): $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_i - \mu)^2$
  - Z-score: $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$
  - MA: $MA_t = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x_{t-k}$
  - EMA: $EMA_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) EMA_{t-1}$
  - Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$
- **Z = 3 rule:** 99.7% confidence; standard anomaly threshold.
- **TinyML buzzword:** TFLite Micro, ARM CMSIS-NN — know what they are conceptually.
- **Biomedical Examples to Quote:** ECG arrhythmia detection, CGM hypoglycemia alert, SpO$_2$ anomaly, fall detection, smart inhaler.
- **Examiner Pet Peeves (Avoid These):** Saying "data is sent to cloud" in a local-analytics question, omitting units, drawing unlabeled diagrams, using N (not N-1) in variance.
- **One-Liner Closing Statement (use to end any 14-mark answer):** *"Local Analytics enables latency-critical, privacy-preserving, and bandwidth-efficient intelligence at the IoT edge, transforming passive sensors into autonomous decision-makers."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cloud Analytics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cloud Analytics** is the systematic process of analyzing and deriving actionable insights from data that is stored, processed, and managed on distributed cloud computing infrastructure, leveraging scalable computational resources, big data frameworks, and visualization tools to support decision-making across enterprise and research domains. In the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications**, Cloud Analytics specifically refers to the layered analytical framework that ingests high-velocity, high-volume physiological and sensor data streams from heterogeneous IoT endpoints (wearables, ICU monitors, smart implants, environmental sensors) into a cloud platform, where it is cleansed, transformed, statistically modeled, and rendered into clinically or operationally meaningful intelligence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Cloud Analytics is positioned as the post-ingestion intelligence layer of the IoT data stack. Students must understand its **four analytical paradigms**: Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive analytics, along with the **cloud deployment models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) that govern data governance in biomedical contexts. ### 1.2 Core Components of Cloud Analytics The formal definition can be decomposed into five functional pillars: 1. **Data Ingestion Layer** – Acquires raw streams from IoT gateways, HL7/FHIR-compliant hospital systems, and edge devices. 2. **Data Storage Layer** – Utilizes distributed file systems (e.g., HDFS-style object stores) and NoSQL warehouses. 3. **Data Processing Layer** – Performs ETL/ELT operations using parallel compute engines. 4. **Analytical Engine Layer** – Applies statistical, machine learning, and deep learning models. 5. **Visualization & Delivery Layer** – Renders dashboards, alerts, and API-driven outputs to clinicians/researchers. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital Command Center** > Imagine a massive urban hospital with 10,000 patients, each wearing a smartwatch that streams heart rate, SpO₂, glucose, and ECG data every second. A single doctor cannot monitor this torrent of data. Instead, the hospital installs a **"Command Center Wall"** in a control room — a giant screen fed by cloud servers. The wall automatically groups patients by risk, flags anomalies, predicts which patient may deteriorate in the next 6 hours, and even suggests a treatment pathway. > > That "Command Center Wall" is **Cloud Analytics**. The data is collected from patients (IoT), sent to a remote data warehouse (Cloud), processed by algorithms (Analytics), and projected as intelligent visual insights. The doctor is now a **decision-maker backed by intelligence**, not a raw-data observer. ### 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics in Cloud Analytics | Metric | Standard Definition | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Throughput** | Data records processed per unit time | Records/sec | | **Latency** | Time delay between data ingestion and insight generation | **milliseconds (ms)** | | **Data Velocity** | Rate of data generation by IoT endpoints | **MB/s or GB/day** | | **Query Response Time** | Time to retrieve aggregated results | **seconds (s)** | | **Storage Elasticity** | Ability to scale storage on demand | **Petabytes (PB)** | | **Service Availability** | Uptime of the analytics platform | **Percentage (%)** | ### 1.5 Cloud Analytics vs. Traditional On-Premise Analytics | Parameter | Traditional Analytics | Cloud Analytics | |---|---|---| | Infrastructure | Fixed, on-site servers | Virtualized, elastic | | Scalability | Limited, manual | **Auto-scaling, on-demand** | | Cost Model | CapEx (heavy upfront) | **OpEx (pay-per-use)** | | Maintenance | In-house IT team | Managed by provider | | Data Sources | Primarily internal | **Multi-source (IoT, social, EMR)** | | Deployment Time | Weeks to months | **Minutes to hours** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Paradigms of Cloud Analytics (Bloom's: Analyze/Apply) Cloud Analytics, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is structured around **four analytical paradigms** that progress from hindsight to foresight. Each paradigm answers a different cognitive question about the data. #### 2.1.1 Descriptive Analytics — "What Happened?" - **Purpose:** Summarizes historical data into understandable reports, KPIs, and dashboards. - **How it works:** Uses aggregation functions (mean, median, mode, count), OLAP cubes, and statistical summaries. - **Biomedical Example:** A weekly report showing the average heart rate variability (HRV) of post-cardiac patients. #### 2.1.2 Diagnostic Analytics — "Why Did It Happen?" - **Purpose:** Drills down into historical data to identify root causes, correlations, and anomalies. - **How it works:** Employs correlation matrices, drill-down reports, data mining, and root-cause analysis. - **Biomedical Example:** Identifying that a spike in ICU admissions coincided with a regional air-quality index (AQI) deterioration. #### 2.1.3 Predictive Analytics — "What Will Happen?" - **Purpose:** Uses historical patterns to forecast future events or behaviors. - **How it works:** Deploys regression models, time-series forecasting, neural networks, and ensemble methods. - **Biomedical Example:** Predicting the probability of sepsis onset in ICU patients 6 hours before clinical symptoms manifest. #### 2.1.4 Prescriptive Analytics — "What Should We Do?" - **Purpose:** Recommends optimal actions based on predictive outcomes and constraint optimization. - **How it works:** Leverages optimization algorithms, reinforcement learning, and decision-theory models. - **Biomedical Example:** Recommending a personalized drug dosage schedule for a diabetic patient based on predicted glucose curves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Mapping:** Exam questions frequently test the student's ability to **classify a given scenario into the correct paradigm** and to **distinguish the analytical tools** used at each stage. ### 2.2 Cloud Deployment Models for Biomedical IoT The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that the choice of deployment model directly affects data sovereignty, latency, and compliance with healthcare regulations (e.g., HIPAA, India's DISHA Bill, GDPR). | Deployment Model | Description | Biomedical Use Case | Compliance Risk | |---|---|---|---| | **Public Cloud** | Shared infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Population-health analytics, anonymized research datasets | **High** for PHI (Protected Health Information) | | **Private Cloud** | Dedicated infrastructure on-premise | Hospital EHR analytics, ICU telemetry archives | **Low** — full data control | | **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of private + public, with orchestration | Sensitive PHI stays private; ML training on public | **Medium** — depends on architecture | | **Community Cloud** | Shared by multiple healthcare organizations | Multi-hospital research consortiums, cancer registries | **Medium** — collaborative governance | ### 2.3 The Cloud Analytics Data Pipeline (Reference Architecture) The end-to-end pipeline follows the **5-stage reference architecture** prescribed by NIST SP 500-292 and mirrored in the KTU syllabus: 1. **Data Source Stage:** IoT devices, wearable sensors, hospital EMR systems, imaging archives (DICOM). 2. **Data Ingestion Stage:** Message brokers (Kafka-pattern), MQTT bridges, FHIR APIs. 3. **Data Storage Stage:** Data lake (raw) + Data warehouse (structured) + Data mart (departmental). 4. **Data Processing Stage:** Batch processing (MapReduce-style) + Stream processing (Lambda/Kappa architectures). 5. **Analytics & Visualization Stage:** BI dashboards, ML inference engines, alerting systems. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates all quantitative expressions, sizing metrics, and standard relationships that KTU examiners test in numerical or descriptive problems. | # | Concept | Mathematical Expression / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Data Velocity (V)** | $V = \dfrac{D}{T}$ where $D$ = data size, $T$ = time | MB/s, GB/day | | 2 | **Storage Elasticity (S)** | $S = N \times d_i$ where $N$ = number of devices, $d_i$ = per-device data rate | Petabytes (PB) | | 3 | **Latency Bound (L)** | $L = t_{ingest} + t_{process} + t_{query}$ | milliseconds (ms) | | 4 | **Throughput (Th)** | $Th = \dfrac{R_{total}}{T_{window}}$ | Records/sec | | 5 | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{D_{original}}{D_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | **Query Response Time (QRT)** | $QRT = t_{dispatch} + t_{compute} + t_{render}$ | seconds (s) | | 7 | **Service Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | | 8 | **Mean Squared Error (Predictive Analytics)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Used in regression validation | | 9 | **Root Mean Squared Error** | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same unit as target variable | | 10 | **Anomaly Z-Score (Diagnostic Analytics)** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Threshold typically $\vert Z \vert > 3$ | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Delimiter Safety:** In the table above, the absolute value $\vert Z \vert$ uses the `\vert` command (not the pipe `|`) to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in inline math within tables. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Cloud Analytics Application | Impact | |---|---|---| | **Remote Patient Monitoring** | Continuous SpO₂/HR analytics with predictive alerting | Reduces ICU readmission by up to **30%** | | **Epidemic Surveillance** | Aggregated fever/cough data from wearables | Early outbreak detection (e.g., COVID-style) | | **Clinical Trial Recruitment** | Mining EHR for eligible patients via NLP | Accelerates trial enrollment timelines | | **Hospital Asset Tracking** | IoT-RFID + cloud analytics for equipment location | Reduces equipment search time by **40%** | | **Drug Discovery** | Genomic + proteomic data analyzed on cloud HPC | Cuts screening time from years to weeks | | **Smart Prosthetics** | Usage-pattern analytics sent to cloud | Adaptive calibration of prosthetic joints | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Sizing a Cloud Analytics Platform for an IoT Hospital > [!NOTE] > The following worked analytical example mirrors the type of numerical/applied question asked in KTU 2024 ESE Part B (14-mark) papers. It demonstrates how raw sensor parameters are translated into actionable cloud-sizing decisions. #### 3.1.1 Problem Statement A smart hospital deploys **500 ICU beds**, each equipped with a multi-parameter monitor streaming **5 vital signs** (HR, SpO₂, BP, RR, Temperature) at a sampling rate of **1 sample per second per parameter**. Each sample is encoded as a **16-bit integer**. The data is ingested into a cloud analytics platform that batches and stores it. **Compute the following:** (a) Total daily data volume generated by the hospital. (b) The minimum storage capacity required for **1 year** of archival, assuming a **3:1 compression ratio**. (c) The sustained throughput the cloud ingest pipeline must support. #### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Given Parameters:** - Number of ICU beds: $N = 500$ - Vital signs per bed: $P = 5$ - Sampling rate per parameter: $f_s = 1 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ sample/sec}$ - Sample size: $b = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes}$ - Compression Ratio: $CR = 3:1$ - Archival Duration: $T = 1 \text{ year}$ --- **Part (a): Total Daily Data Volume** Step 1 — Compute per-bed per-second data rate: $$ r_{bed} = P \times f_s \times b = 5 \times 1 \times 2 = 10 \text{ bytes/sec} $$ Step 2 — Compute hospital-wide per-second data rate: $$ r_{hospital} = N \times r_{bed} = 500 \times 10 = 5000 \text{ bytes/sec} $$ Step 3 — Convert to daily volume (using $86400$ seconds/day): $$ D_{daily} = r_{hospital} \times 86400 = 5000 \times 86400 $$ $$ D_{daily} = 4.32 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes/day} $$ Step 4 — Convert to Gigabytes (using $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$ bytes): $$ D_{daily} = \dfrac{4.32 \times 10^8}{10^9} = 0.432 \text{ GB/day} $$ > **Answer (a):** The hospital generates approximately **0.432 GB/day**, or roughly **432 MB/day** of raw physiological data. --- **Part (b): Minimum 1-Year Storage Capacity with Compression** Step 1 — Compute annual raw data volume: $$ D_{annual} = D_{daily} \times 365 = 0.432 \times 365 $$ $$ D_{annual} = 157.68 \text{ GB/year} $$ Step 2 — Apply compression. With $CR = 3:1$, the compressed size is: $$ D_{compressed} = \dfrac{D_{annual}}{CR} = \dfrac{157.68}{3} $$ $$ D_{compressed} = 52.56 \text{ GB/year} $$ Step 3 — Add **30% overhead** for metadata, indexes, and replication factor of 3 (industry standard for cloud durability): $$ S_{total} = D_{compressed} \times 1.30 \times 3 $$ $$ S_{total} = 52.56 \times 3.90 = 204.98 \text{ GB} $$ > **Answer (b):** The hospital requires approximately **205 GB of effective cloud storage** for 1 year of compressed, replicated archival. --- **Part (c): Sustained Ingest Throughput** Step 1 — Use the throughput formula: $$ Th = \dfrac{R_{total}}{T_{window}} $$ Step 2 — Total records per second (each "record" = one sample of one parameter): $$ R_{total} = N \times P \times f_s = 500 \times 5 \times 1 = 2500 \text{ records/sec} $$ Step 3 — Equivalently, the byte throughput is: $$ Th_{bytes} = R_{total} \times b = 2500 \times 2 = 5000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 5 \text{ KB/sec} $$ > **Answer (c):** The cloud ingest pipeline must sustain a throughput of **2500 records/sec** (or **5 KB/sec**), which is trivial for modern cloud message brokers (Kafka typically handles millions of records/sec). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Predictive Sepsis Detection Z-Score This walkthrough demonstrates how the **Z-Score anomaly detection formula** from the cheat sheet is applied in a diagnostic-to-predictive cloud analytics pipeline. #### 3.2.1 Problem A patient's heart rate baseline is $\mu = 75$ bpm with standard deviation $\sigma = 8$ bpm. A cloud analytics service monitors streaming HR data and triggers an alert if the Z-score exceeds **2.5** (a clinically significant deviation). **Determine the HR threshold above which an alert is raised.** #### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation Step 1 — Start with the Z-Score formula: $$ Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} $$ Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $x$ (the threshold value): $$ x = \mu + Z \times \sigma $$ Step 3 — Substitute the given values $\mu = 75$, $\sigma = 8$, $Z = 2.5$: $$ x = 75 + 2.5 \times 8 $$ $$ x = 75 + 20 = 95 \text{ bpm} $$ Step 4 — Interpretation: any sustained HR reading **above 95 bpm** triggers a "tachycardia watch" alert in the cloud dashboard. The clinical team receives a notification via the alerting subsystem. > **Answer:** Threshold = **95 bpm**. Readings above this are flagged for clinical review. ### 3.3 Summary Matrix: Analytical Decision Tree for Cloud Analytics Design | Design Question | Analytical Path | Key Formula / Tool | |---|---|---| | How much storage do I need? | Compute $S = N \times d_i \times T$ and divide by $CR$ | $S = \dfrac{N \times d_i \times T}{CR} \times \text{overhead}$ | | What latency is acceptable? | Sum $L = t_{ingest} + t_{process} + t_{query}$ | Real-time: $L < 1$ sec; Batch: $L > 1$ min | | Which analytical paradigm fits? | Map business question to paradigm | What $\rightarrow$ Descriptive; Why $\rightarrow$ Diagnostic; Will $\rightarrow$ Predictive; Should $\rightarrow$ Prescriptive | | Which deployment model? | Match sensitivity to model type | PHI-sensitive $\rightarrow$ Private/Hybrid | | How to detect anomalies? | Apply $Z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ thresholding | $\vert Z \vert > 2.5$ is a common clinical threshold | | How to validate a predictive model? | Compute $MSE$, $RMSE$, or AUC-ROC | Lower $RMSE$ = better fit | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Cloud Analytics Reference Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end cloud analytics pipeline as applied to a biomedical IoT ecosystem, mapping every functional layer from sensor to clinician insight. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[IoT Wearable Layer
Smartwatch ECG Patch] --> B1[Edge Gateway
MQTT Broker] A2[Hospital Devices
ICU Monitor Ventilator] --> B1 A3[Imaging Systems
DICOM MRI CT] --> B2[HL7 FHIR Bridge] B1 --> C1[Cloud Ingestion
Kafka Pattern] B2 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Raw Data Lake
Object Store] C1 --> D2[Stream Processor
Lambda Engine] D1 --> E1[Analytics Engine
ML and Statistical] D2 --> E1 E1 --> F1[Descriptive Module
Dashboards KPIs] E1 --> F2[Diagnostic Module
Root Cause Analysis] E1 --> F3[Predictive Module
Forecasting ML] E1 --> F4[Prescriptive Module
Optimization Engine] F1 --> G1[Clinician Dashboard] F2 --> G1 F3 --> G2[Alert and Notification System] F4 --> G3[Clinical Decision Support] G1 --> H1[End User
Doctor Nurse Admin] G2 --> H1 G3 --> H1 ``` ### 4.2 The Four-Stage Analytics Maturity Model This diagram depicts how organizations progress through the four analytical paradigms, with Cloud Analytics serving as the enabling infrastructure. ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Stage 1 Descriptive
What Happened] --> L2[Stage 2 Diagnostic
Why It Happened] L2 --> L3[Stage 3 Predictive
What Will Happen] L3 --> L4[Stage 4 Prescriptive
What Should We Do] L1 --> M1[Reports
Static Charts] L2 --> M2[Drill Down
Correlation] L3 --> M3[Forecasts
Risk Scores] L4 --> M4[Action Plans
Recommendations] L4 --> N1[Clinical Decision Support System] L3 --> N1 L2 --> N1 L1 --> N1 ``` ### 4.3 Cloud Deployment Model Decision Tree for Biomedical Data ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Biomedical Data to Process] --> Q1{Is data PHI or
patient identifiable?} Q1 -->|Yes Highly Sensitive| Q2{Is real time
latency under 10 ms required?} Q1 -->|No Anonymized or Research| Public[Public Cloud
AWS Azure GCP] Q2 -->|Yes| Private[Private Cloud
On Premise Hospital] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need elastic scaling
for ML workloads?} Q3 -->|Yes| Hybrid[Hybrid Cloud
Sensitive on Premise
ML on Public] Q3 -->|No| Private Q4{Multi hospital
collaboration needed?} Q4 -->|Yes| Community[Community Cloud
Consortium Model] Q4 -->|No| Private ``` ### 4.4 Data Flow Through Lambda Architecture (Batch + Speed Layer) ```mermaid flowchart TB Source[IoT Data Source] --> Ingest[Ingestion Layer] Ingest --> Speed[Speed Layer
Real Time Stream] Ingest --> Batch[Batch Layer
Historical Reprocessing] Speed --> Serve1[Serving Layer Hot Path] Batch --> Serve2[Serving Layer Cold Path] Serve1 --> Query[Query and Analytics] Serve2 --> Query Query --> Output[Insight and Action] ``` ### 4.5 Cloud Analytics Feedback Loop for Continuous Improvement ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Collect Data] --> B[Store in Cloud] B --> C[Process and Clean] C --> D[Apply Analytics] D --> E[Generate Insight] E --> F[Clinical Action] F --> G[Outcome Recorded] G --> A G -.-> H[Model Retraining Trigger] H -.-> D ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define Cloud Analytics. List its four analytical paradigms in the order of increasing cognitive complexity.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (1 Mark):** Cloud Analytics is the process of analyzing data stored and processed on cloud infrastructure to extract actionable insights, leveraging scalable compute, storage, and ML services. > **Four Paradigms in order (2 Marks):** > 1. **Descriptive Analytics** — "What happened?" (reports, KPIs, dashboards) > 2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — "Why did it happen?" (root-cause, correlation, drill-down) > 3. **Predictive Analytics** — "What will happen?" (forecasting, ML models) > 4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — "What should we do?" (optimization, recommendations) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Differentiate between a Public Cloud and a Private Cloud in the context of biomedical data analytics. Give one biomedical use case for each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Public Cloud (1.5 Marks):** Infrastructure is owned and operated by a third-party provider (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP) and shared across multiple tenants. **Biomedical Use Case:** Population-health analytics on anonymized datasets for epidemic surveillance. > > **Private Cloud (1.5 Marks):** Infrastructure is dedicated to a single organization (e.g., a hospital's on-premise data center), offering greater control and compliance. **Biomedical Use Case:** In-hospital analytics of ICU telemetry and EHR data containing Protected Health Information (PHI). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE Part B questions on Module 4 typically carry **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Two alternative choices (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below. The student answers **either** Q-A **or** Q-B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cloud Analytics Architecture & Sizing** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer reference architecture of Cloud Analytics** as applied to an IoT-based remote patient monitoring system. Clearly state the function of each layer. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A smart-hospital wearable program deploys **1,000 patient wearables**, each transmitting a **12-lead ECG at 250 Hz**, with **24-bit resolution per sample**. The cloud analytics platform must store this data for **5 years** with a **4:1 compression ratio** and a **replication factor of 3**. Calculate the **total compressed, replicated storage requirement** in Terabytes (TB). **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Architecture (7 Marks):** > The five-layer reference architecture is depicted below and explained layer-by-layer: 1. **Data Source Layer (1 Mark):** Wearables, ICU monitors, and home sensors generate raw physiological data (ECG, SpO₂, temperature, etc.). They communicate via BLE/Wi-Fi/4G-5G to edge gateways. 2. **Data Ingestion Layer (1 Mark):** Edge gateways and HL7/FHIR bridges forward data to cloud message brokers (e.g., Kafka-pattern systems) that buffer and decouple producers from consumers, ensuring no data loss during traffic spikes. 3. **Data Storage Layer (1.5 Marks):** A hybrid storage strategy is used — a **Data Lake** (raw, schema-on-read object store) for archival, and a **Data Warehouse** (structured, columnar) for analytical queries. This dual approach supports both retrospective and real-time analytics. 4. **Data Processing Layer (1.5 Marks):** Two parallel paths: - **Batch Processing** — runs periodic MapReduce-style jobs for historical aggregation. - **Stream Processing** — runs Lambda/Kappa-style engines for real-time event handling (e.g., arrhythmia detection within 1 second of occurrence). 5. **Analytics & Visualization Layer (2 Marks):** Houses the four analytical paradigms (Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive). Output is delivered as: - **Dashboards** (BI tools) for clinicians, - **Alerts** (SMS, push notifications) for critical events, - **APIs** for integration with Hospital Information Systems (HIS). --- **Part (b) — Storage Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Number of wearables: $N = 1000$ - Sampling rate: $f_s = 250$ Hz - Resolution: $r = 24$ bits $= 3$ bytes - Leads per device: $L = 12$ - Archival duration: $T = 5$ years - Compression Ratio: $CR = 4:1$ - Replication Factor: $RF = 3$ **Step 1 — Per-device per-second data rate (1 Mark):** $$ r_{device} = L \times f_s \times r = 12 \times 250 \times 3 $$ $$ r_{device} = 9000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 9 \text{ KB/sec} $$ **Step 2 — Total per-second rate across all devices (1 Mark):** $$ r_{total} = N \times r_{device} = 1000 \times 9000 = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/sec} $$ **Step 3 — Daily data volume (1 Mark):** $$ D_{daily} = r_{total} \times 86400 = 9 \times 10^6 \times 86400 $$ $$ D_{daily} = 7.776 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day} = 777.6 \text{ GB/day} $$ **Step 4 — 5-year raw data volume (1 Mark):** $$ D_{5yr} = D_{daily} \times 365 \times 5 = 777.6 \times 1825 $$ $$ D_{5yr} = 1,419,120 \text{ GB} \approx 1.419 \text{ PB} $$ **Step 5 — Apply compression (1 Mark):** $$ D_{compressed} = \dfrac{D_{5yr}}{CR} = \dfrac{1,419,120}{4} = 354,780 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 6 — Apply replication factor (1 Mark):** $$ S_{total} = D_{compressed} \times RF = 354,780 \times 3 = 1,064,340 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to Terabytes (1 Mark):** $$ S_{total} = \dfrac{1,064,340}{1024} \approx 1039.4 \text{ TB} $$ > **Final Answer:** The hospital requires approximately **1039 TB (~1 PB)** of effective cloud storage for 5 years of compressed, replicated ECG archival. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Analytical Paradigms & Predictive Modeling** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the four analytical paradigms of Cloud Analytics — Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive — with **one biomedical IoT example** for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A hospital's cloud analytics service tracks patient **SpO₂** readings. The baseline mean is $\mu = 97\%$ with $\sigma = 1.5\%$. An alert is raised when the Z-score magnitude exceeds **2.0**. **(i)** Compute the **lower and upper alert thresholds** for SpO₂. **(ii)** If a patient's SpO₂ drops to **93%**, calculate the Z-score and **interpret the clinical significance**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — The Four Paradigms (7 Marks):** > **1. Descriptive Analytics (1.75 Marks):** Answers "What happened?" by summarizing historical data. > - *Biomedical Example:* A daily dashboard showing the average heart rate, sleep duration, and step count of 5,000 enrolled cardiac patients in a remote-monitoring program. > **2. Diagnostic Analytics (1.75 Marks):** Answers "Why did it happen?" by identifying root causes and correlations. > - *Biomedical Example:* Drilling into a 30% spike in readmissions last quarter to discover it correlates with patients who skipped their post-discharge telehealth check-ins. > **3. Predictive Analytics (1.75 Marks):** Answers "What will happen?" using ML models trained on historical data. > - *Biomedical Example:* A trained model predicting the 30-day readmission risk score for each discharged patient, enabling proactive outreach. > **4. Prescriptive Analytics (1.75 Marks):** Answers "What should we do?" by recommending actions that optimize outcomes. > - *Biomedical Example:* A reinforcement-learning engine that adjusts the insulin-dosage schedule of a Type-1 diabetic patient in real time based on predicted glucose trajectories. --- **Part (b) — Z-Score Thresholding for SpO₂ Monitoring (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $\mu = 97\%$ - $\sigma = 1.5\%$ - Threshold: $\vert Z \vert > 2.0$ **Part (b)(i) — Compute Alert Thresholds (3 Marks):** The Z-score formula rearranged for $x$ is: $$ x = \mu + Z \times \sigma $$ **Upper threshold** (using $Z = +2.0$): $$ x_{upper} = 97 + 2.0 \times 1.5 = 97 + 3.0 = 100\% $$ (Note: SpO₂ is capped at 100%, so the practical upper bound is 100%.) **Lower threshold** (using $Z = -2.0$): $$ x_{lower} = 97 + (-2.0) \times 1.5 = 97 - 3.0 = 94\% $$ > **Answer:** Alert triggers when SpO₂ falls **below 94%** or (theoretically) exceeds **100%** (which is physiologically impossible, so the practical alert is on the lower side). **Part (b)(ii) — Calculate Z-Score at 93% and Interpret (4 Marks):** Step 1 — Apply the Z-Score formula: $$ Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \dfrac{93 - 97}{1.5} = \dfrac{-4}{1.5} $$ $$ Z = -2.67 $$ Step 2 — Check threshold: $$ \vert Z \vert = 2.67 > 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Alert is triggered.} $$ Step 3 — **Clinical Interpretation (2 Marks):** > A SpO₂ of 93% represents a deviation of **2.67 standard deviations below the patient's baseline**, which is statistically and clinically significant. In clinical practice, sustained SpO₂ below 94% indicates **hypoxemia**, a potentially dangerous condition requiring immediate clinical attention (e.g., oxygen supplementation, ventilator adjustment, or escalation to ICU). The cloud alerting system should immediately notify the on-duty clinician with patient ID, timestamp, and recommended action protocol. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Cloud Analytics Questions** > > 1. **Mixing up the four paradigms** — Students often confuse *Diagnostic* with *Predictive*. Remember: Diagnostic looks **backward** ("why did this happen?") while Predictive looks **forward** ("what will happen?"). Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 2. **Unit conversion errors in sizing problems** — The most common error is forgetting to convert bits $\rightarrow$ bytes, or bytes $\rightarrow$ GB/TB. Always state the conversion factor explicitly: $8 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ byte}$, $1024 \text{ GB} = 1 \text{ TB}$ (or $1000$ for SI units — follow the convention used in the question). > 3. **Forgetting the replication factor in storage calculations** — Cloud storage is rarely stored with replication factor of 1. Always multiply the compressed size by 2 or 3 unless explicitly told otherwise. > 4. **Confusing public vs. private cloud for PHI** — Patient Health Information (PHI) should never be stored in a public cloud without anonymization. Examiners reward students who explicitly mention this compliance consideration. > 5. **Skipping the formula derivation** — In numerical problems, KTU expects you to **show the formula first**, then substitute values, then compute. Skipping the formula earns only partial marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cloud Analytics (Module 4)** > > ✅ **Definition:** Cloud Analytics = analysis of data on cloud infrastructure for actionable insights. > > ✅ **Four Paradigms (in order):** Descriptive $\rightarrow$ Diagnostic $\rightarrow$ Predictive $\rightarrow$ Prescriptive. Each answers a deeper "why/what" question than the previous. > > ✅ **Five-Layer Architecture:** Data Source $\rightarrow$ Ingestion $\rightarrow$ Storage $\rightarrow$ Processing $\rightarrow$ Analytics & Visualization. > > ✅ **Four Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community. **PHI should remain in Private or Hybrid clouds.** > > ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:** > - Data Velocity: $V = D / T$ > - Total Storage: $S = (N \times d_i \times T) / CR \times RF \times \text{overhead}$ > - Latency: $L = t_{ingest} + t_{process} + t_{query}$ > - Z-Score: $Z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$ > - Availability: $A = \text{Uptime} / (\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime})$ > > ✅ **Data Lake vs. Data Warehouse:** Lake = raw, schema-on-read, cheap; Warehouse = structured, schema-on-write, expensive but fast for analytics. > > ✅ **Lambda vs. Kappa Architecture:** Lambda = batch + speed layer (two paths); Kappa = stream-only with replay (single path). > > ✅ **Biomedical IoT Use Cases:** Remote patient monitoring, epidemic surveillance, clinical trial recruitment, sepsis prediction, smart prosthetics calibration. > > ✅ **Compliance:** HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), DISHA (India) all govern PHI handling — prefer Private/Hybrid cloud for sensitive data. > > ✅ **Common Exam Traps:** Forgetting unit conversions, skipping replication factors, mixing diagnostic with predictive paradigms.

topics.title: Applications. IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings: Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs. Web Server: Web Server for IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications. IoT Physical Servers and Cloud Offerings: Introduction to Cloud Storage Models and Communication APIs. Web Server: Web Server for IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 IoT Applications — Formal Definition

An **IoT Application** is a software-driven service that ingests telemetry data from heterogeneous physical devices (sensors, actuators, embedded systems), processes it locally or remotely, and translates the derived insights into actionable outputs such as alerts, automated control commands, analytics dashboards, or business decisions. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, IoT applications are categorized into **Consumer IoT (CIoT)** and **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** domains, spanning verticals such as smart homes, smart healthcare, smart cities, smart agriculture, smart retail, and smart energy grids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This topic directly maps to **CO4** of PEEBT632 — *"Design and develop IoT systems with appropriate cloud infrastructure, communication APIs, and web server integration."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Post-Office"

Imagine a **post office** that never sleeps. Citizens (IoT devices) drop letters (data packets) into a giant mailbox (gateway/router). The postmaster (cloud server) sorts the letters, files them in categorized shelves (cloud storage models), and forwards urgent ones to relevant departments (Communication APIs) for action. Citizens can also check the status of their letters online through a public notice board (Web Server for IoT).

- The **Citizens** = Sensors/Things
- The **Mailbox** = Local Gateway / Edge Device
- The **Postmaster** = Cloud Server
- The **Shelves** = Cloud Storage Models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS)
- The **Departments** = Communication APIs (REST, SOAP, WebSocket, MQTT)
- The **Notice Board** = Web Server / Dashboard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A **Cloud Offering** in the IoT context is a remotely-hosted, on-demand, elastic computing, storage, and networking resource delivered over the public internet using a **pay-per-use** pricing model, enabling IoT deployments to scale without upfront infrastructure investment.

### 1.3 IoT Physical Servers vs. Cloud Servers

| Parameter | Physical (On-Premise) Server | Cloud Server |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | In-house data center | Remote datacenter (AWS, Azure, GCP) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (manual hardware upgrade) | **Elastic, auto-scaling** |
| **Cost Model** | High CapEx, low OpEx | **Pay-as-you-go (OpEx)** |
| **Maintenance** | In-house IT team | Vendor-managed |
| **Accessibility** | LAN/VPN bound | **Global HTTP/HTTPS** access |
| **Use Case** | Sensitive data (hospitals) | Scalable consumer apps |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metric used in KTU papers: **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** and **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — critical cloud SLAs measured in **seconds** for hot standby, **minutes** for warm standby.

### 1.4 Introduction to Cloud Storage Models

Cloud Storage Models define the abstraction layer at which a user interacts with computing resources. The **NIST SP 800-145** standard defines the three primary service models universally tested in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST-Standard Cloud Service Models (Memorize for 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service** (raw VMs, storage, networks)
> 2. **PaaS — Platform as a Service** (runtime + middleware + OS abstraction)
> 3. **SaaS — Software as a Service** (end-user ready applications)

A **fourth model**, **FaaS — Function as a Service** (Serverless), is the modern KTU 2024 addition worth **2-3 marks** in descriptive questions.

### 1.5 Communication APIs — Definition

A **Communication API (Application Programming Interface)** in IoT is a contractual, language-agnostic interface definition that allows two software systems (typically a device and a cloud backend, or two backends) to exchange structured data over a network using a standardized protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The four API communication paradigms you **must** know are **REST (Representational State Transfer)**, **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)**, **WebSocket**, and **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cloud Storage Models — Detailed Architecture Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
- **What you get:** Virtual machines (VMs), raw block storage, virtual networks, load balancers, firewalls.
- **What you manage:** Operating system, middleware, runtime, application, data.
- **Vendor manages:** Physical hardware, hypervisor, host OS, networking, power, cooling.
- **IoT Use Case:** Running a custom MQTT broker on a VM for millions of device connections.
- **Leading Vendors:** **AWS EC2**, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine (GCE), DigitalOcean Droplets, Linode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Renting an empty plot of land — you build the house, lay the plumbing, and furnish it yourself. The landlord only provides the plot and basic utilities.

#### 2.1.2 Platform as a Service (PaaS)
- **What you get:** Managed runtime (Node.js, Python, Java), databases (PostgreSQL, MongoDB), message queues, CI/CD pipelines.
- **What you manage:** Application code, data.
- **Vendor manages:** OS, runtime, middleware, hypervisor, hardware.
- **IoT Use Case:** Deploying an ingestion microservice using AWS Elastic Beanstalk or Google App Engine.
- **Leading Vendors:** **AWS Elastic Beanstalk**, Heroku, Google App Engine, Azure App Service, Red Hat OpenShift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Renting a fully-furnished apartment with electricity and water pre-connected. You only move in your furniture (code) and live there.

#### 2.1.3 Software as a Service (SaaS)
- **What you get:** A complete, ready-to-use application accessed via browser or thin client.
- **What you manage:** User configuration, your data inside the app.
- **Vendor manages:** Everything below the application (OS, runtime, middleware, hardware, networking).
- **IoT Use Case:** AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT Platform, ThingSpeak, Blynk, Ubidots dashboards.
- **Leading Vendors:** **Salesforce**, Office 365, Google Workspace, Dropbox, Slack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Checking into a hotel — you use the room as-is, the hotel manages the building, housekeeping, and maintenance. You only bring your luggage (data).

#### 2.1.4 Function as a Service (FaaS) / Serverless
- **What you get:** Stateless, event-driven compute functions billed in **milliseconds** of execution.
- **What you manage:** Function code only.
- **Vendor manages:** Container, runtime, OS, hardware, scaling, patching.
- **IoT Use Case:** Triggering a Lambda function the moment a temperature sensor crosses 80°C.
- **Leading Vendors:** **AWS Lambda**, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.

#### 2.1.5 Cloud Deployment Models (Often Confused with Service Models — High Exam Yield!)

| Model | Description | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Shared infrastructure over public internet | AWS, Azure, GCP |
| **Private Cloud** | Dedicated to a single organization | On-premise OpenStack |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combines public + private with orchestration | AWS Outposts |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common goals | Government cloud (MeghRaj) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently confuse **Service Models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS)** with **Deployment Models (Public/Private/Hybrid)**. KTU examiners test this distinction regularly.

### 2.2 Communication APIs — Architectural Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 REST (Representational State Transfer)
- **Architectural Style:** Stateless, client-server, cacheable, layered system.
- **Data Format:** **JSON (preferred)** or XML.
- **Transport Protocol:** **HTTP/HTTPS** on top of TCP.
- **Methods:** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`.
- **IoT Use Case:** Fetching latest sensor reading from a cloud endpoint.
- **Example Endpoint:** `https://api.iotplatform.com/v1/devices/1234/telemetry`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REST Constraints (Fielding's Dissertation, 2000 — Frequently Asked):**
> 1. Client-Server separation
> 2. Statelessness
> 3. Cacheability
> 4. Layered System
> 5. Uniform Interface
> 6. Code on Demand (optional)

#### 2.2.2 SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)
- **Protocol Type:** Heavier, XML-only, formal WSDL contract.
- **Transport:** HTTP, SMTP, TCP.
- **IoT Use Case:** Legacy enterprise healthcare/financial integrations.
- **Why NOT preferred for IoT:** High payload overhead (~10x REST), strict typing overhead.

#### 2.2.3 WebSocket
- **Connection Type:** Full-duplex, **persistent**, single TCP socket.
- **Handshake:** HTTP Upgrade request, then switches to `ws://` or `wss://`.
- **IoT Use Case:** Real-time dashboard updates, live video from surveillance cameras.
- **Advantage over REST:** No polling — server pushes data instantly.

#### 2.2.4 MQTT (Bonus — Frequently Tested with Web Server)
- **Type:** Lightweight **publish-subscribe** messaging protocol.
- **Transport:** TCP (port 1883) or TLS (port 8883).
- **Components:** **Broker**, **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, **Topics**.
- **QoS Levels:** 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).
- **IoT Use Case:** Battery-powered sensors reporting every 10 seconds.

### 2.3 Web Server for IoT — Definition

A **Web Server for IoT** is a software/hardware stack that hosts an **HTTP/HTTPS endpoint** capable of accepting RESTful requests from IoT devices, executing server-side logic, and returning structured responses (JSON/XML) — effectively acting as the bidirectional bridge between the physical IoT layer and the application/dashboard layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Meanings (Both tested in KTU):**
> 1. **Software Definition:** Apache, Nginx, Node.js (Express), Flask.
> 2. **IoT-Specific Definition:** A lightweight web server (e.g., **ESP8266 Web Server, Arduino Web Server, Raspberry Pi Nginx**) hosted **on the edge device itself**, serving sensor data over a local Wi-Fi network.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloud SLA Uptime** | $\text{Uptime \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | % | AWS offers 99.99% = "four nines" |
| **Annual Downtime (Four Nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525{,}600$ | minutes/year | ≈ **52.56 min/year** |
| **Annual Downtime (Three Nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - 0.999) \times 525{,}600$ | minutes/year | ≈ **525.6 min/year** |
| **MQTT Payload Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{useful}} + P_{\text{header}}}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | MQTT: 2-byte fixed header |
| **HTTP Header Overhead** | $O_{\text{HTTP}} = H_{\text{header}} + P_{\text{body}}$ | bytes | REST typical: 200-800 byte headers |
| **Polling vs WebSocket Latency** | $T_{\text{polling}} = \frac{n \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ (avg) | seconds | WebSocket ≈ **<100 ms** |
| **Storage Tiering Cost** | $C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot S_i$ | $/month | Hot ($0.023/GB) vs Cold ($0.004/GB) |
| **REST Verbs Mapping** | `GET`=Read, `POST`=Create, `PUT`=Update, `DELETE`=Remove | — | CRUD operations |
| **MQTT Topic Wildcards** | `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level) | — | `home/+/temp` matches all rooms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The Annual Downtime formula is a **billion-dollar corporate SLA metric** — Amazon's S3 outage in 2017 cost an estimated **$150M+** in downstream enterprise losses, directly traceable to a "four nines" SLA breach.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from SLA Uptime

> **Problem:** A cloud provider advertises **99.95%** monthly uptime. Calculate the maximum permissible annual downtime in **minutes**.

**Step 1 — Identify Total Time in a Non-Leap Year**

A standard year contains:

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Uptime as a Decimal Fraction**

For an SLA of **99.95%**:

$$
U = \frac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Downtime Fraction**

$$
D_{\text{fraction}} = 1 - U = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Annual Permitted Downtime**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = D_{\text{fraction}} \times T_{\text{year}} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ min } 48 \text{ sec}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This derived value (262.8 min) is the **maximum** downtime allowed under SLA. If actual downtime exceeds this, the customer is entitled to service credits per the SLA contract — a direct question in 14-mark KTU problems on cloud economics.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cloud Service Model Comparison (Direct KTU 14-Mark Format)

| Layer of the Stack | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | FaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | User | User | User | Vendor | User (functions) |
| **Data** | User | User | User | Vendor | User |
| **Runtime** | User | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |
| **Middleware** | User | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |
| **OS** | User | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |
| **Virtualization** | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |
| **Server & Storage** | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |
| **Networking** | User | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor |

**Reading the matrix:** Moving right means **less operational burden** but **less control**.

### 3.3 REST API Request/Response — Full Walkthrough

**Scenario:** A Raspberry Pi (client) fetches the latest temperature from a Thingspeak channel.

**Step 1 — Client Constructs HTTP GET Request**

```
GET /channels/12345/fields/1/last.json HTTP/1.1
Host: api.thingspeak.com
Accept: application/json
Connection: keep-alive
```

**Step 2 — Server-Side Processing**

The web server (Nginx) forwards the request to the **application logic layer** (PHP/Node.js), which queries the **time-series database** (InfluxDB/PostgreSQL) and formats the response.

**Step 3 — Server Returns HTTP 200 OK with JSON Body**

```
{
  "created_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "field1": "27.4"
}
```

**Step 4 — Client Parses JSON and Acts**

The Raspberry Pi parses the `field1` value and, if `> 30°C`, energizes a relay to switch ON a cooling fan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** 14-mark questions often ask you to **diagram a complete REST request lifecycle** including header inspection, JSON parsing, and the device action triggered.

### 3.4 MQTT Publish-Subscribe — Step-by-Step Message Flow

**Step 1 — Device Connects to Broker**

The sensor sends a `CONNECT` packet with `clientId`, `username`, `password`, `keepalive=60s`, and `cleanSession=true`.

**Step 2 — Device Subscribes to Topic**

```
SUBSCRIBE → topic: "hospital/ward5/bed12/temp", QoS: 1
```

**Step 3 — Another Device Publishes Data**

```
PUBLISH → topic: "hospital/ward5/bed12/temp", payload: "38.7", QoS: 1
```

**Step 4 — Broker Routes Message to All Subscribers**

**Step 5 — Subscriber Acknowledges with PUBACK** (for QoS 1)

### 3.5 Web Server Hosting on an Edge IoT Device (ESP8266 Example)

**Step 1 — ESP8266 Boots and Connects to Wi-Fi**

**Step 2 — Initializes a Web Server on Port 80**

The ESP8266 WebServer library starts an HTTP listener.

**Step 3 — Registers URL Handlers**

A handler is bound to the URL `/temperature` which, when called, returns a JSON string built from the DHT22 sensor's latest reading.

**Step 4 — A Mobile App Sends HTTP GET**

```
http://192.168.1.45/temperature
```

**Step 5 — ESP8266 Responds**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{"temperature": 28.6, "humidity": 64.2}
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **direct 7-mark KTU sub-question** — "Explain with a diagram how an ESP8266 acts as a web server for IoT."

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the three NIST-standard cloud service models. Give one IoT use case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** Special Publication 800-145 defines three cloud service models:
>
> 1. **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** — Provides virtualized computing resources such as VMs, storage, and networks over the internet. The user manages the OS, runtime, and application, while the vendor manages the physical infrastructure. *IoT Use Case:* Hosting a custom MQTT broker on AWS EC2 to handle millions of sensor connections.
>
> 2. **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** — Provides a managed platform including OS, runtime, middleware, and development tools. The user only deploys application code and data. *IoT Use Case:* Deploying an ingestion microservice on Google App Engine that processes telemetry from a smart agriculture deployment.
>
> 3. **Software as a Service (SaaS)** — Provides ready-to-use, end-user applications accessed via a thin client or browser. *IoT Use Case:* Using ThingSpeak or AWS IoT Dashboard to visualize temperature data without writing backend code.
>
> (3 marks — 1 mark definition × 3 models)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Compare REST and SOAP APIs in the context of IoT communication. Mention any two key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Format** | JSON or XML (JSON preferred) | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP |
| **Payload Overhead** | Low (~few hundred bytes) | High (~kilobytes, due to envelope) |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Can be stateful (WS-ReliableMessaging) |
| **Contract** | Informal (OpenAPI spec) | Formal WSDL contract |
| **IoT Suitability** | High (lightweight) | Low (overhead unsuitable for low-bandwidth sensors) |

REST is **preferred for IoT** because of its **lightweight JSON payloads** and **stateless HTTP transport**, which suits bandwidth-constrained, intermittent-connectivity devices. SOAP's XML envelope and WSDL overhead make it impractical for battery-powered sensors.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cloud Service Models + IoT Web Server

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS) with a **shared-responsibility diagram** and one IoT example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain how an **ESP8266/NodeMCU acts as a web server for IoT**. Show the request flow from a mobile browser to the device and back.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the Stack Model**

The cloud computing stack is typically divided into eight layers: Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Server, and Network. The four service models are distinguished by **where the vendor's responsibility boundary ends and the user's responsibility begins**.

**Step 2 — IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — 2 marks**

- **Boundary:** Vendor manages hardware, hypervisor, and physical networking. User manages the OS upward.
- **IoT Example:** An enterprise runs a self-hosted **Kafka cluster on AWS EC2 instances** to ingest 1 million sensor messages per second. The user patches the OS, configures Kafka brokers, and manages ZooKeeper.
- **Vendors:** AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.

**Step 3 — PaaS (Platform as a Service) — 1.5 marks**

- **Boundary:** Vendor manages OS, runtime, and middleware. User only deploys code.
- **IoT Example:** A developer deploys a **Python Flask API on AWS Elastic Beanstalk** that ingests sensor POSTs. The platform auto-scales the application across multiple containers during traffic spikes.
- **Vendors:** Heroku, Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

**Step 4 — SaaS (Software as a Service) — 1.5 marks**

- **Boundary:** Vendor manages everything up to the application UI. User only configures and consumes.
- **IoT Example:** A facility manager uses **Ubidots** to monitor 200 air-quality sensors. No backend code is written — sensors publish to Ubidots' MQTT broker, and the dashboard is configured via drag-and-drop widgets.
- **Vendors:** Salesforce, ThingSpeak, Google Workspace.

**Step 5 — FaaS (Function as a Service) / Serverless — 2 marks**

- **Boundary:** Vendor manages the container, runtime, and auto-scaling. User only writes stateless function code.
- **IoT Example:** A temperature sensor exceeds 80°C. An **AWS Lambda function** is triggered via an IoT Core rule, queries a DynamoDB table for the device's location, and publishes an SMS via SNS to the on-call engineer. Billing: **0.2 seconds of execution × $0.00001667/GB-s**.
- **Vendors:** AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.

**Shared Responsibility Diagram (Verbal — to be drawn on paper):**

```
+---------------------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
| Layer               | IaaS   | PaaS   | SaaS   | FaaS   |
+---------------------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
| Application         | USER   | USER   | VENDOR | USER   |
| Data                | USER   | USER   | VENDOR | USER   |
| Runtime             | USER   | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
| Middleware          | USER   | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
| Operating System    | USER   | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
| Virtualization      | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
| Server / Storage    | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
| Network             | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR | VENDOR |
+---------------------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Architecture Explanation:**

The **ESP8266 NodeMCU** is a low-cost Wi-Fi SoC with a built-in TCP/IP stack. It can host a lightweight HTTP web server directly on-chip, eliminating the need for an external server.

**Step 1 — Initialization**

The `ESP8266WebServer server(80);` statement creates a web server listening on **port 80** (default HTTP port).

**Step 2 — Wi-Fi Connection**

```
WiFi.begin("SSID", "PASSWORD");
while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); }
Serial.println(WiFi.localIP());
```

**Step 3 — URL Handlers**

Three handlers are registered:
- `server.on("/temperature", handleTemperature);`
- `server.on("/led", handleLED);`
- `server.on("/", handleRoot);`

**Step 4 — Sensor Read**

A DHT22 sensor provides temperature/humidity over a single-wire digital protocol.

**Step 5 — JSON Construction and Response**

```
String json = "{\"temp\":" + String(t) + ",\"hum\":" + String(h) + "}";
server.send(200, "application/json", json);
```

**Step 6 — Mobile Client Flow**

1. Mobile phone connects to the **same Wi-Fi** as the ESP8266.
2. Opens `http://192.168.1.45/temperature` in a browser.
3. ESP8266 receives the GET, reads DHT22, returns JSON.
4. Browser/JavaScript app parses and displays the value.

**Diagram (Verbal — to be drawn on paper):**

```
   +------------+        Wi-Fi HTTP        +--------------+
   | Mobile App | <---------------------> | ESP8266 MCU  |
   | Browser    | 192.168.1.45:80/.....  | + WebServer  |
   +------------+                          +--------------+
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                          +--------------+
                                          | DHT22 Sensor |
                                          +--------------+
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Communication APIs + Cloud SLA

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **REST and WebSocket** communication APIs in detail. Compare them in terms of connection model, latency, payload format, and IoT suitability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud IoT platform advertises **99.95% monthly uptime**. Calculate the **maximum annual permitted downtime** in minutes. If a hospital signs an SLA and experiences a **6-hour outage** in a year, what is the **service credit percentage** if the policy offers 100% credit above 4 nines and 50% credit between 3.5 and 4 nines?

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**REST (Representational State Transfer) — 3.5 marks**

- **Architectural Style:** Stateless, client-server, cacheable, uniform interface.
- **Transport:** HTTP/HTTPS (TCP-based).
- **Methods:** `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (update), `DELETE` (remove).
- **Payload:** JSON (lightweight, ~50-200 bytes typical) or XML.
- **Connection:** Short-lived, closed after each request/response cycle.
- **IoT Use Case:** Polling a sensor value every 10 seconds, sending a one-time alert.

**WebSocket — 3.5 marks**

- **Protocol Type:** Full-duplex, persistent connection over a **single TCP socket**.
- **Handshake:** Begins as an HTTP request with `Upgrade: websocket` and `Connection: Upgrade` headers. Server responds with `101 Switching Protocols`.
- **URL Scheme:** `ws://` (insecure) or `wss://` (TLS-encrypted).
- **Data Frames:** Lightweight binary or text frames (~2-14 byte overhead vs ~200-800 byte HTTP headers).
- **IoT Use Case:** Real-time patient vitals streaming, live video from surveillance cameras, two-way control of industrial robots.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | REST | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection** | Short-lived, request/response | Persistent, full-duplex |
| **Latency** | High (polling overhead) | Low (<100 ms) |
| **Server Push** | No (client polls) | Yes (native) |
| **Overhead** | High (HTTP headers per request) | Low (frame overhead) |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Stateful connection |
| **IoT Suitability** | Periodic telemetry, control commands | Real-time streaming, live dashboards |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Advertised SLA: **99.95%** monthly uptime
- Actual outage: **6 hours** in a year
- Service credit policy:
  - **100% credit** if uptime falls below 4 nines (99.99%)
  - **50% credit** if uptime is between 3.5 nines (99.95%) and 4 nines (99.99%)

**Step 1 — Convert SLA to Decimal**

$$
U_{\text{SLA}} = \frac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Permitted Downtime**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{annual}} = 262.8 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Actual Outage to Minutes**

$$
D_{\text{actual}} = 6 \text{ hours} \times 60 = 360 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Actual Uptime Percentage**

$$
U_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{360}{525{,}600} = 1 - 0.000685 = 0.999315 \approx 99.93\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with SLA Tiers**

- 4 nines = 99.99%
- 3.5 nines = 99.95%
- Actual uptime = 99.93% (between 3 and 3.5 nines — below the 50% credit tier)

**Step 6 — Apply Service Credit Rule**

Since the actual uptime (99.93%) is **below 3.5 nines (99.95%)**, the SLA is breached. The hospital is entitled to a **service credit** per the contractual agreement (often the highest tier — 100% of monthly fees in many enterprise cloud contracts, but in this specific question, the tier of 50% credit applies only between 3.5 and 4 nines; below 3.5 nines typically triggers the maximum penalty tier defined elsewhere in the contract — KTU accepts both interpretations if reasoned correctly).

> **Final Answer:** Maximum annual permitted downtime = **262.8 minutes**. Actual outage = **360 minutes** exceeds this, triggering SLA breach and corresponding service credit entitlement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "monthly" with "annual" SLA** — many students calculate downtime for 30 days instead of 365 days. **Always re-read the question's time window.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours to minutes** before multiplying by 525,600.
> 3. **Mixing up Service Models vs Deployment Models** in 7-mark questions — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS are *what* you get; Public/Private/Hybrid are *where* it is deployed.
> 4. **Drawing REST diagrams without labeling HTTP methods and status codes** — examiners explicitly check for `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `404 Not Found`, etc.
> 5. **Forgetting MQTT QoS levels (0, 1, 2)** in any question mentioning MQTT — it is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 6. **Not distinguishing between a "web server on the cloud" and a "web server on the edge device"** — both definitions are tested separately in PEEBT632.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NIST SP 800-145** defines **three primary cloud service models**: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS**. **FaaS** is the modern 4th model.
- **Shared Responsibility** is the central theme — the *further right* you go in the service-model matrix, the *less* you manage, but also the *less control* you have.
- **Deployment Models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) are **separate** from Service Models — this is a frequently-tested distinction.
- **REST** = Stateless, HTTP-based, JSON payloads, CRUD operations, ideal for *periodic* IoT telemetry.
- **SOAP** = XML-only, heavy, formal WSDL, **not preferred** for IoT.
- **WebSocket** = Persistent, full-duplex, low-latency, ideal for *real-time* IoT streaming.
- **MQTT** = Lightweight pub/sub, TCP-based, QoS levels 0/1/2, ideal for *low-bandwidth* sensor networks.
- **Web Server for IoT** has two meanings: (1) cloud-hosted (Nginx + Node.js) and (2) edge-device-hosted (ESP8266 WebServer library).
- **SLA Uptime Formula** must be memorized: $\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = (1 - U) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.
- **Common SLA tiers**: 99% ("two nines"), 99.9% ("three nines" = 525.6 min/yr), 99.99% ("four nines" = 52.56 min/yr), 99.999% ("five nines" = 5.26 min/yr).
- **REST Methods Mapping**: `GET` = Read, `POST` = Create, `PUT` = Update, `DELETE` = Remove — this CRUD mapping is a **2-mark KTU classic**.
- **MQTT Topics** use hierarchical strings separated by `/` with wildcards `+` (single-level) and `#` (multi-level).
- **Always label diagram arrows with protocol names** (HTTP, HTTPS, MQTT, CoAP, LoRaWAN) — this is a guaranteed **1-mark differentiator** in KTU valuation.
- **Edge Web Server Example**: ESP8266 hosts HTTP on port 80, returns JSON, mobile browser consumes it via Wi-Fi — this is the **most asked 7-mark sub-question** in this module.
- **FaaS billing** is in **milliseconds** — memorize the per-GB-second cost structure of AWS Lambda for any 14-mark numerical question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud for IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud for IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cloud Computing in IoT

**Cloud Computing for IoT** is the on-demand delivery of IT resources, storage, processing power, networking, and analytics services over the Internet on a pay-as-you-go basis, specifically architected to ingest, store, process, and visualize the massive heterogeneous data streams generated by distributed IoT devices, sensors, and actuators.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEEBT632 – IoT & Biomedical Applications), **Cloud for IoT** is treated as the *central data plane* that complements the constrained edge/fog layer, enabling long-term storage, big-data analytics, and cross-domain integration (e.g., between biomedical wearables, smart-grid sensors, and hospital information systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> *Cloud for IoT is a service-oriented paradigm that provides elastic, scalable, and virtualized computing resources to IoT applications through service models such as IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, eliminating the need for direct active management of physical infrastructure by the user.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Cloud for IoT** as the **"municipal power grid"** for a smart city.

- A single **solar panel on a rooftop** (your IoT sensor) produces only a little electricity. It is wasteful for every home to build its own coal plant.
- The **municipal power grid** (the Cloud) collects energy from *thousands* of sources, balances supply and demand, and provides electricity to anyone who plugs in — measured by a meter.
- You pay only for the units you consume (**pay-per-use**), and if your factory suddenly needs 10× more power tomorrow, the grid scales automatically (**elasticity**).

In the same way, a single IoT device (temperature sensor, ECG monitor) generates small data. Instead of building a local data center, the device *pushes* its readings to a shared, massive, virtualized data center (the Cloud). The Cloud:
- Stores petabytes of data,
- Runs machine-learning analytics,
- Pushes insights back to a doctor's phone or an actuator,
- And charges only for what is used.

## 1.3 Essential Characteristics (NIST-Aligned, KTU-Relevant)

| # | Characteristic | Meaning in IoT Context |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **On-demand self-service** | Sensors/Apps provision storage or compute without human admin intervention. |
| 2 | **Broad network access** | Accessible via MQTT/HTTPS from any device, anywhere. |
| 3 | **Resource pooling** | Multi-tenant servers serve thousands of IoT customers. |
| 4 | **Rapid elasticity** | Auto-scales when 1 million new medical wearables connect. |
| 5 | **Measured service** | Billing per API call, per GB stored, per stream-minute. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to remember these **5 NIST characteristics** verbatim — they appear frequently as 3-mark direct questions.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics to Remember

- **Latency:** The end-to-end delay from sensor to cloud response. *Cloud-only IoT* typically has **50 – 500 ms** latency, which is *insufficient* for robotic surgery or closed-loop insulin control.
- **Scalability:** Ability to handle growth from **N = 1,000** to **N = 10,000,000** devices without redesign.
- **Availability (SLA):** Commercial clouds guarantee **99.9 % ("three nines")** to **99.999 % ("five nines")** uptime per year.
- **Data Velocity:** Modern IoT pipelines ingest at **GB/s to TB/s**.

All these four parameters are **bolded KTU-favourite terms** and must be memorized in numerical form.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why Cloud?" for IoT — Five Engineering Drivers

IoT is the natural "killer app" of cloud computing because of the **4 V's of Big Data** that constrained edge devices cannot handle alone:

1. **Volume** — A single jet engine produces **~10 TB per flight hour**. Storing 10 years of fleet data needs Petascale storage.
2. **Velocity** — Smart-grid PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units) stream at **120 samples/sec**; aggregating 50,000 of them is impossible locally.
3. **Variety** — ECG waveforms (time-series), X-rays (images), and HL7 records (structured text) need different schemas.
4. **Veracity** — ML-based anomaly detection (e.g., arrhythmia) requires training on millions of labelled samples — only feasible in the cloud.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why Cloud for IoT?"*, the model answer should map each IoT pain-point to a cloud capability.

## 2.2 Cloud Service Models — The "Stack Pizza" View

The three classical service models can be thought of as layers of a pizza — the more you take off the plate (manage yourself), the cheaper but more effortful it becomes.

| Layer | Service Model | Who Manages Servers? | Who Manages OS/Middleware? | Who Manages Application? | IoT Use-Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| You (most control) | **On-Premise** | You | You | You | Hospital's private MRI archive |
| Bottom | **IaaS** (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) | Cloud Provider | You | You | EC2 instance running custom MQTT broker |
| Middle | **PaaS** (Platform-as-a-Service) | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | You | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub ingestion pipeline |
| Top (least control) | **SaaS** (Software-as-a-Service) | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | CGM (Continuous Glucose Monitor) dashboard seen by patient |
| Newer | **FaaS / Serverless** | Cloud Provider | Cloud Provider | Function code only | Lambda function triggered by an MQTT "high-BPM" alert |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module-4 specifically emphasizes **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** in the IoT context. A 14-mark question often asks you to *compare* them with a biomedical example.

## 2.3 Cloud Deployment Models for IoT

| Deployment | Ownership | Accessibility | Typical IoT Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Internet | Consumer fitness trackers |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Intranet / VPN | Hospital PACS archive, defence IoT |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Mixed | Both | Burst processing of genomics from hospital to public cloud |
