| **Community Cloud** | Shared organizations with a common goal | Restricted | Multi-hospital clinical research consortium |
| **Multi-Cloud** | ≥ 2 public providers | Internet | Avoiding vendor lock-in for a global pharma trial |

## 2.4 The Cloud-of-Things (CoT) Reference Architecture

A KTU 2024 expected architectural stack (bottom-up):

1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors/actuators (the IoT *things*).
2. **Network / Edge Layer** — Gateways, 5G, LPWAN, BLE.
3. **Cloud Ingestion Layer** — Message broker (MQTT broker, Kafka), API gateway.
4. **Cloud Platform Layer** — Stream processing, time-series DB, data lake.
5. **Cloud Analytics / AI Layer** — ML, deep learning, anomaly detection.
6. **Application Layer** — Web/mobile dashboards, alerting, control loops.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{xmit}} + T_{\text{cloud}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{return}}$ | End-to-end IoT latency | ms | Sum of sensing, transmission, cloud ingress, processing, return delays. |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{devices}} \times S_{\text{sample}} \times B_{\text{bits}}}{T_{\text{sec}}}$ | Aggregate data rate from IoT to cloud | bps | $N_{\text{devices}}$ sensors, $S_{\text{sample}}$ samples/s, $B_{\text{bits}}$ bits/sample. |
| 3 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Service uptime fraction | dimensionless | MTBF = mean time between failures; MTTR = mean time to repair. |
| 4 | $\text{Storage}_{\text{annual}} = N_{\text{dev}} \times R_{\text{msg}} \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 \times B_{\text{bytes}}$ | Annual cloud storage required | Bytes | Crucial for biomedical archival calculations. |
| 5 | $\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = (B_{\text{stored}} \times P_{\text{GB}}) + (D_{\text{processed}} \times P_{\text{GB}}) + N_{\text{API}} \times P_{\text{call}}$ | Monthly cloud bill | ₹ / $ | $P$ = price per unit. |
| 6 | $\text{SLA}_{\text{annual downtime}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Tolerated downtime per year | minutes | For 99.9 %, ≈ 43.8 min/year. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuation scripts award full marks **only** when units are written alongside numerical answers. Memorize the units in the right-most column.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare (Biomedical):** Cloud aggregates ECG/EHR from thousands of patients to train arrhythmia-detection neural networks that the bedside monitor cannot host.
- **Smart Grid:** Cloud runs load-forecasting models on smart-meter data to schedule generation.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Cloud-based CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems) process vibration data from thousands of motors to predict bearing failure.
- **Connected Vehicles:** Telematics data uploaded to the cloud enables fleet-wide route optimization and over-the-air (OTA) firmware updates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Designing Cloud Storage for a Hospital IoT Network

**Problem Statement (KTU-style numerical):**
A tertiary hospital deploys **N = 500** bedside patient monitors. Each monitor streams vital signs (HR, SpO₂, BP, Temp) at **R_msg = 1 message every 5 seconds** with each message being **B = 256 bytes**. Calculate:

(a) The daily data volume produced.
(b) The monthly storage requirement if compressed 50 %.
(c) The monthly AWS S3 cost (assume **₹1.40 per GB-month** Standard storage, **1 GB = 10⁹ bytes**).
(d) Whether the cloud or a local NAS is more economical for *5 years* of retention.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert message rate to per-second rate.**

$$
R_{\text{sec}} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ message}}{5 \text{ s}} \;=\; 0.2 \text{ msg/s per monitor}
$$

**Explanation:** Every monitor emits 0.2 messages per second. This conversion is needed because SI units are in seconds, not seconds-per-message.

**Step 2 — Compute total per-second data rate across the hospital.**

$$
D_{\text{sec}} \;=\; N_{\text{dev}} \times R_{\text{sec}} \times B_{\text{bytes}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
D_{\text{sec}} \;=\; 500 \times 0.2 \times 256
$$

$$
D_{\text{sec}} \;=\; 100 \times 256 \;=\; 25{,}600 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

**Explanation:** Multiply the number of monitors by the per-monitor rate, then by the per-message size to get the aggregate data rate.

**Step 3 — Convert to per-day data volume.**

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; D_{\text{sec}} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 25{,}600 \times 86{,}400
$$

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 2{,}211{,}840{,}000 \text{ bytes/day} \;\approx\; 2.21 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Explanation:** 86,400 is the number of seconds in a day. A monitor stream that looks "tiny" individually becomes gigabytes when scaled hospital-wide.

**Step 4 — Apply 50 % compression for monthly storage.**

$$
D_{\text{month-compressed}} \;=\; D_{\text{day}} \times 30 \times 0.5
$$

$$
D_{\text{month-compressed}} \;=\; 2.21 \times 30 \times 0.5
$$

$$
D_{\text{month-compressed}} \;=\; 33.19 \text{ GB/month (raw: 2.21 × 30 ≈ 66.38 GB)}
$$

**Explanation:** Vital-signs waveforms have high temporal correlation, so 50 % lossless compression is realistic. KTU examiners accept the factor as a given.

**Step 5 — Convert to billing units (GB) using 1 GB = 10⁹ bytes.**

$$
D_{\text{GB-month}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{month-compressed}} \times 10^9}{10^9} \;=\; 33.19 \text{ GB}
$$

**Explanation:** Since we already expressed $D_{\text{day}}$ in GB, no further conversion is required, but explicitly stating the factor avoids unit-mismatch deductions.

**Step 6 — Compute monthly cost.**

$$
C_{\text{monthly}} \;=\; D_{\text{GB-month}} \times P_{\text{GB}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{monthly}} \;=\; 33.19 \times 1.40 \;\approx\; 46.47 \text{ ₹/month}
$$

**Explanation:** Multiply billable storage by the unit price. Even though 46 ₹ seems cheap, this is *only 500 monitors*. A multi-hospital chain can scale this to lakhs.

**Step 7 — 5-year total cost comparison.**

$$
C_{\text{5yr-cloud}} \;=\; C_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 \times 5
$$

$$
C_{\text{5yr-cloud}} \;=\; 46.47 \times 60 \;\approx\; 2{,}788 \text{ ₹ for 5 years}
$$

**Discussion (for part d):** At this scale, cloud is *unambiguously cheaper* than buying a NAS + tape backup + IT staff. However, for *millions* of devices, the *uncompressed* raw cost would justify **private / hybrid cloud** with on-premise tiered storage.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical numerical pattern** KTU 2024 has repeated in Module 4. Memorize the *form* of the derivation, not just numbers.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cloud Service-Model Selection for Biomedical IoT

| Use-Case | Best Service Model | Justification | Counter-Model | Why NOT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Storing raw MRI images | **IaaS** (object storage) | High control over DICOM, encryption, lifecycle | SaaS | Vendor cannot comply with hospital's PACS schema |
| Real-time arrhythmia alert | **PaaS** (managed Kafka + ML) | Focus on logic, not on broker maintenance | IaaS | High operational burden |
| Patient-facing glucose dashboard | **SaaS** (managed portal) | No patient-side DevOps | IaaS | Waste of resources; 99 % of features unused |
| One-shot ECG-to-PDF conversion triggered by event | **FaaS** (serverless) | Pay only per alert; idle cost = 0 | PaaS | Always-on server is wasteful |
| Genome sequencing pipeline | **Hybrid** (private storage + public HPC burst) | Sensitive data stays on-prem; compute scales in public cloud | Public-only | Genomic data is PHI and must be on-prem |

> [!TIP]
> In the 14-mark KTU question on cloud models, draw *this* matrix in the answer sheet — examiners award a 2-mark bonus for clean tabular presentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Cloud-of-Things (CoT) Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IoT Devices Layer\nSensors, Wearables, Actuators] --> B[Edge Gateway Layer\nBLE, Zigbee, LoRa, 5G]
    B --> C[Cloud Ingestion Layer\nMQTT Broker, API Gateway, Kafka]
    C --> D[Cloud Platform Layer\nTime-Series DB, Data Lake, Stream Processing]
    D --> E[Cloud Analytics Layer\nML Models, AI Inference, Anomaly Detection]
    E --> F[Application Layer\nWeb Dashboard, Mobile App, Alerting]
    F --> G[End User Layer\nDoctor, Patient, Admin, Operator]
    E --> H[Actuation Feedback\nSmart Hospital, Smart Grid]
    H --> A
    D --> I[Long-term Storage\nCold Archive, Compliance Logs]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The leftmost vertical chain (`A → B → C → D → E → F → G`) shows the *forward data path* from sensor to user.
- The bottom loop (`E → H → A`) shows the *feedback actuation* path: cloud analytics can issue control commands back to the physical world (e.g., adjust an insulin pump).
- The branch (`D → I`) shows *long-term archival*, which is critical for biomedical compliance (e.g., FDA 21 CFR Part 11, GDPR).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Service-Model Layering (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS → FaaS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4[FaaS - Serverless]
        F1[Lambda Function\nEvent-triggered code only]
    end
    subgraph L3[SaaS - Software as a Service]
        S1[Ready-made apps\nPatient portal, EHR view]
    end
    subgraph L2[PaaS - Platform as a Service]
        P1[Managed MQTT Broker\nManaged DB, ML tools]
    end
    subgraph L1[IaaS - Infrastructure as a Service]
        I1[Raw VMs, Storage, Network\nEC2, S3, VPC]
    end
    USER[Developer / Hospital IT] --> L1
    USER --> L2
    USER --> L3
    USER --> L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> This nested-subgraph form is the KTU 2024-recommended way of showing cloud *abstraction layers* in answer sheets.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hybrid Cloud Data Routing for a Hospital

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEV[Medical IoT Devices\nECG, BP, SpO2] --> GW[Edge Gateway\nOn-Premise]
    GW --> PRIV[Private Cloud\nHospital Data Center]
    PRIV -->|PHI data stays on-prem| STORE[Encrypted DICOM Store]
    PRIV -->|Anonymized data| PUB[Public Cloud\nAWS, Azure, GCP]
    PUB --> ML[ML Analytics Engine]
    ML --> PRIV
    PRIV --> DOC[Doctor Dashboard]
```

**Interpretation:** Sensitive PHI (Protected Health Information) never leaves the hospital. Only *anonymized* feature vectors are sent to the public cloud for model training. The trained model is *downloaded* back to the private cloud for inference. This is the textbook **hybrid cloud** pattern KTU expects.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Cloud Message Flow with MQTT Broker (Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor
    participant Broker as Cloud MQTT Broker
    participant DB as Time-Series DB
    participant ML as ML Analytics
    participant App as Doctor App
    Sensor->>Broker: PUBLISH vitals/ECG {QoS 1}
    Broker->>DB: Persist message
    Broker->>ML: Stream to inference
    ML->>App: PUSH alert "Tachycardia detected"
    App->>Broker: SUBSCRIBE alerts/icu
```

**Interpretation:** Shows how a single message *fans out* to storage, analytics, and the user-facing app — the canonical **pub-sub** pattern in IoT-cloud integration.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**"List the essential characteristics of Cloud Computing as defined by NIST. How do they apply to an IoT data-pipeline for remote patient monitoring?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five NIST characteristics are:

1. **On-demand self-service** — IoT devices auto-provision storage and compute without human intervention; e.g., a wearable ECG patch uploads to a database table created *automatically* on first connection.
2. **Broad network access** — Data is accessible from any device (mobile, desktop, tablet) over standard protocols (HTTPS, MQTT).
3. **Resource pooling** — The same physical servers serve thousands of hospital tenants, ensuring cost efficiency.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — During a pandemic, cloud auto-scales from 10,000 to 1,000,000 connected wearables in minutes.
5. **Measured service** — Cloud bills the hospital per GB ingested and per ML inference, enabling predictable IT budgeting.

*(Examiner's tip: Naming all five correctly = 2 marks; one-sentence IoT link = 1 mark.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**"Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in the context of an IoT-based biomedical monitoring system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Provider manages | Servers, storage, network | + OS, middleware, runtimes | + Application itself |
| User manages | OS up to app | Application only | Only configuration |
| Biomedical example | Custom DICOM server on EC2 | AWS IoT Core ingesting device data | Readymade patient-dashboard portal |
| Best for | Highly customized hospital workflows | Standard pipelines with custom analytics | Out-of-the-box clinician access |

*(Examiner's tip: Tabular comparison = full 3 marks; bullet form = 2 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the reference architecture of *Cloud of Things (CoT)*. Draw a neat block diagram showing the six layers from perception to application, and describe the role of each layer in an IoT-based remote cardiac-monitoring system.

**(b) [7 marks]** A smart-hospital deployment has **N = 1,000** wearable ECG patches. Each patch streams at **R = 200 samples/s**, with **B = 16 bits/sample**. Calculate the **daily** and **annual** storage required. If cloud storage costs **₹1.20 per GB-month** (with **1 GB = 10⁹ bytes**), estimate the **annual storage cost**. Assume 50 % compression.

---

#### Model Solution — Choice 1

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The **Cloud of Things (CoT) architecture** integrates IoT devices with cloud infrastructure through six layers:

1. **Perception / Sensor Layer (1 mark):** Physical sensors, RFID tags, biomedical wearables (ECG patch, SpO₂ probe). Their role is to acquire the *raw cardiac electrical signal* from the patient.

2. **Network / Edge Layer (1 mark):** Gateways using BLE, Wi-Fi, 5G, or LoRa. The role is to *pre-aggregate* and *securely transmit* ECG data to the cloud over the public Internet.

3. **Cloud Ingestion Layer (1 mark):** MQTT brokers, Kafka topics, API gateways. The role is to *accept millions of concurrent connections*, perform load balancing, and route the stream to downstream services.

4. **Cloud Platform Layer (1 mark):** Time-series databases (InfluxDB, Timescale), data lakes (S3, ADLS). The role is to *persist* raw waveforms, indexed for fast query by date, patient ID, or anomaly tag.

5. **Cloud Analytics / AI Layer (1 mark):** ML/DL models (CNN, LSTM) for arrhythmia classification. The role is to *infer* clinical events (e.g., atrial fibrillation) in real time.

6. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Dashboards for cardiologists, mobile apps for patients, alerting systems for ICUs. The role is to *close the loop* with the human caregiver.

7. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the six layers vertically with arrows showing data flow. (See Diagram 4.1 in SECTION 4.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Per-monitor data rate (1 mark).**

$$
D_{\text{per-dev}} \;=\; R \times B
$$

$$
D_{\text{per-dev}} \;=\; 200 \text{ samples/s} \times 16 \text{ bits/sample}
$$

$$
D_{\text{per-dev}} \;=\; 3{,}200 \text{ bits/s} \;=\; 400 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

**Explanation:** Each sample is 16 bits = 2 bytes; multiplying by sample rate gives bytes per second per device.

**Step 2 — Aggregate hospital data rate (1 mark).**

$$
D_{\text{agg}} \;=\; N \times D_{\text{per-dev}}
$$

$$
D_{\text{agg}} \;=\; 1{,}000 \times 400 \;=\; 400{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily volume with compression (2 marks).**

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; D_{\text{agg}} \times 86{,}400 \times 0.5
$$

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 400{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \times 0.5
$$

$$
D_{\text{day}} \;=\; 17{,}280{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/day} \;\approx\; 17.28 \text{ GB/day (compressed)}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual volume (1 mark).**

$$
D_{\text{year}} \;=\; D_{\text{day}} \times 365
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} \;=\; 17.28 \times 365 \;\approx\; 6{,}307.2 \text{ GB/year} \;\approx\; 6.31 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual cost (1 mark).**

$$
C_{\text{year}} \;=\; D_{\text{year-GB}} \times P_{\text{GB-month}} \times 12
$$

$$
C_{\text{year}} \;=\; 6{,}307.2 \times 1.20 \times 12 \;\approx\; 90{,}823.68 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The hospital must budget **≈ ₹90,800 per year** for ECG archival in the cloud. At this scale, a *hybrid* model (recent data in cloud, 5+ year data in cold private storage) is recommended.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **public, private, hybrid, and community** cloud deployment models. Which model is most suitable for a multi-hospital clinical research consortium that processes sensitive patient genomic data? Justify.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **NIST cloud computing reference model** with a focus on the **five essential characteristics, three service models, and four deployment models**. How do these collectively address the *security, scalability, and elasticity* demands of IoT-based biomedical systems?

---

#### Model Solution — Choice 2

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Deployment Model (1 mark each) | Description | Pros | Cons | Suitable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Owned by third-party; open Internet access | Low cost, high scalability | Data-sovereignty risk for PHI | ❌ No |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization; on-premise or hosted | Full data control, HIPAA-friendly | High CapEx, low utilization | Partially |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of private + public with orchestration | Sensitive data stays on-prem; compute bursts to public | Complex integration | ✅ Yes |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by orgs with a common mission | Shared cost, shared governance | Requires strong consortium agreement | ✅ Yes (variant) |

**Best choice:** A **Hybrid Cloud** combined with a *community-governed* framework is most suitable (2 marks). Genomic data is PHI and must remain on the **private** tier. ML model *training*, however, can burst to the **public** tier using *federated* or *differentially-private* techniques, satisfying both compliance and compute scalability.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model** has three pillars (1 mark each):

- **Five Essential Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- **Three Service Models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (now extended to include FaaS).
- **Four Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community (some NIST drafts also include *multi-cloud*).

**Mapping to IoT-biomedical demands (4 marks):**

1. **Security** — Resource pooling + private deployment ensure PHI isolation; on-demand provisioning of *per-tenant encryption keys* preserves confidentiality.
2. **Scalability** — Rapid elasticity handles surges (e.g., 50,000 new Holter monitors during a clinical trial) by auto-scaling MQTT brokers and time-series DBs.
3. **Elasticity & Cost** — Measured service + IaaS/PaaS allow the hospital to *pay only for the storage and inference used*, ideal for variable biomedical workloads.
4. **Accessibility** — Broad network access lets clinicians view patient dashboards on any device, anywhere — critical for tele-cardiology.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The NIST model is not just a definition; it is the *engineering checklist* a KTU student must apply when designing a cloud-backed IoT system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark mistakes that cost students 4–6 marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the compression factor** in storage numericals — examiners explicitly test whether you multiplied by 0.5.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** — writing "GB" when the calculation was in MB. KTU 2024 strictly penalizes missing units.
> 3. **Vague service-model answers** — Saying "PaaS is easier" without giving a *biomedical example* loses the application mark.
> 4. **No diagram in CoT questions** — A 14-marker with a missing block diagram loses a guaranteed 1–2 marks even if text is perfect.
> 5. **Confusing "scalability" with "elasticity"** — Scalability = handle growth; Elasticity = handle *spikes* automatically. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Ignoring security/PHI** in deployment-model answers — for biomedical data, always mention HIPAA/GDPR compliance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Cloud for IoT = on-demand, elastic, measured IT resources delivering IoT data storage, processing, and analytics.
- **NIST 5 Characteristics:** On-demand, broad access, pooling, elasticity, measured.
- **3 Service Models:** IaaS (infra), PaaS (platform), SaaS (software). Add FaaS for modern answers.
- **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- **CoT 6 Layers (top to bottom):** Application → Analytics → Platform → Ingestion → Network/Edge → Perception.
- **Latency numbers:** Cloud-only ≈ 50–500 ms (too high for closed-loop control). Fog/Edge ≈ 1–10 ms.
- **Availability:** 99.9 % = 43.8 min/yr downtime; 99.999 % = 5.26 min/yr.
- **Canonical numerical pattern:** $D_{\text{agg}} = N \times R \times B \times t \times \text{compression factor}$.
- **Always state units** (bytes/GB, ₹/$, ms).
- **Biomedical = PHI = prefer Private or Hybrid.**
- **One neat diagram = guaranteed 1–2 marks** in any 14-mark answer.
- **Pub-Sub (MQTT) is the *de facto* IoT-to-cloud protocol** — name it whenever the question mentions ingestion.
- **Serverless (FaaS) is the right answer for *sporadic, event-driven* biomedical alerts** — avoid saying it is "always cheaper"; it is cheaper *only when traffic is bursty*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Python Web Application Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Python Web Application Framework # 📘 MODULE 4: Python Web Application Framework > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic belongs to **Module 4 – IoT Data Management & Cloud Platforms**, and bridges the gap between raw sensor data acquisition and human-accessible visualization/control. The **Python Web Application Framework** is the de-facto standard for building dashboards, REST APIs, and cloud connectors in IoT systems. --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Python Web Application Framework** is a software library/collection of modules that provides a standardized, reusable foundation for developing web applications and services using the Python programming language. It abstracts low-level protocols such as **HTTP**, **TCP/IP**, and **socket programming**, offering developers pre-built components for **routing**, **templating**, **session management**, **database integration**, and **RESTful API handling**. For the KTU 2024 scheme, the syllabus explicitly focuses on two dominant frameworks: 1. **Flask** – A lightweight *microframework* (classified under the **WSGI** toolkit category). 2. **Django** – A full-stack, batteries-included framework following the **MVT (Model-View-Template)** architectural pattern. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Term – WSGI:** The **Web Server Gateway Interface (WSGI)** is the **PEP 3333** standard specification that defines a simple and universal interface between web servers and Python web applications. Any framework that conforms to WSGI (Flask, Django, Bottle, Pyramid) is interchangeable at the deployment level. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you want to build a **house** (your web app). - **Without a framework** → You must manufacture every brick, mix your own cement, and even forge your own nails before laying a single wall. You also have to figure out how the door connects to the lock, where water pipes go, and how electricity flows. *(This is raw socket programming with `socket` and `http.server` in Python.)* - **With a Python Web Framework** → You are given a **pre-engineered blueprint**, ready-made bricks, pre-wired electrical conduits, and standardized door frames. You just decide the **color of the walls** (your business logic) and the **number of rooms** (your routes/endpoints). > 🏠 **The House = Web App** | **Blueprint = Framework** | **Doors & Windows = Routes/Endpoints** | **Electrical Wiring = Middleware** | **Furniture = Templates (HTML)** In an **IoT context**, the house is a **dashboard** showing temperature, humidity, and heart-rate sensors, the blueprint is **Flask/Django**, the doors are the **API endpoints** (`/api/sensor/data`), and the wiring is the **JSON payload** flowing from the microcontroller to the cloud. ### 1.3 Why Python for IoT Web Applications? | Property | Engineering Value in IoT | |---|---| | **Concise Syntax** | Faster prototyping of sensor dashboards | | **Rich Ecosystem** | Libraries like `paho-mqtt`, `requests`, `Flask-SocketIO` | | **Hardware Interfacing** | `pyserial`, `RPi.GPIO`, `pymodbus` integration | | **Asynchronous Support** | `asyncio` + `FastAPI` for handling concurrent sensor streams | | **Cloud SDKs** | Native AWS, Azure, GCP client libraries | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "justify" Python for IoT web development in a 3-mark question, always emphasize the **rapid-prototyping cycle**, **hardware-to-cloud bridge capability**, and **cross-platform deployment** (Linux on Raspberry Pi, Windows, macOS). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Pattern: MVC vs MVT Modern Python web frameworks implement a separation-of-concerns design pattern to keep code maintainable. ### 2.1.1 MVC (Model–View–Controller) — Used by Flask, Bottle - **Model** → Manages data and business logic (database, file I/O, sensor data buffer). - **View** → Presentation layer (HTML templates, JSON serializers). - **Controller** → Handles user input and orchestrates the Model and View (in Flask, this is the *route function*). ### 2.1.2 MVT (Model–View–Template) — Used by Django - **Model** → Same as MVC's Model. - **View** → A *callback function* that decides *which data* to send to the template (equivalent to Controller in MVC). - **Template** → HTML file with placeholder tags (equivalent to View in MVC). > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU tests this:** The architectural pattern is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A. A common trap is assuming Django's "View" performs UI rendering — it does NOT. Rendering is done by the **Template**. ## 2.2 Request–Response Lifecycle in a Python Web App A typical HTTP transaction in a Flask/Django app follows this sequence: 1. **Client (Browser/Mobile/Sensor Node)** sends an HTTP request (`GET / POST / PUT / DELETE`). 2. **Web Server (Gunicorn / uWSGI / Waitress)** receives the request and passes it to the framework via the **WSGI interface**. 3. **Middleware Stack** processes the request (authentication, logging, CORS). 4. **URL Router** matches the URL pattern to a registered route function. 5. **Route Handler (Controller/View)** executes business logic, interacts with the Model layer (database / sensor API). 6. **Response Object** is built (HTML page, JSON, XML, plain text). 7. **WSGI Server** serializes the response and sends it back over **HTTP/1.1** or **HTTP/2**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Status Codes KTU Students MUST Memorize:** `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `500 Internal Server Error`. These appear repeatedly in 3-mark questions. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Python web frameworks are not math-heavy, but the following quantitative metrics and HTTP semantics are high-yield for KTU exams. | Concept | Standard / Formula / Value | Unit | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | HTTP Request Methods | `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, OPTIONS` | — | Direct 3-mark question | | HTTP Success Range | **2xx** (200–299) | status code | Defines successful REST calls | | HTTP Client Error Range | **4xx** (400–499) | status code | Client-side issue (bad URL/auth) | | HTTP Server Error Range | **5xx** (500–599) | status code | Server-side crash (KTU favourite) | | WSGI Spec Version | **PEP 3333** | spec number | Defines Python web standard | | Flask Default Port | **5000** | TCP port | Frequently asked | | Django Default Port | **8000** | TCP port | Frequently asked | | URL Parameter Format | `/user/\lang user_id\rangle` | route pattern | Dynamic routing | | Content-Type for JSON API | `application/json` | MIME type | IoT data exchange standard | | Content-Type for Forms | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` | MIME type | HTML form submission | | Flask Request Object | `request.args`, `request.form`, `request.json` | dict | Data extraction points | | Template Engine | **Jinja2** (Flask) / **Django Template Language** | engine name | Server-side rendering | | WSGI Default Thread | `threaded=True` | boolean | Concurrent request handling | | Async Framework | **FastAPI** on **ASGI** (PEP 3133) | — | High-throughput IoT streams | | Throughput (Flask sync) | $\approx 1000$ req/s | requests per second | Single worker baseline | | Throughput (FastAPI async) | $\approx 8000+$ req/s | requests per second | uvloop + httptools | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Healthcare**: Flask-based dashboards displaying real-time patient vitals (SpO₂, ECG) from wearable sensors. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Django REST Framework (DRF) exposes **Modbus/OPC-UA** sensor data as RESTful endpoints for SCADA systems. - **Smart Agriculture**: FastAPI ingests MQTT payloads from soil-moisture sensors and pushes alerts to farmer mobile apps. - **Home Automation**: Flask + `Flask-SocketIO` enables real-time bi-directional control of smart switches via a web UI. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Implementation, Derivation & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Mandate:** Since this is a **Computer Science / IoT course (PEEBT632)**, Python code IS permitted and expected. All code blocks are **runnable, exam-ready, and IoT-flavoured** to match the syllabus context. ## 3.1 Flask — From Installation to IoT Dashboard ### 3.1.1 Step 1: Environment Setup The first step is to create an isolated Python environment to avoid dependency conflicts across IoT projects. ```bash # Create a virtual environment (mandatory in KTU lab exams) python -m venv iot_flask_env # Activate the environment # On Windows: iot_flask_env\Scripts\activate # On Linux/macOS: source iot_flask_env/bin/activate # Install Flask pip install flask ``` **Logic behind each command:** - `python -m venv` invokes Python's built-in `venv` module to create a sandboxed folder `iot_flask_env` containing its own `pip` and `site-packages`. This prevents the classic "works on my machine" syndrome in KTU lab evaluations. - `pip install flask` fetches the Flask wheel from **PyPI (Python Package Index)** and installs it along with its dependencies: **Werkzeug** (WSGI toolkit) and **Jinja2** (template engine) and **MarkupSafe** and **itsdangerous** and **click**. ### 3.1.2 Step 2: A Minimal "Hello IoT" Application Create a file `app.py`: ```python from flask import Flask # Step 1: Instantiate the Flask application object app = Flask(__name__) # Step 2: Define a route (URL endpoint) and its handler function @app.route("/") def home(): return "

Welcome to KTU IoT Dashboard

" # Step 3: Run the development server if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Line-by-line logical breakdown:** - `from flask import Flask` — Imports the `Flask` class from the `flask` package. - `app = Flask(__name__)` — Creates the WSGI application object. The `__name__` argument tells Flask where to look for templates and static files (relative to the current module). - `@app.route("/")` — A **decorator** that registers the function `home()` as the handler for the URL `/` (the root). - `app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000)` — Starts the **Werkzeug** built-in development server. `0.0.0.0` means the server listens on **all network interfaces**, making it accessible from other devices (e.g., a smartphone viewing the sensor dashboard). - `debug=True` — Enables auto-reload on code changes and the interactive debugger (NEVER use in production). ### 3.1.3 Step 3: Dynamic Routes — Passing IoT Sensor IDs ```python from flask import Flask, jsonify import random import time app = Flask(__name__) # Simulated sensor database (in real IoT: replaced by InfluxDB / MongoDB) sensor_database = { "TEMP_01": {"type": "DHT22", "location": "Ward-A"}, "HUM_01": {"type": "DHT22", "location": "Ward-A"}, "ECG_01": {"type": "AD8232", "location": "ICU-1"} } @app.route("/api/sensor/") def get_sensor_data(sensor_id): """ Returns simulated real-time data for a given sensor. URL Example: http://localhost:5000/api/sensor/TEMP_01 """ if sensor_id not in sensor_database: return jsonify({ "error": "Sensor not found", "status_code": 404, "valid_sensors": list(sensor_database.keys()) }), 404 info = sensor_database[sensor_id] return jsonify({ "sensor_id": sensor_id, "type": info["type"], "location": info["location"], "value": round(random.uniform(20.0, 40.0), 2), "unit": "°C" if "TEMP" in sensor_id else "%", "timestamp": time.time() }), 200 if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Analytical breakdown:** - `` in the route is a **variable rule** captured by Flask and passed as a positional argument to `get_sensor_data(sensor_id)`. - `jsonify()` converts a Python dictionary to a proper **JSON response** with `Content-Type: application/json`. - Returning a tuple `(response, status_code)` follows Flask's convention for explicit HTTP status signaling — critical for IoT clients that branch on success/failure. - `time.time()` returns **Unix epoch time in seconds** (floating-point), which is the de-facto timestamp standard for IoT telemetry. ### 3.1.4 Step 4: HTML Templating with Jinja2 Create folder structure: ``` project/ ├── app.py └── templates/ └── dashboard.html ``` **`templates/dashboard.html`:** ```html KTU IoT Dashboard

🏥 Live Patient Monitoring

{% for sensor in sensors %}

{{ sensor.name }}

Reading: {{ sensor.value }} {{ sensor.unit }}

Status: {{ sensor.status }}

{% endfor %} ``` **`app.py` (updated):** ```python from flask import Flask, render_template app = Flask(__name__) @app.route("/dashboard") def dashboard(): sensors = [ {"name": "Temperature", "value": 36.8, "unit": "°C", "status": "Normal"}, {"name": "Heart Rate", "value": 78, "unit": "bpm", "status": "Normal"}, {"name": "SpO₂", "value": 98, "unit": "%", "status": "Optimal"}, ] return render_template("dashboard.html", sensors=sensors) if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(debug=True) ``` **Logic:** - `render_template()` is a Flask helper that uses the **Jinja2** engine to inject Python variables into the HTML. - `{% for sensor in sensors %}` is a **Jinja2 control block** that loops over the Python list — a server-side rendering pattern. - `{{ sensor.name }}` is a **Jinja2 expression** that outputs the variable value, automatically HTML-escaped to prevent **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** attacks — an important KTU security point. ### 3.1.5 Step 5: Handling HTTP POST from IoT Devices ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) # In-memory storage (replace with InfluxDB/PostgreSQL in production) readings = [] @app.route("/api/readings", methods=["POST"]) def ingest_reading(): """ Endpoint for IoT devices (e.g., ESP32) to push sensor data. Expected JSON body: { "device_id": "ESP32_001", "sensor": "DHT22", "value": 27.4, "unit": "°C" } """ data = request.get_json(force=True) # Validation required = ["device_id", "sensor", "value", "unit"] if not all(k in data for k in required): return jsonify({"error": "Missing fields", "required": required}), 400 data["received_at"] = datetime.utcnow().isoformat() readings.append(data) return jsonify({"message": "Reading stored", "id": len(readings)}), 201 @app.route("/api/readings", methods=["GET"]) def list_readings(): return jsonify(readings), 200 if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000) ``` **Analytical breakdown:** - `methods=["POST"]` restricts the endpoint to only POST requests. Flask responds with **405 Method Not Allowed** if a GET is attempted. - `request.get_json(force=True)` parses the body as JSON regardless of `Content-Type` header — useful when IoT firmware sends imperfect headers. - The validation block demonstrates **defensive programming**: the server never trusts client input, a principle OWASP ranks as **A04: Insecure Design**. - `datetime.utcnow().isoformat()` produces a standardized ISO-8601 string, ensuring cross-system time consistency. ## 3.2 Django — Quick Skeleton for KTU Django is more opinionated than Flask. It enforces project structure: ```bash django-admin startproject ktu_iot_project cd ktu_iot_project python manage.py startapp sensors ``` This creates: ``` ktu_iot_project/ ├── manage.py ├── ktu_iot_project/ │ ├── settings.py # Project configuration │ ├── urls.py # Root URL routing │ └── wsgi.py # WSGI entry point └── sensors/ ├── models.py # Database schema ├── views.py # Controller logic └── urls.py # App-level routing ``` **`sensors/models.py`:** ```python from django.db import models class SensorReading(models.Model): device_id = models.CharField(max_length=50) sensor_type = models.CharField(max_length=30) value = models.FloatField() unit = models.CharField(max_length=10) timestamp = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True) class Meta: ordering = ['-timestamp'] def __str__(self): return f"{self.device_id} - {self.sensor_type}: {self.value}{self.unit}" ``` **`sensors/views.py`:** ```python from django.http import JsonResponse from django.views.decorators.csrf import csrf_exempt import json from .models import SensorReading @csrf_exempt def ingest(request): if request.method == "POST": data = json.loads(request.body) reading = SensorReading.objects.create( device_id=data['device_id'], sensor_type=data['sensor'], value=data['value'], unit=data['unit'] ) return JsonResponse({"status": "ok", "id": reading.id}, status=201) return JsonResponse({"error": "POST only"}, status=405) ``` **`sensors/urls.py`:** ```python from django.urls import path from . import views urlpatterns = [ path('ingest/', views.ingest, name='ingest'), ] ``` ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Flask vs Django | Criterion | **Flask** | **Django** | |---|---|---| | Type | Microframework | Full-stack framework | | Philosophy | Minimal, "do it yourself" | "Batteries included" | | Boilerplate | Low | High (but generated) | | ORM | None (use SQLAlchemy) | Built-in Django ORM | | Admin Panel | Manual (Flask-Admin) | Auto-generated | | Template Engine | Jinja2 | DTL (own) | | Async Support | Partial (Flask 2.0+) | Full (Django 4.1+ ASGI) | | IoT Use Case | Lightweight REST APIs, dashboards | Complex data-management backends | | Lines for "Hello World" | 4 | ~20 (after scaffolding) | | Learning Curve | Gentle | Steep | | KTU Recommendation | **First framework to teach** | **Second / advanced module** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End IoT + Python Web Framework Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SensorLayer["Sensor / Hardware Layer"] S1["DHT22 Temperature"] S2["AD8232 ECG"] S3["Soil Moisture"] MCU["ESP32 / Arduino / Raspberry Pi"] end subgraph CommsLayer["Communication Layer"] WIFI["Wi-Fi / MQTT Broker"] HTTP["HTTP / HTTPS"] end subgraph AppLayer["Python Web Application Layer - Flask"] WSGI["WSGI Server - Gunicorn / Werkzeug"] ROUTE["URL Router - Flask Routes"] HANDLER["Route Handler - Business Logic"] DB[("Database - SQLite / InfluxDB")] end subgraph ClientLayer["Client / Presentation Layer"] DASH["HTML Dashboard - Jinja2"] MOBILE["Mobile App / Postman"] end S1 --> MCU S2 --> MCU S3 --> MCU MCU --> WIFI MCU --> HTTP WIFI --> WSGI HTTP --> WSGI WSGI --> ROUTE ROUTE --> HANDLER HANDLER <--> DB HANDLER --> DASH HANDLER --> MOBILE ``` ## 4.2 Flask Request–Response Lifecycle Flowchart ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Client Browser participant Server as WSGI Server participant Flask as Flask App participant Route as Route Handler participant DB as Database Client->>Server: GET /api/sensor/TEMP_01 HTTP/1.1 Server->>Flask: Invoke WSGI callable with environ Flask->>Route: Match URL pattern, call handler Route->>DB: Query sensor data DB-->>Route: Return dataset Route-->>Flask: Build JSON response Flask-->>Server: Return iterable response Server-->>Client: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + JSON body ``` ## 4.3 MVC Pattern Mapping in Flask ```mermaid flowchart LR USER["User Request"] --> CTRL["Controller - Flask Route Function"] CTRL --> MODEL["Model - SQLAlchemy / Sensor Data"] MODEL --> CTRL CTRL --> VIEW["View - Jinja2 Template"] VIEW --> RESPONSE["HTML / JSON Response"] RESPONSE --> USER ``` ## 4.4 Django MVT Pattern Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB REQ["Incoming HTTP Request"] --> URL["urls.py - URL Dispatcher"] URL --> VIEW["views.py - View Function"] VIEW --> MODEL["models.py - ORM Query"] MODEL --> DB[("PostgreSQL / SQLite")] DB --> MODEL MODEL --> VIEW VIEW --> TEMPLATE["Template - DTL HTML File"] TEMPLATE --> RESP["Rendered HTML Response"] RESP --> CLIENT["Browser / IoT Client"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A = 3 marks each (Short answer). Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice. Questions are mapped to **CO4 / CO5** of the PEEBT632 syllabus and span **Bloom Levels L1–L4** (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze). --- ## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the term WSGI. How is it relevant to Python web frameworks?** **Model Answer:** WSGI stands for **Web Server Gateway Interface**, defined in **PEP 3333**. It is a specification that establishes a **standard contract** between a Python web application and a web server. WSGI ensures that any WSGI-compliant framework (Flask, Django, Bottle, Pyramid) can run on any WSGI-compliant server (Gunicorn, uWSGI, mod_wsgi). It is relevant because it promotes **interoperability, portability, and reuse** — the same Flask app can be deployed on Apache, Nginx, or a standalone server without code changes. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "WSGI is a framework" or "WSGI is a server." Neither is correct. It is a **specification / interface contract**. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between Flask and Django in terms of architecture and IoT suitability.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Flask | Django | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Microframework, MVC-like | Full-stack, MVT pattern | | Boilerplate | Minimal | Heavy, auto-generated | | ORM | External (SQLAlchemy) | Built-in | | Admin UI | Requires extension | Auto-generated | | IoT Suitability | Lightweight REST APIs, dashboards | Complex data management, multi-app backends | Flask is preferred for **simple IoT telemetry ingestion** with low overhead, while Django is suited for **enterprise IoT platforms** with authentication, admin panels, and complex relational data. --- ## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Request–Response lifecycle** of a Flask application. List the key components involved. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Flask program to create a REST API that accepts **JSON sensor data** (device_id, temperature, humidity) via a POST endpoint, stores it in a list, and exposes a GET endpoint to retrieve all stored readings. --- ### **Model Answer (a):** The Flask request–response lifecycle consists of the following steps: 1. **Client Request Initiation** — A client (browser, mobile app, or IoT device) sends an HTTP request to the server on a specific URL, e.g., `GET /api/data HTTP/1.1`. 2. **WSGI Server Reception** — The WSGI server (Werkzeug development server or Gunicorn in production) accepts the TCP connection, parses the HTTP headers, and constructs an `environ` dictionary. 3. **Middleware Processing** — Optional middleware (e.g., Flask-Login, CORS handlers) inspects/modifies the request before it reaches the application. 4. **URL Routing** — Flask's `url_map` matches the path to a registered route. If no match, Flask returns **404**. 5. **View Function Execution** — The matched route function runs, optionally interacting with the Model layer (database, sensor API). 6. **Response Construction** — The function returns a string, dict, or `Response` object. Flask wraps it with status code and headers. 7. **WSGI Server Transmission** — The server serializes the response per HTTP spec and sends it back over the socket. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Client"] --> B["WSGI Server"] B --> C["Middleware Stack"] C --> D["URL Router"] D --> E["Route Handler"] E --> F["Model / Database"] F --> E E --> G["Response Object"] G --> B B --> A ``` **Key components:** WSGI Server, Middleware, URL Router, Route Handler (View), Model layer, Response Object, Template Engine (Jinja2). --- ### **Model Answer (b):** ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify app = Flask(__name__) # In-memory storage simulating an IoT database sensor_readings = [] @app.route("/api/sensor", methods=["POST"]) def ingest_data(): """ Accepts JSON: {"device_id": "ESP32_01", "temperature": 27.4, "humidity": 65.2} """ payload = request.get_json(force=True) # Validation if not all(k in payload for k in ["device_id", "temperature", "humidity"]): return jsonify({ "error": "Missing required fields", "required": ["device_id", "temperature", "humidity"] }), 400 sensor_readings.append(payload) return jsonify({ "message": "Data stored successfully", "record_id": len(sensor_readings) }), 201 @app.route("/api/sensor", methods=["GET"]) def get_data(): return jsonify({ "count": len(sensor_readings), "readings": sensor_readings }), 200 @app.route("/api/sensor/", methods=["GET"]) def get_by_device(device_id): filtered = [r for r in sensor_readings if r.get("device_id") == device_id] if not filtered: return jsonify({"error": "Device not found"}), 404 return jsonify(filtered), 200 if __name__ == "__main__": app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000, debug=True) ``` **Explanation of key points:** - `request.get_json(force=True)` parses the JSON body regardless of `Content-Type`. - Validation ensures data integrity — never trust IoT device payloads. - `201 Created` is the correct status code for successful resource creation (POST). - `404 Not Found` is returned when a device has no readings. - The `` variable rule enables **dynamic URL routing**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by: > 1. **Forgetting validation** — Always check required fields before processing. > 2. **Wrong status code** — `200` is for GET success; `201` is for POST creation. > 3. **No error handling** — A single `KeyError` will crash the IoT data pipeline. > 4. **Hardcoding port** — Use `5000` (Flask default) unless the question specifies otherwise. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MVT architecture** of Django. How does it differ from the traditional MVC pattern? Illustrate with a suitable diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design and implement a Django application with a `PatientVitals` model (fields: patient_id, heart_rate, spo2, body_temp, recorded_at). Write the view and URL configuration to **POST** new vitals and **GET** the latest vital for a given `patient_id`. --- ### **Model Answer (a):** Django follows the **MVT (Model–View–Template)** pattern: - **Model** (`models.py`): Defines the data schema and provides an ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) for database operations. Each model class maps to a database table. - **View** (`views.py`): A Python function (or class) that receives an HTTP request, processes it (queries models, applies business logic), and returns an HTTP response. In MVT, the View is essentially the **Controller** of MVC. - **Template** (`templates/`): An HTML file with Django Template Language (DTL) tags that renders the response. This is equivalent to the **View** in MVC. **Key Difference from MVC:** Django's terminology renames Controller → View and View → Template. The framework itself acts as the *Controller*, dispatching requests based on `urls.py`. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB REQ["HTTP Request"] --> URL["urls.py - URLconf"] URL --> V["views.py - View Function"] V --> M["models.py - ORM"] M <--> DB[("Database")] M --> V V --> T["Template - DTL HTML"] T --> RESP["HTTP Response"] ``` --- ### **Model Answer (b):** **`patients/models.py`:** ```python from django.db import models class PatientVitals(models.Model): patient_id = models.CharField(max_length=20) heart_rate = models.IntegerField(help_text="Beats per minute") spo2 = models.IntegerField(help_text="Oxygen saturation %") body_temp = models.FloatField(help_text="Celsius") recorded_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True) class Meta: ordering = ['-recorded_at'] def __str__(self): return f"Patient {self.patient_id} @ {self.recorded_at}" ``` **`patients/views.py`:** ```python from django.http import JsonResponse from django.views.decorators.csrf import csrf_exempt import json from .models import PatientVitals @csrf_exempt def add_vitals(request): if request.method == "POST": data = json.loads(request.body) vital = PatientVitals.objects.create( patient_id=data['patient_id'], heart_rate=data['heart_rate'], spo2=data['spo2'], body_temp=data['body_temp'] ) return JsonResponse({"id": vital.id, "status": "stored"}, status=201) return JsonResponse({"error": "POST only"}, status=405) def latest_vitals(request, patient_id): vital = PatientVitals.objects.filter(patient_id=patient_id).first() if not vital: return JsonResponse({"error": "No data found"}, status=404) return JsonResponse({ "patient_id": vital.patient_id, "heart_rate": vital.heart_rate, "spo2": vital.spo2, "body_temp": vital.body_temp, "recorded_at": vital.recorded_at.isoformat() }) ``` **`patients/urls.py`:** ```python from django.urls import path from . import views urlpatterns = [ path('vitals/add/', views.add_vitals, name='add_vitals'), path('vitals//', views.latest_vitals, name='latest_vitals'), ] ``` **Project-level `urls.py` inclusion:** ```python from django.contrib import admin from django.urls import path, include urlpatterns = [ path('admin/', admin.site.urls), path('api/', include('patients.urls')), ] ``` **Explanation:** - `@csrf_exempt` disables CSRF verification for the POST endpoint — required when IoT clients (which don't carry Django CSRF tokens) submit data. - `auto_now_add=True` automatically stamps the `recorded_at` field with the current UTC time when a record is created. - `filter(...).first()` retrieves the most recent record because of `Meta.ordering = ['-recorded_at']`. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse `auto_now_add` (set only on creation) with `auto_now` (updated on every save). Using the wrong one can break the temporal ordering of IoT readings. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **WSGI (PEP 3333)** is the contract that decouples Python web apps from servers. - ✅ **Flask** is a microframework — minimal, flexible, ideal for lightweight IoT APIs. - ✅ **Django** is a full-stack, opinionated framework — best for complex IoT backends. - ✅ **MVC (Flask)** and **MVT (Django)** are architectural patterns; Django's "View" is actually a *Controller*. - ✅ **Jinja2** is Flask's template engine; DTL is Django's. Both auto-escape HTML to prevent XSS. - ✅ **HTTP status codes** are mandatory knowledge: **200** (OK), **201** (Created), **400** (Bad Request), **404** (Not Found), **500** (Server Error). - ✅ **Flask listens on port 5000** by default; **Django on 8000**. - ✅ Always use **`request.get_json(force=True)`** in Flask to robustly parse IoT payloads. - ✅ Use **CSRF exemption** carefully — only for trusted IoT endpoints, never for browser-facing forms. - ✅ `0.0.0.0` host binding is required to access the dev server from another device on the LAN. - ✅ **JSON (`application/json`)** is the de-facto IoT data interchange format. - ✅ KTU lab exams expect: `venv` setup, route definition, template rendering, and CRUD via REST. - ✅ The two most common **2-mark** questions: *"List HTTP methods"* and *"Differentiate Flask vs Django"*. - ✅ For **IoT context**, the typical exam problem involves: **sensor → Flask/Django → database → dashboard**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "WFRD"**: **W**SGI, **F**lask, **R**outing, **D**jango. These four pillars cover ~90% of KTU questions on this topic.

topics.title: Designing a RESTful Web API
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT632 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Designing a RESTful Web API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **RESTful Web API** (Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interface) is an architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems that uses the stateless HTTP protocol to communicate between clients and servers. In the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications**, a RESTful Web API is the standardized interface that allows biomedical sensors, wearable devices, gateways, and cloud platforms to exchange structured data (typically in **JSON** or **XML** formats) over the internet using uniform resource identifiers and standard HTTP methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *REST is an architectural style, NOT a protocol or standard. It is a set of constraints that, when applied to web service design, yields a system that is scalable, stateless, cacheable, and layered — ideal for IoT-biomedical data exchange.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Library Card" Analogy)

Imagine you walk into a **library**:

- The **books** are **Resources** (e.g., a patient's ECG record, a heart rate reading).
- The **call numbers** (e.g., "RA 616.12 W37") are the **URIs** (unique addresses for every resource).
- The **library rules** (you may "view a book," "borrow," "return," or "reserve") are the **HTTP Methods** (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- The **librarian** is the **Server** — she does not remember what you borrowed last week (statelessness).
- You (the patron) are the **Client** — your phone, laptop, or medical gateway requesting the data.

Just as a library card lets *any* patron access *any* book via a *uniform* process, a RESTful API lets *any* device access *any* biomedical resource via a *uniform* HTTP request.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Biomedical IoT:** A wearable ECG monitor, a clinician's tablet, and a hospital's cloud server can all "talk" to the *same* API without custom code for each — this is the power of REST.

## 1.3 The Six Guiding Constraints of REST (Roy Fielding, 2000)

For an API to qualify as "RESTful," it must respect:

1. **Client–Server Separation** — UI and data storage concerns are independent.
2. **Statelessness** — Each request carries *all* information the server needs. The server stores **no client session state**.
3. **Cacheability** — Responses must indicate whether they can be cached (e.g., vital signs from 5 minutes ago).
4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized resource identification, manipulation via representations, self-descriptive messages, and **HATEOAS** (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State).
5. **Layered System** — Client cannot tell if it is talking to the end server or an intermediate proxy (e.g., a hospital gateway).
6. **Code-On-Demand (Optional)** — Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) to the client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Statelessness is the *single most-tested* REST property. A question worth 7 marks will often ask: *"Why is REST well-suited for IoT devices with limited memory?"* — the answer is **statelessness eliminates the need for the server to remember clients**.

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standards (Industry-Specified)

- **HTTP Version Used:** **HTTP/1.1** (universally) and increasingly **HTTP/2** (multiplexed).
- **Default Data Formats:** **JSON** (~95% of modern IoT APIs) and **XML** (legacy HL7/FHIR medical systems).
- **Content Negotiation Header:** `Accept: application/json`
- **Authentication Standards:** **OAuth 2.0**, **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)**, **API Keys**, **mTLS**.
- **Standard Port:** **80** (HTTP) and **443** (HTTPS).
- **Medical Extension Standard:** **HL7 FHIR** (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) — built on REST.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resource Identification — The Core of REST Design

In REST, **everything is a Resource**. A resource is *any* named piece of information that can be addressed. In an IoT-Biomedical API, resources include:

- A specific patient: `Patient/12345`
- A vital sign reading: `Observation/heart-rate-987`
- A medical device: `Device/bp-monitor-A12`
- An alert: `Alert/high-bp-2024`

Each resource is uniquely identified by a **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier)**. Designing good URIs is the heart of API design.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind HTTP Method Semantics

HTTP methods are **verbs** applied to resource **nouns**. Their semantics are standardized by **RFC 7231** (and RFC 9110):

- **GET** — *Read* a resource. **Safe** and **Idempotent**. No side-effects.
- **POST** — *Create* a new resource, or trigger a process. NOT idempotent.
- **PUT** — *Replace* an existing resource *completely* (or create at a known URI). **Idempotent**.
- **PATCH** — *Partial update* of a resource. Neither safe nor strictly idempotent.
- **DELETE** — *Remove* a resource. **Idempotent** (deleting a deleted resource yields the same final state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotency** means: *making the same request N times produces the same result as making it once.* This is **critical for IoT** — when a sensor in a basement with poor connectivity retransmits a "turn off insulin pump" command 5 times, the patient must not receive 5 shutdowns.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Design Element** | **Convention / Rule** | **Biomedical/IoT Example** | **Why It Matters** |
|---|---|---|---|
| URI Noun Form | Always **plural nouns**, never verbs | `/api/v1/patients` (✅) vs `/api/v1/getPatient` (❌) | Verbs are encoded in HTTP methods, not the URI |
| URI Hierarchy | Reflects **parent–child relationships** | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/ecg-readings` | Mirrors natural data ownership |
| HTTP Method | Maps to **CRUD** operation | `GET`→Read, `POST`→Create, `PUT`→Update, `DELETE`→Delete | Uniform interface |
| Status Code (2xx) | **200 OK**, **201 Created**, **204 No Content** | 201 on POST creating a new reading | Tells client success state |
| Status Code (4xx) | **400 Bad Request**, **401 Unauthorized**, **404 Not Found**, **409 Conflict** | 404 for unknown patient ID | Client error (no retry) |
| Status Code (5xx) | **500 Internal Server Error**, **503 Service Unavailable** | 503 when biometric DB is down | Server error (retry safe) |
| Content-Type | `application/json` for IoT, `application/fhir+json` for medical | Header in every request/response | Required for parsing |
| Authentication | **Bearer Token / OAuth 2.0** in `Authorization` header | `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOi...` | Secures PHI (Protected Health Information) |
| Versioning | Prefix URI with `/v1/`, `/v2/` | `/api/v2/patients` | Future-proofing |
| Pagination | Query params: `?limit=20&offset=40` | `GET /api/v1/alerts?limit=50` | Avoids IoT gateway memory overflow |
| Filtering | Query params: `?status=critical&since=2024-01-01` | `GET /api/v1/patients?status=admitted` | Reduces payload size |
| HATEOAS Link | Response contains navigation links | `{"self": "...", "next": "..."}` | Discoverability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe safety:** All absolute-value / delimiter notations in formulas use LaTeX `\vert` (e.g., $status \in \{200, 201, 204\}$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 REST vs. Other IoT Protocols — Strategic Positioning

| **Property** | **REST/HTTP** | **MQTT** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| Payload Size | Larger (JSON headers) | Very small | Tiny (binary) |
| Power Consumption | High (not for coin-cell) | **Low** | **Lowest** |
| Best Use | Cloud ↔ App, EHR integration | Sensor ↔ Gateway | Sensor ↔ Sensor |
| Standardization | RFC + HL7 FHIR | OASIS | IETF RFC 7252 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** REST is **not** the best choice for constrained sensor-to-sensor links. It dominates the **Application Layer** between gateways, cloud, and clinical dashboards. In a typical biomedical IoT stack, MQTT/CoAP carry data *to* the gateway, and REST carries data *from* the gateway *to* the cloud/EHR.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Apple HealthKit & Google Fit Cloud APIs** — RESTful endpoints expose user health data to authorized apps.
- **Philips HealthSuite** — REST API for hospital device telemetry.
- **HL7 FHIR** — The global medical standard (used by NHS, Mayo Clinic) is REST-based.
- **AWS IoT Core** — Provides a REST API endpoint (`https://<endpoint>/things/<thingName>/shadow`) for device shadow management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The RESTful API Design Pipeline (Logical Derivation)

Designing a RESTful API for a biomedical IoT system follows a strict 7-step logical pipeline. We derive each step from the previous, mimicking the way a KTU examiner expects a "design" question to be answered.

### Step 1 — Identify the Domain & Use Cases

**Logic:** Before any URI is written, we *must* know what the system does.

**Derivation:** Let $\mathcal{D}$ be the biomedical domain. We enumerate actors $\mathcal{A}$ and operations $\mathcal{O}$.

$$
\mathcal{A} = \{ \text{Patient}, \text{Clinician}, \text{Device Gateway}, \text{Cloud Server} \}
$$

$$
\mathcal{O} = \{ \text{Register device}, \text{Submit vital sign}, \text{Retrieve history}, \text{Trigger alert} \}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Every operation $\mathcal{O}_i$ must map to exactly **one** HTTP method and **one** URI. This is the *Uniform Interface* constraint.

---

### Step 2 — Model Resources (Nouns, Not Verbs)

**Logic:** Extract the *nouns* from the use cases. A common mistake is to encode actions in the URI (e.g., `/getPatient`) — this is **anti-REST**.

**Derivation:** From $\mathcal{O}$, we derive the resource set $\mathcal{R}$:

$$
\mathcal{R} = \{ \text{Patient}, \text{Device}, \text{Observation}, \text{Alert} \}
$$

**Rule Applied:** URIs are **pluralized nouns** representing collections, and *singular nouns* (or IDs) representing individual instances.

---

### Step 3 — Design URI Hierarchy (Parent–Child Derivation)

**Logic:** A resource may "belong to" another resource. We model this with URI path nesting.

**Derivation Rule:** If a child resource $\mathcal{R}_c$ has no meaning without its parent $\mathcal{R}_p$, then:

$$
\text{URI}(\mathcal{R}_c) = \text{URI}(\mathcal{R}_p) + \text{"/"} + \text{child-segment}
$$

**Worked Example — A Patient's ECG Readings:**

$$
\text{URI}_{\text{patient}} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{id\}}
$$

$$
\text{URI}_{\text{ecg}} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{id\}}/ \text{ecg-readings/\{readingId\}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The path `/patients/123/ecg-readings/987` reads naturally as *"the ECG reading number 987 belonging to patient number 123"* — a self-describing URI, satisfying the *Uniform Interface* sub-constraint of **identification of resources**.

---

### Step 4 — Map HTTP Methods to CRUD Operations (Method Selection Matrix)

**Logic:** For every URI template, we determine the legal HTTP methods.

**Derivation:** The standard CRUD-to-HTTP mapping is:

| **CRUD Operation** | **HTTP Method** | **URI** | **Body** | **Idempotent?** | **Safe?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Create | `POST` | `/patients` | New patient JSON | ❌ | ❌ |
| Read (all) | `GET` | `/patients` | None | ✅ | ✅ |
| Read (one) | `GET` | `/patients/{id}` | None | ✅ | ✅ |
| Update (full) | `PUT` | `/patients/{id}` | Complete patient JSON | ✅ | ❌ |
| Update (partial) | `PATCH` | `/patients/{id}` | Delta JSON | ❌ | ❌ |
| Delete | `DELETE` | `/patients/{id}` | None | ✅ | ❌ |

**Conversion Logic:** *Idempotent* means $f(x) = f(f(x))$. In a biomedical IoT context, this guarantees that a duplicate alert transmission (due to network jitter) does not duplicate the patient alarm.

---

### Step 5 — Define Representations (Payload Schema)

**Logic:** A resource has *multiple representations* (JSON, XML). The client requests via the `Accept` header.

**Derivation:** Let the representation of a `Patient` resource be:

$$
\text{Patient}_{\text{JSON}} = \{ \text{id}, \text{name}, \text{dob}, \text{devices} : [ \dots ] \}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Representations are decoupled from the resource itself — this is the *decoupling of representation from resource* sub-constraint of the Uniform Interface.

---

### Step 6 — Status Code & Error Model

**Logic:** Every response *must* carry a precise status code.

**Derivation:** The HTTP status code $S$ is chosen as:

$$
S = \begin{cases}
2xx & \text{if request succeeded (200, 201, 204)} \\
4xx & \text{if client error (400, 401, 403, 404, 409)} \\
5xx & \text{if server error (500, 502, 503, 504)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A 4xx code tells the client *"do not retry — fix the request"*, whereas a 5xx tells the client *"retry with exponential backoff"*. This is **critical for medical IoT gateways** that manage their own retry logic.

---

### Step 7 — Apply Cross-Cutting Concerns

**Logic:** Authentication, rate-limiting, logging, and pagination are *not* in the URI but in headers.

**Derivation:** Each cross-cutting concern $C_i$ is expressed as an HTTP header $H_{C_i}$:

$$
H_{\text{auth}} = \text{"Authorization: Bearer <token>"}
$$

$$
H_{\text{rate}} = \text{"X-RateLimit-Remaining: 487"}
$$

$$
H_{\text{cache}} = \text{"Cache-Control: max-age=60"}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** By keeping these out of the URI, we preserve the **idempotency** of URIs — the same URI with different headers is still the *same resource*.

---

## 3.2 The Analytical Design Matrix (Master Reference)

The following table consolidates the entire design derivation into a single decision matrix for the case study *"Remote Cardiac Monitor API"*:

| **Use Case** | **HTTP Method** | **URI Template** | **Status Code (Success)** | **Status Code (Error)** | **Auth Required?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register new patient | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients` | 201 | 400, 409 | Yes (Clinician) |
| List all patients | `GET` | `/api/v1/patients` | 200 | 401, 403 | Yes (Clinician) |
| Get patient details | `GET` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}` | 200 | 404 | Yes |
| Update patient info | `PUT` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}` | 200 | 400, 404 | Yes (Clinician) |
| Register new device | `POST` | `/api/v1/devices` | 201 | 400 | Yes (Admin) |
| Submit ECG reading | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/ecg-readings` | 201 | 400, 503 | Yes (Device) |
| Retrieve ECG history | `GET` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/ecg-readings` | 200 | 404 | Yes |
| Trigger emergency alert | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/alerts` | 202 | 503 | Yes (Device) |
| Acknowledge alert | `PATCH` | `/api/v1/alerts/{alertId}` | 200 | 404, 409 | Yes (Clinician) |
| Delete patient record | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}` | 204 | 404, 403 | Yes (Admin) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RESTful API Architecture in an IoT–Biomedical System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a RESTful Web API sits between the *device-edge layer* and the *clinical-application layer*, which is the canonical placement in modern biomedical systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeLayer["EDGE LAYER - Sensors and Gateways"]
        sensorA["Wearable ECG Sensor"]
        sensorB["Blood Pressure Cuff"]
        sensorC["Pulse Oximeter"]
        gateway["IoT Gateway MCU"]
    end

    subgraph NetLayer["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        mqttLink["MQTT Broker TLS 8883"]
        httpLink["HTTPS REST 443"]
    end

    subgraph APILayer["REST API LAYER - The Core"]
        router["API Gateway Router"]
        authMW["Auth Middleware OAuth 2"]
        rateMW["Rate Limiter 100 req min"]
        ctrlPatient["Patient Controller"]
        ctrlDevice["Device Controller"]
        ctrlObs["Observation Controller"]
        ctrlAlert["Alert Controller"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["DATA LAYER"]
        dbPat["Patient DB PostgreSQL"]
        dbObs["Time Series DB InfluxDB"]
        queueAlert["Alert Queue Redis"]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer["CLINICAL APPLICATION LAYER"]
        ehr["Hospital EHR HL7 FHIR"]
        docDash["Clinician Dashboard"]
        mobileApp["Patient Mobile App"]
    end

    sensorA --> gateway
    sensorB --> gateway
    sensorC --> gateway
    gateway --> mqttLink
    mqttLink --> router
    docDash --> httpLink
    mobileApp --> httpLink
    ehr --> httpLink
    httpLink --> router
    router --> authMW
    authMW --> rateMW
    rateMW --> ctrlPatient
    rateMW --> ctrlDevice
    rateMW --> ctrlObs
    rateMW --> ctrlAlert
    ctrlPatient --> dbPat
    ctrlObs --> dbObs
    ctrlAlert --> queueAlert
    queueAlert --> docDash
    dbPat --> ehr
    dbObs --> ehr
```

## 4.2 Request-Response Lifecycle (Sequence Diagram)

The following Mermaid sequence diagram shows the full lifecycle of a *POST /api/v1/patients/123/ecg-readings* call, which is the most-tested flow in KTU board exams.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as ECG Device Gateway
    participant AUTH as Auth Middleware
    participant CTRL as Observation Controller
    participant DB as Time Series DB
    participant NOTI as Notification Service
    participant DOC as Clinician Dashboard

    DEV->>AUTH: POST /patients/123/ecg-readings Bearer JWT
    AUTH->>AUTH: Verify JWT signature and expiry
    AUTH->>CTRL: Forward request with user context
    CTRL->>CTRL: Validate JSON schema heartRate QRS
    CTRL->>DB: INSERT observation row
    DB-->>CTRL: 201 Created with readingId
    CTRL->>NOTI: Async fire alert if HR > 120
    NOTI-->>DOC: Push notification abnormal reading
    CTRL-->>DEV: 201 Created Location header
    DOC->>AUTH: GET /patients/123/ecg-readings
    AUTH->>CTRL: Forward
    CTRL->>DB: SELECT last 100 readings
    DB-->>CTRL: 200 OK JSON array
    CTRL-->>DOC: 200 OK with payload
```

## 4.3 URI Design Tree (Resource Hierarchy)

The following Mermaid graph depicts the URI resource tree for a complete Hospital IoT API, satisfying the KTU expectation that students "draw a resource hierarchy."

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["api/v1"]
    root --> patients["patients"]
    root --> devices["devices"]
    root --> alerts["alerts"]

    patients --> patientOne["patients id"]
    patientOne --> ecg["ecg-readings"]
    patientOne --> bp["bp-readings"]
    patientOne --> spo2["spo2-readings"]
    patientOne --> patAlerts["alerts"]

    ecg --> ecgOne["ecg-readings readingId"]
    bp --> bpOne["bp-readings readingId"]
    spo2 --> spo2One["spo2-readings readingId"]
    patAlerts --> patAlertOne["alerts alertId"]

    devices --> deviceOne["devices deviceId"]
    deviceOne --> telemetry["telemetry"]

    alerts --> alertOne["alerts alertId"]

    style root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style patients fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style devices fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style alerts fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define REST. List the six architectural constraints proposed by Roy Fielding for a system to be considered RESTful.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems, introduced by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It defines a set of constraints that, when applied to web service design, yields a scalable, stateless, and loosely coupled system.

**The Six Constraints:**

1. **Client–Server** — Separation of UI from data storage.
2. **Stateless** — Each request is independent; no client context is stored on the server.
3. **Cacheable** — Responses define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable.
4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized resource identification and manipulation.
5. **Layered System** — Architecture composed of hierarchical layers (proxies, gateways).
6. **Code-On-Demand** *(Optional)* — Server may extend client functionality by transferring executable code.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining the six constraints.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is REST particularly suitable for IoT and biomedical data exchange? Explain in the context of statelessness and interoperability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

REST is highly suitable for biomedical IoT systems for two primary reasons:

1. **Statelessness** — IoT gateways and biomedical devices often have limited memory and intermittent connectivity. Because REST requires *no server-side session state*, the server does not need to remember the device between requests. Each request carries full authentication and data, allowing any server instance to handle it — a property that enables **horizontal scaling** and **fault tolerance**.

2. **Interoperability** — REST uses standard HTTP and standardized data formats (JSON/XML). When combined with the **HL7 FHIR** standard, REST APIs enable seamless data exchange between diverse medical devices, Electronic Health Records (EHR), and clinical dashboards, irrespective of manufacturer.

*(Award 1.5 marks for each reason with biomedical context.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**Q: A hospital is deploying a Remote Patient Monitoring system. Design a RESTful Web API for the following operations: (a) Register a new patient and assign a wearable heart-rate monitor. (b) Submit a heart-rate reading from the device and retrieve the latest 10 readings for a clinician.**

#### Part (a) — Patient and Device Registration (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Resource Identification**

We identify the following resources (nouns):

- `Patient` — represents a registered hospital patient.
- `Device` — represents a wearable monitor assigned to a patient.

**Step 2 — URI Design**

We use the *plural-noun, hierarchical* convention:

$$
\text{URI}_{\text{patient}} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{patientId\}}
$$

$$
\text{URI}_{\text{device}} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{patientId\}/\text{devices}/\{deviceId\}}
$$

**Step 3 — HTTP Method Mapping**

| **Action** | **HTTP Method** | **URI** | **Status Code** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create patient | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients` | 201 Created |
| Assign device | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients/{patientId}/devices` | 201 Created |

**Step 4 — Request & Response Example (JSON Representation)**

*Request to create patient:*

```json
{
  "name": "Anil Kumar",
  "dob": "1968-04-12",
  "gender": "male",
  "wardId": "ICU-3"
}
```

*Response:*

```json
{
  "patientId": "P-20241",
  "self": "/api/v1/patients/P-20241",
  "status": "registered"
}
```

**Step 5 — Headers & Auth**

`Authorization: Bearer <JWT issued to clinician>` and `Content-Type: application/json`.

**Step 6 — Error Handling**

If patient ID conflicts, return `409 Conflict`. If JWT is invalid, return `401 Unauthorized`. *(1 mark reserved for cross-cutting concerns.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Heart-Rate Submission and Retrieval (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Resource Identification**

A heart-rate reading is a child resource of `Patient` and `Device`. We model it as an `Observation`.

**Step 2 — URI Design**

$$
\text{URI}_{\text{reading}} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{patientId\}/\text{devices}/\{deviceId\}/\text{readings}}
$$

**Step 3 — Method Mapping**

| **Action** | **HTTP Method** | **URI** | **Idempotent?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Submit a new reading | `POST` | `/api/v1/.../readings` | No (each is a new sample) |
| Get latest 10 readings | `GET` | `/api/v1/.../readings?limit=10&sort=-timestamp` | Yes |

**Step 4 — Pagination via Query Parameters**

The expression `?limit=10&sort=-timestamp` requests the *10 most recent* readings. The leading minus sign denotes *descending order*. This is the **RQL (Resource Query Language)** convention used by HL7 FHIR.

**Step 5 — Response Payload**

```json
{
  "patientId": "P-20241",
  "deviceId": "DEV-A12",
  "readings": [
    { "timestamp": "2024-05-10T08:15:00Z", "bpm": 78 },
    { "timestamp": "2024-05-10T08:14:00Z", "bpm": 80 }
  ],
  "total": 2,
  "self": "/api/v1/patients/P-20241/devices/DEV-A12/readings?limit=10"
}
```

**Step 6 — Critical Clinical Concern**

The system must trigger an *asynchronous alert* (e.g., webhook, push notification) when `bpm > 120` or `bpm < 50`. The HTTP response itself returns `201 Created`; the alert is **decoupled** via a message queue, preserving REST's statelessness.
