### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Q: With reference to IoT–biomedical systems, (a) Compare REST, MQTT, and CoAP as communication protocols. (b) Design a RESTful API for a glucose-monitoring wearable, including URI hierarchy, HTTP methods, and status codes.**

#### Part (a) — Protocol Comparison (7 marks)

| **Property** | **REST/HTTP** | **MQTT** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture Style** | Request–Response, Client–Server | Publish–Subscribe, Broker-based | Request–Response over UDP |
| **Transport Protocol** | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| **Typical Port** | 80, 443 | 1883, 8883 (TLS) | 5683, 5684 (DTLS) |
| **Message Header Size** | Large (hundreds of bytes) | Very small (2 bytes minimum) | Tiny (4 bytes) |
| **Power Consumption** | **High** (unsuitable for coin-cell sensors) | **Low** | **Lowest** |
| **Best Suited For** | Cloud ↔ Application, EHR integration | Sensor ↔ Gateway | Constrained device ↔ device |
| **Security** | HTTPS, OAuth 2.0, mTLS | TLS, username/password, X.509 | DTLS, PSK, X.509 |
| **QoS Levels** | None (rely on TCP) | 0, 1, 2 | Confirmable / Non-confirmable |
| **Biomedical Use Case** | Patient app fetching history from cloud | Glucose sensor publishing to phone gateway | Direct sensor-to-insulin-pump link |
| **Standardization Body** | IETF (HTTP), HL7 (FHIR) | OASIS | IETF (RFC 7252) |

**Conclusion:** In a typical biomedical IoT stack, **CoAP/MQTT** are used on the *device-to-gateway* link (low power), and **REST/HTTP** is used on the *gateway-to-cloud/EHR* link (high interoperability).

---

#### Part (b) — Glucose Monitor RESTful API Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Resource Identification**

- `Patient` — The user wearing the device.
- `Device` — The continuous glucose monitor (CGM).
- `GlucoseReading` — A single glucose concentration value at a timestamp.

**Step 2 — URI Hierarchy**

$$
\text{Root} = \text{/api/v1}
$$

$$
\text{Glucose readings} = \text{/api/v1/patients/\{patientId\}/\text{devices}/\{deviceId\}/\text{glucose-readings/\{readingId\}}
$$

**Step 3 — Full HTTP Method Map**

| **Action** | **Method** | **URI** | **Success Code** | **Error Codes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register CGM device | `POST` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/devices` | 201 | 400, 409 |
| Get device info | `GET` | `/api/v1/devices/{deviceId}` | 200 | 404 |
| Submit reading | `POST` | `/api/v1/.../glucose-readings` | 201 | 400, 503 |
| Get latest reading | `GET` | `/api/v1/.../glucose-readings?limit=1` | 200 | 404 |
| Get 24-hour history | `GET` | `/api/v1/.../glucose-readings?since=24h` | 200 | 404 |
| Acknowledge alert | `PATCH` | `/api/v1/patients/{id}/alerts/{alertId}` | 200 | 404, 409 |
| Deregister device | `DELETE` | `/api/v1/devices/{deviceId}` | 204 | 403, 404 |

**Step 4 — Clinical Threshold Logic**

The API server computes, server-side, whether a reading crosses clinical thresholds (e.g., $< 70$ mg/dL = hypoglycemia, $> 180$ mg/dL = hyperglycemia). When triggered, a `POST` is made *internally* to `/alerts`, decoupling the reading submission from the alert lifecycle.

**Step 5 — HATEOAS Response Example**

```json
{
  "readingId": "G-99821",
  "value_mg_dL": 65,
  "timestamp": "2024-12-10T03:42:00Z",
  "flag": "HYPO",
  "links": {
    "self": "/api/v1/patients/P-12/devices/D-7/glucose-readings/G-99821",
    "patient": "/api/v1/patients/P-12",
    "alert": "/api/v1/patients/P-12/alerts/A-3210"
  }
}
```

This response is *self-describing* — a clinician's dashboard can navigate the `links` without out-of-band documentation. *(1 mark reserved for HATEOAS demonstration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using verbs in URIs** (`/getPatient`, `/deleteReading`) — **ZERO marks** for the URI design section. Always use plural nouns.
> 2. **Forgetting Status Codes** — A correct URI with no status code discussion loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `PUT` and `PATCH`** — `PUT` replaces the *entire* resource; `PATCH` modifies *part* of it. Examiners frequently test this.
> 4. **Omitting Authentication** — A "Design a REST API" question without OAuth/JWT/API Key discussion loses 1–2 marks in the cross-cutting concerns section.
> 5. **Forgetting Idempotency vs. Safety** — `GET` is both safe *and* idempotent. `POST` is neither. `PUT`/`DELETE` are idempotent but not safe. State this explicitly in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Choosing REST for sensor-to-sensor links** — REST/HTTP is too heavy for constrained IoT links. Examiners deduct marks if you propose REST for direct sensor communication.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **REST = architectural style** (not a protocol); defined by Roy Fielding in 2000.
- ✅ **Six constraints:** Client–Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-On-Demand (optional).
- ✅ **Resources** are nouns; **HTTP methods** are verbs; **URIs** identify resources.
- ✅ **HTTP methods mapping (CRUD):** `POST`=Create, `GET`=Read, `PUT`=Update (full), `PATCH`=Update (partial), `DELETE`=Delete.
- ✅ **Idempotency** is critical for biomedical IoT: retransmissions must be safe.
- ✅ **Status codes:** 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- ✅ **URIs:** plural nouns, hierarchical, versioned (`/v1/`), kebab-case.
- ✅ **Authentication:** OAuth 2.0 / JWT for clinical APIs; API Keys for device APIs.
- ✅ **Data formats:** JSON (~95% of modern APIs); XML only for legacy/FHIR.
- ✅ **HL7 FHIR** is the REST-based global standard for medical data exchange.
- ✅ **REST sits at the Application Layer** — between gateway and cloud/EHR, *not* between sensors and gateway.
- ✅ **MQTT/CoAP** are preferred for sensor-to-gateway; REST for gateway-to-cloud.
- ✅ **HATEOAS** = responses include navigation links for discoverability.
- ✅ **Pagination via `?limit=` and `?offset=`** prevents IoT gateway memory overflow.
- ✅ **Statelessness = horizontal scalability** — a key reason REST dominates cloud APIs.
- ✅ **Statelessness = no server-side session** — every request must carry full auth and data.
- ✅ **HTTPS (port 443)** is mandatory for any biomedical API handling PHI.
- ✅ **Cache-Control headers** reduce redundant queries (e.g., `/vitals?max-age=60`).
- ✅ **Error response** should always be a structured JSON object (not plain text) in modern APIs.
